<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Size Reduction in Post-Harvest Engineering?

**Size Reduction** (also termed **comminution** or **milling**) is a unit operation in which solid food and agricultural materials are mechanically broken into smaller particles through the application of **impact, compression, attrition, shear, or cutting** forces. In the context of Post-Harvest Engineering, it is the critical first step in converting raw farm produce (grains, oilseeds, spices, fruits, vegetables) into forms suitable for storage, packaging, processing, extraction, and consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> *"Size reduction is a mechanical process that reduces the dimensions of solid agricultural materials by applying external forces to overcome the internal molecular cohesion, thereby increasing the surface area per unit mass, improving digestibility, facilitating heat/mass transfer, and enabling separation of components."*

### Key Metrics Governing Size Reduction

- **Feed Size (D_f):** Average particle diameter of material entering the mill.
- **Product Size (D_p):** Average particle diameter of material leaving the mill.
- **Reduction Ratio (R):** Ratio R = D_f / D_p.
- **Specific Energy (E):** Energy consumed per unit mass, expressed in **kWh/ton** or **J/kg**.
- **Work Index (W_i):** Material property representing resistance to grinding, in **kWh/ton**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Post-Harvest Engineering focuses heavily on Size Reduction:**
> 1. Increases surface area → faster drying, cooking, extraction.
> 2. Enhances mixing uniformity in feed/food formulations.
> 3. Improves downstream separation (e.g., oil from oilseeds, flour from bran).
> 4. Reduces bulk volume for economical storage and transport.
> 5. Prepares material for thermal processing (roasting, baking, extrusion).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rock-Crushing Chef" Analogy

Imagine you are a **chef** with a hammer, a rolling pin, scissors, a grater, and a food processor. To break a whole coconut:
- A **jaw crusher** acts like a giant nutcracker (compression).
- A **hammer mill** acts like a mallet hitting downward (impact).
- A **roll mill** acts like a rolling pin pressing and shearing (compression + shear).
- A **disc attrition mill** is like rubbing the coconut between two rough sandpaper discs (attrition/friction).
- A **colloid mill** is like forcing a paste through a very narrow gap (shear + fluid shear).
- A **slicer/dicer** is like using a sharp knife in a defined geometry (cutting).

Different agricultural products need different "tools." Hard wheat needs a roller mill (compression), turmeric needs a hammer mill (impact), and fruit pulp for jam needs a pulper (cutting). The **right choice of equipment** depends on:
- Hardness and brittleness of material
- Moisture content
- Required final particle size
- Sensitivity to heat (heat-labile spices, sugar)

### 1.3 Classification of Size Reduction Forces

| Force Type | Mechanism | Typical Equipment | Example Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression** | Squeezing between two surfaces | Jaw crusher, Gyratory, Roll mill | Sugarcane, oilseeds |
| **Impact** | Sudden collision of particle with rotating body | Hammer mill, Impactor | Cereal grains, spices |
| **Attrition** | Rubbing/scraping of particle against another | Attrition mill, Burr mill | Wheat, cocoa |
| **Shear** | Sliding of one surface over another | Roller mill, Disc mill | Grain to flour |
| **Cutting** | Sharp edges slicing material | Slicer, Dicer, Shredder | Fruits, vegetables |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Three Laws of Comminution

The energy required for size reduction is mathematically described by three classical laws. Each is based on a different physical assumption about how the material fails.

### 2.1.1 Kick's Law (1885)
- **Assumption:** Energy required is proportional to the **reduction ratio** of particle size, ignoring surface area change.
- **Validity:** **Coarse crushing** (primary crushing from large lumps to smaller chunks).
- **Equation:**
$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

where K_K is Kick's constant.

### 2.1.2 Rittinger's Law (1867)
- **Assumption:** Energy required is proportional to the **new surface area created** during fracture.
- **Validity:** **Fine grinding** (producing very small particles from larger ones).
- **Equation:**
$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

### 2.1.3 Bond's Law (1952) — Most Widely Used in Industry
- **Assumption:** Energy is proportional to the **square root of the reciprocal of size** (geometric mean between Kick and Rittinger).
- **Validity:** Intermediate grinding; universal industrial application.
- **Equation:**
$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{100}{\mu}}$$

where W_i is the **Bond Work Index** and μ is the product fineness in microns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bond Work Index Values (Common Agricultural Materials)**
> - Wheat: ~7.4 kWh/ton
> - Corn (Maize): ~7.5 kWh/ton
> - Barley: ~7.0 kWh/ton
> - Oats: ~7.5 kWh/ton
> - Cottonseed: ~9.0 kWh/ton
> - Soybean: ~7.5 kWh/ton

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | E = K_K · ln(D_f / D_p) | K_K: kWh/ton | Coarse crushing (Kick) |
| 2 | E = K_R · (1/D_p − 1/D_f) | K_R: kWh·μm/ton | Fine grinding (Rittinger) |
| 3 | E = W_i · (10/√D_p − 10/√D_f) | D in μm, W_i: kWh/ton | General (Bond) |
| 4 | R = D_f / D_p | Dimensionless | Reduction ratio |
| 5 | N_c = 42.3 / √D (rpm) | D: mill internal diameter (m) | Critical speed of ball mill |
| 6 | P = m · E (kW) | m: feed rate (ton/h) | Power requirement |
| 7 | Q = (N · L · (2G + 2S)) / 144 | Q: ton/h, L: length (in), G: set, S: throw, N: strokes/min | Jaw crusher capacity |
| 8 | V_b = (π · d² / 4) · L | d: dia, L: length | Ball mill volume |
| 9 | E_kin = ½ · m · v² | Mass (kg), v: m/s | Hammer mill energy |
| 10 | v = π · D · N / 60 | D: m, N: rpm | Tip speed of hammer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units in KTU Exams:** Use SI units in derivations but **convert to μm for D** in Bond's equation. Always show unit conversion steps — these fetch full marks.

### 2.3 Why Are Three Laws Necessary?

The real-world utility is that **no single law** accurately predicts energy across the entire size range. Engineers use:
- **Kick's Law** for **primary crushing** (jaw crusher output: 100 mm → 10 mm)
- **Bond's Law** for **secondary grinding** (ball mill output: 1 mm → 0.1 mm)
- **Rittinger's Law** for **ultra-fine grinding** (jet mill output: 100 μm → 10 μm)

In a rice/flour mill, the same grain might pass through three different laws' regimes: paddy dehusker (compression/Kick), roller mill (shear/Bond), and pin mill (impact/Rittinger).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Speed of a Tumbling Ball Mill

A ball mill rotates on a horizontal axis. The grinding balls are carried up the inside wall of the mill and fall back to impact the charge. At a **critical speed**, the centrifugal force equals gravitational force at the mill wall, so the balls are pinned to the wall and do not cascade.

**Step 1:** Force balance at the mill wall (radius R, ball mass m, angular velocity ω):

$$F_{centrifugal} = F_{gravitational}$$

**Step 2:** Write both forces explicitly:

$$\frac{m \cdot v^2}{R} = m \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** Express linear velocity v in terms of angular velocity and radius:

$$v = \omega \cdot R \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^2 = \omega^2 \cdot R^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the force balance:

$$\frac{m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R^2}{R} = m \cdot g$$

$$\omega^2 \cdot R = g$$

**Step 5:** Convert angular velocity to rotational speed N (rev/s), noting ω = 2πN:

$$(2\pi N)^2 \cdot R = g$$

**Step 6:** Solve for N:

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

**Step 7:** Convert R to mill diameter D (R = D/2) and g = 9.81 m/s²:

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2g}{D}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 9.81}{D}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{19.62}{D}}$$

**Step 8:** Numerically simplify with D in metres:

$$N_c = 0.705 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{D}} \quad \text{(revolutions/second)}$$

**Step 9:** Convert to rpm by multiplying by 60:

$$N_c = 42.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{D}} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for critical speed. The **operating speed** is typically 50–80% of N_c for optimum grinding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Required by a Hammer Mill

**Step 1:** Each hammer of mass m moves with tip speed v. The kinetic energy of one hammer:

$$E_{hammer} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

**Step 2:** If the rotor carries n hammers and rotates at N (rpm), the angular frequency:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 3:** The tip speed of a hammer at radius R:

$$v = \omega \cdot R = \frac{2\pi N R}{60}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting v into the energy equation:

$$E_{hammer} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N R}{60}\right)^2$$

$$E_{hammer} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \frac{4\pi^2 N^2 R^2}{3600}$$

$$E_{hammer} = \frac{m \cdot \pi^2 \cdot N^2 \cdot R^2}{1800}$$

**Step 5:** Total energy per second (Power) from n hammers:

$$P = n \cdot E_{hammer} \cdot N / 60$$

$$P = \frac{n \cdot m \cdot \pi^2 \cdot N^3 \cdot R^2}{108000}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute numbers for a typical hammer mill (n = 16 hammers, m = 0.5 kg, N = 3600 rpm, R = 0.25 m):

$$P = \frac{16 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot (3600)^3 \cdot (0.25)^2}{108000}$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate stepwise — first compute (3600)³ = 4.67 × 10¹⁰, then (0.25)² = 0.0625:

$$P = \frac{16 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.87 \cdot 4.67 \times 10^{10} \cdot 0.0625}{108000}$$

**Step 8:** Numerator: 16 × 0.5 = 8; 8 × 9.87 = 78.96; 78.96 × 0.0625 = 4.935; 4.935 × 4.67 × 10¹⁰ = 2.305 × 10¹¹

**Step 9:** Divide by 1.08 × 10⁵:

$$P = \frac{2.305 \times 10^{11}}{1.08 \times 10^5} = 2.13 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 2134 \text{ kW}$$

This is theoretical; actual power is lower due to impact efficiency (~30–50%).

## 3.3 Numerical Application: Bond's Law (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A feed of 5 mm average size is ground to 0.1 mm in a ball mill. The Bond Work Index for the material is 12 kWh/ton. Calculate the energy required per ton of feed.

**Step 1:** Convert sizes to microns (D in μm, but formula uses 10/√D; standard form):
- D_f = 5 mm = 5000 μm
- D_p = 0.1 mm = 100 μm

**Step 2:** Apply Bond's equation:

$$E = W_i \cdot \left[\frac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Compute √D_p = √100 = 10, so 10/√D_p = 10/10 = 1.0

**Step 4:** Compute √D_f = √5000 = 70.71, so 10/√D_f = 10/70.71 = 0.1414

**Step 5:** Subtract:

$$\frac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}} = 1.0 - 0.1414 = 0.8586$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by W_i = 12:

$$E = 12 \cdot 0.8586 = 10.30 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

**Answer:** Energy required = **10.30 kWh per ton of feed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem is a classic KTU ESE question. The units trap: If D is in mm, formula changes. Always convert to μm and use the standard Bond form.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection for Agricultural Products

| Material | Hardness | Moisture | Recommended Equipment | Final Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheat grain | Hard | 12–14% | Roller mill (Break rolls → Reduction rolls) | Flour, atta |
| Paddy (rice) | Hard | 12% | Rubber roll sheller → Paddy separator | Milled rice |
| Maize (corn) | Medium-hard | 13% | Hammer mill / Degerminator | Cornmeal, grits |
| Oilseeds (mustard, soybean) | Hard | 8% | Roll crusher → Flaking rolls | Oil extraction |
| Spices (turmeric, chilli) | Brittle, hard | 8–10% | Hammer mill with cyclone | Powder |
| Sugarcane | Fibrous | 70% | Cane crusher (3-roller) | Juice |
| Tomato, mango | Soft, high moisture | 80–90% | Pulper / Fruit mill | Pulp, juice |
| Apple, potato | Firm | 80% | Slicer / Dicer | Chips, dehydration |
| Coconut (copra) | Hard | 6% | Hammer mill | Oil extraction |
| Coffee beans | Brittle | 8% | Burr mill | Ground coffee |
| Sugar (crystals) | Hard | <1% | Roller mill | Icing sugar |
| Leafy greens | Soft, fibrous | 90% | Shredder / Cutter | Drying, packaging |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow Chart of Size Reduction Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SIZE REDUCTION EQUIPMENT] --> B[CRUSHERS]
    A --> C[GRINDERS]
    A --> D[CUTTERS]

    B --> B1[Jaw Crusher]
    B --> B2[Gyratory Crusher]
    B --> B3[Smooth Roll Crusher]

    C --> C1[Impact Grinders]
    C --> C2[Attrition Grinders]
    C --> C3[Tumbling Mills]
    C --> C4[Ultra-Fine Grinders]

    C1 --> C1a[Hammer Mill]
    C1 --> C1b[Impactor]
    C2 --> C2a[Disc Attrition Mill]
    C2 --> C2b[Burr Mill]
    C3 --> C3a[Ball Mill]
    C3 --> C3b[Rod Mill]
    C4 --> C4a[Fluid Jet Pulverizer]
    C4 --> C4b[Colloid Mill]

    D --> D1[Slicing Machine]
    D --> D2[Dicing Machine]
    D --> D3[Shredding Machine]
    D --> D4[Pulping Machine]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hammer Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper] --> B[Feed Control Gate]
    B --> C[Rotating Shaft with Hammers]
    C --> D[Screens and Liners]
    D --> E[Discharge Outlet]
    F[Electric Motor] --> G[Belt Drive]
    G --> C
    H[Air Suction Fan] --> D
    H --> I[Cyclone Separator]
    I --> J[Bag Filter]
    J --> K[Atmospheric Exhaust]
```

**Operating Cycle:** Feed enters the hopper → feed gate controls the rate → rotor spins hammers at high tip speed (typically 60–120 m/s) → particles are struck → fractured particles pass through the perforated screen → air suction carries fines to the cyclone → oversize recirculates.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Material Flow through a Roller Milling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cleaned Wheat] --> B[First Break Roller Mill]
    B --> C[Plansifter]
    C --> D1[Coarse Bran]
    C --> D2[Semolina]
    C --> D3[Flour]
    D2 --> E[Second Break Roller Mill]
    E --> F[Purifier]
    F --> G1[Pure Semolina]
    F --> G2[Residue]
    G1 --> H[Reduction Roller Mill]
    H --> I[Final Plansifter]
    I --> J1[Flour Streams]
    I --> J2[Feed Grade Bran]
```

**Function:** Sequential size reduction — first break opens the grain, plansifters separate by size, purifiers remove bran, reduction rolls progressively grind to fine flour. This is the standard "gradual reduction" system in **commercial roller flour mills**.

## 4.4 Schematic — Jaw Crusher Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Hopper] --> B[Fixed Jaw Plate]
    B --> C[Swinging Jaw Plate]
    C --> D[Eccentric Shaft]
    D --> E[Toggle Plate and Tension Rod]
    E --> F[Discharge Opening]
    G[Flywheel Drive] --> D
```

**Mechanism:** Material enters between the fixed jaw and swinging jaw. The eccentric shaft drives the swing jaw in a vertical elliptical motion, compressing material against the fixed jaw. Discharge size is controlled by the **toggle plate position** at the bottom.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State and explain the three laws of size reduction. Mention one limitation of each law."**

**Model Answer:**

The three classical laws describing energy consumption in size reduction are:

**1. Kick's Law (1885):** Energy required is proportional to the logarithm of the reduction ratio.
$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$
*Limitation:* Does not account for new surface area created; valid only for **coarse crushing** where the particle is much larger than the fractured product.

**2. Rittinger's Law (1867):** Energy required is proportional to the new surface area created.
$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$
*Limitation:* Assumes all energy goes to creating new surface; in reality much energy is lost as heat/sound; valid only for **fine grinding**.

**3. Bond's Law (1952):** Energy required is proportional to the difference in reciprocals of square roots of product and feed sizes.
$$E = W_i \cdot \left[\frac{10}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right]$$
*Limitation:* Empirical; requires accurate work index data; works best for intermediate sizes (1–10 mm range).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students write the three laws but **forget the limitation** — this loses 1 mark out of 3.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Distinguish between a hammer mill and a ball mill with respect to principle, equipment construction, and product fineness."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hammer Mill | Ball Mill |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Impact (sudden striking) | Impact + Attrition (cascading balls) |
| **Construction** | Rotor with pivoted hammers + perforated screen | Horizontal cylindrical drum with grinding media (balls) |
| **Particle size fed** | 10–50 mm | 1–10 mm |
| **Product fineness** | Coarse to medium (50–500 μm) | Medium to fine (10–200 μm) |
| **Feed moisture** | Can handle up to 15% | Limited to <5% for fine grinding |
| **Application** | Spices, grains, oilcake, wood | Flour, cement, ceramic, fine chemicals |
| **Power consumption** | Lower per ton | Higher per ton for fine product |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the construction and working of a **smooth roll crusher** with a neat sketch. Explain how reduction ratio is controlled in it.

**(b) [7 marks]** A feed of limestone with average size 2 cm is fed to a smooth roll crusher. The rolls have a diameter of 1 m and rotate at 100 rpm. The gap setting is 5 mm. The coefficient of friction between roll and particle is 0.3. Find the **angle of nip**, **maximum feed size**, and **theoretical capacity** in ton/h. Take density of limestone = 2500 kg/m³.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction:**
- Two heavy, smooth, cylindrical rolls mounted on a horizontal, parallel axis.
- One roll is **fixed** (bearing is stationary); the other is **movable** (spring-loaded against a fixed screw or hydraulic jack).
- Each roll is driven through a **belt drive** from a separate motor, or both rolls from a common motor with gears.
- A **hopper** above feeds material by gravity.
- A **scraper blade** on each roll removes any adhering material.
- The gap between the rolls is adjustable via a **wedge mechanism** or screw.

**Working:**
- Feed enters from the top, is drawn between the two rolls by friction, and is crushed by **compression** and **shear**.
- The nipping angle determines the maximum particle size that can be gripped.
- Discharge size ≈ **gap setting** (the spacing between rolls).

**Reduction Ratio Control:**
1. **Adjustable gap** between rolls (by screw or hydraulic packer).
2. **Spring-loaded safety mechanism** — allows the movable roll to recede if a tramp metal piece passes, preventing damage.
3. **Speed differential** between rolls (faster roll can be 1.1–1.3× the slower) adds shear.
4. Typical R.R. for smooth rolls = **4:1 to 6:1**.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Angle of Nip (θ)**

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left[\frac{(r_1 + r_2) - S}{(r_1 + r_2)}\right]$$

Here r_1 = r_2 = 0.5 m (roll radius), S = 0.005 m (gap).

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left[\frac{1.0 - 0.005}{1.0}\right] = \cos^{-1}(0.995) = 5.73°$$

**Step 2: Maximum Feed Size**

The maximum particle diameter D_max that can be nipped:

$$D_{max} = S + 2 \cdot R \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$$

Using the approximation (when θ is small, D_max ≈ S + 2·R·(1−cosθ)):

$$D_{max} = 0.005 + 2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0.995)$$

$$D_{max} = 0.005 + 1.0 \cdot 0.005 = 0.010 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Capacity (Q)**

$$Q = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot N \cdot S \cdot \rho$$

Where D = roll diameter = 1 m, L = roll length (assume standard L = 0.5 m), N = 100/60 = 1.667 rev/s, S = 0.005 m, ρ = 2500 kg/m³.

$$Q = 3.1416 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1.667 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 2500$$

$$Q = 3.1416 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1.667 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 2500$$

**Step-by-step:**
- 3.1416 × 0.5 = 1.5708
- 1.5708 × 1.667 = 2.6186
- 2.6186 × 0.005 = 0.01309
- 0.01309 × 2500 = 32.73 kg/s
- Convert to ton/h: 32.73 × 3.6 = **117.8 ton/h**

**Answer:** Angle of nip = **5.73°**, Maximum feed size = **10 mm**, Capacity = **117.8 ton/h**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a **fluid energy pulverizer (jet mill)** with a diagram. List its **two advantages** and **two limitations** for food processing.

**(b) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **wet grinding in a colloid mill** and **dry grinding in a hammer mill** for spice processing. Compute the **tip speed of the hammer** for a hammer mill of rotor diameter 0.4 m rotating at 3000 rpm.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Principle of Jet Mill:**

A fluid energy pulverizer uses **high-velocity gas jets** (air, steam, or inert gas) to accelerate particles and cause **inter-particle collisions** — no moving grinding parts come into contact with the material.

**Working:**
1. **Compressed gas (air/steam at 7–20 bar)** is introduced tangentially into a shallow circular grinding chamber through **multiple nozzles**.
2. The gas forms a **spiral vortex**.
3. Feed is injected from the side into the vortex.
4. Particles are **accelerated to supersonic speeds (200–300 m/s)** and collide with each other, causing **fracture**.
5. The vortex motion also creates centrifugal classification: **fine particles** migrate inward and exit through a central outlet; **oversize particles** stay on the periphery and are recirculated.
6. Typical particle size produced: **1 to 50 μm** (ultrafine).

**Diagram (Block form):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Injector] --> B[Grinding Chamber]
    C[Compressed Gas Nozzles] --> B
    B --> D[Vortex Zone]
    D --> E[Fines Outlet Center]
    D --> F[Oversize Recirculation]
    F --> B
```

**Advantages for Food Processing:**
1. **No contamination** — no metal-to-material contact, ideal for sterile/pharma food applications.
2. **Negligible heat generation** — suitable for **heat-sensitive materials** (spice oils, vitamins, enzymes).

**Limitations:**
1. **High energy consumption** due to compressed gas; expensive to operate.
2. **Limited throughput** — not economical for high-tonnage applications (only used for very fine, low-volume products).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Colloid Mill (Wet) | Hammer Mill (Dry) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Shear + hydraulic shear | Impact |
| **Feed state** | Liquid slurry / paste | Dry solid |
| **Product** | Paste, suspension, emulsion | Dry powder |
| **Particle size** | 1–100 μm | 50–500 μm |
| **Moisture** | High (60–90%) | Low (<15%) |
| **Spice suitability** | For **oleoresin, paste** (ginger, garlic paste) | For **dry powders** (turmeric, chilli) |
| **Heat generation** | High (cooling jacket required) | Moderate (air cooling) |
| **Contamination** | Low (stone/metal discs) | Possible (hammer wear) |

**Tip Speed Calculation:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

Given D = 0.4 m, N = 3000 rpm:

$$v = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 3000}{60}$$

$$v = \frac{3769.9}{60} = 62.83 \text{ m/s}$$

**Answer:** Tip speed of hammer = **62.83 m/s** (typical for hammer mills: 60–120 m/s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit conversions** in Bond's law — students forget to convert mm to μm and get wrong answer (lose 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s** in ball mill capacity — silent error.
> 3. **Confusing between angle of nip (θ) and maximum feed size** — they are different calculations.
> 4. **Not writing the formula before substitution** in numerical problems — even if the answer is correct, lose 1 mark for "no formula".
> 5. **In descriptive answers**, writing only the construction but **not the working principle** — KTU expects both.
> 6. **In cutting machine answers**, students forget to mention the **final product form** (slice, dice, shred, pulp) — important to specify.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Size Reduction Equipment**

### A. Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- Definition of size reduction, reduction ratio R = D_f / D_p
- Three laws: Kick (coarse), Rittinger (fine), Bond (intermediate)
- Bond's Law formula: E = W_i · [10/√D_p − 10/√D_f] (D in μm)
- Critical speed formula: N_c = 42.3/√D (D in m, N in rpm)
- Operating speed = 50–80% of critical speed for ball mills
- Hammer mill tip speed: 60–120 m/s

### B. Equipment — Quick Recall
- **Jaw crusher** → compression, primary crushing, 3:1 to 6:1 R.R.
- **Gyratory crusher** → compression, continuous feed, higher capacity than jaw
- **Smooth roll crusher** → compression + shear, soft to medium materials (sugarcane, oilcake)
- **Hammer mill** → impact, spices/grains, 20:1 to 40:1 R.R.
- **Disc attrition mill** → friction, grain to flour, fine grinding
- **Burr mill** → attrition, coffee, wheat germ
- **Ball mill** → impact + attrition, fine flour, cement
- **Rod mill** → line contact, prevents over-grinding
- **Fluid jet mill** → inter-particle impact, ultrafine 1–50 μm
- **Colloid mill** → shear, paste/emulsion (1–100 μm)
- **Slicer/Dicer/Shredder/Pulper** → cutting action, fruit & vegetable processing

### C. Numerical Problem Triggers
- "Calculate angle of nip" → cos⁻¹[(r_1+r_2−S)/(r_1+r_2)]
- "Maximum feed size" → D_max = 0.5·S + 0.5·[scaling using friction coefficient]
- "Capacity of roll crusher" → Q = π·D·L·N·S·ρ
- "Energy by Bond's law" → E = W_i·[10/√D_p − 10/√D_f] (μm units)
- "Critical speed" → N_c = 42.3/√D
- "Tip speed" → v = π·D·N/60

### D. Application Mapping
- Wheat → Roller mill (Bond's law region)
- Oilseeds → Roll crusher (Kick's law region)
- Spices → Hammer mill (Rittinger's law region)
- Pulps/pastes → Colloid mill (shear)
- Ultrafine powders → Jet mill
- Fruits/vegetables → Slicer/Dicer/Cutter

### E. Examiner's "Catch" Questions to Prepare
1. "Why is Bond's law preferred over Kick and Rittinger?" — *Universal validity, empirical verification.*
2. "Why do ball mills operate below critical speed?" — *Below critical: balls cascade and impact; at critical: balls are pinned to wall, no grinding.*
3. "Why is the angle of nip important?" — *Determines maximum feed size that can be gripped; too large particles slip and not crush.*
4. "Why is fluid jet mill preferred for heat-sensitive foods?" — *No moving parts, negligible heat, no contamination.*
5. "Difference between coarse, medium, and fine grinding — what equipment?" — *Crushers / Hammer / Tumbling mills / Ultra-fine grinders.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drying: Principle, Moisture and Its Removal, Utilities of Drying, Moisture Content and Its Measurements, Moisture Content Representation, Determination Methods: Direct and Indirect Methods, Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC), Method for Determination of EMC: Dynamic Methods and Static Methods, Drying Theory, Methods of Drying, Drying Rate Periods, Mechanical Drying Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Drying — Formal Definition

**Drying** is a unit operation in post-harvest engineering defined as the removal of moisture (usually water) from a biological material (grains, seeds, fruits, vegetables, food products) by the application of thermal energy, resulting in simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** phenomena. The moisture is evaporated from the product and carried away by a drying medium (typically heated air).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Drying is a process in which moisture is removed from a product by thermal means, involving the transfer of heat from a heating medium to the product and the transfer of mass (moisture) from inside the product to the surrounding atmosphere. It is a simultaneous heat- and mass-transfer operation governed by the principles of thermodynamics and transport phenomena.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sponge on a Hot Tawa"

Imagine a freshly washed kitchen sponge placed on a hot tawa (griddle):
- The heat from the tawa transfers into the sponge.
- Water from the sponge rises to the surface and evaporates.
- The water vapor escapes into the surrounding air.
- The sponge dries out over time.

**In drying:**
- The **tawa** = heating medium (hot air, hot surface, or microwave energy).
- The **sponge** = biological material (grain, seed, fruit).
- The **water escaping as steam** = moisture being removed.
- The **drying time** depends on temperature, air velocity, and how tightly water is held inside the material.

> [!NOTE]
> The "tighter the water is held" inside the material corresponds to **bound moisture**, which requires more energy to remove than free surface moisture. This is why different drying periods (constant rate, falling rate) occur.

## 1.3 Moisture and Its Removal — The Two Forms of Water

Biological materials contain water in **two distinct forms**:

| Type of Moisture | Definition | Energy Required | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free Moisture** | Water held loosely on the surface and in large pores/capillaries; behaves like pure water. | Low (≈ latent heat of vaporization, **2260 kJ/kg**) | Surface and macro-capillaries |
| **Bound Moisture** | Water held tightly by physical-chemical forces (adsorption, absorption, osmotic bonds). | Higher (over and above latent heat) | Inside cell walls, micro-capillaries, chemical bonds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bound water** is the water that is **not easily removed** by ordinary drying. The point up to which moisture can be removed by given drying conditions defines the **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)**.

## 1.4 Utilities / Purposes of Drying in Post-Harvest Engineering

Drying is one of the **most important unit operations** in post-harvest processing. Its key utilities are:

1. **Preservation of agricultural produce** — Reduces water activity (a_w) below the threshold for microbial growth, preventing spoilage.
2. **Safe long-term storage** — Lowers moisture content to safe storage levels (e.g., paddy: **12–14% w.b.**, wheat: **12–13% w.b.**, maize: **13–14% w.b.**).
3. **Reduction in mass and volume** → Lower transportation and storage costs.
4. **Prevents insect infestation and fungal attack** (Aspergillus, Penicillium).
5. **Improves milling quality** and seed viability.
6. **Preparation for further processing** — milling, oil extraction, parboiling.
7. **Value addition** — Production of dried fruits, powders, flakes, chips.

## 1.5 Moisture Content — Definition

**Moisture content (M)** is the quantity of water contained in a material, expressed as a percentage of either the wet mass or the dry mass of the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is moisture content critical?**
> It directly determines:
> - **Storage life** (safe moisture for storage is product-specific).
> - **Market price** (graded on moisture content).
> - **Energy required for drying**.
> - **End-use quality** (milling, germination, cooking quality).

## 1.6 Moisture Content Representation — Two Bases

There are **two standard ways** to represent moisture content in KTU/Post-Harvest Engineering:

### (a) Wet Basis (w.b.) — Most commonly used in commerce & practice
$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100 = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

Where:
- $W_w$ = mass of water (kg)
- $W_d$ = mass of dry matter (kg)
- $W_w + W_d$ = total wet mass

### (b) Dry Basis (d.b.) — Used in engineering calculations
$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \;\; (\text{kg water/kg dry solid})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Relationship (frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> $$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$$
> $$M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}} \times 100$$

## 1.7 Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) — Definition

**Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** is the moisture content at which a hygroscopic biological material is in **dynamic equilibrium** with the surrounding air — i.e., the rate of moisture desorption from the material equals the rate of moisture adsorption from the air. **No net moisture transfer** occurs at EMC.

**Controlling factors:** Temperature (T) and Relative Humidity (RH) of the surrounding air.

> [!NOTE]
> At EMC, the **vapor pressure of water in the material** equals the **partial pressure of water vapor in the air**.

**Common EMC equations used in KTU syllabus:**

1. **Henderson Equation:**
$$1 - RH = \exp(-C \cdot T_{abs} \cdot M_{db}^{\,n})$$

2. **Chung-Pfost Equation:**
$$RH = \exp\left(-\frac{A}{T_{abs} \cdot M_{db}} \cdot \exp(B \cdot M_{db})\right)$$

3. **Oswin Equation:**
$$M_{db} = A \cdot \left(\frac{RH}{1 - RH}\right)^{B}$$

Where $A$, $B$, $C$, $n$ are empirical constants; $T_{abs}$ is in Kelvin.

## 1.8 Quick Recap of Section 1
- Drying = simultaneous heat & mass transfer.
- Two moisture forms: **free** and **bound**.
- Moisture content: **wet basis** (commercial) vs **dry basis** (engineering).
- **EMC** = moisture equilibrium between material and air.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Drying — The Underlying Physics

Drying is fundamentally governed by **two coupled transport processes**:

### A. Heat Transfer (from hot air → product)
$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{air} - T_{surface}) \quad (\text{W})$$

Where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area (m²)
- $T_{air} - T_{surface}$ = temperature driving force (K)

### B. Mass Transfer (moisture from product → air)
$$\frac{dm}{dt} = h_m \cdot A \cdot (p_{s,m} - p_{v,air}) \quad (\text{kg/s})$$

Where:
- $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·Pa)
- $p_{s,m}$ = vapor pressure at material surface (Pa)
- $p_{v,air}$ = partial pressure of vapor in bulk air (Pa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The driving forces for drying are temperature and vapor-pressure gradients.** When $T_{air} > T_{surface}$ and $p_{s,m} > p_{v,air}$, drying occurs.

## 2.2 Moisture Content Measurement Methods — Direct vs Indirect

### Direct Methods (Absolute, Destructive)
Direct methods physically isolate and measure the water removed.

| Method | Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Oven drying** (air-oven at **103°C** for 24 h) | Loss in weight = moisture | Standard for grains (IS/ISO/AOAC) |
| **Vacuum oven** (60–70°C, low pressure) | Drying at lower T prevents degradation | Heat-sensitive materials, oilseeds |
| **Distillation method** (toluene/xylene) | Co-distill water with solvent | High-fat/oil products, spices |
| **Desiccation method** | Sample over P₂O₅ in desiccator | Reference method |
| **Chemical (Karl Fischer titration)** | Reacts with water | Trace moisture in powders |
| **Thermogravimetric** | Mass loss vs temperature | Research |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU standard reference method:** Hot-air oven at **130 ± 3°C for 1 hour** (cereals) or **103°C for 24 h** (general biological materials) — gravimetric determination.

### Indirect Methods (Secondary, Non-destructive)
Indirect methods measure a physical property correlated to moisture.

| Method | Property Measured | Advantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical resistance** | Resistance (ohms) of grain mass | Cheap, portable |
| **Capacitance / dielectric** | Dielectric constant | Rapid, accurate at low MC |
| **Infrared / NIR** | Absorbance at 1940 nm | Multi-parameter, on-line |
| **Microwave** | Attenuation/reflection | Penetrates bulk |
| **NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance)** | Hydrogen proton relaxation | Highly accurate, lab only |
| **Acoustic** | Sound velocity/attenuation | Emerging tech |

## 2.3 Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) — Determination Methods

### A. Static Methods (No air flow / stagnant)
- Material is held in a **sealed environment** over **saturated salt solutions** producing known RH.
- Weighed periodically until **constant mass** (EMC reached).
- Common salts: LiCl (11% RH), MgCl₂ (33%), NaCl (75%), K₂SO₄ (97%).

### B. Dynamic Methods (Continuous air flow)
- Conditioned air of known T and RH is passed **continuously** over the sample.
- Faster equilibration, more accurate.
- Equipment: **EMC chamber**, **climate-controlled cabinets**, **Sorption analyzer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Desorption EMC** > **Adsorption EMC** at the same T, RH — this hysteresis is critical for storage design.

## 2.4 Drying Theory — Internal Moisture Movement

Inside the material, moisture moves by:
1. **Liquid diffusion** (Fick's law): through pores and capillaries.
2. **Capillary flow** (surface tension driven).
3. **Vapor diffusion** (Knudsen/molecular) inside pores.
4. **Thermal diffusion** (Soret effect).
5. **Moisture migration due to temperature gradients**.

For most grains, **liquid diffusion** dominates — leading to Fick's second law:
$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$$

Where $D_{eff}$ = effective moisture diffusivity (m²/s).

## 2.5 Methods of Drying — Classification

### A. Sun Drying (Traditional)
- Open sun exposure on threshing floor, mats, or platforms.
- Slow, weather-dependent, contamination risk.

### B. Shade Drying
- For heat-sensitive products (medicinal herbs, mushrooms).

### C. Mechanical Drying (Engineered)
- **Heated air dryers** — convective.
- **Contact/drum dryers** — conductive.
- **Radiation dryers** — infrared.
- **Dielectric dryers** — microwave, RF.
- **Fluidized bed, spouted bed** — for particulate materials.
- **Lyophilization** (freeze drying) — high quality.

## 2.6 Drying Rate Periods — The Fundamental Drying Curve

A typical drying curve (**M vs t**) and rate curve (**dM/dt vs M**) has these distinct periods:

| Period | Description | Rate Controlling Step |
|---|---|---|
| **I — Initial Adjustment (A-B)** | Material warms up to wet-bulb temperature; transient | Heat transfer only |
| **II — Constant Rate (B-C)** | Surface is saturated; behaves like free water; constant rate | External mass transfer (evaporation from surface) |
| **III — First Falling Rate (C-D)** | Surface dries partially; wet patches shrink | Liquid diffusion through partially dry layer |
| **IV — Second Falling Rate (D-E)** | Surface fully dry; moisture inside moves by diffusion; rate drops sharply | Internal diffusion |

> [!NOTE]
> **For most grains and seeds, the constant-rate period is short or absent.** Most of the drying occurs in the **falling-rate regime**, controlled by **internal moisture diffusion**.

**Drying rate (R) is defined as:**
$$R = -\frac{dM_{db}}{d\theta} = \frac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}}$$

## 2.7 Mechanical Drying Types — Detailed Classification

### A. Batch Dryers
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Bin dryer** | Grain in a bin; heated air blown through perforated floor |
| **Flatbed / Tray dryer** | Thin layers of product on trays with cross-flow air |
| **Vertical baffle (LSU type)** | Column with baffles; air zigzags through |
| **Batch recirculating** | Grain recirculates for uniform drying |
| **Sack dryer** | Perforated sacks stacked; air blown through |

### B. Continuous Flow Dryers
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Columnar (mixed-flow) dryer** | Vertical columns with air ducts and grain columns |
| **Cross-flow dryer** | Air flows perpendicular to grain flow |
| **Concurrent / Counter-flow dryer** | Air and grain flow in same/opposite directions |
| **Rotary dryer** | Inclined rotating drum; tumbling action |
| **Fluidized bed dryer** | Air suspends particles; high heat transfer |
| **Belt / Conveyor dryer** | Product on perforated moving belt |
| **Tunnel dryer** | Truck/trolley passes through a tunnel |
| **Drum dryer** | Liquid/paste dried on rotating heated drum |
| **Spray dryer** | Liquid atomized into hot air stream |
| **Pneumatic dryer** | Particles conveyed in hot air stream |

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Wet basis moisture content (%) |
| 2 | $M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$ | Dry basis moisture content (kg/kg) |
| 3 | $M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ | Wet → Dry basis conversion |
| 4 | $M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}} \times 100$ | Dry → Wet basis conversion |
| 5 | $1 - RH = \exp(-C \cdot T \cdot M_{db}^{\,n})$ | Henderson EMC equation |
| 6 | $RH = \exp\left(-\frac{A}{T \cdot M_{db}} \exp(B M_{db})\right)$ | Chung-Pfost EMC equation |
| 7 | $M_{db} = A \left(\frac{RH}{1 - RH}\right)^B$ | Oswin EMC equation |
| 8 | $Q = h A \Delta T$ | Heat transfer rate to material |
| 9 | $\frac{dm}{dt} = h_m A (p_s - p_a)$ | Mass transfer rate (moisture) |
| 10 | $R = -\frac{dM_{db}}{d\theta}$ | Drying rate definition |
| 11 | $\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's 2nd law (internal diffusion) |
| 12 | $t = \frac{(M_1 - M_2) W_d}{A \cdot R}$ | Time for batch drying |
| 13 | $W_{air} = \frac{m_w}{H_1 - H_2}$ | Air required (kg dry air/kg water) |
| 14 | $Q_{total} = m_w \cdot \lambda$ | Total heat for evaporation |
| 15 | $t = \frac{L^2}{\pi^2 D_{eff}} \ln\left(\frac{M_1 - M_e}{M_2 - M_e}\right)$ | Diffusion-based drying time (slab) |

Where:
- $m_w$ = mass of water removed (kg)
- $H_1, H_2$ = inlet/outlet air humidity ratios (kg water/kg dry air)
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization ≈ **2260 kJ/kg** at 100°C
- $L$ = half-thickness of slab (m)
- $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture content (d.b.)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wet Basis to Dry Basis Conversion (Frequently asked in KTU)

**Given:** A sample of paddy weighing $W$ kg loses $w$ kg of water when oven-dried.

**Step 1 — Define total mass before drying (initial wet mass):**
$$W_{initial} = W = W_w + W_d$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry matter is the mass after complete drying:**
$$W_{final} = W_d$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water removed:**
$$W_w = W_{initial} - W_{final}$$

**Step 4 — Write wet basis definition (decimal form):**
$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} = \frac{W_w}{W}$$

**Step 5 — Write dry basis definition:**
$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}$$

**Step 6 — Express $W_w$ in terms of $M_{wb}$ and $W$:**
$$W_w = M_{wb} \cdot W$$

**Step 7 — Express $W_d$ in terms of $W$:**
$$W_d = W - W_w = W - M_{wb} W = W(1 - M_{wb})$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $W_w$ and $W_d$ into $M_{db}$ equation:**
$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb} \cdot W}{W(1 - M_{wb})}$$

**Step 9 — Simplify (W cancels):**
$$\boxed{M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}}$$

**Step 10 — To convert back, solve for $M_{wb}$:**

Starting from $M_{db}(1 - M_{wb}) = M_{wb}$:
$$M_{db} - M_{db} M_{wb} = M_{wb}$$
$$M_{db} = M_{wb} + M_{db} M_{wb} = M_{wb}(1 + M_{db})$$

$$\boxed{M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** Paddy with $M_{wb} = 24\% = 0.24$ → $M_{db} = 0.24 / (1 - 0.24) = 0.316$ (kg water/kg dry solid). Always use decimals in formulas!

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Drying Time in Constant Rate Period

**Given:** Material with initial moisture $M_1$ (d.b.), dried to $M_2$ (d.b.), surface area $A$, drying rate $R$ (kg/m²·h), dry mass $W_d$.

**Step 1 — Water removed (mass balance):**
$$m_w = W_d (M_1 - M_2)$$

**Step 2 — Drying rate definition (constant rate regime):**
$$R = \frac{m_w}{A \cdot t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{m_w}{A \cdot R}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $m_w$:**
$$t = \frac{W_d (M_1 - M_2)}{A \cdot R}$$

$$\boxed{t = \frac{W_d (M_1 - M_2)}{A \cdot R} \quad (\text{constant rate period})}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Air Requirement for Drying

**Given:** A dryer removes $m_w$ kg of water per hour; inlet air humidity ratio $H_1$ (kg water/kg dry air); outlet $H_2$.

**Step 1 — Moisture balance on the air stream:**
- Water picked up by air = water lost by material
$$m_w = \dot{m}_{air} (H_2 - H_1)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for dry air mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{m_w}{H_2 - H_1} \;\; (\text{kg dry air/s})$$

**Step 3 — Total heat supplied (sensible + latent):**
$$Q = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} (T_1 - T_0) + m_w \cdot \lambda$$

Where $T_1$ = inlet air temperature, $T_0$ = ambient, $c_{p,air} \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Falling Rate Period — Diffusion Model (Fickian)

**Assumption:** One-dimensional moisture diffusion, slab geometry, $D_{eff}$ constant.

**Step 1 — Apply Fick's 2nd law:**
$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions:**
- $M(x, 0) = M_1$ (uniform initial)
- $M(L, t) = M_e$ (surface at EMC)
- $\partial M / \partial x = 0$ at $x = 0$ (symmetry, center of slab)

**Step 3 — Solution by separation of variables (Fourier series, first term):**
$$MR = \frac{\bar{M}(t) - M_e}{M_1 - M_e} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{4 L^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Take natural log:**
$$\ln(MR) = \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2}\right) - \frac{\pi^2 D_{eff}}{4 L^2} t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for time to reach average moisture $\bar{M}$:**
$$\boxed{t = \frac{4 L^2}{\pi^2 D_{eff}} \ln\left(\frac{8 (M_1 - M_e)}{\pi^2 (\bar{M} - M_e)}\right)}$$

**Approximate form (drop $8/\pi^2$):**
$$t \approx \frac{L^2}{\pi^2 D_{eff}} \ln\left(\frac{M_1 - M_e}{M_2 - M_e}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Effective diffusivity estimation for grains** (typical values):
- Paddy: $D_{eff} \approx 10^{-11}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s
- Wheat: $D_{eff} \approx 10^{-11}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s
- Maize: $D_{eff} \approx 10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Henderson EMC Equation — Linearized Form

**Step 1 — Start with Henderson equation:**
$$1 - RH = \exp(-C \cdot T_{abs} \cdot M_{db}^{\,n})$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log of both sides:**
$$\ln(1 - RH) = -C \cdot T_{abs} \cdot M_{db}^{\,n}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by –1:**
$$-\ln(1 - RH) = C \cdot T_{abs} \cdot M_{db}^{\,n}$$

**Step 4 — Take ln again:**
$$\ln[-\ln(1 - RH)] = \ln(C) + \ln(T_{abs}) + n \ln(M_{db})$$

**Step 5 — Re-arrange for linear regression:**
$$\ln[-\ln(1 - RH)] = n \ln(M_{db}) + [\ln(C) + \ln(T_{abs})]$$

**Step 6 — Plot $\ln[-\ln(1 - RH)]$ vs $\ln(M_{db})$:**
- Slope = **$n$**
- Intercept gives $C$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical use:** Henderson constants for common grains are tabulated in ASABE Standards (e.g., for paddy: $C = 1.9187 \times 10^{-5}$, $n = 1.582$ in some references — **always verify from lab data in KTU numericals**).

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1: Moisture Content Conversion
A wheat sample has wet basis moisture content of **20%**. Find (a) dry basis moisture content, (b) moisture content if 50 kg wet wheat is dried to 40 kg.

**Solution:**

(a) Using conversion:
$$M_{db} = \frac{0.20}{1 - 0.20} = 0.25 \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

(b) Initial water = 0.20 × 50 = 10 kg. After drying, water = 50 − 40 − 0 = ... wait, the dry mass is constant:

Dry mass = 50 − 10 = 40 kg.
Final water = 40 − 40 = 0 kg. **That gives 0% — not realistic.**

**Recalculation:** Final wet mass = 40 kg; dry mass = 40 kg → water = 0. **The problem implies dry mass = 40 kg remains, but final water = 0 is too much drying.** Assume 40 kg is the **final wet mass**:

Final water = $W_f - W_d$ ... we need $W_d$. The dry mass is constant:
$W_d = 50 \times (1 - 0.20) = 40$ kg.

Final wet = 40 kg → final water = 0 kg → unrealistic.

**Correct interpretation:** If 50 kg wet (at 20% w.b.) is dried to wet mass 40 kg, but dry mass must remain 40 kg, this means all water removed. So 40 kg is final dry mass. Then:
$$M_{wb, final} = 0\%$$
$$M_{db, final} = 0$$

**A more realistic problem:** 50 kg wet paddy at 24% w.b. dried to 14% w.b. Find water removed and final mass.

Dry mass = $50 \times (1 - 0.24) = 38$ kg.
Final wet mass = $38 / (1 - 0.14) = 38 / 0.86 = 44.19$ kg.
Water removed = $50 - 44.19 = 5.81$ kg.

### Example 2: Drying Time in Constant Rate Period
A 100 kg batch of paddy (initial MC = 30% w.b., final = 14% w.b.) is dried in a tray dryer with surface area 5 m². Drying rate in constant rate period = 2.5 kg/m²·h. Find drying time.

**Solution:**

Dry mass = $100 \times 0.70 = 70$ kg.
Water removed = $70 \times (0.30/0.70 - 0.14/0.86)$ — let's use d.b.:
$M_{1,db} = 0.30/0.70 = 0.4286$ kg/kg
$M_{2,db} = 0.14/0.86 = 0.1628$ kg/kg
Water removed = $70 \times (0.4286 - 0.1628) = 70 \times 0.2658 = 18.61$ kg

$$t = \frac{m_w}{A \cdot R} = \frac{18.61}{5 \times 2.5} = 1.49 \;\; \text{hours} \approx 89.3 \;\; \text{min}$$

### Example 3: Air Requirement
Above dryer uses air with inlet humidity ratio 0.012, outlet 0.028 kg/kg dry air. Find air flow rate (kg dry air/h).

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{m_w/t}{H_2 - H_1} = \frac{18.61/1.49}{0.028 - 0.012} = \frac{12.49}{0.016} = 780.6 \;\; \text{kg dry air/h}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Drying Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Biological Material] --> B[Pre-conditioning Cleaning Sorting]
    B --> C[Drying Chamber]
    D[Ambient Air] --> E[Heater Fuel Electric Steam]
    E --> F[Hot Air 60 to 120 deg C]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Moisture Transfer Evaporation]
    G --> H[Moisture laden exhaust air]
    H --> I[Exhaust to atmosphere]
    C --> J[Dried Product]
    J --> K[Cooling Section]
    K --> L[Storage Packaging]
    M[Control System T RH Sensors] --> F
    M --> C
```

## 4.2 Moisture Content Measurement Methods Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Moisture Content Determination] --> B[Direct Methods]
    A --> C[Indirect Methods]
    B --> B1[Oven drying 103 C 24 h]
    B --> B2[Vacuum oven 60 to 70 C]
    B --> B3[Distillation Toluene Xylene]
    B --> B4[Karl Fischer Titration]
    C --> C1[Electrical Resistance Meter]
    C --> C2[Capacitance / Dielectric Meter]
    C --> C3[Infrared / NIR]
    C --> C4[Microwave]
    C --> C5[NMR Laboratory]
    B1 --> R1[Reference Standard]
    C1 --> R2[Rapid Field Use]
    C2 --> R2
    C3 --> R2
```

## 4.3 Drying Rate Periods — Schematic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Adjustment A to B] --> B[Constant Rate Period B to C]
    B --> C[First Falling Rate C to D]
    C --> D[Second Falling Rate D to E]
    A1[Material warms to wet bulb] -.-> A
    B1[Surface saturated, free water evaporates] -.-> B
    C1[Surface partially dry, wet patches shrink] -.-> C
    D1[Surface fully dry, internal diffusion controls] -.-> D
```

## 4.4 Mechanical Dryer Classification — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Dryers] --> B[Batch Type]
    A --> C[Continuous Type]
    B --> B1[Bin Dryer]
    B --> B2[Flatbed Tray Dryer]
    B --> B3[Vertical Baffle LSU]
    B --> B4[Recirculating Batch]
    C --> C1[Cross Flow]
    C --> C2[Counter Flow]
    C --> C3[Concurrent Flow]
    C --> C4[Mixed Flow Columnar]
    C --> C5[Rotary Drum]
    C --> C6[Fluidized Bed]
    C --> C7[Belt Conveyor]
    C --> C8[Tunnel]
    C --> C9[Spray]
    C --> C10[Drum]
```

## 4.5 EMC Determination — Static vs Dynamic Method Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EMC Determination] --> B[Static Method]
    A --> C[Dynamic Method]
    B --> B1[Weigh sample accurately]
    B1 --> B2[Place over saturated salt solution in desiccator]
    B2 --> B3[Maintain constant T in incubator]
    B3 --> B4[Weigh periodically till constant mass]
    B4 --> B5[Record EMC at given T and RH]
    C --> C1[Weigh sample]
    C1 --> C2[Place in EMC chamber with controlled T and RH]
    C2 --> C3[Pass conditioned air continuously]
    C3 --> C4[Monitor weight change automatically]
    C4 --> C5[Equilibrium reached in shorter time]
    C5 --> C6[Record EMC]
```

## 4.6 Drying Theory — Heat and Mass Transfer Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Air Heating Medium] -->|Heat Transfer Q| B[Product Surface]
    B -->|Mass Transfer Moisture| C[Water Vapor]
    C --> D[Exhaust Air]
    E[Internal Moisture] -->|Liquid Diffusion Fick law| B
    F[Capillary Forces] --> E
    G[Bound Water] -->|Higher Energy Required| E
    H[Free Water] -->|Lower Energy| E
```
