<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define moisture content on dry basis. How is it different from wet basis?**
*(Marks: 3, CO: CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
Moisture content on **dry basis** ($M_{db}$) is the mass of water present per unit mass of **oven-dry** material of the product, expressed as kg water per kg dry solid.

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d}$$

**Wet basis** ($M_{wb}$) is the mass of water per unit mass of **wet** material, expressed as a percentage.

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100$$

**Key differences:**
- $M_{db}$ is preferred in engineering calculations (mass is conserved).
- $M_{wb}$ is used in trade, commerce, and standard specifications.
- $M_{db} = M_{wb} / (1 - M_{wb}/100)$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)? State any two factors affecting it.**
*(Marks: 3, CO: CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
**Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** is the moisture content at which a hygroscopic biological material is in **dynamic equilibrium** with the surrounding air — the rate of moisture desorption equals the rate of moisture adsorption, with **no net moisture transfer**.

**Two factors affecting EMC:**
1. **Relative Humidity (RH)** of surrounding air — Higher RH → Higher EMC.
2. **Temperature (T)** of surrounding air — Higher T → Lower EMC (for desorption).

**Other factors:** Hysteresis (adsorption vs desorption), product type, and previous moisture history.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Always state units** with every numerical answer — losing 1 mark for "no unit" is the #1 KTU complaint.
> 2. In moisture conversion problems, **convert % to decimals** before substituting into formulas. Students often get 0/7 because they write $M_{wb} = 24$ instead of $0.24$.
> 3. For EMC questions, mention **both** temperature and humidity dependence.
> 4. Show the **Henderson equation form** with $T$ in **Kelvin**, not °C — common mistake.
> 5. In drying rate period diagrams, clearly label **all four periods** (A–B, B–C, C–D, D–E) with the rate-controlling mechanism.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
**Define drying. Explain the principle of drying with a neat diagram. Discuss the various utilities of drying in post-harvest engineering.**
*(Marks: 7, CO: CO1, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Drying is a unit operation involving the **removal of moisture** (usually water) from a biological material by the application of thermal energy, where simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** occur. The moisture is vaporized and carried away by a drying medium (typically hot air).

**Principle of Drying:** Drying is governed by the **coupled transport of heat (from hot air to the product)** and **mass (moisture from inside the product to the surrounding air)**. The driving force is:
- Temperature difference: $T_{air} - T_{surface}$
- Vapor pressure difference: $p_{s,m} - p_{v,air}$

When these are favorable, evaporation takes place at the product surface, and the internal moisture migrates outward by diffusion, capillary action, or vapor flow.

**Diagram:** Refer Section 4.6 — the Hot Air → Product → Vapor block diagram.

**Utilities of Drying in Post-Harvest Engineering:**
1. **Preservation:** Lowers water activity ($a_w$) below threshold for microbial growth, preventing spoilage.
2. **Safe storage:** Reduces moisture to safe levels (e.g., paddy 12–14% w.b., wheat 12–13% w.b., maize 13–14% w.b.).
3. **Reduces mass & volume** → cuts transport and storage costs.
4. **Prevents insect and fungal infestation.**
5. **Improves milling, germination, and end-product quality.**
6. **Prepares produce for value addition** (parboiling, oil extraction, processing into flours, flakes).
7. **Enables year-round availability** of seasonal produce through dried forms.

---

**Q. (b) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
**Explain in detail the various methods of moisture content determination. Differentiate between direct and indirect methods with examples.**
*(Marks: 7, CO: CO2, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

Moisture content determination is broadly classified into **Direct methods** and **Indirect methods**.

### Direct Methods
Direct methods physically isolate and measure the water content. They are **absolute and destructive** but are the standard reference methods.

1. **Oven Drying Method (Air-Oven Method):** A known mass of sample is dried in a hot-air oven at **103°C for 24 hours** (or **130°C for 1 hour** for cereals) until constant mass is achieved. The mass loss is reported as moisture content. It is the **AOAC / BIS standard** method for grains.

2. **Vacuum Oven Method:** Drying at low pressure (e.g., 60–70°C, 25–100 mmHg). Suitable for **heat-sensitive** and high-fat products (oilseeds, spices). Reduces oxidation.

3. **Distillation Method:** Water is co-distilled with an immiscible solvent like **toluene** (BP 110.6°C) or xylene. The condensed water is measured volumetrically. Used for **oilseeds, fats, waxes**.

4. **Desiccation Method:** Sample is placed in a desiccator over a desiccant (P₂O₅, CaCl₂) and weighed periodically. Slow but very accurate.

5. **Chemical Method (Karl Fischer titration):** Sample is titrated with Karl Fischer reagent which selectively reacts with water. Used for **trace moisture** in powders, oils, and pharmaceuticals.

### Indirect Methods
Indirect methods measure a physical property that is correlated with moisture. They are **rapid, non-destructive**, and suitable for online measurement.

1. **Electrical Resistance Method:** Measures the resistance of a compressed grain mass. Resistance decreases as moisture increases. Used in **portable moisture meters**.

2. **Capacitance / Dielectric Method:** Measures the dielectric constant of grain, which depends strongly on moisture content. Suitable for **wide range of moisture**, often used in continuous dryers.

3. **Infrared / Near-Infrared (NIR) Method:** Measures absorbance of light at characteristic water absorption bands (e.g., **1940 nm**). Allows simultaneous measurement of **multiple constituents** (protein, oil, starch).

4. **Microwave Method:** Measures attenuation or phase shift of microwave signal passing through grain.

5. **NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance):** Measures hydrogen proton relaxation; highly accurate but laboratory-based.

### Differentiation Table

| Aspect | Direct Methods | Indirect Methods |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Physical removal of water | Measurement of a property vs MC |
| Accuracy | High (reference) | Lower, requires calibration |
| Speed | Slow (hours) | Fast (seconds-minutes) |
| Sample destruction | Yes (oven, KF) | No (most) |
| Cost | Low-medium | Medium-high |
| Examples | Oven, distillation, KF | Resistance, capacitance, NIR |

> [!NOTE]
> Indirect methods are **calibrated against** direct (oven) methods. ISO 712 / AOAC specify oven drying as the **primary standard**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q. (a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Define Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC). Describe the static and dynamic methods for EMC determination. State the Henderson EMC equation.**
*(Marks: 7, CO: CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** EMC is the moisture content at which a hygroscopic material neither gains nor loses moisture to the surrounding air, i.e., the vapor pressure of water in the material equals the partial pressure of water vapor in the air.

**Importance:** Knowing EMC allows us to predict the **lowest moisture** a product can reach in given T and RH conditions — crucial for **dryer design and storage engineering**.

### A. Static Method (No air movement)
**Principle:** Material is held in a **sealed environment** (desiccator) over a saturated salt solution that produces a **known constant RH**.

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh a clean, dry sample accurately ($W_1$).
2. Place the sample in a desiccator over a saturated salt solution (e.g., **NaCl for 75% RH**).
3. Maintain constant temperature in a **BOD incubator** (e.g., 25°C or 40°C).
4. Weigh the sample **periodically** (every 3–5 days) until **two consecutive weights are equal** (constant mass = $W_2$).
5. Calculate EMC (d.b.) = $(W_1 - W_2)/W_2$ after oven-drying $W_2$ to dry mass.
6. Repeat with different salt solutions to get the **EMC vs RH isotherm** at constant T.

**Common salt solutions and their RH (at 25°C):**
- LiCl: 11.3% RH
- MgCl₂: 32.8% RH
- NaCl: 75.3% RH
- K₂SO₄: 97.3% RH

**Disadvantage:** Very slow (weeks to months for full isotherm).

### B. Dynamic Method (Continuous air flow)
**Principle:** Conditioned air of known T and RH is passed **continuously** over the sample, accelerating the approach to equilibrium.

**Procedure:**
1. Place a weighed sample in an **EMC chamber** (small chamber with controlled air inlet).
2. Condition air to desired T and RH using a **temperature-humidity controller**.
3. Pass air continuously through the chamber at a controlled velocity.
4. Monitor sample weight using a **precision balance** connected to a data logger.
5. Record equilibrium weight automatically.
6. Multiple samples can be run at different T, RH conditions simultaneously.

**Advantages over static method:**
- Faster (hours to days).
- More accurate (continuous equilibrium).
- Suitable for automated systems (e.g., **AquaLab, Decagon sorption analyzer**).

### Henderson Equation
$$1 - RH = \exp(-C \cdot T_{abs} \cdot M_{db}^{\,n})$$

Where:
- $RH$ = relative humidity (decimal)
- $T_{abs}$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $M_{db}$ = dry basis moisture content
- $C$, $n$ = empirical product-specific constants

> [!NOTE]
> This equation allows prediction of EMC for given T, RH — useful in **drying simulation and storage design**.

---

**Q. (b) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Explain the drying rate periods with a typical drying curve. Discuss the mechanisms controlling each period.**
*(Marks: 7, CO: CO3, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

The **drying rate** is defined as the rate of moisture removal per unit time per unit area of the drying surface:
$$R = -\frac{dM_{db}}{d\theta} \;\; (\text{kg water/m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$$

A **typical drying curve** shows moisture content (M) vs time (θ), and a **drying rate curve** shows drying rate (R) vs moisture content (M).

### Drying Rate Periods

**Period I — Initial Adjustment Period (A → B)**
- The wet material, initially at ambient temperature, is placed in the dryer.
- The material **warms up** to the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air.
- Drying rate may rise or fall briefly.
- This period is **very short** and often neglected.

**Period II — Constant Rate Period (B → C)**
- The material surface remains **completely saturated** with free moisture.
- Evaporation occurs **as if from a free water surface**.
- Drying rate is **constant** and is controlled by:
  - **External mass transfer** (moisture diffusion from surface to air).
  - Air velocity, temperature, and humidity.
- Heat supplied is used for **evaporation of free water** at the wet-bulb temperature.
- **For grains and seeds, this period is short or absent** because of the low initial free moisture.

**Period III — First Falling Rate Period (C → D)**
- The surface begins to **dry partially**; the **wetted area shrinks**.
- Rate of drying **decreases linearly** with moisture content.
- The rate is controlled by a combination of:
  - **Internal moisture diffusion** (Fickian).
  - **External mass transfer**.
- Critical moisture content $M_c$ marks the transition.

**Period IV — Second Falling Rate Period (D → E)**
- The surface is **completely dry**; no more external evaporation.
- Moisture must diffuse from the **interior** to the surface.
- Drying rate is **very low** and asymptotically approaches the **EMC ($M_e$)**.
- The rate is **entirely controlled by internal diffusion** (Fick's law).
- This is the **dominant period** for grain drying.

### Diagram (Rate vs Moisture)

```
R (kg/m^2.h)
 |
 |--- Period II (B-C) -- constant
 |   /
 |  /  Period III (C-D) -- linearly falls
 | /   /
 |/   /  Period IV (D-E) -- exponential decay
 |   /
 |__/____________________ M (d.b.)
 M_e   M_c        M_1
```

### Controlling Mechanisms Summary

| Period | Controlling Mechanism | Location of Evaporation |
|---|---|---|
| I — Initial | Heat transfer (warm-up) | — |
| II — Constant Rate | External mass transfer | Surface (saturated) |
| III — 1st Falling | Combined internal & external | Surface (partial) |
| IV — 2nd Falling | Internal diffusion (Fick's law) | Inside pores |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **safe grain drying**, drying must occur mostly in the falling-rate regime. **Drying at high temperatures during the falling-rate period can cause thermal damage** to seeds, hence the **two-pass / tempering drying** strategy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 High-Yield KTU Topics (Most Repeated)
- ✅ **Wet basis vs Dry basis** conversion formula — asked almost every year.
- ✅ **Henderson EMC equation** with $T$ in Kelvin.
- ✅ **Direct (oven) vs Indirect (resistance/capacitance)** methods.
- ✅ **Drying rate periods** with controlling mechanism.
- ✅ **Bin dryer, cross-flow, LSU, fluidized bed** — basic working principles.

### 📌 Critical Formulas to Memorize
1. $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1 - M_{wb})$
2. Henderson: $1 - RH = e^{-C T_{abs} M_{db}^n}$
3. $R = -dM_{db}/d\theta$
4. $t = W_d (M_1 - M_2) / (A \cdot R)$ (constant rate)
5. $\dot{m}_{air} = m_w / (H_2 - H_1)$

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting to convert % to decimal in formulas.
- ❌ Using °C instead of K in Henderson's equation.
- ❌ Confusing "free" and "bound" moisture.
- ❌ Drawing drying curve without labeling all four periods.
- ❌ Missing units in numerical answers.

### 🧠 Conceptual Quick-Fire
- **EMC depends on?** T and RH of air.
- **Hygroscopic material?** Material that holds bound moisture (e.g., grain).
- **Constant rate period is controlled by?** External mass transfer.
- **Falling rate period is controlled by?** Internal diffusion.
- **Safe storage MC for paddy?** ~12–14% w.b.
- **Standard oven temperature for grain moisture?** 103°C / 24 h OR 130°C / 1 h.
- **Which EMC method is faster?** Dynamic method.
- **Why tempering between drying passes?** To allow internal moisture to equalize, reducing stress cracks.

### 🎯 KTU Module 2 — Mind Map Summary
```
DRYING
├── Principle (Heat + Mass transfer)
├── Moisture forms (Free / Bound)
├── Moisture Content
│   ├── Wet basis (commercial)
│   ├── Dry basis (engineering)
│   └── Conversion formulas
├── Measurement
│   ├── Direct (Oven, Distillation, KF)
│   └── Indirect (Resistance, Capacitance, NIR)
├── EMC
│   ├── Definition
│   ├── Henderson, Chung-Pfost, Oswin equations
│   ├── Static (Salt solutions)
│   └── Dynamic (Controlled air)
├── Drying Theory (Fick's law, Internal diffusion)
├── Methods
│   ├── Sun, Shade
│   └── Mechanical (Batch / Continuous)
├── Drying Rate Periods
│   ├── Initial Adjustment
│   ├── Constant Rate
│   ├── 1st Falling Rate
│   └── 2nd Falling Rate
└── Mechanical Dryer Types
    ├── Batch (Bin, Flatbed, Baffle, Recirculating)
    └── Continuous (Cross, Counter, Mixed-Flow, Rotary, Fluidized, Belt, Spray, Drum)
```
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Layer Drying and Deep Bed Drying
Rice Milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Rice Milling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Rice Milling** is the post-harvest unit operation in which harvested paddy (rough rice, *Oryza sativa* L.) is mechanically and thermally processed to remove the non-edible outer layers — the **husk (hull)**, **bran layers**, and **germ** — and to separate the **edible endosperm** into standardized grades of white rice, with controlled levels of broken kernels and degree of milling (DOM). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim spirit):** > Rice milling is a sequence of mechanical size-reduction and separation unit operations — **cleaning → de-stoning → parboiling (optional) → husking (de-hulling) → paddy separation → whitening/polishing → grading → blending** — whose engineering objective is to maximize **Total Rice Yield (TRY)** and **Head Rice Recovery (HRR)** while minimizing grain breakage, energy consumption, and nutritional loss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three primary physical outputs of a rice mill:** > 1. **Husk (20–22 %)** — fibrous outer cover; used as fuel for boilers. > 2. **Bran (8–10 %)** — oil-rich pericarp + aleurone + germ; used for rice bran oil extraction. > 3. **Milled Rice (≈ 68–70 %)** — edible starchy endosperm (white rice). > 4. **Brokens (variable, target < 5–15 %)** — separated and sold at lower price. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Layered Onion Model" Imagine a **paddy grain as a tiny onion** that grew in the field. Each concentric layer must be peeled off in the correct order to obtain the clean, edible "core" (the endosperm): | Layer | Paddy Anatomy | Kitchen Analogy | Unit Operation | |---|---|---|---| | Outer straw/dust | Field debris | Dust on the onion skin | **Cleaning** | | Sticks, stones, weeds | Foreign matter | Rotten leaves stuck | **De-stoning** | | Husk (silica-rich hull) | Papery yellow skin | Dry, brittle outer peel | **Husking (De-hulling)** | | Bran + germ (oil-rich) | Brownish layer | Moist inner brown skin | **Whitening / Polishing** | | Endosperm (white starch) | White interior | Sweet edible core | **Milled rice (final product)** | > If you peel too aggressively (over-milling), you lose nutrients and break the kernel — just like slicing an onion too deep wastes the edible part. **Engineering precision = right pressure + right sequence + right residence time.** --- ## 1.3 Why Rice Milling is a Critical Unit Operation > [!IMPORTANT] > - Rice is the **staple food for > 65 % of India's population** and ~ 50 % of the world. > - India is the **2nd largest producer and largest exporter** of rice globally. > - A **1 % improvement in milling recovery** across India = **~ 1.2 million tonnes of additional edible rice** (≈ ₹2,500 crores additional value at MSP). > - Hence milling is not just a process — it is a **national food-security lever**. --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Rice Milling (KTU 2024 Must-Know) - **Paddy (rough rice)** – harvested grain with husk intact. - **Brown rice** – de-husked grain with bran layer still attached. - **Milled rice (white rice)** – brown rice with bran removed. - **Head rice** – milled rice kernels **≥ ¾ of original kernel length**. - **Brokens** – milled rice kernels **< ¾ of original length**. - **Degree of Milling (DOM)** – fraction of bran removed, expressed as **%**. - **Parboiled rice** – paddy soaked, steamed, and dried *before* milling to harden the kernel and improve HRR. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Rice Milling Process — Operational Sequence A modern rice mill follows a strict 7–9 stage flow. Skipping or reordering any stage reduces quality and yield. ### Stage 1 — Pre-Cleaning - **Objective:** Remove straw, dust, empty grains, weed seeds, immature grains, and light impurities. - **Equipment:** Vibratory / rotary **pre-cleaner** with oscillating sieves, aspirator (air-stream), magnetic separator. - **Engineering parameters:** Air velocity **4–6 m/s** (for aspiration), sieve slope **4–6°**, oscillation frequency **300–500 rpm**. ### Stage 2 — De-stoning (Destoning) - **Objective:** Separate **heavier stones** from paddy using density difference. - **Equipment:** **Gravity de-stoner** (vibrating deck with air-flow stratification). - **Principle:** Stratification by terminal velocity — paddy floats, stone sinks; deck tilt **6–10°**, air velocity **1.2–2.0 m/s**. ### Stage 3 — Parboiling (Optional but Industry-Standard) - **Objective:** Soak paddy in hot water (60–70 °C, 3–4 h) → steam at **0.5–1.0 kg/cm²** for 10–20 min → dry to **12–14 % moisture**. - **Why?** Gelatinization of starch → harder, translucent kernel → **higher HRR (+5 to +10 %)**, fewer brokens, better nutritional retention (B-vitamins migrate inward). ### Stage 4 — Husking (De-hulling) - **Objective:** Remove the **silica husk (hull)** by friction/shear. - **Equipment:** - **Rubber roll sheller** (most common): two rubber rolls rotating at different speeds (e.g., 800 rpm & 600 rpm; differential speed ≈ 200–300 rpm). - **Under-runner disc sheller** (older design). - **Centrifugal husker** (newer, for hard parboiled paddy). - **Husk removal efficiency (HRE):** **75–90 %** per pass; rest is recirculated. - **Linear pressure** between rubber rolls: **2–5 kg/cm**. ### Stage 5 — Paddy Separation - **Objective:** Separate **un-husked paddy** from the brown rice stream. - **Equipment:** **Paddy separator** (tray-type with indentations or compartment-type). - **Principle:** Length-based separation — paddy is **longer** than brown rice, so it lodges in cylindrical indentations and is lifted out. - **Separation efficiency (SE):** **≥ 95 %** desired. ### Stage 6 — Whitening / Polishing (Bran Removal) - **Objective:** Abrade the **bran layer (pericarp + aleurone + germ)** to produce white rice. - **Equipment:** - **Abrasive whitener** (vertical or horizontal emery / carborundum cone). - **Friction whitener** (rice-to-rice rubbing). - **Jet pearler** (newer, abrasive + friction combo). - **Whitening pressure (WP):** **0.3–1.5 kg/cm²** (controlled to avoid breakage). - **Number of passes:** 2–4 stages for **progressive** whitening (multi-stage is gentler → less breakage). ### Stage 7 — Polishing (Optional) - **Objective:** Apply water mist + gentle friction to give **glossy appearance**. - **Equipment:** **Mist polisher** with leather / nylon cone. - **Water addition:** **0.2–0.5 % of rice mass**. ### Stage 8 — Grading (Length Grading) - **Objective:** Separate head rice from brokens. - **Equipment:** **Indented cylinder grader** or **plane-sieve sifter**. - **Cylinder indent size:** 4.5 mm, 5.0 mm, 5.5 mm, 6.0 mm — to recover different length fractions. ### Stage 9 — Blending, Weighing & Packaging - **Objective:** Homogenize lots, weigh (50 kg, 25 kg, 5 kg PP bags), and pack for market. - **Quality standards:** FSSAI, AGMARK, ISO 7301. --- ## 2.2 Types of Rice Milling Systems | Type | Description | Typical HRR | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Traditional / Single-Stage** | One machine does husking + whitening → high breakage | 40–55 % | Village-level, obsolete | | **Two-Stage Modern** | Husker + separate polisher | 55–62 % | Small mills | | **Multi-Stage Modern (Satake / Buhler)** | Husker → separator → 3–4 whiteners → grader | 60–70 % | Export-oriented | | **Modern + Parboiling** | Includes soaking + steaming | **65–72 %** | South India (Kerala, TN) | | **Compact Rice Mill (CRM)** | Integrated unit for small holders | 60–65 % | Kisan call centers | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essential.** Every numerical problem in KTU 2024 scheme is solved from this list. $$ \boxed{\text{Milling Yield (MY)} = \frac{W_{mr}}{W_p} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Head Rice Recovery (HRR)} = \frac{W_{hr}}{W_p} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Brown Rice Recovery (BRR)} = \frac{W_{br}}{W_p} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Total Rice Yield (TRY)} = \text{BRR} \times \text{Milling Recovery}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Milling Recovery (MR)} = \frac{W_{mr}}{W_{br}} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Broken \%} = \frac{W_{brk}}{W_{mr}} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Degree of Milling (DOM)} = \frac{W_{bran}}{W_{br}} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Milling Efficiency (ME)} = \frac{\text{HRR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{HRR}_{\text{theoretical max}}} \times 100} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Bulk Density (BD)} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}} \quad \text{[kg/m}^3\text{]}} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Throughput} = \frac{W_p}{t} \quad \text{[tonnes/hour]}} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{\text{husker}} = \frac{W_{hr,\,\text{from husker}}}{W_p} \times 100} $$ | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | $W_p$ | Mass of input paddy | kg | | $W_{mr}$ | Mass of milled (white) rice | kg | | $W_{hr}$ | Mass of head rice (≥ ¾ length) | kg | | $W_{br}$ | Mass of brown rice | kg | | $W_{brk}$ | Mass of broken kernels | kg | | $W_{bran}$ | Mass of bran removed | kg | | $t$ | Milling time | h | | $\eta$ | Efficiency | % | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Industry Benchmarks (KTU 2024 numerical answers accept these):** > - Paddy → Husk = **20 %** > - Brown rice → Bran = **8–10 %** > - Head rice (good mill) = **60–65 %** > - Brokens (good mill) = **< 15 %** --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Capacity design of a rice mill** — Throughput in tonnes/h drives the choice of husker roll diameter, whitener cone size, and grader cylinder length. 2. **Economic viability** — A mill processing 5 t/h paddy at 65 % HRR earns more than one at 50 % HRR despite identical husk removal. 3. **Export-grade processing** — Buyers demand HRR ≥ 60 %, brokens < 5 %; milling engineering directly controls compliance. 4. **Energy engineering** — Rubber roll husker uses ~ **3–5 kWh per tonne**; friction polisher ~ **8–12 kWh per tonne** — informs motor sizing. 5. **Parboiling technology** — Critical in Kerala because parboiled rice gives **+5 % HRR** and is the regional preference (red parboiled, *Matta* rice). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem #1 — Full Milling Mass Balance > **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):** > A rice mill processes **1,000 kg of paddy** in one hour. After the process, the following are recorded: > - Husk recovered: **210 kg** > - Bran removed: **85 kg** > - Total milled rice: **690 kg** > - Of the milled rice, head rice (≥ ¾ length) = **580 kg** > > Calculate: > (a) Brown Rice Recovery (BRR) > (b) Milling Yield (MY) > (c) Head Rice Recovery (HRR) > (d) Broken percentage > (e) Degree of Milling (DOM) > (f) Milling Efficiency, if theoretical max HRR for this variety = 70 % ### Given Data - $W_p = 1000$ kg (paddy input) - $W_{husk} = 210$ kg - $W_{bran} = 85$ kg - $W_{mr} = 690$ kg (milled rice output) - $W_{hr} = 580$ kg (head rice) - $W_{brk} = ?$ (broken kernels) - $W_{br} = ?$ (brown rice) --- ### Step (a) — Brown Rice Recovery (BRR) Brown rice is what remains **after husking but before bran removal**. $$ \begin{aligned} W_{br} &= W_p - W_{husk} \\ W_{br} &= 1000 - 210 \\ W_{br} &= 790 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{BRR} &= \frac{W_{br}}{W_p} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{790}{1000} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{BRR} = 79.0 \%} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 79 % of paddy mass became brown rice (i.e., husk removal efficiency = 21 %; ideal husk content is 20 %, so this mill is well-tuned). --- ### Step (b) — Milling Yield (MY) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MY} &= \frac{W_{mr}}{W_p} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{690}{1000} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{MY} = 69.0 \%} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 69 % of paddy mass became edible milled white rice — at the upper end of the standard 68–70 % range. --- ### Step (c) — Head Rice Recovery (HRR) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HRR} &= \frac{W_{hr}}{W_p} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{580}{1000} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{HRR} = 58.0 \%} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 58 % of paddy became head rice. Acceptable for raw rice; **parboiled would give 60–65 %**. --- ### Step (d) — Broken Percentage $$ \begin{aligned} W_{brk} &= W_{mr} - W_{hr} \\ &= 690 - 580 \\ &= 110 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Broken \%} &= \frac{W_{brk}}{W_{mr}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{110}{690} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{Broken \%} = 15.94 \%} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Slightly above the 15 % industry target. A multi-stage polisher would reduce this. --- ### Step (e) — Degree of Milling (DOM) DOM is the fraction of brown rice that is abraded away as bran. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DOM} &= \frac{W_{bran}}{W_{br}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{85}{790} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{DOM} = 10.76 \%} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** ~11 % of brown rice was removed as bran. Fully-milled rice is achieved at **DOM = 8–10 %**; this is **lightly over-milled**, which may have caused the higher brokens. --- ### Step (f) — Milling Efficiency $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ME} &= \frac{\text{HRR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{HRR}_{\text{theoretical max}}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{58.0}{70.0} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{ME} = 82.86 \%} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The mill is operating at 82.86 % of its theoretical maximum — **good** (industry benchmark ≥ 85 %). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — Throughput & Capacity Design > **Problem Statement:** > A rubber roll husker processes paddy with a roll length of **10 inches (254 mm)** and a linear pressure of **3 kg/cm**. The differential speed of the two rolls is **250 rpm**, and the average husk removal efficiency is **80 %**. If the throughput is **2.5 tonnes/h**, calculate: > (a) Length of rolls required (in mm) for 1.0 t/h capacity. > (b) Effective husking rate in kg/min. ### Solution ### Part (a) — Roll Length for 1 t/h Throughput is **linearly proportional** to roll length (assuming constant roll speed and pressure). $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{L_2}{L_1} &= \frac{C_2}{C_1} \\ L_2 &= L_1 \times \frac{C_2}{C_1} \\ L_2 &= 254 \text{ mm} \times \frac{1.0 \text{ t/h}}{2.5 \text{ t/h}} \\ L_2 &= 254 \times 0.4 \\ \boxed{L_2 = 101.6 \text{ mm} \approx 4 \text{ inches}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (b) — Effective Husking Rate $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Feed rate} &= \frac{2.5 \times 1000}{60} = 41.67 \text{ kg/min} \\ \text{Husked output} &= 41.67 \times 0.80 = 33.33 \text{ kg/min} \\ \boxed{\text{Effective husking rate} \approx 33.33 \text{ kg/min}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Parboiling Improves HRR | Property | Raw Paddy | Parboiled Paddy | |---|---|---| | Kernel hardness | Soft, brittle | **Hard, glassy** (starch gelatinized) | | HRR (typical) | 50–58 % | **60–68 %** | | Brokens | 15–25 % | **5–12 %** | | Color of milled rice | **White, dull** | **Translucent, golden** | | Nutritional loss (B-vitamins) | High (lost with bran) | **Low (B-vitamins diffuse inward)** | | Storage stability of milled rice | Poor (oil in bran) | **Better (oil denatured by heat)** | | Cooking quality | Fluffy, separate | **Firmer, less sticky** (Kerala *Matta* rice) | > **Engineering Logic:** Steam at ~ 1 kg/cm² **gelatinizes the starch granules** in the endosperm, fusing them into a single rigid mass → mechanical resistance to whitening → **fewer cracks → fewer brokens → higher HRR.** --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Rice Milling Process (Stage-by-Stage) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Paddy Intake
1000 kg raw paddy] --> A1[Pre-Cleaner
Vibratory Sieve + Aspirator] A1 --> A2[De-Stoner
Gravity Separator] A2 --> A3{Parboiling
Required?} A3 -->|Yes| A4[Soaking Tank
60 to 70 deg C 3 to 4 h] A3 -->|No| A5[Drying to 12 to 14 percent MC] A4 --> A6[Steam Boiler
0.5 to 1.0 kg per sq cm] A6 --> A5 A5 --> A7[Rubber Roll Husker
Differential 250 rpm] A7 --> A8[Paddy Separator
Indentation Cylinder] A8 --> A9[Brown Rice Stream] A8 --> A10[Un-husked Paddy Recycle] A10 --> A7 A9 --> A11[Whitener Stage 1
Abrasive Cone] A11 --> A12[Whitener Stage 2
Friction Polisher] A12 --> A13[Mist Polisher
Water Spray 0.3 percent] A13 --> A14[Length Grader
Indented Cylinder] A14 --> A15[Head Rice
Greater than 3/4 length] A14 --> A16[Brokens
Less than 3/4 length] A14 --> A17[Bran Collection
8 to 10 percent] A15 --> A18[Blending and Weighing] A16 --> A18 A18 --> A19[Packaging 50 kg Bags
FSSAI Standard] A7 --> A20[Husk to Boiler
20 to 22 percent fuel] A20 --> A6 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Material & Energy Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIDE"] P0[Raw Paddy 1000 kg] end subgraph CLEAN["CLEANING UNIT"] C1[Pre-Cleaner] C2[De-Stoner] end subgraph PARB["CONDITIONING UNIT"] PB1[Soaking] PB2[Steaming] PB3[Drying 12 to 14 percent MC] end subgraph MILL["MILLING UNIT"] H1[Rubber Roll Husker] H2[Paddy Separator] W1[Whitener 1 Abrasive] W2[Whitener 2 Friction] W3[Mist Polisher] end subgraph GRAD["GRADING UNIT"] G1[Length Grader] G2[Color Sorter Optional] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SIDE"] O1[Head Rice 58 to 65 percent] O2[Brokens 5 to 15 percent] O3[Bran 8 to 10 percent] O4[Husk 20 to 22 percent] end subgraph ENERGY["ENERGY RECOVERY"] E1[Husk to Boiler] E2[Steam to Parboiling] E3[Ash to Soil] end P0 --> C1 --> C2 --> PB1 --> PB2 --> PB3 --> H1 --> H2 --> W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> G1 --> G2 G1 --> O1 G1 --> O2 H1 --> O3 H1 --> O4 O4 --> E1 --> E2 --> PB1 E1 --> E3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Mass Balance Sankey-Style Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Paddy 100 percent] --> P2[Husk 21 percent] P1 --> P3[Brown Rice 79 percent] P3 --> P4[Bran 8.5 percent] P3 --> P5[Milled Rice 70.5 percent] P5 --> P6[Head Rice 58 percent] P5 --> P7[Brokens 12.5 percent] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Sankey-style flow:** > - 1000 kg paddy → 210 kg husk + 790 kg brown rice > - 790 kg brown rice → 85 kg bran + 705 kg milled rice > - 705 kg milled rice → 580 kg head rice + 125 kg brokens > - Husk is recycled to boiler → steam → parboiling — **closed-loop energy system**. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]** **Define Head Rice Recovery (HRR) and state two engineering factors that influence it during rice milling.** **Model Answer:** HRR is the percentage of paddy weight that is recovered as **whole milled rice kernels** (length ≥ ¾ of original kernel). $$ \text{HRR} = \frac{W_{head\,rice}}{W_{paddy}} \times 100 $$ **Engineering factors influencing HRR:** 1. **Moisture content of paddy** at husking — optimum **12–14 %** (too dry = brittle breakage; too wet = husk slips but bran smears). 2. **Differential speed of rubber rolls** in husker — typically **200–300 rpm differential**; too high = husk adhered → friction breakage. > **Other valid factors:** number of polishing stages, husking pressure, degree of milling, parboiling vs. raw, variety (Indica vs. Japonica), pre-cleaning efficiency. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for formula + 1 for factors)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]** **Distinguish between brown rice and milled rice. What is the typical DOM for fully-milled white rice?** **Model Answer:** | Property | Brown Rice | Milled Rice (White) | |---|---|---| | Husk | **Removed** | Removed | | Bran layer | **Retained** | Removed | | Color | Brown | White / cream | | Oil content | High (2–3 %) | Low (< 0.5 %) | | Shelf life | Short (oil rancidity) | **Long (1 year)** | | Fiber / nutrition | **High** | Low | | Digestibility | Harder | Softer | **DOM for fully-milled rice = 8–10 %** of brown rice weight removed as bran. *(3 marks — 1.5 for table + 0.5 for definition + 1 for DOM value)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module-Internal Choice --- ### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks, KTU University Exam Pattern]** **Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a flow diagram the **complete modern multi-stage rice milling process** highlighting the function of each machine and the principle of separation used. **Model Answer:** A modern rice mill processes paddy through the following sequential stages: | Stage | Machine | Function | Principle | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Vibratory Pre-Cleaner** | Remove straw, dust, immature grains | Size separation (sieve) + Air aspiration (density) | | 2 | **Gravity De-Stoner** | Remove stones | Density difference — paddy floats on air cushion, stone sinks | | 3 | **Parboiling Unit** (optional) | Soak + steam + dry to harden kernel | Heat-induced starch gelatinization | | 4 | **Rubber Roll Husker** | Remove husk | Differential friction between two rubber rolls (one fast, one slow) | | 5 | **Paddy Separator** | Separate un-husked paddy from brown rice | Length-based — paddy (longer) lodges in cylindrical indentations | | 6 | **Abrasive Whitener (Stage 1)** | Remove outer bran | Abrasion against emery / carborundum cone | | 7 | **Friction Whitener (Stage 2)** | Remove inner bran | Rice-to-rice friction | | 8 | **Mist Polisher** | Glossy finish | Water mist + gentle friction (0.3 % water addition) | | 9 | **Length Grader** | Separate head rice from brokens | Indented cylinders (different indent sizes) | | 10 | **Blender + Bagger** | Homogenize, pack at 50 kg | Volumetric / gravimetric weighing | **Key engineering principles:** - **Size** (sieves) - **Density** (air-classifier, de-stoner) - **Length** (indented cylinder) - **Friction / Abrasion** (whitener) - **Differential speed** (husker) *(7 marks: 1.5 for flow diagram + 1.5 for machine-by-machine + 1.5 for principles + 1.5 for sequence logic + 1 for conclusion)* --- **Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]** From **1,000 kg of paddy**, a mill produces **220 kg husk, 90 kg bran, 600 kg head rice and 90 kg brokens**. Calculate **HRR, MY, DOM, and broken percentage.** State whether the mill is producing export-grade rice (criteria: HRR ≥ 60 %, brokens < 5 %). **Model Answer:** ### Given - $W_p = 1000$ kg - $W_{husk} = 220$ kg - $W_{bran} = 90$ kg - $W_{hr} = 600$ kg - $W_{brk} = 90$ kg ### Step 1 — Total Milled Rice $$ \begin{aligned} W_{mr} &= W_{hr} + W_{brk} \\ &= 600 + 90 = 690 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Brown Rice $$ \begin{aligned} W_{br} &= W_p - W_{husk} \\ &= 1000 - 220 = 780 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Head Rice Recovery (HRR) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HRR} &= \frac{600}{1000} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{HRR} = 60.0 \%} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Milling Yield (MY) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MY} &= \frac{690}{1000} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{MY} = 69.0 \%} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Degree of Milling (DOM) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DOM} &= \frac{W_{bran}}{W_{br}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{90}{780} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{DOM} = 11.54 \%} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Broken Percentage $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Broken \%} &= \frac{90}{690} \times 100 \\ \boxed{\text{Broken \%} = 13.04 \%} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Export-Grade Verdict | Criterion | Required | Achieved | Pass? | |---|---|---|---| | HRR | ≥ 60 % | 60.0 % | **Borderline pass** | | Brokens | < 5 % | 13.04 % | **FAIL** | > **Conclusion:** The mill **fails export-grade** due to excessive brokens (13.04 % > 5 %). Recommendation: add a **third polishing stage** or a **length grader pre-polisher** to reduce breakage. *(7 marks: 1 for brown rice derivation + 1 HRR + 1 MY + 1 DOM + 1 Broken % + 1.5 export verdict + 0.5 conclusion)* --- ### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]** **Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **rubber roll husking mechanism** with a neat sketch. Explain the role of **differential speed, linear pressure, and roll hardness** on husk removal efficiency and breakage. **Model Answer:** ### Construction The rubber roll husker consists of **two rubber-coated rolls** rotating in **opposite directions** at **different angular velocities** (differential speed). - **Fast roll:** Shore-A hardness **85–95**, rotation ~ 800–1000 rpm. - **Slow roll:** Shore-A hardness **70–80**, rotation ~ 500–700 rpm. - **Roll diameter:** 150–250 mm. - **Roll length:** 6, 8, 10 inches. - **Paddy feed** is by gravity from hopper, gripped between the rolls at the **nip**. ### Working Principle - The **fast roll drags** the paddy; the **slow roll grips but does not slip**. - The **relative motion** between paddy surface and rolls creates **shear stress**. - Shear stress **peels off the husk** along the **natural cleavage line** of the husk-aleurone interface. - Paddy tumbles, husk is stripped, and **brown rice falls through** the discharge chute; husk is carried to the **aspirator**. ### Role of Operating Parameters | Parameter | Effect on Husk Removal | Effect on Breakage | |---|---|---| | **Differential speed (250 rpm ideal)** | ↑ with differential speed (up to ~ 300 rpm) | ↑↑ Above 350 rpm → breakage | | **Linear pressure (2–5 kg/cm)** | ↑ with pressure up to 4 kg/cm | ↑↑ Above 5 kg/cm → kernel crush | | **Roll hardness (Shore-A 70–95)** | Higher hardness = better grip | Too hard = brittle impact = more breakage | | **Roll gap (0.5–1.0 mm)** | Smaller gap = more friction | Too small = crushing | | **Paddy moisture (12–14 %)** | Optimum = clean husking | Too dry = shattering; too wet = slippage | ### Design Equation $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{fast}} &= \pi D N_f \\ V_{\text{slow}} &= \pi D N_s \\ V_{\text{differential}} &= \pi D (N_f - N_s) \\ \text{Shear stress } \tau &= \mu \cdot \frac{P}{A} \end{aligned} $$ where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $P$ = linear pressure, $A$ = contact area. *(7 marks: 1.5 for construction + 1.5 for principle + 2 for parameter table + 1 for design equation + 1 for summary)* --- **Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]** What is **parboiling**? Explain its **scientific principle** and state **four advantages** of parboiling in rice milling. Why is parboiled rice preferred in Kerala? **Model Answer:** ### Definition **Parboiling** is a hydrothermal pre-treatment of paddy in which the grain is **soaked, steamed, and dried** *before* milling to gelatinize the starch and harden the kernel. ### Scientific Principle 1. **Soaking (60–70 °C, 3–4 h):** Water penetrates the husk → reaches starch granules in the endosperm → **moisture content rises to ~ 30 %**. 2. **Steaming (0.5–1.0 kg/cm², 10–20 min):** Heat energy breaks hydrogen bonds in starch → **irreversible gelatinization** — starch granules swell, fuse, and recrystallize. 3. **Drying (to 12–14 % MC):** Hard, glassy, translucent endosperm forms → strong mechanical resistance to abrasive whitening. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Starch + Heat + Water} &\rightarrow \text{Gelatinized Starch} \\ \text{(granular)} &\quad \text{(amorphous, glassy)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Four Advantages 1. **Higher HRR** (+5 to +10 %) — hard kernel resists whitening abrasion. 2. **Fewer brokens** (5–12 % vs 15–25 % in raw milling). 3. **Better nutritional retention** — B-vitamins (B₁, B₃, B₆) diffuse from bran into endosperm before bran is removed. 4. **Improved storage of milled rice** — heat denatures lipase enzyme → oil in bran does not rancidify → shelf life doubled. ### Why Parboiled Rice is Preferred in Kerala - Kerala's staple is **red parboiled *Matta* rice (Jyothi, Aishwarya varieties)** — culturally rooted. - Parboiled rice **keeps longer in humid Kerala climate** (anti-fungal, low free fatty acids). - Lower glycemic response → **healthier for diabetic-prone population**. - **Better for *Kanji* (rice gruel)** and *Appam* — characteristic texture. - Domestic price premium supports farmer income in Kerala's paddy belts (Kuttanad, Kole, Pokkali). *(7 marks: 1.5 definition + 1.5 scientific principle + 2 advantages (0.5 each) + 2 Kerala reasoning)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in Rice Milling problems:** > 1. **Confusing BRR with HRR** — Brown rice recovery uses brown rice weight; HRR uses only the head rice weight. Many students interchange them → **2 marks lost per question**. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract husk first** when computing DOM — DOM is based on **brown rice weight** as denominator, NOT paddy. Wrong denominator = wrong answer. > 3. **Mis-stating "broken percentage"** — should be computed on milled rice (or paddy) basis, NOT brown rice basis. Always read the question carefully. > 4. **No clear "Principle of Separation"** in flow diagrams — examiners award 1–1.5 marks specifically for the **principle** at each stage (size, density, length, friction). > 5. **Skipping the moisture content (12–14 %)** — this magic number appears in EVERY rice-milling question; mentioning it is a free 0.5 mark. > 6. **In parboiling questions**, failing to write **"irreversible starch gelatinization"** is a mark killer. Use the term explicitly. > 7. **HRR in Kerala exam context** — Kerala is parboiled-rice territory, so HRR expectations are **higher (60–68 %)** than raw-milled benchmark. Mention parboiling in any Kerala-specific question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Rice Milling (KTU 2024 Module 3):** - **Rice milling** = 7–9 stage process: Cleaning → De-stoning → Parboiling → Husking → Paddy separation → Whitening → Polishing → Grading → Packaging. - **Paddy anatomy** (outer to inner): Straw/dust → Husk → Bran → Endosperm (white rice). - **Standard paddy composition:** 20–22 % husk + 8–10 % bran + 68–70 % milled rice. - **Head Rice Recovery (HRR) ≥ 60 %** = the key quality metric. Higher = better. - **Optimum paddy moisture for milling = 12–14 %** (use this in every numerical). - **Degree of Milling (DOM) = 8–10 %** for fully-milled white rice; > 12 % = over-milled. - **Broken % = broken kernels / total milled rice × 100**; target < 15 % (export grade < 5 %). - **Rubber roll husker:** differential speed 200–300 rpm, linear pressure 2–5 kg/cm, rolls in opposite directions, Shore-A 70–95. - **Whitener = abrasive cone**; polisher = friction + water mist (0.3 %). - **Length grader** uses **indented cylinders** to separate head rice from brokens. - **Parboiling** = Soaking (60–70 °C) → Steaming (1 kg/cm²) → Drying (12–14 % MC) → induces **irreversible starch gelatinization** → +5 to +10 % HRR. - **Parboiled rice** is preferred in **Kerala** (Matta rice, Kuttanad region). - **Formulas to memorize:** HRR, MY, BRR, MR, DOM, Broken %, ME, Throughput. - **Energy:** Husk is the in-house fuel → boiler → steam → parboiling (closed-loop). - **Industry benchmark for milling efficiency = ≥ 85 %** (HRR actual / HRR theoretical). - **Milling yield vs HRR:** MY includes brokens; HRR does not. **HRR < MY always.** - **Export grade:** HRR ≥ 60 % **AND** brokens < 5 %. - **Stone removal = density separation** (gravity de-stoner); **paddy separation = length separation** (indented cylinder); **husk removal = friction separation** (rubber rolls). ---

topics.title: Parboiling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parboiling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parboiling** is a controlled hydrothermal unit operation in post-harvest engineering wherein paddy (rough rice) is subjected to a sequence of three sequential thermal–moisture treatments — **(1) Soaking (Hydration), (2) Steaming (Heat Treatment / Gelatinization), and (3) Drying** — to bring about irreversible starch gelatinization within the endosperm, thereby improving milling recovery, nutritional retention, and storage stability of the milled rice.

In strictly scientific terms, parboiling is defined as:

$$\text{Paddy} \xrightarrow{\text{Soaking}} \text{Hydrated Paddy} \xrightarrow{\text{Steaming}} \text{Gelatinized Paddy} \xrightarrow{\text{Drying}} \text{Parboiled Paddy}$$

The term *parboiling* is etymologically derived from the partial boiling of rice grains, although in modern industrial practice, **direct steam contact** (without immersion in boiling water) is the preferred heat-transfer medium.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bread-Baking Analogy**
> Think of parboiling like **pre-cooking pasta halfway before baking it in a lasagna**. The pasta doesn't become fully soft like boiled noodles, but it absorbs some moisture, firms up internally, and becomes resilient to the later mechanical grinding (milling). Similarly, parboiling "pre-cooks" the rice grain internally — the starch granules swell and fuse — so that when the husk is later removed in the mill, the grain does not shatter. It is essentially **"internal cooking without the fire reaching the kitchen"** — heat and water are used to partially cook the endosperm, but the grain's outer appearance remains intact.

### 1.3 Why Parboiling? — Engineering Objectives

The unit operation is employed to achieve the following engineering objectives:

- **Increase Head Rice Yield (HRY):** Reduces breakage from 20–30% (raw rice) down to **3–5%** in well-parboiled rice.
- **Retention of Thiamine (Vitamin B1):** Migration of water-soluble vitamins from the bran/pericarp into the endosperm.
- **Hardening of the endosperm:** Improves resistance to abrasive milling stresses.
- **Reduction of chalkiness:** Produces translucent, glassy grains preferred in the export market.
- **Pest & Microbial Inactivation:** Steam treatment kills weevils, fungi (e.g., *Aspergillus*), and bacteria.
- **Reduced oil absorption during cooking:** Gelatinized starch reduces surface porosity.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Soaking water temperature | **65 – 75** | °C |
| Soaking duration | **12 – 36** | hours |
| Moisture after soaking | **30 – 35** | % (w.b.) |
| Steaming pressure (open vessel) | Atmospheric | — |
| Steaming pressure (pressure vessel) | **0.5 – 1.5** | kg/cm² |
| Steaming duration | **10 – 40** | minutes |
| Gelatinization temperature of rice starch | **68 – 78** | °C |
| Final moisture after drying | **12 – 14** | % (w.b.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The starch gelatinization temperature range of **68–78 °C** is a *favourite KTU board exam value* — students are expected to remember this range because it directly dictates the minimum steaming temperature required during parboiling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Breakdown

#### Step 1 — Soaking (Hydration)

- Paddy is immersed in **hot water (65–75 °C)** for 12–36 hours.
- Objective: Raise the moisture content of the grain from **~12% (w.b.)** to **~30–35% (w.b.)**.
- Water diffuses from the surface → through the husk → pericarp → into the starchy endosperm.
- The rate of moisture uptake follows **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**:

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$$

Where $D_{eff}$ is the **effective moisture diffusivity** (m²/s), $M$ is the local moisture content, $t$ is time, and $x$ is the diffusion path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hot water, not cold?** Hot water (1) accelerates the diffusion coefficient $D_{eff}$ (Arrhenius-type temperature dependence), (2) reduces the viscosity of water for better capillary penetration, and (3) begins pre-heating the grain, which reduces thermal shock during subsequent steaming.

#### Step 2 — Steaming (Heat Treatment / Gelatinization)

- Hydrated paddy is exposed to **saturated steam** (atmospheric or pressurized).
- Grain temperature rapidly rises to **> 78 °C**, crossing the starch gelatinization threshold.
- **Starch gelatinization** = irreversible loss of crystalline order in starch granules + swelling + amylose leaching.
- The process is endothermic — energy must be supplied to break the hydrogen bonds in the starch crystallites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gelatinization = the heart of parboiling.** Without complete starch gelatinization, the rice is considered *under-parboiled* and will still produce chalky, brittle grains on milling.

#### Step 3 — Drying

- The gelatinized paddy is dried from **~35% to 12–14% (w.b.)** for safe storage (typically below the **14% safe storage moisture threshold** for cereals).
- Drying is usually done in two stages:
  1. **Bulk drying / Mechanical drying** (continuous flow or batch dryers)
  2. **Tempering** — holding the grain at intermediate moisture for 4–8 hours to allow **internal moisture redistribution** and reduce drying-stress cracking.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{wb} = \frac{w_w}{w_w + w_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |
| 2 | $M_{db} = \frac{w_w}{w_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 3 | $M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | Wet-to-dry basis conversion | % |
| 4 | $HRY = \frac{m_{head}}{m_{total\,rice}} \times 100$ | Head Rice Yield | % |
| 5 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature-dependent diffusivity | m²/s |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot L_{gel}$ | Latent heat for gelatinization | kJ |
| 7 | $\eta_{milling} = \frac{m_{milled\,rice}}{m_{paddy}} \times 100$ | Overall milling yield | % |
| 8 | $M_{eq} = M_{db} \cdot \rho_s \cdot V$ | Total moisture mass in grain | kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Always use the **absolute value notation** `\vert x \vert` in any KTU answer sheet, never `|x|`, to ensure correct evaluation by the examiner.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parboiling, as a unit operation, is industrially important in:

- **Rice-export economies** (India, Thailand, Pakistan, Vietnam) where head rice yield directly determines commercial value.
- **Fortified rice production** — parboiled rice retains thiamine (B1), making it nutritionally superior to fully polished raw rice.
- **Animal feed and brewing adjuncts** — parboiled broken rice is preferred in brewing due to better enzymatic conversion.
- **Disaster-relief food grain programs** — parboiled rice has superior storage stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Requirement for Soaking

> **Problem:** A paddy lot weighing **1000 kg** at an initial moisture content of **12% (w.b.)** is to be soaked to a target moisture of **30% (w.b.)**. Calculate the mass of water to be added (neglecting evaporation losses).

**Step 1:** Convert initial moisture to **dry basis**.

$$M_{db,i} = \frac{M_{wb,i}}{100 - M_{wb,i}} \times 100$$

$$M_{db,i} = \frac{12}{100 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{12}{88} \times 100 = 13.636\ \%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **mass of dry matter** in the paddy (this is invariant during soaking).

$$w_d = w_p \times \left(1 - \frac{M_{wb,i}}{100}\right)$$

$$w_d = 1000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{1}\right) = 1000 \times 0.88 = 880\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Convert target moisture to **dry basis**.

$$M_{db,f} = \frac{30}{100 - 30} \times 100 = \frac{30}{70} \times 100 = 42.857\ \%$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the **total water mass** required at the target moisture.

$$w_{w,f} = w_d \times \frac{M_{db,f}}{100}$$

$$w_{w,f} = 880 \times \frac{42.857}{100} = 880 \times 0.42857 = 377.14\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the **initial water mass** to find the water to be added.

$$w_{w,i} = w_p - w_d = 1000 - 880 = 120\ \text{kg}$$

$$\Delta w_w = w_{w,f} - w_{w,i} = 377.14 - 120 = 257.14\ \text{kg}$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **257.14 kg of water** must be absorbed by the paddy during soaking.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Energy Required for Gelatinization

> **Problem:** Estimate the thermal energy required to raise **1000 kg of hydrated paddy** from 30 °C to the gelatinization temperature of 78 °C, and supply the latent heat of gelatinization. Assume specific heat of hydrated paddy $c_p = 3.5\ \text{kJ/(kg·°C)}$ and latent heat of gelatinization $L_{gel} = 33\ \text{kJ/kg}$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the **sensible heat** required to raise the grain temperature.

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 1000 \times 3.5 \times (78 - 30)$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 1000 \times 3.5 \times 48 = 168{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **latent heat** for starch gelatinization.

$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot L_{gel} = 1000 \times 33 = 33{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the two components.

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} = 168{,}000 + 33{,}000 = 201{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

> **Answer:** The total energy required is **201,000 kJ ≈ 55.83 kWh** per 1000 kg of paddy.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Parboiling vs. Raw Rice

| Property | Raw Rice | Parboiled Rice | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head Rice Yield | 55–60% | 65–70% | Gelatinized endosperm is hard and crack-resistant |
| Chalky grains | High | Very low | Starch granules re-associate on cooling |
| Thiamine content (mg/100g) | 0.07 | 0.21 | Vitamin migration from bran to endosperm |
| Cooking time | 18–22 min | 25–30 min | Pre-gelatinized starch re-hydrates slowly |
| Storage insect damage | High | Low | Steam treatment kills insects/eggs |
| Colour | White | Amber/Yellow | Maillard reaction products (minor) |
| Hardness | Low | High | Protein–starch matrix densification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Examiners often ask "Why is parboiled rice nutritionally superior to raw rice?" — the correct answer must emphasize **vitamin migration from pericarp/aleurone to the endosperm**, *not* the addition of nutrients.

---

### 3.4 Defects in Parboiling — Diagnostic Matrix

| Defect | Cause | Visible Symptom | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Under-parboiling | Insufficient steaming time / low steam pressure | Chalky white core inside grain | Increase steaming time/pressure |
| Over-parboiling | Excessive steaming | Dark brown, burnt odour, cracked grains | Reduce steaming duration |
| Yellow / discoloured grains | High soaking temperature, long soaking | Yellow-amber tint | Lower soak temp to 65 °C |
| Cracking during drying | Rapid drying, no tempering | Visible fissures | Add 4–8 h tempering step |
| Fungal growth | Soak water not changed; long storage | Mouldy odour | Refresh soak water; add HMT treatment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Parboiling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Paddy\n12% w.b.] --> B[Cleaning & Grading]
    B --> C[Soaking Tank\n65-75 C, 12-36 h]
    C --> D[Hydrated Paddy\n30-35% w.b.]
    D --> E[Steam Boiler\n0.5-1.5 kg/cm2]
    E --> F[Steaming Vessel\n10-40 min]
    F --> G[Gelatinized Paddy]
    G --> H[Mechanical Dryer\nStage 1]
    H --> I[Tempering Bins\n4-8 h]
    I --> J[Mechanical Dryer\nStage 2]
    J --> K[Parboiled Paddy\n12-14% w.b.]
    K --> L[Milling Operation]
    L --> M[Parboiled Milled Rice]
    L --> N[By-products: Husk, Bran]
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Starch Gelatinization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Native Starch Granule\nCrystalline, Birefringent] --> B{Water Uptake\nduring Soaking}
    B --> C[Hydrated Granule\nSwollen, Intact]
    C --> D{Heat Application\nT greater than 78 C}
    D --> E[Amorphous Region Melts\nH-bonds Break]
    E --> F[Amylose Leaches Out\nGranule Swells 5-10x]
    F --> G[Fusion of Adjacent Granules\n3D Network Forms]
    G --> H[On Cooling: Retrograded Gel\nHard, Translucent Endosperm]
    H --> I[Resistant to Milling Stress\nHigh Head Rice Yield]
```

---

### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture — Three Industrial Parboiling Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parboiling Methods] --> B[1. Traditional\nSoaking Pit Method]
    A --> C[2. CFTRI Pressure\nParboiling Method]
    A --> D[3. Modern Continuous\nSteam Parboiling]
    B --> B1[Open pit, cold soak\n36-72 h, low HRY]
    C --> C1[Hot soak 75 C\n3-4 h, 1.0-1.5 kg/cm2 steam]
    D --> D1[Continuous rotary\nsoaker, flash steam, high throughput]
    C1 --> E[Improved HRY 65-70%]
    D1 --> E
    B1 --> F[Low HRY 50-55%]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Energy & Mass Balance Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input: Paddy\n1000 kg, 12% w.b.] --> SYS((Parboiling\nSystem))
    IN2[Input: Water\n257 kg, 70 C] --> SYS
    IN3[Input: Steam Energy\n201000 kJ] --> SYS
    SYS --> OUT1[Output: Parboiled Paddy\n1137 kg, 30% w.b.]
    SYS --> OUT2[Output: Spent Water\nOrganic load, leachate]
    SYS --> OUT3[Output: Vent Steam\nMoist air, latent loss]
    SYS --> LOSS[Heat Loss\nto Surroundings]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define parboiling. List any two engineering advantages of parboiled rice over raw rice. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Parboiling is a controlled hydrothermal treatment of paddy consisting of soaking, steaming, and drying to achieve starch gelatinization within the endosperm.
*Advantages:*
1. **Higher head rice yield (65–70%)** due to hardened, crack-resistant endosperm.
2. **Better nutritional retention** — thiamine (Vitamin B1) migrates from the bran into the endosperm during steaming.

---

**Q2. What is starch gelatinization? State the gelatinization temperature range for rice starch. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Starch gelatinization is the **irreversible transformation** of starch granules from an ordered, semi-crystalline state to an amorphous, swollen state when heated in the presence of water above a critical temperature. During this process, hydrogen bonds in the crystalline regions break, water is absorbed, and amylose leaches out of the granule.
- **Gelatinization temperature range for rice starch: 68 – 78 °C.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Bank (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**Q.A (a) Explain the unit operation of parboiling of paddy in detail with a flow diagram. Discuss the physical and chemical changes that occur during the process. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Parboiling is a three-stage hydrothermal process — soaking, steaming, and drying — applied to paddy to gelatinize the starch endosperm.

**2. Process Stages (3 marks):**
- **Soaking:** Paddy immersed in 65–75 °C water for 12–36 h until MC reaches 30–35% (w.b.).
- **Steaming:** Saturated steam at atmospheric or 0.5–1.5 kg/cm² pressure for 10–40 min.
- **Drying:** Two-stage drying to 12–14% (w.b.) with intermediate tempering.

**3. Physical Changes (1.5 marks):**
- Starch granule swelling and amylose leaching.
- Endosperm becomes translucent, hard, and glassy.
- Volume increases ~5–10%; grain density changes.

**4. Chemical Changes (1.5 marks):**
- Irreversible starch gelatinization.
- Vitamin B1 migration from aleurone → endosperm.
- Maillard browning (minor) → amber colour.

**5. Flow Diagram (to be drawn by student):** Cleaning → Soaking → Steaming → Drying → Milling.

---

**Q.A (b) A 2000 kg batch of paddy at 14% (w.b.) moisture is to be soaked to 32% (w.b.). Calculate: (i) the dry matter mass, (ii) the total water required, and (iii) the additional water to be added. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert initial moisture to dry basis.

$$M_{db,i} = \frac{14}{100 - 14} \times 100 = 16.28\ \%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate dry matter.

$$w_d = 2000 \times \left(1 - \frac{14}{100}\right) = 2000 \times 0.86 = 1720\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Convert final moisture to dry basis.

$$M_{db,f} = \frac{32}{100 - 32} \times 100 = 47.06\ \%$$

**Step 4:** Final water mass.

$$w_{w,f} = 1720 \times 0.4706 = 809.4\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5:** Initial water mass.

$$w_{w,i} = 2000 - 1720 = 280\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6:** Additional water.

$$\Delta w_w = 809.4 - 280 = 529.4\ \text{kg}$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) Dry matter = **1720 kg**, (ii) Total water = **809.4 kg**, (iii) Water to be added = **529.4 kg**.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2

**Q.B (a) Compare the traditional soaking pit method, CFTRI pressure parboiling, and modern continuous steam parboiling methods. State the advantages and disadvantages of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Method | Soaking | Steaming | Throughput | HRY | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional Pit | Cold, 36–72 h | Open boiling, 1–2 h | Low (≤ 1 t/h) | 50–55% | Very low |
| CFTRI Pressure | Hot 75 °C, 3–4 h | Pressure vessel 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² | Medium (3–5 t/h) | 65–70% | Medium |
| Modern Continuous | Continuous rotary, hot 70 °C | Flash steam, seconds | High (10–30 t/h) | 68–72% | High |

- **Traditional:** Lowest cost, lowest quality, prone to fermentation & odour.
- **CFTRI:** Best balance of cost and quality; widely adopted in India.
- **Modern Continuous:** Industrial-scale; capital-intensive; best for export-quality rice.

---

**Q.B (b) Explain the principles of moisture diffusion in paddy during soaking. State Fick's second law and explain how soaking temperature affects the effective diffusivity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Fick's Second Law (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$$

This describes unsteady-state moisture diffusion through the grain, where $D_{eff}$ is the effective moisture diffusivity (m²/s), $M$ is local moisture, $t$ is time, and $x$ is the spatial coordinate.

**2. Solution for long-time soaking (2 marks):** Assuming one-dimensional diffusion and constant surface concentration, the moisture content approaches the equilibrium value exponentially:

$$\frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \exp(-k t)$$

where $k$ is a rate constant dependent on $D_{eff}$ and grain geometry.

**3. Temperature effect on $D_{eff}$ (2 marks):** $D_{eff}$ follows an **Arrhenius-type relationship**:

$$D_{eff} = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

- Increasing the soak temperature from 25 °C to 70 °C typically increases $D_{eff}$ by **3–5 times**, drastically reducing soaking time.

**4. Practical significance (1 mark):** This is why **hot soaking (65–75 °C)** is industrially preferred over cold soaking (24–48 h).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Moisture basis confusion:** Students frequently write 32% (w.b.) but apply 32% (d.b.) formulas, losing 1–2 marks. Always **declare the basis** before calculation.
> 2. **Confusing gelatinization with retrogradation:** Retrogradation is the *re-association* of amylose on cooling, not the cooking step. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram:** In 7-mark questions, students omit the flow diagram and lose 1 mark even when the content is correct. Always include a labeled flow diagram.
> 4. **Forgetting latent heat of gelatinization:** When asked for total energy, students calculate only sensible heat. Mention the **33 kJ/kg** latent heat term.
> 5. **Not converting units in final answer:** Always express water mass in **kg** and energy in **kJ or kWh** with units written explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Parboiling = Soaking + Steaming + Drying** (three sequential unit operations).
- ✅ **Primary objective:** Starch gelatinization → improved Head Rice Yield (65–70% vs. 55–60% raw).
- ✅ **Gelatinization temperature of rice starch: 68–78 °C** — *favourite KTU value*.
- ✅ **Soaking:** 65–75 °C, 12–36 h, MC rises from ~12% to ~30–35% (w.b.).
- ✅ **Steaming:** 0.5–1.5 kg/cm² for 10–40 min (CFTRI pressure method).
- ✅ **Drying target:** 12–14% (w.b.) for safe storage; **tempering** between stages reduces cracking.
- ✅ **Vitamin migration:** Thiamine (B1) moves from aleurone layer into the endosperm.
- ✅ **Color change:** White → Amber/Yellow due to Maillard-type reactions and pigment migration.
- ✅ **Methods:** Traditional pit, CFTRI pressure, modern continuous steam.
- ✅ **Common defects:** Under-parboiling (chalky core), over-parboiling (dark, cracked), poor drying (fissures).
- ✅ **Fick's Second Law** governs moisture uptake during soaking.
- ✅ **Arrhenius equation** governs temperature dependence of $D_{eff}$.
- ✅ **Latent heat of gelatinization ≈ 33 kJ/kg** (sensible heat of ~3.5 kJ/kg·°C).
- ✅ **Industrial utility:** Maximizes HRY, improves thiamine retention, kills pests/microbes, reduces cooking-time oil absorption.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling Equipments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Milling Equipments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Milling in Post-Harvest Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Milling** in post-harvest engineering is defined as a **size-reduction unit operation** that involves the application of mechanical forces — such as **impact, attrition, compression, shear, and cutting** — to agricultural produce in order to separate the edible kernel/germ/fraction from the inedible husk, shell, bran, or seed coat. It is a controlled mechanical detachment process that simultaneously performs **dehusking (hulling), polishing (whitening), grading, and separation** of grains, pulses, and oilseeds.

Milling is not a single machine but a **sequence of coordinated unit operations** that convert raw harvested grains into edible, market-ready products with defined particle size, purity, and appearance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Peeling an Onion in Reverse"

Imagine you have a tennis ball completely wrapped in a thick sock, a thin cotton layer, and finally a layer of paint. To get the ball, you would:
1. **Rip off the sock** (Dehusking/Hulling)
2. **Scrape the cotton layer** (Bran removal / Whitening)
3. **Wipe the paint** (Polishing)
4. **Sort the clean balls by size and color** (Grading/Sorting)

That is exactly what a **rice mill** or **dal mill** does to a grain of paddy or a pulse — it performs these same steps using precisely engineered machines in a sequence.

### 1.3 Classification of Milling Equipments (Based on KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> Milling equipments are classified based on the **type of produce processed** and the **function** they perform in the milling chain.

| Class | Example Produce | Primary Equipments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rice Milling** | Paddy | Rubber roll sheller, Engleberg huller, Whitener, Polisher, Paddy separator, Length grader, Colour sorter |
| **Pulse (Dal) Milling** | Lentil, Tur, Gram, Moong | Under-runner disc sheller, CFTRI dhal mill, Roller mill, Attrition mill |
| **Oilseed Milling** | Groundnut, Mustard, Coconut | Expeller, Ghani, Hydraulic press, Solvent extractor |
| **Dry Corn (Maize) Milling** | Maize | Beall degermer, Roller mill, Hammer mill |
| **Spice / General Flour Milling** | Wheat, Chilli, Turmeric | Hammer mill, Pin mill, Disc mill, Burr mill, Roller mill |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics for Milling Equipments

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU examiner expects the following standard parameters (with units in **bold**) for every milling problem:
> - **Capacity (kg/h or t/h)**
> - **Dehusking efficiency (%)**
> - **Broken percentage (%)** — typically should be **< 5 %** for premium grade rice
> - **Head rice recovery (%)** — typically **55–65 %** in a well-run modern mill
> - **Whitening / polishing degree (%)**
> - **Specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne)** — modern standards: **10–15 kWh/tonne**
> - **Bypassed fraction / Un-milled fraction (%)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principles of Major Milling Equipments

#### 2.1.1 Rubber Roll Sheller (Paddy Dehusker)

- **Operating principle:** Two rubber rolls of equal diameter rotate at **unequal speeds** in opposite directions.
- **Speed ratio:** Typically **1.20 to 1.30** (fast roll : slow roll).
- **Linear velocity of fast roll:** **14–18 m/s**.
- **Mechanism of dehusking:** The paddy is sheared between the rolls; the higher tangential velocity of one roll twists the husk, creating a shearing force that cracks and detaches it.
- **Why two speeds?** Equal speeds cause grain to roll without husk removal. Differential speed creates the *shear* that the husk cannot resist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The clearance between rolls is **0.5 to 1.0 mm**, just enough to allow the unhulled paddy to pass without crushing the kernel.

#### 2.1.2 Engleberg Huller (Centrifugal / Steel Huller)

- **Operating principle:** Centrifugal and frictional action of a **rotating steel cylinder** with emery coating against a stationary screen.
- **Function:** Combines dehusking, bran removal, and polishing in a single machine.
- **Drawback:** Produces **excessive broken grains (15–25 %)** and high bran content in head rice due to uncontrolled impact.
- **Status:** Phased out of premium mills; still used in rural low-capacity settings.

#### 2.1.3 Disc Sheller (Under-Runner)

- **Operating principle:** Two horizontal discs — one **rotating** and one **stationary** — with emery-coated abrasive surfaces.
- **Used for:** Pulse dehusking (arhar/tur, moong, urad).
- **Action:** Grains fed centrally move outward by centrifugal force; husk is scraped off by abrasive action.
- **Adjustment:** Disc clearance determines hulling efficiency vs. breakage trade-off.

#### 2.1.4 Whitening / Polishing Machines

| Type | Mechanism | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Abrasive Whitener** | Kernel rubs against a rough emery cone/cylinder | First-stage bran removal |
| **Friction Whitener** | Grain is forced through a perforated screen by a rotating slotted iron cylinder; grain-on-grain friction removes bran | Second-stage polishing |
| **Mist Polisher** | Jet of water mist + friction action; creates starch-coated glossy surface | Final cosmetic gloss |
| **Jet Air Polisher** | High-velocity air stream removes loose bran without friction | Premium low-breakage polishing |

#### 2.1.5 Paddy Separator (Compartment Type)

- **Function:** Separates brown rice (dehusked but unhulled paddy still mixed) from unhusked paddy.
- **Principle:** Difference in **specific gravity (SG)** — paddy ≈ **1.18 g/cm³**, brown rice ≈ **1.20–1.30 g/cm³**.
- **Mechanism:** A vibrating indented compartment allows the heavier brown rice to rise; the lighter paddy slides back to re-shelling.

#### 2.1.6 Hammer Mill

- **Principle:** Impact — swinging hammers rotate at high speed inside a perforated screen.
- **Equation of impact energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$, where $m$ is hammer mass, $v$ is tip speed.
- **Use:** Spice grinding, feed milling, wheat flour.
- **Tip speed range:** **80–120 m/s**.

#### 2.1.7 CFTRI Dal Mill (Pulse Milling)

- **Developed by:** Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysore.
- **Operation sequence:** Premixing of oil (0.5–2 % edible oil) → tempering → sun-drying → dehusking on abrasive rollers → splitting & cleaning.
- **Oil treatment** loosens husk layer from cotyledon, reducing breakage.

#### 2.1.8 Expeller (Oilseed Milling)

- **Principle:** Continuous mechanical expression in a **horizontal screw shaft** inside a perforated barrel.
- **Pressure range:** **1000–3500 kg/cm²** at the choke point.
- **Capacity:** 1–50 tonnes/day per unit.
- **Residual oil in cake:** 5–8 % (vs. 18–22 % in traditional ghani).

#### 2.1.9 Ghani (Traditional Oil Expeller)

- **Principle:** Mortar and pestle action — a wooden or stone pestle rotates inside a stone mortar driven by bullock/power.
- **Batch type**, slow (10–15 kg/batch), cold-pressed, gives premium aromatic oil.
- **Modern relevance:** Cold-pressed / virgin oil branding.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — Milling Equipments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorised verbatim for KTU ESE problems.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Capacity (throughput) | $C = \dfrac{W}{t}$ | kg/h | Machine sizing |
| 2 | Dehusking Efficiency | $\eta_{D} = \dfrac{W_{dh}}{W_{t}} \times 100$ | % | Huller / Sheller |
| 3 | Head Rice Recovery (HRR) | $HRR = \dfrac{W_{hk}}{W_{t}} \times 100$ | % | Rice mill economics |
| 4 | Broken Percentage | $BP = \dfrac{W_{bk}}{W_{hk}+W_{bk}} \times 100$ | % | Quality control |
| 5 | Whitening Efficiency | $\eta_{W} = \dfrac{W_{br} - W_{bw}}{W_{br}} \times 100$ | % | Polisher |
| 6 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_{s} = \dfrac{P}{C}$ | kWh/t | Energy audit |
| 7 | Impact Energy (Hammer) | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | J | Hammer mill design |
| 8 | Hertzian Contact Stress (rolls) | $\sigma_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F}{\pi b L} \cdot \dfrac{E_{r}^{*}}{(R)^{*}}}$ | N/mm² | Roller mill design |
| 9 | Pressure in Expeller | $P = \dfrac{F}{A_{choke}}$ | kg/cm² | Oil expeller |
| 10 | Moisture Reduction in Drying | $M_{R} = \dfrac{M_{i} - M_{f}}{M_{i}} \times 100$ | % | Pre-milling conditioning |
| 11 | Bond's Law (size reduction) | $E = k \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{f}}} \right)$ | kWh/tonne | Energy for grinding |
| 12 | Volumetric Throughput | $Q_{v} = A_{c} \cdot v_{a}$ | m³/s | Pneumatic conveying in mill |
| 13 | Screen Area (for sifter) | $A_{s} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{sp}}$ | m² | Sifter design |
| 14 | Rittinger's Number (Cₜ) | $C_{T} = E \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{L_{e}} - \dfrac{1}{L_{f}} \right)^{-1}$ | - | New surface created |
| 15 | Reynolds No. of aspiration air | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Aspirator duct design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on symbols used:**
> $W$ = weight, $W_{dh}$ = dehusked weight, $W_{t}$ = total weight input, $W_{hk}$ = head kernel weight, $W_{bk}$ = broken kernel weight, $W_{br}$ = brown rice weight, $W_{bw}$ = bran weight, $P$ = power, $F$ = force, $b$ = roll length, $L$ = contact length, $E_{r}$ = reduced modulus, $R$ = roll radius, $M_{i},M_{f}$ = initial/final moisture, $d_{p},d_{f}$ = product/feed size, $A_{c}$ = cross-section area, $v_{a}$ = air velocity, $C_{sp}$ = specific capacity, $L_{e},L_{f}$ = final/feed size, $\rho$ = density, $d$ = duct dia, $\mu$ = viscosity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice export industry (Kerala — Kuttanad, Palakkad):** Selection of rubber roll sheller + multi-stage abrasive whitener + paddy separator gives the **5 % broken or less** required by Saudi Arabia, UAE, and European buyers.
- **Pulse dehusking in Madhya Pradesh & Karnataka:** CFTRI technology doubles yield over traditional chakki methods.
- **Cold-pressed coconut oil (Kerala — Malabar region):** Modern ghani meets export-grade virgin oil standards of **FSSAI**.
- **Energy efficiency drive:** Replacing Engleberg huller with rubber roll sheller alone reduces broken % from 22 % to 4 % and energy by 30 %.
