## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Head Rice Recovery (HRR) from Paddy Mill Mass Balance

**Problem Statement:** A rice mill processes 10,000 kg of cleaned paddy per day. After milling, the following fractions are recovered: brown rice to whitener = 7,800 kg; head rice (whole kernels) from whitener = 6,000 kg; broken rice = 1,200 kg; bran + husk = 2,800 kg. Compute HRR, Broken %, and Dehusking efficiency.

**Step 1 — Identify all output streams**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{t, \text{ input}} &= 10{,}000 \text{ kg (cleaned paddy)} \\
W_{brown\ rice} &= 7{,}800 \text{ kg (output of sheller)} \\
W_{head\ rice} &= 6{,}000 \text{ kg (whole kernels after whitening)} \\
W_{broken\ rice} &= 1{,}200 \text{ kg} \\
W_{husk+bran} &= 2{,}800 \text{ kg} \\
W_{head} + W_{broken} &= 6{,}000 + 1{,}200 = 7{,}200 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: 600 kg difference between brown rice [7,800] and head+broken [7,200] is the bran removed in whitening.)*

**Step 2 — Apply the HRR formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRR \% &= \dfrac{W_{head\ rice}}{W_{total\ paddy}} \times 100 \\
      &= \dfrac{6{,}000}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
      &= 60.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Broken Percentage formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP \% &= \dfrac{W_{broken}}{W_{head} + W_{broken}} \times 100 \\
     &= \dfrac{1{,}200}{7{,}200} \times 100 \\
     &= 16.67 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Dehusking Efficiency formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D} \% &= \dfrac{W_{dehusked\ out\ of\ sheller}}{W_{total\ paddy}} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{7{,}800}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
           &= 78.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Whitening Efficiency (bonus, KTU often asks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{W} \% &= \dfrac{W_{brown\ rice} - W_{total\ milled\ rice}}{W_{brown\ rice}} \times 100 \\
           &= \dfrac{7{,}800 - 7{,}200}{7{,}800} \times 100 \\
           &= 7.69 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> - $HRR = 60\%$ → **acceptable** for a multi-stage modern mill (premium mills achieve **62–66 %**).
> - $BP = 16.67\%$ → **POOR**; this mill has a maintenance/conditioning issue.
> - $\eta_{D} = 78\%$ → **moderate**; an efficient rubber roll sheller should achieve **85–90 %**.
> - $\eta_{W} = 7.69\%$ → **low**; indicates under-polishing, possibly one-pass instead of multi-stage.
> - **Remediation suggested:** Add paddy separator (recycle 22 % unhusked back) → $\eta_{D}$ rises to 90 %; add second whitening pass → $\eta_{W}$ to 12 %; condition paddy at 13–14 % moisture → $BP$ falls to 4–5 %.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Energy Consumption of a Hammer Mill

**Problem Statement:** A hammer mill is driven by a 7.5 kW motor and grinds turmeric at 250 kg/h. Compute the specific energy consumption.

**Step 1 — Capacity in tonnes per hour**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 250 \text{ kg/h} = 0.25 \text{ t/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power in kW**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 7.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{s} &= \dfrac{P}{C} = \dfrac{7.5 \text{ kW}}{0.25 \text{ t/h}} \\
      &= 30 \text{ kWh/t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with KTU benchmark for spice grinding**

> $E_{s}$ for turmeric = **30 kWh/t** — within **20–40 kWh/t** benchmark range.
> **If particle size < 100 µm required, expect 50–80 kWh/t.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bond's Law Energy for Particle Size Reduction

**Problem Statement:** Grind wheat from an initial mean particle size $d_{f} = 1{,}000$ µm to final $d_{p} = 200$ µm using a roller mill. Compute energy per tonne if Bond work index for wheat is $W_{i} = 8.0 \text{ kWh/tonne}$.

**Step 1 — Bond's Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= W_{i} \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{f}}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (sizes in metres, or consistent µm basis with Wi accordingly)**

Working in µm and using Bond's index in kWh/tonne for the given unit basis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8.0 \times \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{200}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1000}} \right) \\
  &= 8.0 \times \left( 0.07071 - 0.03162 \right) \\
  &= 8.0 \times 0.03909 \\
  &= 0.3127 \text{ kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

> Very low energy (0.31 kWh/t) because wheat is friable and pre-broken; for tough materials like turmeric or dhal, $E$ can rise 10–50×.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Milling Equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is a high-yield KTU question source.** Memorise it.

| Property of Input | Recommended Equipment | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Paddy, hard husk | Rubber Roll Sheller | Differential shear, low breakage |
| Paddy, low capacity (< 200 kg/h) | Engleberg huller | Simplicity, low cost |
| Pulse with tight husk bond | CFTRI dhal mill with pre-oil treatment | Loosens husk, reduces breakage |
| Oilseed, large scale | Expeller | Continuous, high oil recovery |
| Oilseed, premium cold-pressed | Ghani | Cold-press, retains aroma |
| Spice, fine powder | Hammer mill + Pin mill combination | Two-stage impact + attrition |
| Wheat flour | Multi-stage roller mill | Progressive reduction |
| Maize for grits | Beall degermer + Roller mill | Removes germ first |
| Grain with stones/heavy impurities | Air-screen cleaner (pre-mill) | Density separation |

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Capacity of an Expeller

**Problem Statement:** An expeller takes 1.2 t/h of copra. The screw rotates at 28 rpm; effective screw length is 1.5 m; effective screw volume is 0.012 m³. The density of copra cake is 600 kg/m³. Compute (i) volumetric throughput, (ii) average cake density in barrel, (iii) residence time.

**Step 1 — Mass throughput**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= 1{,}200 \text{ kg/h} = 0.333 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric throughput of solid cake**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{cake}} = \dfrac{0.333}{600} \\
       &= 5.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
       &= 2.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean residence time in barrel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{V_{barrel}}{\dot{V}} = \dfrac{0.012}{5.56 \times 10^{-4}} \\
     &= 21.6 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Screw pitch linear velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{s} &= \dfrac{L_{screw}}{\tau} = \dfrac{1.5}{21.6} \\
      &= 0.0694 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering check**

> [!NOTE]
> Typical expeller residence time is **15–25 s**; 21.6 s is **within range**. Higher residence time → more oil yield but more friction heating → degrading oil quality.

### 3.6 Derivation 5: Energy Released by Hammer Impact (for design of hammer mill)

**Problem Statement:** A 2 kg hammer rotates at 3,200 rpm; tip radius = 25 cm. Compute (a) tip speed, (b) kinetic energy per hammer.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3200}{60} \\
        &= 335.1 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Tip speed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{tip} &= \omega \times r = 335.1 \times 0.25 \\
        &= 83.78 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_k &= \tfrac{1}{2} m v_{tip}^{2} = 0.5 \times 2 \times (83.78)^{2} \\
    &= 7{,}017 \text{ J per hammer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — For 24 hammers (typical)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 24 \times 7{,}017 = 168{,}408 \text{ J/s} \times 0.1 \text{ (loss factor)} \\
          &= 16.84 \text{ kW usable}
\end{aligned}
$$

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define milling in post-harvest engineering. List any four types of milling equipment.**

**Model Answer:**

Milling is the controlled mechanical size-reduction and separation of edible fractions from inedible coatings of grains, pulses, and oilseeds using impact, attrition, compression, shear, or cutting forces.

Four types of milling equipment:
1. **Rubber Roll Sheller** — for paddy dehusking.
2. **Under-runner Disc Sheller** — for pulse dehusking.
3. **Expeller** — for oilseed oil extraction.
4. **Hammer Mill** — for spice and feed grinding.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 4 types = 1+2)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Engleberg huller and rubber roll sheller.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Engleberg Huller | Rubber Roll Sheller |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Principle | Centrifugal + friction (emery cylinder) | Differential shearing between two rubber rolls |
| Broken % | 15–25 % | 2–5 % |
| Head rice recovery | 35–45 % | 55–65 % |
| Energy consumption | High | Low |
| Use | Obsolete, rural | Modern, export-grade mills |

*(3 marks: tabulated comparison with 3 key distinctions)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Set A]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **rubber roll sheller**. Explain why differential speed is essential for paddy dehusking.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A rice mill processes **1,200 kg/h** of paddy. The dehusker produces **900 kg/h** of brown rice, and the paddy separator rejects **150 kg/h** of unhusked paddy back to the sheller. The whitener produces **720 kg/h** of head rice and **120 kg/h** of broken rice. Calculate the **head rice recovery, dehusking efficiency, and broken percentage**. Comment on the quality.

##### Model Solution A(a)

**Construction of Rubber Roll Sheller:**

1. **Two rubber rolls** of equal diameter (typically 150–250 mm) and length (typically 100–250 mm), both coated with vulcanized rubber of **Shore hardness 75–90**.
2. **One roll** is fixed (slow roll) and the other (fast roll) is driven at **1.20–1.30 times** the slow roll speed by means of a **variable-pitch pulley** or **gear box**.
3. A **feeding tray** with a magnetic trap delivers paddy in a single kernel layer to the nip of the rolls.
4. A **suction aspirator** below the rolls lifts the loose husk by pneumatic air stream and conveys it to a cyclone separator.
5. **Roll clearance** is precisely adjustable between **0.5 and 1.0 mm** by a screw mechanism.

**Working:**

- Paddy grains fall by gravity between the two counter-rotating rolls.
- The **differential speed** generates a tangential shearing action that grips the husk and pulls it tangentially away from the kernel.
- The husk is detached; the brown rice, having no grip with rubber, exits between the rolls.
- The aspirator sucks away the lighter husk.

**Why differential speed is essential:**

- If both rolls rotate at the **same speed**, the paddy is simply nipped and carried through without relative motion between the rolls, so the husk is **not sheared**.
- With differential speed, the **velocity gradient** at the contact point is non-zero, producing the **shear stress** that the husk layer (fibrous, low tensile strength) cannot resist.
- Mathematically, the shear rate is $\dot{\gamma} = (v_1 - v_2) / h$, where $h$ is the husk thickness.
- This shear force is greater than the husk-kernel bond, but less than the kernel's compressive strength, ensuring dehusking without breakage.

*(Marks allocation: 3 construction + 3 working + 1 differential speed logic.)*

##### Model Solution A(b)

**Given data:**

$$
\dot{m}_{input} = 1{,}200 \text{ kg/h}, \quad \dot{m}_{brown\ rice} = 900 \text{ kg/h}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{reject} = 150 \text{ kg/h (recycled)}, \quad \dot{m}_{head} = 720 \text{ kg/h}, \quad \dot{m}_{broken} = 120 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 1 — Head Rice Recovery (HRR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRR \% &= \dfrac{720}{1{,}200} \times 100 = 60.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dehusking Efficiency (single pass)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D} &= \dfrac{900}{1{,}200} \times 100 = 75.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overall dehusking efficiency (including recycle, single-pass effective)**

Effective input to dehusker = 1,200 + 150 = 1,350 kg/h (steady state)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{D, eff} &= \dfrac{900}{1{,}350} \times 100 = 66.67 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Broken Percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP \% &= \dfrac{120}{720 + 120} \times 100 \\
     &= \dfrac{120}{840} \times 100 = 14.29 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Quality**

> [!NOTE]
> - $HRR = 60\%$ → **acceptable for export grade (target ≥ 60 %)**.
> - $\eta_{D} = 75\%$ → **moderate; target is 85–90 %**. With proper paddy conditioning (moisture 13–14 %), $\eta_{D}$ can rise to 88 %.
> - $BP = 14.29\%$ → **POOR**; this mill fails export spec (< 5 %). Probable causes: over-dried paddy (< 12 % moisture), worn rubber rolls, or excessive roll pressure.
> - **Remediation:** Replace rubber rolls if worn > 6 months; re-condition paddy moisture to 13–14 %; reduce roll pressure by 10 %.

*(Marks allocation: HRR 2 marks, $\eta_D$ 2 marks, BP 1 mark, quality comment 2 marks.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Set B]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the construction and working of an **expeller** for oilseed processing. Sketch a block diagram of oil extraction by mechanical pressing. State two advantages of an expeller over a ghani.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A hammer mill grinds **500 kg/h** of turmeric from **2 mm** to **250 µm**. The Bond work index for turmeric is $W_{i} = 9.5$ kWh/tonne. Compute the energy required per tonne and total power consumed by the mill. If the actual motor rating is 18 kW, compute the **specific energy consumption** and the **mechanical efficiency** of the mill.

##### Model Solution B(a)

**Construction of Expeller:**

1. **Horizontal barrel (cage)** — a multi-stage cylindrical cage composed of **vertical bars** separated by small spacers, allowing oil to escape sideways.
2. **Worm shaft (screw)** — a tapered horizontal shaft with **increasing root diameter** along its length.
3. **Choke mechanism** at the discharge end (cone / collar) that builds up back-pressure.
4. **Hopper** at feed end, **kettle cooker** for pre-conditioning in some designs.
5. **Drive assembly** with gearbox and main motor.

**Working:**

- Crushed, heated oilseed enters the worm shaft.
- The screw **conveys and compresses** the material, building pressure.
- Oil escapes through the **cage spacers** under high pressure.
- The cake emerges at the choke with **5–8 % residual oil**.

**Block Diagram (verbal, 2 marks):**

`Seed → Cleaning → Cooking → Expeller → Crude Oil → Refining → Final Oil` and `Cake → Solvent Extraction → Meal`

**Advantages of Expeller over Ghani (2 marks each):**

1. **Continuous operation** — expeller is continuous; ghani is batch. Expeller throughput is **10–50 t/day** vs. ghani's **0.1–0.5 t/day**.
2. **Higher oil recovery** — expeller extracts 92–95 % of available oil; ghani recovers only 70–80 %.
3. **Lower labour cost** — expeller needs 1–2 operators; ghani needs 4–5.
4. **Better hygiene** — expeller is enclosed; ghani has higher dust/foreign matter contamination.

*(Total: 3 construction + 2 working + 1 block diagram + 2 advantages.)*

##### Model Solution B(b)

**Given:**

$$
C = 500 \text{ kg/h} = 0.5 \text{ t/h}, \quad d_{f} = 2{,}000 \text{ µm}, \quad d_{p} = 250 \text{ µm}, \quad W_{i} = 9.5 \text{ kWh/t}
$$

**Step 1 — Energy per tonne using Bond's Law**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= W_{i} \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{f}}} \right) \\
  &= 9.5 \times \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{250}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2000}} \right) \\
  &= 9.5 \times \left( 0.06325 - 0.02236 \right) \\
  &= 9.5 \times 0.04089 \\
  &= 0.3885 \text{ kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total power consumed at 0.5 t/h**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{req} &= E \times C = 0.3885 \times 0.5 \\
        &= 0.1943 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual motor rating**

$$
P_{motor} = 18 \text{ kW (given)}
$$

**Step 4 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) of the mill**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \dfrac{P_{motor}}{C} = \dfrac{18 \text{ kW}}{0.5 \text{ t/h}} \\
    &= 36 \text{ kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{mech} &= \dfrac{P_{req}}{P_{motor}} \times 100 \\
            &= \dfrac{0.1943}{18} \times 100 \\
            &= 1.08 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

> [!WARNING]
> The 1.08 % efficiency is **artificially low** because Bond's Law is valid for **per tonne of size reduction energy**; in practice the motor also drives the **feed system, fan, cyclone, screen and bearings**, all of which consume most of the 18 kW. The **size-reduction energy** itself is small. A more representative metric is the **net milling efficiency** = (theoretical / actual size-reduction energy) which is typically 60–80 %.

> **Revision tip:** If the question asks for energy in Bond's Law, report 0.39 kWh/t; if it asks SEC, divide motor kW by throughput.

*(Marks allocation: 2 for E, 1 for P_req, 1 for SEC, 1 for efficiency, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marking deductions to avoid in this topic:**
> 1. **Wrong unit in capacity** — write **kg/h** or **t/h** consistently. A 1,200 kg/h input should not be used as 1,200 t/h in subsequent formulas.
> 2. **Confusing dehusking efficiency with HRR** — they are **different**. Dehusking efficiency is the % of paddy successfully dehusked. HRR is the % of head (whole) rice recovered from paddy. They look similar but their numerators differ.
> 3. **Forgetting the recycled stream in dehusking efficiency** — the effective input increases when recycle is added; omitting this is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Bond's Law unit confusion** — $d_{f}$ and $d_{p}$ must be in the **same units**, typically µm for food materials. Mixing mm and µm gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 5. **Block diagram missing arrows** — a block diagram without arrows is a 0.5-mark loss in construction questions.
> 6. **Mistaking Engleberg huller for a modern sheller** — Engleberg is **obsolete**; the examiner may deduct marks for recommending it for new mills.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Milling Equipments**

- **Milling** = controlled size reduction + separation of edible kernel from husk/coat/seed shell.
- **Five modes of force application in size reduction:** Impact, Attrition, Compression, Shear, Cutting.
- **Rubber roll sheller** uses **differential speed ratio 1.20–1.30**; roll clearance **0.5–1.0 mm**; **lowest breakage (2–5 %)**.
- **Engleberg huller** = obsolete; high breakage (15–25 %); still found in rural areas.
- **Head Rice Recovery (HRR)** = head rice / total paddy × 100. **Target ≥ 60 %** for export-grade mills.
- **Broken percentage** = broken / (head + broken) × 100. **Target ≤ 5 %**.
- **Dehusking efficiency** = brown rice out / paddy in × 100. **Target ≥ 85 %**.
- **Paddy separator** separates on **specific gravity basis** (paddy 1.18 vs. brown rice 1.20–1.30 g/cm³).
- **Whitening** = removal of bran layer; **polishing** = final cosmetic surface treatment.
- **Abrasive whitener** uses emery; **friction whitener** uses grain-on-grain friction; **mist polisher** uses water + friction.
- **CFTRI dal mill** uses **0.5–2 % oil premixing + tempering** to loosen husk for low-breakage pulse milling.
- **Expeller** = continuous mechanical oil press; pressure **1000–3500 kg/cm²**; residual oil in cake **5–8 %**.
- **Ghani** = batch cold-press; premium virgin oil; low capacity (**10–15 kg/batch**).
- **Hammer mill** energy per particle $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$; tip speed **80–120 m/s**.
- **Bond's Law:** $E = W_i (1/\sqrt{d_p} - 1/\sqrt{d_f})$; **Rittinger's Law** for fine grinding; **Kick's Law** for coarse.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) = P/C (kWh/t)** — modern rice mill SEC = **10–15 kWh/t**.
- **Milling sequence (rice):** Paddy → Cleaner → De-stoner → Rubber roll sheller → Paddy separator → Abrasive whitener (2-stage) → Friction whitener → Mist polisher → Length grader → Colour sorter → Packing.
- **Pulse milling sequence:** Cleaning → Oil premix → Tempering → Sun drying → Abrasive sheller → Pneumatic husk aspiration → Dal splitter → Sieve separator.
- **Oil milling sequence:** Cleaning → Crushing → Cooking (80–100 °C) → Expeller → Oil filtration → Refining → Cake (further solvent extraction optional).
- **Always compute recycle** when a separator rejects material back to upstream equipment — it changes effective throughput.
- **Always state units** with every numerical answer; **always show the formula before substitution**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hullers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hullers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Huller (Hulling Machine):** A huller is a primary post-harvest mechanical processing device designed to remove the **husk** (also called hull, glume, lemma, or palea depending on the crop) from the **caryopsis** (grain/kernel) of cereal grains, oilseeds, and pulses by employing controlled mechanical actions such as **impact, shear, friction, abrasion, or a combination** of these forces, while ensuring minimum kernel breakage and maximum de-hulling efficiency.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEAGT752 – Unit Operations in Post Harvest Engineering), hullers occupy a critical position in the **primary processing** sequence of cereals, particularly **paddy (Oryza sativa)**, where the lignified glumes must be removed before milling, parboiling, or consumption.

The standard KTU terminology requires students to differentiate between:
- **Hulling** – removal of the **husk** (outer chaffy cover)
- **Shelling** – removal of grain from the cob (e.g., maize)
- **Dehulling** – removal of the seed coat (testa) from oilseeds
- **Decortication** – removal of the fibrous outer layer of nuts/pulses
- **Milling** – removal of the bran and germ layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The huller is the *first* unit operation in a typical rice milling flow line. Its performance directly dictates the quality, head-rice yield, and whiteness of the final product. A poorly designed or operated huller will shatter 5–10% of the kernels, cascading into economic losses downstream.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are cracking a **peanut** in your palm. You apply a small **twisting shear force** with your thumb to snap the brittle outer shell *without* crushing the soft kernel inside. That is exactly what a huller does — thousands of times per minute, in a controlled mechanical environment.

Think of paddy grain as a tiny "wrapped candy." The **husk** is the crinkly, non-edible wrapper. A huller is a machine that:
1. **Grips** the grain (between two rolls, a disc, or a rotating impeller and a stator).
2. **Applies a sudden force** (shear, impact, or friction) just strong enough to crack and strip the husk.
3. **Releases** the de-hulled grain (called **brown rice** in paddy processing) gently enough to avoid breaking it.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Parallel:** A rubber roll huller works like running a grain between two rubber "erasers" spinning in opposite directions — one faster than the other — generating a gentle *scuffing* action. A centrifugal (impact) huller, in contrast, throws the grain like a baseball against a curved wall.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following metrics are universally used in KTU board examinations to evaluate huller performance:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Hulling Efficiency** | $\eta_h$ | **%** (typically 75–90%) |
| **Throughput Capacity** | $Q$ | **kg/h** or **t/h** |
| **Head Grain Recovery** | $H_r$ | **%** |
| **Broken Fraction** | $B_f$ | **%** (must be < 5%) |
| **Specific Energy Consumption** | $E_s$ | **kWh per tonne** |
| **Moisture Content (input)** | $M$ | **% w.b.** (optimum 13–14%) |
| **Roller Speed** | $N$ | **rpm** |
| **Roller Linear Velocity** | $v$ | **m/s** (typically 12–15 m/s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All hullers are most efficient when the grain moisture is in the **optimum range of 13–14% (wet basis)**. Above 16%, the husk becomes too pliable (rubbery) and resists removal; below 12%, the kernel becomes brittle and shatters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Working Principles of Hulling

Hulling is fundamentally a **mechanical separation process** that exploits the **differential mechanical strength** between the **husk** (brittle, low-tensile strength) and the **kernel** (relatively tough and elastic). The action can be classified into **four primary modes**:

### A. Shear Action (Rubber Roll Huller)
- Grain is nipped between two parallel rolls rotating at **differential speeds**.
- The faster roll drags the grain, creating a **scissoring shear stress** along the husk–kernel interface.
- The husk is peeled off in flakes; the brown rice remains intact.

### B. Impact Action (Centrifugal / Pneumatic Huller)
- Grain is accelerated to high velocity and struck against a hardened surface.
- The brittle husk shatters on impact; the resilient kernel bounces off.

### C. Abrasion / Friction Action (Disc Huller)
- Grain is rubbed between two abrasive discs, one rotating and one stationary.
- The frictional shear removes the husk layer-by-layer.

### D. Combined Action (Under-runner Disc Huller)
- Combines impact + abrasion + shear; common in traditional small-scale paddy hullers in Kerala.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Rubber-Roll Huller (KTU Favourite)

The **rubber-roll huller** is the **gold standard** in modern rice mills and the most frequently asked huller type in KTU exams. Its operation follows a clean 5-step logic:

1. **Metering (Feeding):** Paddy from the hopper is metered via a **variable-speed feed roll** at a controlled mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s).
2. **Acceleration:** Paddy grains fall by gravity into the nip zone between two rubber rolls.
3. **Nipping & Shear:** The rolls, rotating at speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$ (rpm), grip the grain. The speed differential $\Delta N = N_1 - N_2$ generates a **tangential velocity difference** $\Delta v$ that produces shear.
4. **Husk Removal:** The husk is sheared off and propelled tangentially into the aspiration hood.
5. **Separation:** De-hulled brown rice falls vertically and is conveyed to a **paddy separator** (a gravity-based destoner/separator using the density difference between paddy ≈ 560 kg/m³ and brown rice ≈ 750 kg/m³).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rubber rolls and not steel?** Rubber provides a high coefficient of friction (μ ≈ 0.7–0.9) against the husk but a *low* effective friction against the polished brown rice. This means the rubber "grabs" the husk and slips over the kernel — minimizing kernel breakage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — at least 3 appear in every KTU Board ESE for this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_h = \dfrac{M_{dh}}{M_{in}} \times 100$ | Hulling efficiency (mass of de-hulled grain ÷ input mass) | **%** |
| 2 | $B_f = \dfrac{M_{br}}{M_{dh}} \times 100$ | Broken fraction (broken grain ÷ de-hulled grain) | **%** |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Linear velocity of roll at diameter D, speed N | **m/s** |
| 4 | $\Delta v = v_1 - v_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{60}(D_1 N_1 - D_2 N_2)$ | Velocity difference between two rolls | **m/s** |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | Mass flow rate through nip gap (A = gap area) | **kg/s** |
| 6 | $E_s = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{Q}$ | Specific energy consumption (P in kW, Q in t/h) | **kWh/t** |
| 7 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta v^2}{2 \eta_t}$ | Theoretical power required for impact huller | **W** |
| 8 | $\theta_{nip} = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{r+d}\right)$ | Nip angle for two-roll nipping (r = grain radius, d = half-gap) | **radians** |
| 9 | $C_c = 1 - e^2$ | Coefficient of restitution-related collision energy loss | **dimensionless** |
| 10 | $F_{shear} = \mu \cdot N_{normal}$ | Shear force developed at roll–grain contact | **N** |

**Critical KTU 2024 Definition:**
- **Head Rice Recovery (HRR):** $HRR = \dfrac{M_{whole \, kernels}}{M_{paddy \, input}} \times 100$ — *Note: measured as a fraction of the original paddy, not the brown rice.*

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Note:** In the rubber-roll huller, the *linear velocity difference* $\Delta v$ — not absolute velocity — determines the shearing intensity. A typical design uses $v_1 \approx 12\text{–}15$ m/s and $v_2 \approx 9\text{–}11$ m/s, giving $\Delta v \approx 2.5\text{–}4.5$ m/s.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hullers are the workhorse of the **rice milling industry** (Kerala, Punjab, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu), the **edible oil industry** (mustard, sunflower decortication), and the **pulse processing industry** (tur, chana, moong). Engineers design huller feed systems, aspiration ducts, and downstream paddy separators as an integrated process line. A 1 t/h modern rice mill uses **2 rubber roll hullers in parallel** and consumes approximately **8–12 kWh per tonne** of paddy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Velocity of a Roller

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the linear (tangential) velocity at the surface of a rotating roller of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute.

**Step 1 — Circumference in one revolution:**
A point on the roll surface travels one full circumference per revolution.

$$C = \pi D$$

**Step 2 — Distance per minute:**
At $N$ revolutions per minute, the distance covered in one minute is:

$$L = N \cdot C = N \pi D$$

**Step 3 — Distance per second (divide by 60):**

$$v = \dfrac{N \pi D}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Unit check:**

$$v = \dfrac{\text{rev}}{\text{min}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{rev}} \cdot \dfrac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \;\; \text{m/s}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity Difference & Shear Rate Between Two Rolls

**Statement:** Two rolls of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ rotate at $N_1$ and $N_2$ rpm respectively. Derive the expression for the tangential velocity difference that drives the shearing action on the grain.

**Step 1 — Velocity of roll 1 surface:**

$$v_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of roll 2 surface:**

$$v_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to get velocity difference:**

$$\Delta v = v_1 - v_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} - \dfrac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta v = \dfrac{\pi}{60}(D_1 N_1 - D_2 N_2) \;\; \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**
The shearing strain rate at the husk–kernel interface is proportional to $\Delta v / h$, where $h$ is the husk thickness (≈ 0.05 mm in paddy).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hulling Efficiency and Broken Fraction

**Statement:** Derive the standard KTU expressions for hulling efficiency $\eta_h$ and broken fraction $B_f$ from first principles of mass balance.

**Step 1 — Define system boundary:**
Let $M_{in}$ = mass of paddy entering the huller per unit time.

**Step 2 — Mass balance (no accumulation at steady state):**

$$M_{in} = M_{dh} + M_{unh} + M_{br}$$

where:
- $M_{dh}$ = mass of **properly de-hulled** brown rice
- $M_{unh}$ = mass of **un-hulled** paddy (escaped hulling)
- $M_{br}$ = mass of **broken** kernels (de-hulled but fractured)

**Step 3 — Hulling efficiency definition:**
By definition, efficiency is the useful output over input:

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{M_{dh}}{M_{in}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Broken fraction definition:**
Broken fraction is computed *with respect to de-hulled output*, not the total input:

$$B_f = \dfrac{M_{br}}{M_{dh}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often compute $B_f$ using $M_{in}$ as the denominator. This is wrong — the broken fraction is always a *quality* parameter of the de-hulled output stream, not the feed.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Required for a Centrifugal (Impact) Huller

**Statement:** A centrifugal huller impeller of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ rpm and accelerates paddy grains of effective mass $m_g$ to a tangential velocity $v_t$ before impact. Derive the theoretical power required to achieve this acceleration.

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy imparted to one grain:**

$$KE = \dfrac{1}{2} m_g v_t^2$$

**Step 2 — Number of grains handled per second:**
If mass flow rate is $\dot{m}$ (kg/s), and each grain has mass $m_g$:

$$n_g = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{m_g} \;\; \text{grains/s}$$

**Step 3 — Total power (kinetic energy per second):**

$$P_{ideal} = n_g \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} m_g v_t^2 = \dfrac{\dot{m} v_t^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Account for transmission efficiency $\eta_t$:**

$$\boxed{P_{req} = \dfrac{\dot{m} v_t^2}{2 \eta_t} \;\; \text{Watts}}$$

**Step 5 — Tangential velocity in terms of impeller diameter:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting:**

$$P_{req} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{2 \eta_t} \left( \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \right)^2$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Type):**
> Given: $\dot{m} = 1000 \text{ kg/h} = 0.278$ kg/s, $D = 0.25$ m, $N = 1500$ rpm, $\eta_t = 0.85$.
>
> Calculate $P_{req}$.
>
> $v_t = \dfrac{\pi (0.25)(1500)}{60} = 19.63$ m/s
>
> $P_{req} = \dfrac{0.278 \times (19.63)^2}{2 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{0.278 \times 385.4}{1.70} = \dfrac{107.14}{1.70}$
>
> $\boxed{P_{req} \approx 63.0 \text{ kW}}$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Nip Angle for Two-Roll Nipping

**Statement:** Two rolls of equal radius $r$ with centre-to-centre distance $2(r+d)$ (where $d$ is half the gap) nip a spherical grain of radius $r_g$. Derive the expression for the nip angle $\theta_{nip}$.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup:**
The grain is gripped when the line connecting the two roll centres subtends an angle such that the grain is "wedged" into the converging gap.

**Step 2 — Condition for nipping (geometric):**
By drawing the geometry, the nip angle is given by:

$$\theta_{nip} = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r_{roll}}{r_{roll} + r_{g}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — For equal rolls of radius $r$ gripping grain of radius $r_g$:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{nip} = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{r + r_g}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Physical meaning:**
If $\theta_{nip} > $ friction angle $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$, the grain will be *nipped* (dragged in) by the rolls. If $\theta_{nip} < \phi$, the grain will be *expelled* and fail to enter the nip zone — the huller will "starve."

> [!TIP]
> **Design check:** For paddy grain with $r_g \approx 1.75$ mm and roll radius $r = 100$ mm:
>
> $\theta_{nip} = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{100}{101.75}\right) = \cos^{-1}(0.9828) = 10.65°$
>
> Friction angle $\phi$ for rubber-on-husk is $\approx 35°-40°$ → $10.65° < 35°$ → **Nipping is guaranteed.** ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Rubber-Roll Huller

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture of a rubber-roll hulling system, the **principal huller type** examined in KTU Board papers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper\nPaddy Storage] --> B[Feed Roll\nVariable Speed]
    B --> C[Accelerator Chute\nGravity Drop]
    C --> D[Nip Zone\nRubber Roll 1\nFast 12-15 m/s]
    C --> E[Nip Zone\nRubber Roll 2\nSlow 9-11 m/s]
    D --> F[Aspiration Hood\nHusk Removal]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Husk Collection\nCyclone Separator]
    D --> H[Brown Rice Outlet\nVertical Drop]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Paddy Separator\nDensity Based]
    I --> J[Brown Rice\nTo Polisher]
    I --> K[Unhulled Paddy\nRecycle Loop]
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Textual Block Layout)

```
                    +---------------------------+
                    |     ASPIRATION HOOD       |  <-- Husk suction
                    |  (negative pressure duct) |
                    +-------------||------------+
                                  ||
                                  ||  (husk particles)
        +-----------+   ==================   +-----------+
        |  FEED     |  /                  \  |  ASPI-    |
        |  ROLL     | /   NIP ZONE         \ |  RATOR    |
        |  (mtr)    |/  R1  -->   <-- R2   \| |  DUCT     |
        +-----+-----+   ==================  +-----+-----+
              |    \    |   ||   |           /   |
              |     \   | brown rice (drops)|   |
              |      \  |   ||   |         /    |
        PADDY     \  +-+   ++  ++        /   HUSK
        IN         \ |       ||         /   OUT
                   +--------++--------+
                            ||
                     BROWN RICE OUTLET
                            ||
                            \/
                    [PADDY SEPARATOR]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Huller Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HULLERS[Hullers in Post-Harvest Engineering] --> IMP[Impact Huller\nCentrifugal Type]
    HULLERS --> RRH[Rubber Roll Huller\nShear Type]
    HULLERS --> DSH[Disc Huller\nAbrasion Type]
    HULLERS --> URDH[Under-runner Disc Huller\nTraditional]

    IMP --> I1[High capacity]
    IMP --> I2[More broken grains]
    IMP --> I3[Used in: large rice mills]

    RRH --> R1[Low broken fraction]
    RRH --> R2[Highest HRR]
    RRH --> R3[Used in: modern rice mills]

    DSH --> D1[Moderate capacity]
    DSH --> D2[Versatile for multiple grains]
    DSH --> D3[Used in: pulse processing]

    URDH --> U1[Low cost]
    URDH --> U2[Manual/small-scale]
    URDH --> U3[Used in: Kerala traditional units]
```

## 4.4 Mass-Flow Block Architecture Through a Hulling Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Input Stream]
        A1[Paddy at\n13-14% MC wb]
    end

    subgraph CLEAN [Pre-Cleaning]
        B1[Drum Sieve]
        B2[Aspirator]
    end

    subgraph HULL [Hulling Operation]
        C1[Rubber Roll Huller]
        C2[Husk Aspiration]
    end

    subgraph SEP [Separation]
        D1[Paddy Separator]
        D2[Thickness Grader]
    end

    subgraph POLISH [Polishing & Grading]
        E1[Whitener]
        E2[Length Grader]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [Output Streams]
        F1[Head Rice]
        F2[Brokens]
        F3[Bran]
        F4[Husk - fuel]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1 --> D2 --> E1 --> E2
    C2 --> F4
    D1 --> A1
    E1 --> F3
    E2 --> F1
    E2 --> F2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Board Exams:** In the rubber-roll huller block diagram, the **closed recycle loop** (from paddy separator back to feed roll) is critical. Approximately **15–25% of paddy remains un-hulled** in a single pass and must be recycled for 90%+ hulling efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Hulling Efficiency" as applied to a paddy huller. State the standard expression and the typical range of values in commercial hullers.**

**Model Answer:**
Hulling efficiency $\eta_h$ is defined as the ratio of the mass of properly de-hulled grain (brown rice) obtained at the huller outlet to the total mass of paddy fed into the huller, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_h = \dfrac{M_{dh}}{M_{in}} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

For a well-designed **rubber roll huller**, $\eta_h$ typically ranges from **80% to 90%** in a single pass and **95–98%** in a double-pass system with recycle. The remaining 2–5% is un-hulled paddy that is recycled to the feed.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between "hulling," "shelling," and "decortication" with one crop example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Operation | Definition | Crop Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hulling** | Removal of the outer husk (glume) from a caryopsis | Paddy → Brown rice |
| **Shelling** | Removal of grains from a cob/spike/ear | Maize, sorghum |
| **Decortication** | Removal of the fibrous outer skin (testa) from a seed/nut | Groundnut, cashew |

> Key distinction: hulling deals with the *chaffy* bracts on a grain, shelling deals with the *cob-to-grain* separation, and decortication deals with the *seed coat* of a dicot or nut.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — Rubber Roll Huller Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A rubber roll huller is to be designed for a small rice mill. The two rolls have diameters **D₁ = 220 mm** and **D₂ = 200 mm** and rotate at **N₁ = 950 rpm** and **N₂ = 750 rpm**, respectively. The input paddy mass flow rate is **800 kg/h** and the moisture content is **13.5% (w.b.)**.

#### **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the linear surface velocities of both rolls, the velocity difference at the nip, and the corresponding shear rate (assume husk thickness $h = 0.05$ mm).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of roll 1:**

$$v_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.220 \times 950}{60} = \dfrac{656.6}{60} = 10.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity of roll 2:**

$$v_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.200 \times 750}{60} = \dfrac{471.2}{60} = 7.85 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity difference:**

$$\Delta v = v_1 - v_2 = 10.94 - 7.85 = 3.09 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Shear strain rate at husk interface:**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\Delta v}{h} = \dfrac{3.09}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.18 \times 10^4 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

> This high shear rate is responsible for the *peeling* of the husk from the brown rice.

#### **(b)** [7 Marks] If the hulling efficiency achieved is **85%** and the broken fraction is **3%**, calculate the mass of (i) brown rice obtained, (ii) broken kernels, and (iii) un-hulled paddy per hour. Also estimate the **specific energy consumption** if the motor draws **6.5 kW**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $M_{in} = 800$ kg/h, $\eta_h = 85\%$, $B_f = 3\%$, $P = 6.5$ kW.

**Step 1 — Mass of properly de-hulled brown rice (whole kernels):**

$$M_{dh} = \eta_h \times M_{in} = 0.85 \times 800 = 680 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of broken kernels:**

$$M_{br} = B_f \times M_{dh} = 0.03 \times 680 = 20.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of un-hulled paddy (mass balance):**

$$M_{unh} = M_{in} - M_{dh} - M_{br} = 800 - 680 - 20.4 = 99.6 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Specific energy consumption:**

$$E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{6.5 \text{ kW}}{0.8 \text{ t/h}} = 8.125 \text{ kWh/t}$$

> This falls within the industry-standard 8–12 kWh/t range, indicating an efficient huller.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Alternative: Impact Huller Design
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

A centrifugal (impact) huller has an impeller of diameter **D = 0.30 m** rotating at **N = 1800 rpm**. The transmission efficiency is **85%** and the paddy mass flow rate is **1200 kg/h**.

#### **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the tangential velocity of the impeller, the theoretical power required, and the motor rating you would specify (with a 25% safety factor).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate in SI units:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{1200}{3600} = 0.3333 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Tangential velocity:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1800}{60} = 28.27 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical power required:**

$$P_{req} = \dfrac{\dot{m} v_t^2}{2 \eta_t} = \dfrac{0.3333 \times (28.27)^2}{2 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{0.3333 \times 799.2}{1.70}$$

$$P_{req} = \dfrac{266.36}{1.70} = 156.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Motor rating with 25% safety factor:**

$$P_{motor} = 1.25 \times 156.7 = 195.9 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 200 \text{ kW (round to next standard size)}}$$

#### **(b)** [7 Marks] The huller delivers brown rice at **78% efficiency** with **5% breakage**. Calculate: (i) brown rice output per hour, (ii) un-hulled paddy per hour, (iii) total head-rice recovery if 90% of the brown rice is whole.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $M_{in} = 1200$ kg/h, $\eta_h = 78\%$, $B_f = 5\%$, whole-kernel fraction in brown rice = 90%.

**Step 1 — De-hulled brown rice:**

$$M_{dh} = 0.78 \times 1200 = 936 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Broken kernels:**

$$M_{br} = 0.05 \times 936 = 46.8 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Un-hulled paddy:**

$$M_{unh} = 1200 - 936 - 46.8 = 217.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Whole (head) rice:**

$$M_{whole} = 0.90 \times 936 = 842.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Head Rice Recovery (HRR), referenced to input paddy:**

$$HRR = \dfrac{842.4}{1200} \times 100 = 70.2\%$$

> This is significantly lower than the 75–80% HRR typical of rubber-roll hullers — a key reason why modern mills prefer rubber-roll over impact hullers for premium rice grades.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Mixing rpm with rps, or using mm instead of m in velocity calculations. Always convert to SI *before* substituting.
> 2. **Wrong denominator in broken fraction:** $B_f$ uses $M_{dh}$, not $M_{in}$. This single error can cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting transmission efficiency:** Power calculations without $\eta_t$ will give an *underrated* motor specification.
> 4. **Moisture content confusion:** KTU often specifies moisture on wet basis (w.b.) — convert to dry basis only if the formula demands it.
> 5. **Confusing "husk" with "bran":** Husk is the outer chaffy layer removed by the huller; bran is the inner oily layer removed by the polisher. Examiners will deduct marks if these are interchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Huller = primary post-harvest device** that removes the **outer husk** from a grain kernel using **shear, impact, abrasion, or a combination**.
- **Four main types** of hullers: **Rubber roll, Centrifugal (impact), Disc, and Under-runner disc**. The rubber roll huller is the **industry standard** and the **most frequently asked** in KTU exams.
- **Hulling efficiency** $\eta_h = (M_{dh}/M_{in}) \times 100$ — typical range **75–90%** in single pass, **95–98%** with recycle.
- **Broken fraction** $B_f = (M_{br}/M_{dh}) \times 100$ — keep below **5%** for premium rice grades.
- **Linear velocity of a roll:** $v = \pi D N / 60$ — ensure SI units throughout.
- **Velocity difference $\Delta v$** (typically 2.5–4.5 m/s) drives the shearing action in rubber roll hullers.
- **Nip angle condition:** $\theta_{nip} < \phi$ (friction angle) is required for the grain to be *dragged into* the nip — a critical design check.
- **Optimum moisture content for hulling:** **13–14% (w.b.)** — too wet = rubbery husk; too dry = brittle kernel.
- **Centrifugal huller power formula:** $P_{req} = \dot{m} v_t^2 / (2 \eta_t)$ — always account for transmission efficiency.
- **Husk density ≈ 130 kg/m³** (floats in air streams, hence aspiration separation works); **brown rice density ≈ 750 kg/m³** (falls through aspiration, enabling separation).
- **Head Rice Recovery (HRR)** is measured *with respect to paddy input*, **not** the de-hulled output — a frequent KTU trap.
- **Rubber roll material** is typically a **polyurethane compound** of **Shore A hardness 80–95**, providing high friction on husk but low friction on polished rice.
- **Specific energy consumption** for modern rubber roll hullers: **8–12 kWh per tonne** of paddy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "design a huller" in a 14-mark question, always include (1) choice of huller type with justification, (2) capacity calculation, (3) roll/diameter/speed selection, (4) power calculation with safety factor, and (5) a brief mass balance with efficiency and broken fraction. This 5-step structure covers all KTU CO mapping for Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sheller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sheller # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sheller (Dehusker / Decorticator):** A post-harvest processing machine that mechanically separates the edible kernel or seed from its outer protective covering, such as the cob in maize, the pod in groundnut, or the husk in paddy, through the application of impact, shear, rubbing, or rolling forces. In **Post Harvest Engineering**, a sheller is classified as a primary processing unit operation that bridges the gap between **threshing** (separation of grain from the plant) and **cleaning/grading**. It is specifically engineered to handle grains where the kernel remains tightly attached to a non-edible matrix, unlike free-threshing cereals. ### 1.2 Categorization of Shellers by Crop | Crop | Matrix Removed | Generic Term | Key Engineering Challenge | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Maize / Corn** | Kernels from cob | Sheller | High breakage due to brittle kernels | | **Groundnut** | Seed from pod | Decorticator | High oil content causes slippage | | **Paddy** | Husk from paddy | Dehusker / Sheller | High friction, low impact required | | **Sunflower** | Seed from head | Thresher-Sheller | Selective breakage of receptacle | | **Coconut** | Husk from shell | Dehusker | High toughness, manual dominance | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Nutcracker Concept"** Imagine cracking a walnut. You apply a controlled force — too little and the shell stays intact, too much and you crush the edible kernel inside. A **sheller** is an industrial-scale, mechanized "nutcracker." It uses rapidly rotating drums, beaters, or rollers to apply thousands of precisely controlled impacts per second, stripping the protective outer layer (cob/pod) without damaging the fragile kernel inside. The fundamental engineering trade-off in any sheller is the balance between **three competing parameters**: 1. **Shelling Efficiency** (how much kernel is removed) 2. **Mechanical Damage** (broken/cracked kernels) 3. **Throughput Capacity** (tonnes/hour) A good sheller maximizes efficiency and capacity while keeping damage to a minimum — typically below **2–5%** for commercial-grade maize. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Maize Shellers (Hierarchical Breakdown) ### A. Manual / Hand-Operated Shellers - **Hand-held finger-type shellers:** Operator inserts the cob into a toothed ring and rotates manually. - **Hand-cranked rotary shellers:** Single cob capacity; used in small farms. - Capacity: ~30–50 kg/h with high labor input. ### B. Power-Operated Shellers > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** Power-operated shellers are the primary syllabus topic. The classification is based on the **shelling mechanism** (the heart of the machine). #### (i) Rasp-Bar / Drum-Type Sheller - A horizontal rotating **drum with rasp bars (ribbed metal strips)** acts against a stationary **concave screen**. - Cobs are fed tangentially; the rubbing action of rasp bars strips kernels. - Best for **dried cobs** with moisture content **< 14%** (wet basis). #### (ii) Peg-Tooth / Spike-Tooth Cylinder Sheller - Cylinder is fitted with **teeth/pegs arranged in rows** (similar to a thresher cylinder). - Spinning pegs impact kernels off the cob. - Highly aggressive — best for **high-capacity, low-moisture** operations. #### (iii) Centrifugal Sheller - Cobs are fed into a **high-speed rotating disc**; centrifugal force throws kernels off the cob. - Compact, simple, but causes higher breakage. #### (iv) Rubber-Roller Sheller - Two **counter-rotating rubber rollers** rotating at differential speeds apply a **gentle rubbing-shearing** action. - Preferred for **paddy dehusking** and delicate seeds; minimum kernel damage. #### (v) Disc / Cone-Type Sheller - Two abrasive discs (one rotating, one stationary) rub the grain between them. - Used for **paddy and small seeds**. ## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Power Maize Sheller Every power-operated sheller has these **six universal subsystems**: 1. **Frame & Body:** Cast iron / MS angle structure housing the mechanism. 2. **Feed Hopper:** Inclined chute with a flow-control gate; gravity-fed or conveyor-fed. 3. **Shelling Cylinder / Rotor:** The heart — drum fitted with rasp bars, pegs, or beaters. 4. **Concave:** Perforated stationary screen under the cylinder; allows shelled kernels + small debris to fall through while cobs pass over. 5. **Blower / Fan Assembly:** Cross-flow or centrifugal fan for separation of light chaff from kernels. 6. **Power Transmission:** V-belt and pulley system from electric motor or PTO (Power Take-Off) shaft of tractor. ## 2.3 Working Principle (Step-by-Step) - **Step 1:** Dried maize cobs are poured into the feed hopper. A flow regulator controls the feed rate to prevent overloading. - **Step 2:** Cobs enter the clearance between the rotating cylinder and the stationary concave. The clearance is called the **threshing/shelling gap** (typically 15–25 mm for maize). - **Step 3:** The rotating rasp bars/pegs impact the kernels. The impact, combined with rubbing against the concave, detaches kernels from the cob. - **Step 4:** Shelled kernels and small chaff pass through the concave perforations into the cleaning section. - **Step 5:** The cleaned cobs are discharged axially from the tail of the cylinder (axial flow) or radially (tangential flow). - **Step 6:** The blower separates lightweight impurities (husk, broken chaff) from kernels. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Bank for KTU Board Exams** — Memorize these equations and their units. Most numerical problems in Module 3 are direct applications of these formulas. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Shelling Efficiency ($\eta_s$) | $\eta_s = \dfrac{W_k}{W_t} \times 100$ | % | $W_k$ = wt. of shelled kernels recovered, $W_t$ = total kernel wt. in feed | | 2 | Cleaning Efficiency ($\eta_c$) | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_{ck}}{W_t} \times 100$ | % | $W_{ck}$ = wt. of clean kernels after blower | | 3 | Mechanical Damage ($\eta_d$) | $\eta_d = \dfrac{W_{bk}}{W_k} \times 100$ | % | $W_{bk}$ = wt. of broken kernels; **<5% desirable** | | 4 | Unshelled Percentage ($U$) | $U = 100 - \eta_s$ | % | Kernels still attached to discharged cobs | | 5 | Throughput ($Q$) | $Q = \dfrac{M}{t}$ | kg/h or t/h | $M$ = mass of feed, $t$ = time | | 6 | Cylinder Peripheral Speed ($V$) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = cylinder dia. (m), $N$ = rpm | | 7 | Power Requirement ($P$) | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W or kW | $T$ = torque (N·m) | | 8 | Moisture Content (w.b.) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | $W_w$ = wet mass, $W_d$ = dry mass | | 9 | Feed Rate Per Cylinder Length | $q = \dfrac{Q}{L}$ | kg/h·m | $L$ = cylinder length | | 10 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | kWh/tonne | Energy efficiency indicator | > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The shelling efficiency equation is used by ICAR, CIMMYT, and ISO standards to certify commercial maize shellers. The damage percentage governs the **grade** of the produce and its market price — broken kernels fetch 30–50% lower prices. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Peripheral Speed The peripheral speed of the shelling cylinder determines the **impact energy** delivered to the kernels. This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 3. ### Statement Derive an expression for the peripheral speed of the shelling cylinder in terms of its diameter and rotational speed. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define peripheral speed.** Peripheral speed is the linear tangential velocity of a point on the surface of the rotating cylinder. By definition of angular velocity: $$V = \omega \cdot r$$ where $V$ is the peripheral speed in m/s, $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s, and $r$ is the radius of the cylinder in meters. **Step 2: Express angular velocity in terms of RPM.** The rotational speed of the cylinder is typically given in revolutions per minute (N, rpm). One revolution = $2\pi$ radians. So the angular velocity is: $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$ **Step 3: Express radius in terms of diameter.** Since $r = D/2$, substitute into the peripheral speed equation: $$V = \left( \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{D}{2} \right)$$ **Step 4: Simplify the expression.** $$V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$$ **Step 5: State the final result with units.** $$\boxed{V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \text{ m/s}}$$ ### Design Implication For maize shelling, the recommended peripheral speed is **8–12 m/s**. Below 8 m/s causes incomplete shelling; above 15 m/s causes excessive kernel breakage. From this equation, one can back-calculate the required rpm for a given cylinder diameter. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Application — KTU-Style Problem > **Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern):** A power-operated maize sheller has a cylinder diameter of **0.40 m** running at **900 rpm**. The feed rate is **1500 kg/h** of maize cobs with an initial moisture content of **15% (w.b.)**. After shelling, 88% of kernels are recovered cleanly, and **2.4%** of recovered kernels are broken. Calculate: > (a) Peripheral speed of the cylinder. > (b) Shelling efficiency. > (c) Mechanical damage percentage. > (d) Specific energy consumption if the motor draws **2.2 kW**. ### Part (a): Peripheral Speed **Given:** $D = 0.40 \text{ m}$, $N = 900 \text{ rpm}$. Apply the derived formula: $$V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$$ $$V = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 900}{60}$$ $$V = \dfrac{1130.97}{60}$$ $$\boxed{V \approx 18.85 \text{ m/s}}$$ > **Examiner Note:** This speed is on the **higher end** — it will cause elevated kernel damage. A design recommendation would be to reduce N to ~600 rpm for this diameter to bring V into the optimal 8–12 m/s range. ### Part (b): Shelling Efficiency Shelling efficiency is given as **88%** in the problem statement. Hence: $$\eta_s = 88\%$$ For verification, if total kernel weight in feed $W_t$ and recovered clean kernel weight $W_k$ were measured, the formula would be: $$\eta_s = \dfrac{W_k}{W_t} \times 100$$ ### Part (c): Mechanical Damage Percentage The damage percentage is given directly as **2.4%** (i.e., $W_{bk}/W_k = 0.024$): $$\eta_d = \dfrac{W_{bk}}{W_k} \times 100 = 2.4\%$$ This is within the **acceptable commercial limit of < 5%**. ### Part (d): Specific Energy Consumption **Given:** $P = 2.2 \text{ kW}$, $Q = 1500 \text{ kg/h} = 1.5 \text{ t/h}$. $$E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{2.2 \text{ kW}}{1.5 \text{ t/h}}$$ $$\boxed{E_s \approx 1.467 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$$ This is an efficient figure; commercial shellers typically consume **1.5–3.0 kWh/tonne**. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Sheller Selection | Crop/Condition | Best Sheller Type | Cylinder Speed (m/s) | Concave Clearance | Expected Damage | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Dry maize (< 14% MC) | Rasp-bar drum | 8 – 12 | 18 – 25 mm | < 3% | | Wet maize (> 18% MC) | Peg-tooth cylinder | 10 – 14 | 22 – 30 mm | 3 – 6% | | Paddy | Rubber-roller | 12 – 16 | 0.5 – 1.0 mm | < 2% | | Groundnut pods | Oscillating sieve | N/A | Mechanical compression | 2 – 4% | | Sunflower | Combine-thresher | 6 – 9 | 15 – 20 mm | 4 – 7% | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Power-Operated Maize Sheller The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of material and power** through the six universal subsystems of a power maize sheller. This is the most critical schematic for KTU board exams — examiners frequently ask students to "draw and label" or "explain the block diagram." ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cob Feeding Stage
Hopper with Flow Gate] --> B[Shelling Cylinder
Rasp Bars on Rotor] B --> C[Concave Screen
Perforated MS Plate] C --> D[Cleaning Blower
Centrifugal Fan] D --> E[Sieves and Separators
Vibrating Type] E --> F[Clean Kernel Discharge
Primary Outlet] C --> G[Cob Discharge
Axial Tail End] B --> H[Power Source
Electric Motor 3-5 kW] H --> I[Belt Pulley System
V Belt Drive] I --> B I --> D D --> J[Chaff Dust Outlet
Cyclone Collector] ``` ### Diagram Reading Guide - **Material flow:** `A → B → C → D → E → F` (clean kernels) and `C → G` (discharged cobs). - **Power flow:** `H → I → B` (cylinder) and `I → D` (blower). - **Waste flow:** `D → J` (light chaff aspirated by fan). ## 4.2 Rasp-Bar Cylinder Schematic (Top-View Cross Section) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed In] --> B[Rasp Bar Cylinder] B -- Rotation --> C[Kernels Stripped] C --> D[Concave Perforations] D --> E[Kernels Fall Through] B --> F[Empty Cob Traverse] F --> G[Cob Discharge] B -.Shear and Rub Action.-> H[Rasp Bars] H -.Against.-> D ``` ## 4.3 Rubber-Roller Dehusker (Paddy Sheller) Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Paddy Feed] --> B[Top Rubber Roller
Faster Speed] A --> C[Bottom Rubber Roller
Slower Speed] B -- Shear --> D[Dehusking Zone] C -- Shear --> D D --> E[Husk Aspirated
by Blower] D --> F[Brown Rice Discharge] B --> G[Differential Speed
Ratio 1.25 to 1.30] G --> C ``` > **Concept Highlight:** The **differential roller speed** is the key innovation. Both rollers rotate in opposite directions, but the upper one spins ~25–30% faster. This creates a **velocity gradient** at the nip that gently shears the husk off — no impact, minimal breakage. ## 4.4 Comparative Schematic of Sheller Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sheller Type] --> B[Impact Type] A --> C[Rubbing or Shear Type] A --> D[Centrifugal Type] B --> B1[Peg Tooth Cylinder] B --> B2[Spike Tooth Cylinder] C --> C1[Rasp Bar Drum] C --> C2[Rubber Roller] C --> C3[Disc or Cone] D --> D1[Rotary Disc Sheller] B1 --> X[High Capacity, High Damage] B2 --> X C1 --> Y[Balanced Performance] C2 --> Y C3 --> Y D1 --> Z[Compact, Moderate Damage] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Sheller. Differentiate it from a Thresher. **[KTU University Exam – May 2022]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **sheller** is a post-harvest machine that removes grains/seeds from a firmly attached non-edible matrix such as maize cob, groundnut pod, or sunflower head. A **thresher**, on the other hand, separates already-loose grains from the cut plant (e.g., wheat, paddy from straw). The key difference is the **adhesion strength**: shellers deal with tight biological bonding (kernel to cob/pod), while threshers deal with loose straw-grain separation. **(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for difference + 1 mark for example)** ### Question 2: What is Shelling Efficiency? State the standard commercial value for maize. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Shelling efficiency is the ratio of shelled kernel weight recovered to the total kernel weight present in the feed, expressed as a percentage. $$\eta_s = \dfrac{W_k}{W_t} \times 100$$ For commercial-grade maize shellers, the standard acceptable value is **> 95%** in modern power-operated units. **(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for formula and standard value)** --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]** > **(a)** Classify power-operated maize shellers with neat block diagrams. Explain the working of a **rasp-bar drum type maize sheller** in detail. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A rasp-bar maize sheller has a cylinder diameter of **50 cm** and operates at **750 rpm**. If the moisture content of maize is **13% (w.b.)** and the feed rate is **2000 kg/h**, calculate: (i) Peripheral speed of the cylinder, (ii) The expected damage percentage range, and (iii) Specific energy consumption if a **3.7 kW** motor is used. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Classification and Working (7 Marks)** **Classification of Power-Operated Maize Shellers** (2 Marks): - **Impact-type:** Peg-tooth cylinder, spike-tooth cylinder. - **Rubbing/Shear-type:** Rasp-bar drum, rubber-roller, disc/cone. - **Centrifugal-type:** Rotary disc sheller. **Working of Rasp-Bar Drum Sheller** (5 Marks): The **rasp-bar drum sheller** consists of a horizontal rotating drum fitted with 6–8 longitudinal **rasp bars** (corrugated steel strips) arranged helically on its periphery. The drum rotates inside a **stationary concave screen** with perforations of 12–16 mm diameter. **Step 1 — Feeding:** Dried maize cobs (moisture < 14%) are fed through the hopper onto the rotating drum. **Step 2 — Shelling Action:** As the drum rotates at 600–900 rpm, the rasp bars strike the cobs. The combined **impact + shear + rubbing** action between the rasp bars and the abrasive concave surface detaches the kernels from the cob. **Step 3 — Separation:** The detached kernels, along with broken chaff, fall through the concave perforations due to gravity. The cobs continue to travel axially and are discharged from the tail end. **Step 4 — Cleaning:** The mixture of kernels and chaff falls onto an oscillating sieve. A centrifugal blower aspirates the lighter chaff particles; the heavier clean kernels pass through the sieves and are collected at the discharge outlet. **Step 5 — Cob Discharge:** Bare cobs are ejected at the rear and can be collected for use as fuel, fodder, or compost. > **Examiner Tip:** Always state the **clearance range** (18–25 mm) and **peripheral speed** (8–12 m/s) for rasp-bar shellers — these are the "magic numbers" the examiner expects. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** **Given:** $D = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}$, $N = 750 \text{ rpm}$, $Q = 2000 \text{ kg/h}$, $P = 3.7 \text{ kW}$, $M_{wb} = 13\%$. **(i) Peripheral Speed (2.5 Marks):** $$V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.50 \times 750}{60}$$ $$V = \dfrac{1178.10}{60} = 19.63 \text{ m/s}$$ > **Interpretation:** 19.63 m/s is **too high** (optimal 8–12 m/s). This will cause kernel damage > 5%. The recommended rpm is: > $N = \dfrac{60 V}{\pi D} = \dfrac{60 \times 10}{\pi \times 0.50} \approx 382 \text{ rpm}$ **(ii) Expected Damage Percentage (2 Marks):** At 19.63 m/s, the expected damage range is **5–8%** (above the commercial threshold of 5%). **(iii) Specific Energy Consumption (2.5 Marks):** $$Q = 2000 \text{ kg/h} = 2.0 \text{ t/h}$$ $$E_s = \dfrac{P}{Q} = \dfrac{3.7}{2.0} = 1.85 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$ This is within the **efficient range of 1.5–3.0 kWh/tonne**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** > **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **rubber-roller paddy sheller**. Why is differential roller speed essential? **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** What are the performance parameters used to evaluate a sheller? Define and write the formula for **shelling efficiency, cleaning efficiency, and mechanical damage percentage**. A sheller processes 500 kg of maize cobs in 30 minutes. The clean shelled kernel output is 280 kg, and the broken kernel fraction is 8 kg. Calculate the **shelling efficiency** and **damage percentage**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Rubber-Roller Paddy Sheller (7 Marks)** **Construction (3.5 Marks):** - Two parallel **horizontal rubber rollers** of equal diameter (typically 150–250 mm) and length (~200 mm). - The **upper roller** rotates at higher speed; the **lower roller** rotates ~25–30% slower (e.g., upper at 1200 rpm, lower at 950 rpm). - Rollers are covered with **food-grade rubber** of 50–60 Shore A hardness. - A **feed hopper** above the nip; an **aspirator blower** below to lift husks; a **brown rice collection tray** at the base. - Rollers are mounted on spring-loaded bearings to maintain constant pressure. **Working (2 Marks):** Paddy grains enter the nip region between the two rollers. The upper roller pulls the grain in; the differential speed creates a **velocity gradient** that applies a shearing force tangential to the grain surface. This shears off the husk without breaking the brown rice kernel. The husk is blown away by the aspirator; the brown rice falls into the collection tray. **Why Differential Speed is Essential (1.5 Marks):** If both rollers rotated at the same speed, the grain would simply be **compressed** between them — resulting in kernel breakage. The differential speed creates a **scraping/sliding action** at the surface of the grain, removing the husk like peeling. The speed ratio (typically 1.25:1 to 1.30:1) is the critical design parameter. **(b) Performance Parameters and Numerical (7 Marks)** **Performance Parameters (3.5 Marks):** 1. **Shelling Efficiency ($\eta_s$):** Ratio of shelled kernel weight to total kernel weight. $$\eta_s = \dfrac{W_k}{W_t} \times 100$$ 2. **Cleaning Efficiency ($\eta_c$):** Ratio of clean kernel weight to total shelled kernel weight. $$\eta_c = \dfrac{W_{ck}}{W_k} \times 100$$ 3. **Mechanical Damage ($\eta_d$):** Ratio of broken kernel weight to total shelled kernel weight. $$\eta_d = \dfrac{W_{bk}}{W_k} \times 100$$ 4. **Throughput/Capacity** in kg/h or t/h. 5. **Specific Energy Consumption** in kWh/tonne. **Numerical (3.5 Marks):** **Given:** Total feed = 500 kg in 30 min, $W_k = 280 \text{ kg}$, $W_{bk} = 8 \text{ kg}$. Assumption: For maize, the average shelling ratio (kernel : cob) is approximately 0.80 (i.e., 80% of cob weight is kernels). Hence total kernel content in feed: $$W_t = 0.80 \times 500 = 400 \text{ kg}$$ **(i) Shelling Efficiency:** $$\eta_s = \dfrac{W_k}{W_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{280}{400} \times 100 = 70\%$$ This is **low** — a good sheller should achieve > 95%. The remaining 30% are unshelled kernels still on the cobs. **(ii) Damage Percentage:** $$\eta_d = \dfrac{W_{bk}}{W_k} \times 100 = \dfrac{8}{280} \times 100 \approx 2.86\%$$ This is within the **acceptable limit (< 5%)**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Sheller Questions — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Students often forget to convert **cm to m** for diameter, **minutes to hours** for throughput, or **W to kW** for power. A 1–2 mark penalty applies for inconsistent units. > 2. **Confusing Shelling Efficiency with Cleaning Efficiency:** $\eta_s$ uses **total kernel weight in feed ($W_t$)** in the denominator; $\eta_c$ uses **shelled kernel weight ($W_k$)** — they are **not interchangeable**. > 3. **Damage Percentage Denominator:** Damage is always computed on $W_k$ (recovered kernel), **not on the total feed**. Writing $\eta_d = W_{bk}/W_{total\_feed}$ will cost full marks. > 4. **Forgetting to State Assumptions:** In numerical problems, always state the assumed **kernel-to-cob ratio** (typically 0.75–0.85 for maize) and the **moisture content range**. > 5. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** The KTU board mandates SI units in the boxed answer. `19.63` without `m/s` is incomplete. > 6. **Not Interpreting the Result:** For numerical answers, always add a **one-line interpretation** ("This is too high / acceptable / efficient"). Examiners award "understanding marks" for this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - **Definition:** Sheller = mechanical separation of kernel from a firmly attached non-edible matrix (cob/pod/head). - **Six Universal Subsystems:** Frame, Hopper, Cylinder, Concave, Blower, Power Transmission. - **Main Types:** Rasp-bar drum, Peg-tooth, Centrifugal, Rubber-roller, Disc/Cone. - **Best for Maize (Dry):** Rasp-bar drum @ 8–12 m/s peripheral speed. - **Best for Paddy:** Rubber-roller with 1.25–1.30 speed ratio. - **Best for Wet Maize:** Peg-tooth cylinder (accepting higher damage). - **Key Performance Equations:** - $\eta_s = (W_k / W_t) \times 100$ — Target: **> 95%** - $\eta_c = (W_{ck} / W_k) \times 100$ — Target: **> 95%** - $\eta_d = (W_{bk} / W_k) \times 100$ — Target: **< 5%** - $V = (\pi D N) / 60$ — Optimal for maize: **8–12 m/s** - $E_s = P / Q$ — Typical: **1.5–3.0 kWh/tonne** - **Ideal Maize Moisture for Shelling:** **13–15% (w.b.)** — above 18%, the kernels become rubbery and damage increases. - **Concave Clearance:** Maize: 18–25 mm; Paddy: 0.5–1.0 mm. - **Rasp Bars:** Corrugated steel strips, arranged helically (6–8 per drum). - **Differential Roller Speed (Paddy):** Upper 25–30% faster than lower roller. - **Board Exam Favorite:** "Explain with block diagram the construction and working of a rasp-bar maize sheller" — this is the **single most repeated question** in KTU Module 3. - **Numerical Pattern:** Peripheral speed calculation (2.5 marks) + Efficiency/damage calculation (4.5 marks) = a guaranteed 7-mark part-B sub-question every year.

topics.title: Paddy Separator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Paddy Separator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paddy Separator (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *paddy separator* is a **gravity-based classification device** used in the rice milling industry to separate unhusked paddy grains from brown rice (dehusked paddy) after the dehusking operation. It exploits the differences in **specific gravity (density), size, shape, and surface friction characteristics** between fully husked brown rice and unhusked paddy kernels to achieve physical separation without any chemical or thermal treatment.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PEAGT752 – Unit Operations in Post Harvest Engineering**, the paddy separator is positioned as a **post-dehusking cleaning unit** in the modern rice mill flow line. It receives a **heterogeneous mixture** (paddy + brown rice + sometimes broken fractions) from the rubber roll sheller or under-runner disc sheller and delivers two distinct streams:

- **Stream A — Unhusked Paddy:** Recycled back to the sheller for re-dehusking.
- **Stream B — Brown Rice:** Sent forward to the paddy separator and finally to the whitener/polisher.

The most industrially dominant and **KTU-exam-relevant** variant is the **Tray-Type (Compartment-Type) Paddy Separator**, also known as the **Separator (Tray type)**. Other types include the **D-Separator (Drum Type)** and the **Indented Disc Separator**, but the tray type is the principal focus of the syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 3 Highlight**
> The tray-type paddy separator is examined frequently under the topic *"Separation equipment in rice milling"* with stress on: (i) operating principle, (ii) constructional details, (iii) capacity, and (iv) separation efficiency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it like a "SAND vs. STONE" sorting tray in a kitchen sink.**

Imagine you stir a mixture of heavy stones and light sand in a shallow tray filled with water. If you tilt the tray gently and tap it rhythmically:
- The **heavier stones (paddy)** slide **downward** and settle in the upper compartments.
- The **lighter sand (brown rice)** gets carried by the current of water/air and **floats forward** into the lower compartments.

A paddy separator works on **exactly this logic**, but instead of stones and sand, it separates **denser unhusked paddy** (which still has the tough, fibrous husk) from the **less dense brown rice** (which has already lost its husk, making it lighter and smoother).

The key differences that allow separation are:

| Property | Unhusked Paddy | Brown Rice |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Gravity** | **1.12 – 1.18 g/cm³** (higher) | **1.05 – 1.10 g/cm³** (lower) |
| **Surface Texture** | Rough, fibrous husk | Smooth, polished |
| **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** | **0.45 – 0.55** (high) | **0.25 – 0.35** (low) |
| **Bulk Density** | ~560 kg/m³ | ~620 kg/m³ |
| **Length** | Slightly longer (husk attached) | Shorter (husk removed) |

The device uses these four parameter differences — but primarily **specific gravity and surface friction** — combined with an **inclined, oscillating tray** and a **controlled air stream** to achieve continuous, automatic separation.

---

**The KTU board examiner's one-line expectation for Module 3:**
> *"A paddy separator separates paddy from brown rice using specific gravity and friction difference, ensuring that paddy is recycled for re-dehusking and brown rice proceeds to whitening — thereby improving milling recovery."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tray-type paddy separator works on the combined effect of **THREE simultaneous physical principles**:
> 1. **Specific Gravity Difference (Gravity Stratification)**
> 2. **Surface Friction Difference (Frictional Resistance on Incline)**
> 3. **Oscillatory Motion (Controlled Vibration)**

### Step-by-Step Working Logic:

**Step 1 — Feeding the Heterogeneous Mixture**
- The mixture of paddy + brown rice from the sheller is fed through a **vibratory feeder** onto the inclined **perforated metal tray**.
- The tray is mounted at an inclination angle (typically **α = 12° to 18°** with the horizontal) and is kept in **oscillatory motion** by an eccentric drive or cam mechanism.

**Step 2 — Stratification by Density**
- As the tray oscillates, the mixture experiences **vertical inertial forces**. The denser paddy grains (SG ≈ 1.18) sink to the **bottom layer** in contact with the tray surface, while the lighter brown rice (SG ≈ 1.08) rises to the **top layer**.
- This is essentially a **fluidized bed phenomenon** under mechanical vibration.

**Step 3 — Air Classification Through Perforations**
- A **controlled upward air current** is drawn through the perforations of the tray by a fan.
- This upward air stream:
  - **Fluidizes** the upper layer of brown rice.
  - Carries it **downward along the inclined tray** (toward the discharge end) because brown rice has lower friction and the air reduces its effective weight.
  - Leaves the **denser paddy** in the bottom layer, which does not get fluidized and instead moves **upward along the incline** (counter-current motion) due to its higher coefficient of friction with the tray surface.

**Step 4 — Friction-Driven Counter-Current Transport**
- The high-friction paddy grains, when in contact with the inclined oscillating surface, are **dragged upward** along the slope.
- The low-friction brown rice slides **downward** under gravity and the assistance of the air stream.
- This creates a **counter-current separation** within each tray compartment.

**Step 5 — Compartment-wise Collection**
- The tray is divided into **4 to 6 compartments** by adjustable dividers (louvre plates).
- Each successive compartment receives a mixture enriched in brown rice. By the final compartment, the brown rice fraction is **>99% pure**.
- The paddy fraction is collected at the top end and recycled to the sheller.

**Step 6 — Discharge**
- **Brown rice discharge:** Exits at the lower end of the tray, ready for whitening.
- **Paddy discharge:** Exits at the upper end, conveyed by a screw/elevator back to the sheller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "counter-current" matters:** The opposing motion of paddy (upward) and brown rice (downward) ensures **multiple stages of separation within a single tray**, dramatically improving the purity of both output streams.

## 2.2 Constructional Details of Tray-Type Paddy Separator

**Main Components:**

1. **Perforated Tray (Deck):** Made of **galvanized steel sheet** with perforations of **Ø 1.5 – 2.0 mm** arranged in a triangular pitch.
2. **Inclination Adjustment Mechanism:** A hand-wheel/screw-jack system to vary the tray angle (**α**).
