3. **Eccentric Drive (Vibrator):** Operates at **250 – 400 oscillations per minute** with an amplitude of **15 – 25 mm**.
4. **Centrifugal Fan (Blower):** Provides upward air velocity of **0.5 – 1.2 m/s** through the tray.
5. **Adjustable Dividers (Louvre Plates):** Allow tuning of compartment widths.
6. **Feeding Hopper with Vibratory Feeder:** Ensures uniform, monolayer feed.
7. **Discharge Chutes:** Separate outlets for paddy (recycle) and brown rice (forward).
8. **Frame and Supporting Structure:** Mild steel fabrication with anti-vibration mounts.

## 2.3 Operating Parameters (KTU Standard Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tray Inclination Angle** | α | **12 – 18** | degrees |
| **Oscillation Frequency** | f | **250 – 400** | rpm (oscillations/min) |
| **Stroke Length (Amplitude)** | A | **15 – 25** | mm |
| **Air Velocity Through Tray** | v_air | **0.5 – 1.2** | m/s |
| **Tray Length** | L | **2.0 – 3.5** | m |
| **Tray Width** | W | **0.8 – 1.2** | m |
| **Throughput Capacity** | Q | **1.5 – 4.0** | tonnes/hour |
| **Specific Gravity of Paddy** | ρ_p | **1.12 – 1.18** | g/cm³ |
| **Specific Gravity of Brown Rice** | ρ_b | **1.05 – 1.10** | g/cm³ |
| **Power Requirement** | P | **1.5 – 3.0** | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote the **range of values**, not a single number, as the question typically asks for *"mention the operating parameters of a tray-type paddy separator"* (3-mark short answer).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m / t$ | **Throughput Capacity** = mass of mixture processed per unit time | kg/h or t/h |
| 2 | $\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{m_{b,\,out}}{m_{b,\,in}} \times 100\%$ | **Separation Efficiency** based on brown rice recovery | % |
| 3 | $P_p = \dfrac{m_{p,\,out}}{m_{p,\,out} + m_{b,\,out}} \times 100\%$ | **Purity of Paddy Stream** (% paddy in paddy stream) | % |
| 4 | $B_p = \dfrac{m_{b,\,out}}{m_{b,\,out} + m_{p,\,out}} \times 100\%$ | **Purity of Brown Rice Stream** (% brown rice in brown rice stream) | % |
| 5 | $v_{air} = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{A_{tray}}$ | **Air Velocity** through the tray | m/s |
| 6 | $Q_{air} = v_{air} \times W \times L$ | **Air Flow Rate** required for fluidization | m³/s |
| 7 | $A_{tray} = W \times L$ | **Tray Surface Area** | m² |
| 8 | $\Delta\rho = \rho_{p} - \rho_{b}$ | **Density Difference** driving separation | g/cm³ |
| 9 | $\mu_k = \tan(\alpha_{static})$ | **Coefficient of Kinetic Friction** = tangent of static angle of repose | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{fan} = \Delta p \times Q_{air}$ | **Fan Power** for air supply | W |
| 11 | $R_d = \dfrac{M_b}{M_p} \times 100\%$ | **Dehusking Ratio** (output mass of brown rice to paddy input) | % |
| 12 | $L_{min} = \dfrac{v_{air} \cdot t_{res}}{A}$ | **Minimum Tray Length** for full separation | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Symbol Alert (Markdown Table Safety):** In every formula above, the absolute value / division sign is rendered using `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` only when necessary. In the present table, all expressions are written using standard LaTeX fraction forms ($\dfrac{}{}$) to avoid breaking the markdown table grid.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Rice Milling Industry:** Improves **milling recovery (MR)** by 2–5% by ensuring complete dehusking.
- **Quality Control:** Prevents unhusked grains from reaching the whitener, which would otherwise cause **breakage and dust pollution**.
- **Automation:** Replaces manual sorting, reducing **labour cost** and **human error**.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Operates on **purely mechanical + pneumatic** principles — no heat, no chemicals, low power consumption (~2 kW for 3 t/h capacity).
- **Sustainability:** Closed-loop paddy recycling reduces **grain loss** and supports **zero-waste milling** principles aligned with UN SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Air Velocity Required for Selective Fluidization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A tray-type paddy separator receives brown rice (ρ_b = 1.08 g/cm³) and paddy (ρ_p = 1.18 g/cm³). The tray has perforations of 2 mm diameter. Determine the range of upward air velocity that **fluidizes brown rice but NOT paddy**.

### Given Data:
- Density of brown rice: $\rho_b = 1080 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of paddy: $\rho_p = 1180 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Air density (at 30°C): $\rho_{air} = 1.164 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Equivalent diameter of brown rice: $d_b = 3.5 \text{ mm} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Equivalent diameter of paddy: $d_p = 4.2 \text{ mm} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Dynamic viscosity of air at 30°C: $\mu_{air} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Step 1 — Apply Archimedes' Principle for Fluidization Threshold

A grain just begins to **fluidize** when the **drag force** equals its **effective weight** (weight minus buoyancy in air):

$$F_{drag} = F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} C_d \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^2 \cdot A_{proj} = (\rho_{grain} - \rho_{air}) \cdot V_{grain} \cdot g$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (assume spherical: $C_d \approx 0.44$ in the Stokes–Newton transition)
- $A_{proj}$ = projected area of the grain
- $V_{grain}$ = volume of the grain

For a **spherical equivalent** grain:

$$A_{proj} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}, \quad V_{grain} = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute and Simplify

$$\frac{1}{2} C_d \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = (\rho_{grain} - \rho_{air}) \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot g$$

Cancel $\pi d^2$ from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{2} C_d \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = (\rho_{grain} - \rho_{air}) \cdot \frac{d}{6} \cdot g$$

$$\frac{C_d \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^2}{8} = \frac{(\rho_{grain} - \rho_{air}) \cdot d \cdot g}{6}$$

Solve for $v$ (the fluidization velocity):

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{8 \cdot (\rho_{grain} - \rho_{air}) \cdot d \cdot g}{6 \cdot C_d \cdot \rho_{air}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot (\rho_{grain} - \rho_{air}) \cdot d \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_d \cdot \rho_{air}}}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Fluidization Velocity for Brown Rice

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (1080 - 1.164) \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.164}}$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$4 \times 1078.836 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 4 \times 1078.836 \times 0.034335$$

$$= 4 \times 37.041 = 148.164 \text{ (N/m³ units simplify)}$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$3 \times 0.44 \times 1.164 = 1.540$$

Therefore:

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{148.164}{1.540}} = \sqrt{96.21} \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** This is the **terminal fluidization velocity in free suspension**. In practice, the operating air velocity in a tray separator is **1/3 to 1/2 of this value** because the grains are in a packed bed, not isolated. So practical $v_b \approx 0.6$ to $0.9 \text{ m/s}$.

### Step 4 — Calculate Fluidization Velocity for Paddy

$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (1180 - 1.164) \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.164}}$$

Numerator:

$$4 \times 1178.836 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 4 \times 1178.836 \times 0.041202$$

$$= 4 \times 48.571 = 194.28$$

Denominator: $3 \times 0.44 \times 1.164 = 1.540$ (same as before)

$$v_p = \sqrt{\frac{194.28}{1.540}} = \sqrt{126.16} \approx 11.23 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 5 — Determine Operating Window

For selective fluidization, the operating air velocity $v_{air}$ must satisfy:

$$v_{fluidize,\,brown} < v_{air} < v_{fluidize,\,paddy}$$

$$\boxed{0.6 \text{ m/s} \lesssim v_{air} \lesssim 0.95 \text{ m/s} \text{ (practical operating window)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is the central engineering insight of the paddy separator:** the density difference ($\Delta\rho = 100 \text{ kg/m}^3$) creates a **velocity window of ~0.35 m/s** within which ONLY brown rice fluidizes. The operator tunes fan speed to land in this window.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Separation Efficiency from Mass Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A paddy separator processes 2000 kg/h of mixture from the sheller. The mixture contains 30% paddy and 70% brown rice (by mass). The paddy stream output is 95% pure, and the brown rice stream output is 99% pure. Determine the **separation efficiency** and the **mass flow rates** of each stream.

### Given:
- Total feed: $M_{feed} = 2000 \text{ kg/h}$
- Paddy in feed: $M_{p,\,in} = 0.30 \times 2000 = 600 \text{ kg/h}$
- Brown rice in feed: $M_{b,\,in} = 0.70 \times 2000 = 1400 \text{ kg/h}$
- Paddy stream purity: $P_p = 95\%$
- Brown rice stream purity: $B_p = 99\%$

### Step 1 — Set Up Mass Balance Equations

Let:
- $M_{p,\,out}$ = total mass of paddy stream (kg/h)
- $M_{b,\,out}$ = total mass of brown rice stream (kg/h)

**Equation 1 — Total Mass Balance:**

$$M_{p,\,out} + M_{b,\,out} = 2000 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Equation 2 — Paddy Component Balance:**

$$0.95 \times M_{p,\,out} + 0.01 \times M_{b,\,out} = 600 \text{ kg/h}$$

(The 95% of paddy stream is paddy; 1% of brown rice stream is paddy contamination.)

### Step 2 — Solve the System

From Equation 1: $M_{b,\,out} = 2000 - M_{p,\,out}$

Substitute into Equation 2:

$$0.95 \, M_{p,\,out} + 0.01 \times (2000 - M_{p,\,out}) = 600$$

$$0.95 \, M_{p,\,out} + 20 - 0.01 \, M_{p,\,out} = 600$$

$$0.94 \, M_{p,\,out} = 580$$

$$\boxed{M_{p,\,out} = \frac{580}{0.94} = 617.02 \text{ kg/h}}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{M_{b,\,out} = 2000 - 617.02 = 1382.98 \text{ kg/h}}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Separation Efficiency

$$\eta_{sep} = \frac{\text{Brown rice recovered in brown rice stream}}{\text{Total brown rice in feed}} \times 100\%$$

Brown rice in brown rice stream:

$$M_{b,\,in\,output} = 0.99 \times 1382.98 = 1369.15 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\eta_{sep} = \frac{1369.15}{1400} \times 100\% = 97.80\%$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Paddy Recovery

Paddy recovered in paddy stream:

$$M_{p,\,in\,output} = 0.95 \times 617.02 = 586.17 \text{ kg/h}$$

Paddy recovery:

$$\eta_{p,\,recovery} = \frac{586.17}{600} \times 100\% = 97.70\%$$

### Step 5 — Final Tabulated Result

| Stream | Total Mass (kg/h) | Paddy Content (kg/h) | Brown Rice Content (kg/h) | Purity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed (Input) | 2000.00 | 600.00 | 1400.00 | — |
| Paddy Stream (Output) | 617.02 | 586.17 | 30.85 | 95.00% paddy |
| Brown Rice Stream (Output) | 1382.98 | 13.83 | 1369.15 | 99.00% brown rice |
| **Separation Efficiency** | — | — | — | **97.80%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mark Distribution (14-mark question pattern):**
> - Mass balance setup: **3 marks**
> - Solving simultaneous equations: **4 marks**
> - Efficiency calculation: **3 marks**
> - Final tabulated result + interpretation: **2 marks**
> - Units and significant figures: **2 marks**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design vs. Performance Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase → Effect on Paddy Recovery | Increase → Effect on Brown Rice Purity | Increase → Effect on Capacity | KTU Trade-off Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inclination Angle (α)** ↑ | ↓ Decreases (paddy slides back) | ↑ Increases (better separation) | ↓ Decreases (slower flow) | **Sweet spot: 14° – 16°** |
| **Air Velocity (v_air)** ↑ | ↓ Decreases (paddy also fluidizes) | ↓ Decreases (mixing) | ↑ Increases (faster transport) | **Must stay in 0.6 – 0.9 m/s window** |
| **Oscillation Frequency (f)** ↑ | ↑ Increases (more upward throw) | ↓ Decreases (mixing) | ↑ Increases | **Optimum: 300 – 350 rpm** |
| **Stroke Length (A)** ↑ | ↑ Increases (better stratification) | ↑ Slightly improves | ↑ Increases | **Limit: avoid grain breakage** |
| **Feed Rate (Q_feed)** ↑ | ↓ Decreases (overloading) | ↓ Decreases (incomplete separation) | ↑ Increases | **Limit: monolayer feed essential** |
| **Number of Compartments (N)** ↑ | ↑ Marginal increase | ↑ Increases (multi-stage) | ↓ Slightly decreases | **Typical: 4 – 6 compartments** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the direction of paddy movement. Remember: **paddy moves UP the incline** (high friction), **brown rice moves DOWN the incline** (low friction + air drag). This is **counter-current** separation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheller Output Hopper] --> B[Vibratory Feeder]
    B --> C[Inclined Perforated Tray Deck]
    D[Centrifugal Blower Fan] --> C
    E[Eccentric Drive Motor] --> C
    C --> F[Compartment 1: High Paddy Fraction]
    F --> G[Compartment 2: Mixed Fraction]
    G --> H[Compartment 3: Brown Rice Enriched]
    H --> I[Compartment 4: Final Brown Rice - 99% Pure]
    I --> J[Brown Rice Discharge Chute]
    J --> K[Whitener Polisher Stage]
    F --> L[Paddy Discharge Chute]
    L --> M[Screw Conveyor Elevator]
    M --> N[Recycle to Sheller Rehusking]
    C --> O[Air Recirculation Duct]
    O --> D
    P[Inclination Adjustment Handwheel] --> C
    Q[Stroke Length Adjuster] --> E
```

## 4.2 Detailed Component-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedSystem [Feed Supply System]
        A1[Sheller Outlet] --> A2[Hopper with Gate] --> A3[Vibratory Feeder Pan] --> A4[Feed Distributor]
    end
    subgraph SeparationCore [Separation Core Assembly]
        A4 --> B1[Inlet Section of Tray]
        B1 --> B2[Compartment Divider Plates]
        B2 --> B3[Perforated Tray Surface]
        B3 --> B4[Inclined Frame at 14 degrees]
        B4 --> B5[Outlet Section of Tray]
    end
    subgraph AirSystem [Pneumatic Air System]
        C1[Centrifugal Fan 2 kW] --> C2[Air Plenum Chamber Below Tray]
        C2 --> C3[Perforations in Tray 2 mm holes]
        C3 --> B3
    end
    subgraph DriveSystem [Mechanical Drive System]
        D1[Electric Motor 1.5 kW] --> D2[V Belt Pulley]
        D2 --> D3[Eccentric Crank]
        D3 --> D4[Connecting Rod]
        D4 --> D5[Tray Oscillation Linkage]
        D5 --> B4
    end
    subgraph OutputStreams [Output Discharge Streams]
        B1 --> E1[Paddy Collection Trough Top End]
        B5 --> E2[Brown Rice Collection Trough Bottom End]
        E1 --> E3[Screw Conveyor to Recycle]
        E2 --> E4[Gravity Chute to Whitener]
    end
```

## 4.3 Process Flow Logic — Paddy vs. Brown Rice Trajectories

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Mixture Enters Tray]) --> Stratify{Density Stratification}
    Stratify -->|Denser Paddy rho = 1.18| BottomLayer[Bottom Layer Paddy]
    Stratify -->|Lighter Brown Rice rho = 1.08| TopLayer[Top Layer Brown Rice]
    BottomLayer --> FricCheck{High Friction mu = 0.50}
    TopLayer --> AirCheck{Low Friction mu = 0.30 + Air Drag}
    FricCheck -->|Yes| MoveUp[Drags UP the Incline]
    AirCheck -->|Yes| MoveDown[Slides DOWN the Incline]
    MoveUp --> PaddyOut([Paddy Discharge Top End - Recycle to Sheller])
    MoveDown --> BrownOut([Brown Rice Discharge Bottom End - Forward to Whitener])
    PaddyOut --> LoopBack[Rehusking Loop - 2 to 3 Passes]
    LoopBack --> Start
```

## 4.4 Operational Control Parameter Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs [Operator Adjustable Inputs]
        I1[Inclination Angle alpha]
        I2[Air Velocity v_air]
        I3[Oscillation Frequency f rpm]
        I4[Stroke Amplitude A mm]
        I5[Feed Rate Q tonnes per hour]
    end
    subgraph InternalPhysics [Internal Physical Phenomena]
        I1 --> P1[Frictional Force Component]
        I2 --> P2[Fluidization and Drag Force]
        I3 --> P3[Inertial Throw and Settling]
        I4 --> P4[Vertical Mixing Energy]
        I5 --> P5[Monolayer Bed Depth]
    end
    subgraph Outputs [Performance Outputs]
        P1 --> O1[Paddy Stream Purity percent]
        P2 --> O2[Brown Rice Stream Purity percent]
        P3 --> O3[Separation Efficiency percent]
        P4 --> O4[Throughput tonnes per hour]
        P5 --> O5[Broken Grain Percentage percent]
    end
    O1 --> Final[Optimal Setting Achieved]
    O2 --> Final
    O3 --> Final
    O4 --> Final
    O5 --> Final
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the *Process Flow Logic* diagram, follow the two parallel arrows from `Stratify`. The **left branch** (Paddy) ends at the top-end discharge for recycling, while the **right branch** (Brown Rice) ends at the bottom-end discharge for whitening. This visualizes the **counter-current separation principle** that KTU examiners love to test.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define paddy separator. State any four constructional features of a tray-type paddy separator.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A paddy separator is a post-dehusking grain cleaning device that separates unhusked paddy grains from brown rice using the principles of **specific gravity difference** and **surface friction difference**, combined with controlled **oscillatory motion** and **upward air flow** through an inclined perforated tray.

**Four Constructional Features (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Inclined Perforated Tray:** Galvanized steel sheet with Ø 1.5–2.0 mm holes, inclined at 12°–18° to horizontal.
2. **Eccentric Drive Mechanism:** Operates the tray at 250–400 oscillations/min with 15–25 mm stroke.
3. **Centrifugal Blower:** Provides upward air velocity of 0.5–1.2 m/s through the tray.
4. **Adjustable Compartment Dividers (Louvre Plates):** Allow multi-stage separation with 4–6 compartments.
5. *(Optional)* Inclination adjustment hand-wheel and vibratory feeder for uniform monolayer feed.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six operating parameters of a tray-type paddy separator.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tray inclination angle (α) | 12° – 18° |
| 2 | Oscillation frequency (f) | 250 – 400 rpm |
| 3 | Stroke length (A) | 15 – 25 mm |
| 4 | Air velocity through tray (v_air) | 0.5 – 1.2 m/s |
| 5 | Feed rate / capacity (Q) | 1.5 – 4.0 t/h |
| 6 | Tray dimensions (L × W) | 2.0–3.5 m × 0.8–1.2 m |
| 7 | Power consumption (P) | 1.5 – 3.0 kW |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle and construction of a **tray-type paddy separator**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A paddy separator processes **3 tonnes/hour** of sheller output containing **35% paddy** and **65% brown rice**. If the paddy stream is **96% pure** and the brown rice stream is **98.5% pure**, determine: **(i)** the mass flow rates of both output streams, and **(ii)** the overall separation efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Working Principle & Construction (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
A tray-type paddy separator is the most widely used gravity-based classification device in rice mills. It separates paddy (SG ≈ 1.18) from brown rice (SG ≈ 1.08) after the dehusking operation.

**2. Construction (3 marks):**
- **Frame:** Rigid MS fabricated structure on anti-vibration mounts.
- **Perforated Tray:** Inclined at 12°–18°, made of galvanized steel with Ø 2 mm perforations.
- **Eccentric Drive:** 1.5 kW motor with V-belt and eccentric crank, producing 300 rpm oscillation.
- **Centrifugal Fan:** 2 kW blower supplies upward air at 0.5–1.2 m/s.
- **Compartment Dividers:** 4–6 adjustable louvre plates.
- **Feed & Discharge System:** Vibratory feeder at top, separate chutes for paddy and brown rice.

**3. Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The mixture is fed onto the **inclined, oscillating, perforated tray**.
- **Density stratification:** Denser paddy sinks; lighter brown rice rises.
- **Air classification:** Upward air fluidizes the brown rice layer.
- **Counter-current transport:** High-friction paddy moves **UP** the incline; low-friction brown rice slides **DOWN** with air assistance.
- **Multi-stage separation:** 4–6 compartments progressively purify both streams.
- **Discharge:** Paddy recycled to sheller; brown rice sent to whitener.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Mass Balance & Efficiency (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $M_{feed} = 3000 \text{ kg/h}$
- Paddy in feed = 0.35 × 3000 = **1050 kg/h**
- Brown rice in feed = 0.65 × 3000 = **1950 kg/h**
- Paddy stream purity $P_p$ = 96% → 4% brown rice contamination
- Brown rice stream purity $B_p$ = 98.5% → 1.5% paddy contamination

**Step 1 — Set Up Equations (2 marks):**

Let $M_{p,\,out}$ = paddy stream mass, $M_{b,\,out}$ = brown rice stream mass.

$$M_{p,\,out} + M_{b,\,out} = 3000 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$0.96 \times M_{p,\,out} + 0.015 \times M_{b,\,out} = 1050 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve (3 marks):**

From (i): $M_{b,\,out} = 3000 - M_{p,\,out}$

Substitute:

$$0.96 \, M_{p,\,out} + 0.015(3000 - M_{p,\,out}) = 1050$$

$$0.96 \, M_{p,\,out} + 45 - 0.015 \, M_{p,\,out} = 1050$$

$$0.945 \, M_{p,\,out} = 1005$$

$$M_{p,\,out} = 1063.49 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$M_{b,\,out} = 3000 - 1063.49 = 1936.51 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Separation Efficiency (2 marks):**

Brown rice in brown rice stream:

$$= 0.985 \times 1936.51 = 1907.46 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\eta_{sep} = \frac{1907.46}{1950} \times 100\% = 97.82\%$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$\boxed{M_{p,\,out} = 1063.49 \text{ kg/h}, \quad M_{b,\,out} = 1936.51 \text{ kg/h}, \quad \eta_{sep} = 97.82\%}$$

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Supplementary]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **constructional details and working** of a **tray-type paddy separator**. List **five factors** that affect its separation efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **minimum fluidization air velocity** required to selectively fluidize brown rice in a paddy separator. Use the data: $\rho_b = 1080 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $d_b = 3.5 \text{ mm}$, $C_d = 0.44$, $\rho_{air} = 1.164 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Diagram (1.5 marks):** Draw the labelled schematic — tray, blower, eccentric drive, feeder, discharge chutes.

**Construction (2.5 marks):** Same as Question A — frame, perforated tray, eccentric drive, blower, dividers, feeder, chutes.

**Working (1.5 marks):** Counter-current density-friction separation with air fluidization.

**Five Factors Affecting Separation Efficiency (1.5 marks — 0.3 each):**

1. Inclination angle of the tray
2. Air velocity through the perforations
3. Oscillation frequency and stroke length
4. Feed rate (must be monolayer)
5. Moisture content of the grains (affects SG and friction)
6. *(Bonus)* Purity of input mixture and compartment divider settings

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Fluidization Velocity Derivation (7 marks)

**Starting Equation (Archimedes + Drag balance) (2 marks):**

$$\frac{C_d \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^2}{8} = \frac{(\rho_b - \rho_{air}) \cdot d \cdot g}{6}$$

**Solving for v (2 marks):**

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{4 (\rho_b - \rho_{air}) d \, g}{3 \, C_d \, \rho_{air}}}$$

**Substituting Values (2 marks):**

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times (1080 - 1.164) \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.164}}$$

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1078.836 \times 0.0035 \times 9.81}{1.540}}$$

$$v_b = \sqrt{\frac{148.16}{1.540}} = \sqrt{96.21}$$

$$v_b = 9.81 \text{ m/s (theoretical terminal fluidization velocity)}$$

**Practical Operating Note (1 mark):**

> The **actual operating air velocity** in a tray separator is **1/3 to 1/2** of the theoretical value, i.e., **$v_{air} \approx 0.6$ to $0.9$ m/s**, to account for the packed-bed nature of the grain layer and avoid over-fluidization of paddy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong direction of grain flow:** Writing "paddy moves down" instead of UP the incline → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing paddy and brown rice densities:** Interchanging SG values (1.18 vs 1.08) → entire calculation becomes wrong → **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the factor of 0.33–0.5** in practical velocity calculation: Examiner expects realistic industrial values, not just the theoretical 9.81 m/s → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Not writing units** in final answers: KTU board strictly deducts for missing kg/h, m/s, %, etc. → **−0.5 to 1 mark per question**.
> 5. **Forgetting mass balance closure check:** $M_{p,\,out} + M_{b,\,out}$ must equal $M_{feed}$. Not verifying this is a common oversight.
> 6. **Missing the counter-current principle** in working explanation: This is a "must-mention" keyword for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Paddy Separator**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Paddy separator separates **unhusked paddy (SG 1.18)** from **brown rice (SG 1.08)** after the dehusking stage.
- ☐ The dominant industrial type is the **Tray-Type (Compartment-Type) Paddy Separator**.
- ☐ Three simultaneous principles: **(1) density stratification, (2) friction-driven counter-current transport, (3) air-fluidized bed through perforated tray**.

### Direction of Motion (Memorize!)
- ☐ **Paddy → UP the incline** (high friction μ ≈ 0.50, driven by oscillation + tray slope).
- ☐ **Brown rice → DOWN the incline** (low friction μ ≈ 0.30, assisted by air drag + gravity).
- ☐ This is **counter-current separation**.

### Key Numerical Values to Remember
- ☐ Tray inclination: **12° – 18°** (optimal ~14°).
- ☐ Oscillation frequency: **250 – 400 rpm**.
- ☐ Stroke amplitude: **15 – 25 mm**.
- ☐ Air velocity: **0.5 – 1.2 m/s** (practical operating window).
- ☐ Capacity: **1.5 – 4.0 t/h**.
- ☐ Power: **1.5 – 3.0 kW**.
- ☐ Tray perforations: **Ø 1.5 – 2.0 mm**.
- ☐ Compartments: **4 – 6 louvre plates**.

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ $\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{m_{b,\,out,\,pure}}{m_{b,\,in}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ Mass balance: $M_{p,\,out} + M_{b,\,out} = M_{feed}$
- ☐ Fluidization velocity: $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 (\rho - \rho_{air}) d \, g}{3 \, C_d \, \rho_{air}}}$

### Recycle Loop & Industrial Context
- ☐ Paddy stream is **recycled back to the sheller** for 2–3 rehusking passes.
- ☐ Brown rice stream moves to the **whitener/polisher**.
- ☐ Separation efficiency of a well-tuned industrial unit: **97–99%**.
- ☐ Improvement in milling recovery due to paddy separator: **2–5%**.

### Common KTU Buzzwords (use these in answers!)
- ☐ *"Counter-current separation"*
- ☐ *"Specific gravity difference"*
- ☐ *"Friction-driven stratification"*
- ☐ *"Monolayer feed"*
- ☐ *"Selective fluidization"*
- ☐ *"Paddy recycle loop"*
- ☐ *"Multi-stage compartment separation"*

### Industrial Significance
- ☐ Ensures **no unhusked paddy** reaches the whitener (prevents dust, breakage, and quality loss).
- ☐ Operates on **purely mechanical + pneumatic** principles — **no heat, no chemicals**.
- ☐ Power-efficient: only **~2 kW for 3 t/h** capacity.
- ☐ Critical for **milling recovery (MR)** and **head rice yield (HRY)** in commercial rice mills.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Removal of Bran
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Removal of Bran

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bran Removal (Debranning / Polishing):** It is a unit operation in post-harvest engineering wherein the outer **pericarp**, **aleurone layer**, **seed coat (testa)**, and portions of the **embryo (germ)** of a cereal grain (typically rice, wheat, barley, or oats) are mechanically abraded or frictionally separated from the starchy **endosperm** to produce a refined, visually appealing, and storage-stable milled product.

In the context of rice processing, the term **"polishing"** is used synonymously with bran removal, although technically polishing is a sub-stage that follows the initial debranning (whitening) operation. The bran itself is the collective name given to the multi-layered outer covering of the grain, which constitutes roughly **5–8 %** of the total grain weight in paddy.

## 1.2 The Six Anatomical Layers of the Grain

To understand *what* is being removed, a student must first know *where* the bran is:

| Layer | Common Name | Function in Grain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hull / Husk (Lemma + Palea) | Outer fibrous cover, removed during dehusking |
| 2 | Pericarp | Protective fruit coat, outermost bran layer |
| 3 | Seed Coat (Testa) | Pigmented layer; gives brown/red rice its color |
| 4 | Aleurone Layer | Single-cell protein-rich layer; high in oils & vitamins |
| 5 | Embryo (Germ) | Living tissue; lipid-rich (20–35 % oil) |
| 6 | Endosperm | Starchy food reserve — *this is what we keep* |

> Bran consists of layers **2, 3, 4, and 5** of the above table.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Onion-Peeling" of a Grain

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a boiled egg:** The shell is the **husk**. The thin membrane just beneath the shell is the **bran**. The white is the **endosperm**, and the yellow yolk at the center is the **germ**. Bran removal is like carefully peeling the membrane off the egg white using a soft scouring pad — you want to remove the dark outer film *without* scraping away too much of the white underneath. Rub too gently and you leave a brown, nutritious-but-unappealing egg. Rub too hard and you waste the good white. **The art of polishing is finding the perfect middle ground.**

## 1.4 Why Must Bran Be Removed? (Engineering Justification)

1. **Extended Shelf Life:** Bran is rich in lipids (15–22 % oil in rice bran). These unsaturated oils undergo rapid **lipase-mediated hydrolysis** and **oxidative rancidity**, producing free fatty acids (FFA) and off-flavors. Without bran, milled rice can store for **1–3 years** versus 1–3 months for brown rice.
2. **Consumer Preference:** White, polished rice is the cultural default in most South and East Asian markets.
3. **Improved Cooking Quality:** Bran removal reduces fiber content, allowing faster water absorption, softer texture, and predictable gelatinization.
4. **Milling Yield Accounting:** Knowing the precise bran fraction is essential for **mass balance** in any rice mill — a critical KTU 2014/2018/2024 Scheme calculation.
5. **By-product Valorization:** The removed bran is a high-value **livestock feed** and source of **rice bran oil (RBO)**, an economically important extraction industry.

## 1.5 Standard Quality Metrics (Highlight for KTU Board Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Degree of Milling (DOM) or Degree of Polishing:** Expressed as a **percentage (0–100 %)**.
> - **Bran Removal Efficiency:** Ratio of actual bran extracted to theoretical bran available.
> - **Head Rice Yield (HRY):** Percentage of whole kernels (≥ ¾ grain length) remaining after milling.
> - **Whiteness Index:** Measured by a **Kett Whiteness Meter**; commercial polish targets **35–40** whiteness units for raw rice.
> - **Bran Particle Size:** Typically **80–400 mesh (180–45 μm)** depending on abrasive vs. friction system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — How Bran Is Physically Detached

The bran layer is **not chemically bonded** to the endosperm. It is held in place by:
- A thin protein–lipid matrix in the aleurone–subaleurone interface
- Mechanical interlocking of seed-coat cells with peripheral endosperm cells

> Therefore, bran removal is fundamentally a **mechanical surface erosion** process driven by one or more of the following energy inputs:

| Energy Input Mode | Mechanism | Typical Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| **Abrasion** | Hard surface scrapes bran off | Abrasive-type whitener, emery roll |
| **Friction** | Grain-on-grain rubbing | Friction-type polisher, Engleberg huller |
| **Impact** | Sudden collision dislodges bran | Jet pearler, impact whitener |
| **Combined** | Multiple forces in sequence | Multi-stage modern rice mill |

## 2.2 The Two Principal Equipment Families

### A. Abrasive-Type Rice Whitener
- Uses a rapidly rotating **emery (Al₂O₃ / SiC) roll** of grit size **36–60 mesh**.
- The grain is held stationary in a perforated screen while the abrasive surface grinds away the bran.
- Produces **fine, powdery bran** (high-fat, low-fiber).
- High capacity, used as **primary whitener**.

### B. Friction-Type Rice Polisher
- Uses a **steel roll** with grooved or smooth surface.
- Grain is forced against a **perforated cylindrical screen (cage)** under high pressure.
- Bran is removed by **grain-on-grain friction** under controlled temperature (< 50 °C).
- Produces **larger, flaky bran** — preferred for bran oil extraction.
- Used as **secondary polisher** to give a glossy finish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Abrasive machines give high **degree of polishing** but low **head rice yield** (more breakage). Friction machines give better head rice but lower throughput. Modern multi-stage mills use abrasive → friction → friction (mist polisher) to balance both.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Process Logic (The "Why" Behind Each Stage)

1. **Step 1 — Pre-conditioning (optional):** Paddy is tempered at **12–14 % moisture** to toughen the endosperm and loosen the bran layer. Reduces breakage.
2. **Step 2 — Paddy Dehusking (separate unit operation):** Produces **brown rice**.
3. **Step 3 — Primary Whitening:** ~70 % bran is removed in the first abrasive pass. Mass loss ≈ **4–5 %** of brown rice weight.
4. **Step 4 — Secondary Polishing:** Friction polisher removes remaining ~25 % bran and smooths surface.
5. **Step 5 — Mist Polishing (optional):** Fine mist of water is sprayed to give a **glazed, glossy appearance** — common in South Indian and Basmati rice.
6. **Step 6 — Bran Aspiration:** Aspirators with cyclones separate bran from air stream using terminal velocity difference (bran ≈ **0.5–2.5 m/s**; air ≈ **8–15 m/s**).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam favourites** for KTU 2014, 2018, and 2024 Schemes. Practice numerical problems using these.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree of Polishing (DOP)** | $\text{DOP} = \dfrac{W_{\text{polished}}}{W_{\text{brown}}} \times 100$ | $W_{\text{polished}}$ = mass of polished rice; $W_{\text{brown}}$ = mass of brown rice | **%** |
| 2 | **Bran Removal Efficiency ($\eta_{br}$)** | $\eta_{br} = \dfrac{M_{b}}{M_{i} - M_{f}} \times 100$ | $M_{b}$ = bran recovered; $M_{i}$ = initial brown rice mass; $M_{f}$ = final polished rice mass | **%** |
| 3 | **Total Milling Recovery** | $\text{TMR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{head}} + M_{\text{broken}}}{M_{\text{paddy}}} \times 100$ | Sum of head + broken kernels over paddy input | **%** |
| 4 | **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | $\text{HRY} = \dfrac{M_{\text{head}}}{M_{\text{polished}}} \times 100$ | Mass of kernels ≥ ¾ grain length over total polished | **%** |
| 5 | **Throughput of Whitener** | $Q = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{\text{vol}}}{t_{r}}$ | $V$ = effective roll volume; $\rho$ = bulk density (~ 0.55 g/cm³); $\eta_{\text{vol}}$ = fill ratio; $t_{r}$ = residence time (min) | **kg/h** |
| 6 | **Power Requirement (empirical)** | $P = k \cdot D^{2.5} \cdot L \cdot n^{1.5}$ | $D$ = roll diameter (m); $L$ = roll length (m); $n$ = rpm; $k$ = machine constant (2.5–4.0 for abrasive) | **kW** |
| 7 | **Whiteness Index Correction** | $W_{c} = W_{m} + \alpha(T - 25)$ | $W_{m}$ = measured whiteness; $T$ = sample temperature; $\alpha$ = temp. coefficient | **units** |
| 8 | **Bran Oil Yield** | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{M_{oil}}{M_{bran}} \times 100$ | Mass of extracted oil over mass of bran | **%** |
| 9 | **Mass Balance of Rice Mill** | $M_{\text{paddy}} = M_{\text{husk}} + M_{\text{bran}} + M_{\text{polished}} + M_{\text{loss}}$ | All masses in same time window | **kg** |
| 10 | **Aspiration Air Velocity** | $v_{air} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot g \cdot R \cdot (\rho_{g} - \rho_{a})}{\rho_{a} \cdot C_{d}}}$ | $R$ = bran particle radius; $\rho_{g}$ = grain density; $\rho_{a}$ = air density; $C_{d}$ = drag coefficient | **m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam tables, **never write** $ \vert x \vert $ for absolute value. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Industrial Rice Mills** in Kerala (Kuttanad, Palakkad regions) deploy 2–4 stage polishing lines producing 4–10 tonnes/h of finished rice.
2. **Rice Bran Oil Industry:** Bran must be **stabilized** (heat-treated to inactivate lipase) within 4–6 hours of removal, otherwise FFA content rises above **5 %**, making it unfit for edible-grade oil.
3. **Food Fortification Strategy:** Since aggressive polishing removes thiamine and iron, India’s Food Safety and Standards Act (FSSAI, 2018) mandates **fortified rice kernels (FRK)** addition post-polishing.
4. **By-Product Economics:** Bran sells for **₹18–25/kg** (2024 India) versus polished rice at **₹35–55/kg**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Mass Balance of a Two-Stage Polishing System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most repeated numerical type** in KTU Module 3 university exams (2018, 2019, 2021, 2023 papers all featured a variant). Master the logic below.

### Problem Setup (Standard)

> A rice mill processes **1000 kg/h of brown rice** through a two-stage polishing system. Stage 1 (abrasive whitener) removes **70 %** of the available bran. Stage 2 (friction polisher) removes **80 %** of the *remaining* bran. The brown rice contains **8 % bran by weight**. Determine:
> 1. The mass of polished rice obtained per hour.
> 2. The mass of bran collected from each stage.
> 3. The total Degree of Polishing.

### Step 1: Identify the Known Quantities

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{brown, in}} &= 1000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Bran fraction in brown rice} &= 8\% \\
\therefore M_{\text{bran, total}} &= 1000 \times 0.08 = 80 \text{ kg/h} \\
M_{\text{endosperm, total}} &= 1000 - 80 = 920 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The brown rice is treated as a two-component mixture — bran and endosperm. We assume negligible moisture loss during the short polishing residence time (~30–90 seconds), so total mass is conserved at the mill boundary.

### Step 2: Stage 1 (Abrasive Whitener) — 70 % Bran Removal

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bran removed in Stage 1} &= 70\% \times 80 = 56 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Bran remaining after Stage 1} &= 80 - 56 = 24 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Rice output from Stage 1} &= 1000 - 56 = 944 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Abrasive action preferentially detaches bran since the bond strength at the aleurone–endosperm junction is lowest. We assume no endosperm is lost in this stage (acceptable for a 70 % removal efficiency; for higher values, breakage corrections must be added).

### Step 3: Stage 2 (Friction Polisher) — 80 % of *Remaining* Bran

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bran removed in Stage 2} &= 80\% \times 24 = 19.2 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Bran remaining after Stage 2} &= 24 - 19.2 = 4.8 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Final polished rice} &= 944 - 19.2 = 924.8 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Friction polishing works at lower pressure and gentler abrasion, designed to remove only the loosely adhering residual bran and to give a glossy surface. Hence it operates on the *remaining* bran, not the original 80 kg.

### Step 4: Calculate the Degree of Polishing (DOP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOP} &= \frac{M_{\text{polished}}}{M_{\text{brown}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{924.8}{1000} \times 100 \\
&= 92.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** A DOP of 92.48 % is typical for premium-grade white rice. Higher DOP (> 95 %) is **over-polished** (silky rice) with nutritional loss; lower DOP (< 85 %) is **under-polished** (cream-colored) and has poor shelf life.

### Step 5: Verification by Total Mass Balance

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{polished}} + M_{\text{bran}} &= 924.8 + (56 + 19.2) \\
&= 924.8 + 75.2 \\
&= 1000 \text{ kg/h} \quad \checkmark \text{ (matches input)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The discrepancy of 4.8 kg/h between the available 80 kg of bran and the 75.2 kg removed is the **residual bran** left on the kernels. This residual *is* the polish film that gives white rice its characteristic appearance and minor fiber content.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Polishing Technologies

> The matrix below is a **favourite KTU 14-mark structured-answer question** (table-comparison type). Memorize the relative rankings.

| Parameter | Abrasive Whitener | Friction Polisher | Jet Pearler (Impact) | Vertical Whiteners |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Removal mechanism** | Abrasion | Friction | Impact | Mixed |
| **Bran particle size** | Fine (60–200 μm) | Flaky (200–600 μm) | Medium | Fine–medium |
| **Bran oil retention** | Lower (oxidized) | Higher (intact) | Medium | Medium |
| **Head rice yield** | 60–75 % | 75–85 % | 70–80 % | 75–82 % |
| **Throughput (t/h)** | 2–6 | 2–5 | 3–8 | 1.5–4 |
| **Power consumption** | High | Medium | High | Low–Medium |
| **Surface temperature** | 50–70 °C | 35–50 °C | 40–60 °C | 30–45 °C |
| **Ideal stage** | Primary | Secondary | Primary/Secondary | Final |
| **Best suited grain** | Raw paddy | Basmati, parboiled | Long-grain | Short-grain |

## 3.3 Energy and Power Derivation — Residence Time Method

The theoretical residence time of grain in a horizontal abrasive whitener is given by:

$$
t_{r} = \frac{L}{v_{\text{roll}}}
$$

Where $L$ is the effective roll length in metres and $v_{\text{roll}}$ is the peripheral speed:

$$
v_{\text{roll}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot n}{60} \quad \left[ \text{m/s} \right]
$$

The peripheral speed is the key design variable. For abrasive rollers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{roll}} &= \frac{\pi \times 0.20 \times 900}{60} \\
&= \frac{565.49}{60} \\
&= 9.42 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $L = 0.5$ m:

$$
t_{r} = \frac{0.5}{9.42} = 0.053 \text{ min} = 3.18 \text{ s}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This 3-second residence time is typical; the grain undergoes rapid, intense abrasion rather than prolonged gentle contact. Lengthening $t_r$ via higher $L$ or lower $n$ increases DOM but also increases broken-grain fraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Two-Stage Polishing Line

> Below is the complete process flow from brown rice to finished white rice, including bran aspiration. All node IDs are alphanumeric per the KTU rendering safety protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Brown Rice Hopper"] --> B1["Stage 1 Abrasive Whitener"]
    B1 --> C1["Stage 1 Bran Outlet"]
    B1 --> D1["Paddy Separator Inter Stage"]
    D1 --> B2["Stage 2 Friction Polisher"]
    B2 --> C2["Stage 2 Bran Outlet"]
    B2 --> E1["Mist Polisher Optional"]
    E1 --> F1["Aspiration Column"]
    F1 --> F2["Cyclone Separator"]
    F1 --> F3["Bag Filter"]
    F1 --> G1["Finished Rice Grader"]
    G1 --> G2["Head Rice Bin"]
    G1 --> G3["Broken Rice Bin"]
    G1 --> G4["Brewers Rice Bin"]
    C1 --> H1["Bran Collection Cyclone A"]
    C2 --> H2["Bran Collection Cyclone B"]
    H1 --> H3["Bran Stabilization Tank"]
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> H4["Bran Storage Silo"]
    H4 --> I1["Rice Bran Oil Extraction Plant"]
    H4 --> I2["Animal Feed Pelletizing Unit"]
```

**Explanation of flow logic:**

- The brown rice enters the **abrasive whitener** (B1), where the emery roll removes the bulk bran. The bran is sucked out via an air stream to cyclone H1.
- The partially polished rice is then re-cleaned in an inter-stage **paddy separator** (D1) to remove any remaining husk or immature grains.
- It proceeds to the **friction polisher** (B2) for the final bran removal and surface gloss improvement.
- Optional **mist polisher** (E1) sprays a fine water film for premium-grade basmati rice.
- The aspiration train (F1 → F2 → F3) captures airborne bran dust using a **cyclone + bag filter** combination (Baghose filter captures sub-10 μm particles).
- The **grader** (G1) sorts finished rice by length using indented cylinders.
- Bran is heat-stabilized (H3) within hours of collection to inactivate lipase, then routed to oil extraction or feed production.

## 4.2 Schematic of a Horizontal Abrasive Whitener (Block-Level Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1["Feed Inlet Chute"] --> J2["Screw Feeder"]
    J2 --> J3["Emery Roll Shaft 900 RPM"]
    J3 --> J4["Perforated Screen Cage 1.5 mm"]
    J4 --> J5["Grain Pressure Adjustment Lever"]
    J5 --> J6["Discharge Spout"]
    J4 --> J7["Bran Suction Port"]
    J7 --> J8["Aspirator Fan 2500 RPM"]
    J8 --> J9["Bran Collection Hopper"]
    J3 -.-> J10["V Belt Drive from 7.5 kW Motor"]
    J10 -.-> J11["Motor Pulley"]
```

**Explanation:**

- The **emery roll** (J3) is the workhorse, rotating at high speed inside a perforated screen cage (J4).
- Grain is fed centrally via the **screw feeder** (J2) and held under controlled **back-pressure** (J5).
- Friction between the abrasive surface and the grain abrades the bran, which is immediately drawn out through the perforations into the **suction port** (J7).
- The **V-belt drive** (J10) connects to a 5–10 kW motor depending on capacity.

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Choosing Polishing Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1{"Grain type?"} -->|Raw Paddy| K2{"Required Whiteness?"}
    K1 -->|Parboiled| K3["Use Friction Polisher Low Pressure"]
    K2 -->|Standard 35 to 40| K4["Single Stage Abrasive"]
    K2 -->|Premium Glossy 40 to 45| K5["Abrasive + Friction + Mist"]
    K2 -->|Specialty Brown Rice| K6["Light Abrasive Only DOM 90%"]
    K4 --> K7["Capacity 4 to 6 t/h"]
    K5 --> K7
    K6 --> K8["Capacity 1.5 to 2.5 t/h"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test direct recall and concise definition ability. KTU valuation expects a 3–4 sentence answer with one diagram or formula.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 2021 Similar Pattern]
**Define the term "Degree of Polishing" of rice. State its typical range for commercially milled raw rice.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Polishing (DOP)** is defined as the ratio of the mass of polished rice obtained to the mass of brown rice fed, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{DOP} = \frac{W_{\text{polished}}}{W_{\text{brown}}} \times 100
$$

For commercial raw rice, the typical DOP range is **88–92 %**, with **90 %** being the industry-standard target. Premium basmati rice is polished to **90–92 %**, while parboiled rice is often polished to **85–88 %** due to its harder endosperm. A DOP above 95 % leads to excessive nutrient loss (over-polished) and reduced head rice yield due to kernel breakage.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2022, Similar 2024 Pattern]
**Differentiate between abrasive-type and friction-type rice whiteners in terms of mechanism and bran characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Abrasive-Type Whitener | Friction-Type Polisher |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Hard emery surface abrades the bran layer | Grain-on-grain rubbing under pressure |
| **Roll surface** | Emery (Al₂O₃/SiC) of 36–60 mesh | Steel roll, grooved/smooth |
| **Bran particle size** | Fine powder (60–200 μm) | Flaky/larger (200–600 μm) |
| **Bran oil quality** | Lower (oxidized) | Higher (intact cells) |
| **Position in process** | Primary whitener | Secondary polisher |
| **Head rice yield** | Lower (60–75 %) | Higher (75–85 %) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The **"OR" choice** allows you to attempt **either Question A OR Question B**. Both questions are given below — pick the one you can answer better.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 + June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram, the construction and working of a **horizontal abrasive-type rice whitener**. List the design and operating parameters that govern its performance. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A rice mill processes **2000 kg/h of brown rice** (containing 8 % bran). The mill uses a two-stage polishing system. The first stage removes **75 %** of the available bran, and the second stage removes **85 %** of the remaining bran. Calculate: **(i)** Mass of polished rice per hour, **(ii)** Total bran recovered, **(iii)** Degree of polishing, and **(iv)** Bran removal efficiency. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction:**
- **Main shaft with emery roll** (length 0.4–0.8 m, diameter 150–250 mm, grit size 36–60 mesh).
- **Perforated cylindrical screen cage** (perforation diameter 1.0–1.5 mm, slot shape).
- **Feed inlet and screw feeder** for controlled gravity inflow.
- **Pressure adjustment lever / weight-loaded gate** at the discharge to vary retention time.
- **Bran suction port** connected to a centrifugal aspirator fan.
- **Drive system:** 5–15 kW electric motor via V-belt or direct coupling.

**Working Principle:**
- Brown rice enters via the feed chute and is conveyed by the screw feeder into the working chamber between the emery roll and the screen cage.
- The emery roll rotates at **800–1200 rpm** (peripheral speed ~ 8–12 m/s).
- Abrasive action detaches bran particles, which are immediately pulled out through the screen perforations by suction.
- The grain is held against the screen by centrifugal force and back-pressure, with the **retention time** being 30–90 seconds.
- Polished rice exits through the discharge spout under gravity.

**Governing Parameters:**
- Peripheral speed of emery roll (m/s)
- Grain moisture content (12–14 % optimal)
- Screen perforation size
- Back-pressure (kg/cm²)
- Retention time
- Feed rate (kg/h)
- Bran suction airflow rate (m³/min)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $M_{\text{brown}} = 2000$ kg/h
- Initial bran fraction = 8 % → $M_{\text{bran, total}} = 2000 \times 0.08 = 160$ kg/h
- Stage 1 removal = 75 % of total → $M_{1} = 160 \times 0.75 = 120$ kg/h
- Stage 2 removal = 85 % of remaining

**Step 1: Stage 1 Calculation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{1} &= 120 \text{ kg/h (bran removed)} \\
M_{\text{after Stage 1}} &= 2000 - 120 = 1880 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Bran remaining} &= 160 - 120 = 40 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stage 2 Calculation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{2} &= 40 \times 0.85 = 34 \text{ kg/h (bran removed)} \\
M_{\text{polished}} &= 1880 - 34 = 1846 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Bran remaining} &= 40 - 34 = 6 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final Answers**
- (i) **Polished rice = 1846 kg/h**
- (ii) **Total bran recovered = 120 + 34 = 154 kg/h**
- (iii) **DOP = (1846 / 2000) × 100 = 92.3 %**
- (iv) **Bran removal efficiency = (154 / 160) × 100 = 96.25 %**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse *“75 % of total bran”* with *“75 % of remaining bran”*. Read the question carefully! Also, do not forget to subtract the bran removed in **each** stage from the rice output, not just at the end. Many lose 2–3 marks by missing the intermediate mass update.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative — KTU University Exam – June 2023 + Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the degree of polishing** of rice. How does grain moisture influence the milling performance? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A rice mill produces **5000 kg/day of polished rice** at a DOP of **90 %** from brown rice. The bran is sold at **₹22/kg** to an oil extraction plant. The oil yield from bran is **18 %** by weight, and the oil sells at **₹180/kg**. Calculate: **(i)** Daily bran production, **(ii)** Daily revenue from bran as feed, **(iii)** Daily oil production, and **(iv)** Combined daily revenue from bran-oil route. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Factors Affecting Degree of Polishing (DOP):**

1. **Grain Moisture Content (most critical):**
   - Optimal range: **12–14 %** (wet basis).
   - Below 10 %: bran becomes brittle, endosperm shatters, low HRY.
   - Above 16 %: bran becomes elastic, resists removal, requires more passes, and may gelatinize locally causing "gummy" surface.

2. **Kernel Hardness & Variety:**
   - Long slender varieties (Basmati) require gentle friction polishing.
   - Short bold varieties (Jeerakashala, Matta) tolerate abrasive polishing.

3. **Parboiling History:**
   - Parboiled rice has hardened endosperm; bran is more tightly bound; needs higher pressure and longer residence time.

4. **Type of Polisher & Roll Speed:**
   - Abrasive vs. friction affects the rate and selectivity of bran removal.

5. **Screen Perforation Size & Pattern:**
   - Larger perforations allow more bran evacuation but reduce pressure.

6. **Feed Rate & Retention Time:**
   - Lower feed → longer residence → higher DOP but lower throughput.

7. **Aspiration Air Flow Rate:**
   - Inadequate suction → bran re-deposits on grains → uneven DOP.

**Influence of Grain Moisture — Detailed Discussion:**
Moisture acts as a **plasticizer**. At the right level (12–14 %), the bran layer is firm but separable, and the endosperm below has enough elasticity to absorb impact without breaking. This gives the **maximum DOP at minimum breakage**, the economic sweet spot of any rice mill.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Polished rice production = 5000 kg/day
- DOP = 90 % → $M_{\text{brown}} = \dfrac{5000}{0.90} = 5555.56$ kg/day
- Bran produced = 5555.56 − 5000 = **555.56 kg/day**

**(i) Daily bran production = 555.56 kg/day**

**(ii) Revenue from bran sale as feed:**
$$
R_{\text{bran}} = 555.56 \times 22 = \text{₹ } 12{,}222.22
$$

**(iii) Daily oil production:**
$$
M_{\text{oil}} = 555.56 \times 0.18 = 100 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**(iv) Combined revenue (selling bran + oil separately is not realistic — bran is the input for oil. So revenue from oil is calculated assuming bran is processed, and the de-oiled bran is sold as feed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{deoiled bran}} &= 555.56 - 100 = 455.56 \text{ kg/day} \\
R_{\text{oil}} &= 100 \times 180 = \text{₹ } 18{,}000 \\
R_{\text{deoiled bran}} &= 455.56 \times 18 \text{ (assumed lower price)} = \text{₹ } 8{,}200 \\
R_{\text{total}} &= 18{,}000 + 8{,}200 = \text{₹ } 26{,}200 \text{ per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In revenue problems, students often forget to subtract the **deoiled bran mass** from total bran when calculating oil yield input. Also, the **selling price of deoiled bran is lower** than raw bran (₹18 vs. ₹22 assumed here). Always state your assumption clearly in the answer script — examiners reward clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision card — memorize before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Bran** = Pericarp + Seed coat + Aleurone + Germ (NOT the husk).
- ✅ **Brown rice → White rice** happens in the polishing/debranning stage, not in dehusking.
- ✅ **Degree of Polishing (DOP)** is the most-tested formula; values **88–92 %** for commercial rice.
- ✅ **Abrasive whitener** uses emery roll, produces **fine** bran, primary stage, lower HRY.
- ✅ **Friction polisher** uses steel roll, produces **flaky** bran, secondary stage, higher HRY.
- ✅ **Optimal grain moisture for polishing = 12–14 %** (wet basis).
- ✅ **Bran is rich in oil (15–22 %)** → must be **stabilized within 4–6 h** to prevent lipase-driven rancidity.
- ✅ **Two-stage polishing** (abrasive → friction) is industry standard; **three-stage** (adding mist) is for premium basmati.
- ✅ **Mass balance equation:** $M_{\text{paddy}} = M_{\text{husk}} + M_{\text{bran}} + M_{\text{polished}} + M_{\text{loss}}$.
- ✅ **Power formula** for whitener is empirical: $P = k \cdot D^{2.5} \cdot L \cdot n^{1.5}$.
- ✅ **Whiteness index** for commercial raw rice: **35–40 Kett units**.
- ✅ **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** is what determines mill profitability, **NOT** DOP alone.
- ✅ **Bran aspiration** uses cyclone + bag filter; terminal velocity of bran is **0.5–2.5 m/s**.
- ✅ **Mist polishing** adds ~1 % gloss without significant mass loss — used for export-grade basmati.
- ✅ **Rice bran oil (RBO)** is a value-added product; yields ~ 18 % oil by weight of bran.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme focus:** numerical mass-balance problems and comparative table-based questions on polishing equipment.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — REMOVAL OF BRAN** 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polisher
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polisher

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polisher (Rice/ Grain Polisher)** — A post-milling **unit operation device** used in the rice processing industry to remove the residual bran layer (pericarp, aleurone, and germ fragments) adhering to the surface of brown/de-husked rice kernels after the whitening stage, and to impart a **glossy, lustrous finish** by smoothening the kernel surface through controlled **frictional, abrasive, and/or moisturizing action**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PEAGT752 – Unit Operations in Post Harvest Engineering**, a polisher is classified as a **finishing equipment** that operates downstream of the paddy whitener/husk separator in the rice milling flow line. It combines **mechanical abrasion, friction polishing, and sometimes water misting** to achieve both cleaning and surface enhancement of rice grains.

### Synonyms used in KTU syllabus & industry:
- Rice Polisher / Polishing Machine
- Mist Polisher (when water is added)
- Glaze Polisher (when glucose/talc is applied)
- Vertical Polisher / Horizontal Polisher (based on axis orientation)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shoe-Shine Machine for Rice"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you buy a wooden table that has been sanded but is still rough with sawdust sticking to it. A polisher is the **"rotary cloth-buffing machine"** of the rice industry. Just as a shoe-shine boy rubs a soft brush against leather to make it shine, the polisher rubs soft rubber/brushes against rice grains under controlled pressure — the loose bran dust is wiped off and the surface becomes **smooth and shiny**.

In simpler words:
- The **whitener** is like a *rough sandpaper* — it removes the bran.
- The **polisher** is like a *soft cloth & wax* — it cleans, smoothens, and beautifies.

---

## 1.3 Position of Polisher in the Rice Milling Flow Line

| Stage | Equipment | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Paddy Cleaner | Removes dust, stones, straw |
| 2 | De-stoner | Removes heavier impurities |
| 3 | Paddy Separator | Separates paddy from brown rice |
| 4 | **Whitener / Huller** | Removes husk & bran layer |
| 5 | **➡ POLISHER** | **Removes loose bran, adds gloss** |
| 6 | Grader / Sorter | Size and quality separation |
| 7 | Color Sorter | Removes discolored grains |
| 8 | Weighing & Packing | Final dispatch |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Polishers (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask to "Classify polishers" or "Differentiate between friction and abrasive polishers." Memorize this table.

| Type | Action Medium | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Friction Polisher** | Rubber roll friction | General purpose rice finishing |
| **Abrasive Polisher** | Emery/screen abrasive | Heavy bran removal |
| **Water/Mist Polisher** | Water + friction | Head rice recovery, glossy rice |
| **Glaze Polisher** | Talc/glucose + friction | Premium export-grade rice |
| **Vertical Polisher** | Gravity-fed vertical shaft | Compact installations |
| **Horizontal Polisher** | Horizontal rotor | High capacity plants |
| **Jet Polisher (Air-Polisher)** | High-velocity air stream | Modern, low-breakage polishing |

---

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics (must be memorised):**
> - **Polishing Efficiency** = (Mass of bran removed) / (Mass of brown rice fed) × 100  *(target: 1.5–3 %)*
> - **Head Rice Recovery (HRR)** after polishing: **≥ 65–70 %** (premium quality)
> - **Whiteness Index** measured by **Kett Whiteness Meter** *(unitless, 0–100)*
> - **Throughput Capacity** typically **2–10 tonnes/hour** depending on machine size
> - **Power consumption** range: **7.5 kW – 45 kW** per machine
> - **Moisture content** of grain entering polisher: **14 ± 0.5 % (wet basis)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" Behind Polishing

The polisher achieves its objective through **three simultaneous physical mechanisms**:

### Mechanism 1: **Frictional Abrasion (Dominant)**
- The grain kernels are rubbed against:
  - (a) a **stationary cylindrical screen** (perforated metal sheet)
  - (b) a **rotating emery/rough roll**, OR
  - (c) a **rotating rubber/silicone roll** at high peripheral speed
- The **differential velocity** between grain mass and screen creates **shear stress** at the grain surface.
- This shear stress **scrapes off the loosely adhering bran particles** without damaging the starchy endosperm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rubber rolls for final polishing?** Rubber is *elastic* — it deforms around the grain, giving **uniform contact pressure** without causing **grain breakage**. This is why head-rice recovery remains high.

### Mechanism 2: **Air-Suction Dust Removal**
- A **centrifugal fan / aspirator** sucks the dislodged bran dust and immature grains through the perforated screen.
- Suction keeps the polishing zone clean → prevents bran re-deposition on the grain.
- Negative pressure inside the chamber is typically **–50 to –150 mm of water column**.

### Mechanism 3: **Moisture Conditioning (for Mist Polishers)**
- A controlled **water mist (0.3–1.0 % by weight of rice)** is sprayed into the polishing chamber.
- The water:
  - Liquefies the residual bran layer → easier removal
  - Creates a **thin film of starch gel** on the grain surface → natural gloss
  - Reduces **grain temperature rise** (which would otherwise cause fissures)

---

## 2.2 Major Components of a Polisher

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Feed Hopper** | Receives brown/white rice from whitener |
| 2 | **Feed Roll / Screw Conveyor** | Controls feed rate into chamber |
| 3 | **Main Rotor (Roll)** | Provides the polishing action (rubber/emery) |
| 4 | **Cylindrical Screen / Sieve** | Confines the grain, allows bran dust to escape |
| 5 | **Pressure Control Gate** | Adjusts outlet resistance → controls grain residence time |
| 6 | **Aspirator / Blower** | Suction of bran dust |
| 7 | **Water Mist Nozzle** (for mist type) | Sprays fine water droplets |
| 8 | **Discharge Spout** | Releases polished rice |
| 9 | **Drive Motor & Pulley** | Powers the rotor |
| 10 | **Frame & Body** | Structural support |

---

## 2.3 Design Parameters & Governing Variables

The polishing action is governed by the following independent variables:

1. **Peripheral speed of rotor (V)** — typically **8–15 m/s**
2. **Clearance between rotor and screen (c)** — typically **8–15 mm**
3. **Throughput (Q)** — mass flow rate of rice, kg/s
4. **Residence time (t)** — typically **30–90 seconds**
5. **Aspirator suction pressure (ΔP)** — typically **–100 mm H₂O**
6. **Screen perforation size** — typically **1.0–1.4 mm**
7. **Moisture content (MC)** of feed rice — typically **14 % wb**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every KTU ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Polishing Efficiency (η_p) | $\eta_p = \dfrac{m_{bran}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | % | Target: 2–3 % |
| 2 | Head Rice Recovery (HRR) | $HRR = \dfrac{m_{head}}{m_{paddy}} \times 100$ | % | Premium ≥ 65 % |
| 3 | Throughput Capacity (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D L \, n \, \rho \, \phi}{60}$ | kg/h | D = roller dia, L = roller length, n = rpm, ρ = bulk density, φ = filling factor |
| 4 | Peripheral Speed (V) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D n}{60}$ | m/s | Typical 8–15 m/s |
| 5 | Frictional Force on Grain (F) | $F = \mu \, m_{grain} \, g$ | N | μ = coefficient of friction (rubber-rice ≈ 0.4–0.6) |
| 6 | Power Required (P) | $P = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{1000} = \dfrac{T \cdot 2\pi n}{60{,}000}$ | kW | T in N·m, n in rpm |
| 7 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{P}{Q_{mass}}$ | kWh/tonne | Target < 8 kWh/t |
| 8 | Residence Time (t) | $t = \dfrac{V_{chamber}}{Q_{volumetric}}$ | s | Typical 30–90 s |
| 9 | Whiteness Increase (ΔW) | $\Delta W = W_{out} - W_{in}$ | unitless | Measured on Kett meter |
| 10 | Bran Removal Ratio (BRR) | $BRR = \dfrac{m_{bran}}{m_{brown} - m_{polished}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates polishing completeness |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the markdown table above, all absolute-value or modulus expressions have been replaced with descriptive text (e.g., $V = \pi D n / 60$) to avoid the vertical pipe `|` character breaking the table syntax. For inline math where a modulus is genuinely required, use **$\vert x \vert$** (LaTeX vert) instead of **$\vert x \vert$** (keyboard pipe).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice export industry (IR 64, Basmati)** — Glaze polishers give the **silky sheen** demanded in Middle East & European markets.
- **Parboiled rice plants** — Mist polishers are essential to remove the **gummy bran layer** unique to parboiled rice.
- **Modern compact rice mills** — Vertical polishers save **floor space by ~40 %** compared to horizontal models.
- **Energy efficiency drive** — Jet polishers reduce **power consumption by 30 %** and **broken % by 50 %** vs. conventional abrasive polishers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput Capacity of a Polishing Chamber

### Statement
Derive an expression for the **volumetric and mass throughput** of a horizontal cylindrical polishing chamber.

### Given
- Roller diameter: $D$ (m)
- Effective roller length: $L$ (m)
- Roller speed: $n$ (rpm)
- Bulk density of rice bed: $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Filling factor (fraction of chamber occupied by grain): $\phi$ (dimensionless, typically 0.25–0.40)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Determine the **cross-sectional area of the grain bed** inside the cylindrical screen.

$$
A_{bed} = \phi \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

The factor $\phi$ accounts for the fact that the grain does not fill the entire cross-section; voids and free space exist.

**Step 2:** Determine the **linear velocity of grain along the chamber axis**.

The grain is conveyed axially primarily by the **inclined screw action** of the helical blades OR by gravity. Assume a conveying velocity $v_c$ proportional to peripheral speed.

$$
v_c = k_c \cdot \dfrac{\pi D n}{60}
$$

where $k_c$ is the conveying coefficient (typical value 0.05–0.15 for rice).

**Step 3:** Compute the **volumetric throughput** $Q_v$.

$$
Q_v = A_{bed} \cdot v_c = \phi \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot k_c \cdot \dfrac{\pi D n}{60}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$
Q_v = \dfrac{\phi \, k_c \, \pi^2 \, D^3 \, n}{240}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert volumetric to **mass throughput** $Q_m$ (kg/s).

$$
Q_m = \rho \cdot Q_v = \dfrac{\phi \, k_c \, \pi^2 \, \rho \, D^3 \, n}{240}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to **kg/hour** (multiply by 3600).

$$
\boxed{Q_{kg/h} = \dfrac{\phi \, k_c \, \pi^2 \, \rho \, D^3 \, n \cdot 3600}{240} = 15 \pi^2 \phi \, k_c \, \rho \, D^3 \, n}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)
Given: $D = 0.20$ m, $L = 1.0$ m, $n = 900$ rpm, $\rho = 560$ kg/m³, $\phi = 0.30$, $k_c = 0.10$.

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times \pi^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 560 \times (0.20)^3 \times 900
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times 9.8696 \times 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 560 \times 0.008 \times 900
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times 9.8696 \times 0.03 \times 560 \times 7.2
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times 9.8696 \times 0.03 \times 4032
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} \approx 17{,}909 \text{ kg/h} \approx 17.9 \text{ tonnes/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** Industrial rice polishers typically process **2–10 t/h**, not 17.9 t/h. The discrepancy arises because $k_c$ and $\phi$ are not constant — they depend on rice variety, moisture, and feed gate position. The derivation gives the **upper-bound theoretical capacity**. Examiners accept this with a note that real capacity is **60–70 % of theoretical**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Requirement for the Polishing Rotor

### Statement
Determine the **power (kW)** required to drive the polishing roller.

### Given
- Roller torque: $T$ (N·m)
- Roller speed: $n$ (rpm)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Angular velocity of the roller in **rad/s**.

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60}
$$

**Step 2:** Mechanical power formula.

$$
P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega
$$

$$
P_{mech} = T \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60} = \dfrac{\pi n T}{30} \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to **kW**.

$$
\boxed{P_{kW} = \dfrac{\pi n T}{30{,}000}}
$$

**Step 4:** Now estimate the **torque** from frictional load.

The torque is generated by the **frictional force** of grain bed against the screen and rotor.

$$
T = F_{friction} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = \mu \, m_{bed} \, g \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

where $\mu$ is the friction coefficient and $m_{bed}$ is the effective mass of grain in contact.

For a typical polisher:
- $m_{bed} \approx 5$ kg (instantaneous grain mass in contact zone)
- $\mu = 0.45$ (rubber-rice)
- $D = 0.20$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

$$
T = 0.45 \times 5 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 2.21 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute power at $n = 900$ rpm.

$$
P_{kW} = \dfrac{\pi \times 900 \times 2.21}{30{,}000} = \dfrac{6248.6}{30{,}000} \approx 0.208 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6:** Add **no-load + drive losses (~20 %)**.

$$
\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 0.208 \times 1.20 \approx 0.25 \text{ kW per roller}}
$$

For an industrial polisher with **two rollers and aspirator fan (5 kW)**, total connected load ≈ **8–10 kW** per machine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

### Statement
Find the specific energy consumption of a polisher for a given throughput.

### Given
- Total power: $P_{total} = 9$ kW
- Throughput: $Q = 2$ tonnes/h

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Energy per hour.

$$
E = P_{total} \times 1 \text{ h} = 9 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2:** Energy per tonne.

$$
SEC = \dfrac{E}{Q} = \dfrac{9 \text{ kWh}}{2 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

$$
\boxed{SEC = 4.5 \text{ kWh/tonne}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Benchmark:** Good modern polishers achieve **SEC between 3–6 kWh/tonne**. Values above **10 kWh/tonne** indicate an inefficient or over-loaded machine.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polisher vs. Whitener

| Parameter | Whitener | Polisher |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Removes bran layer | Removes loose bran + adds gloss |
| **Aggressiveness** | High (abrasive) | Low (rubber friction) |
| **Roll material** | Emery / Carborundum | Rubber / Silicone / Leather |
| **Bran removal (%)** | 8–10 % | 1.5–3 % |
| **Pressure on grain** | High | Low |
| **Broken % generated** | 3–8 % | 0.5–2 % |
| **Water addition** | Usually dry | Often misted |
| **Position in flow** | Upstream | Downstream |
| **Output appearance** | Dull, off-white | Glossy, lustrous |
