## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a polisher. List any four types of polishers used in rice processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A polisher is a post-harvest finishing equipment used in rice milling to remove residual bran particles adhering to the surface of de-husked rice kernels after the whitening operation and to impart a glossy, lustrous appearance through controlled frictional, abrasive, and/or moisturizing action.

**Types (½ mark each, any four):**
1. Friction Polisher
2. Abrasive Polisher
3. Water/Mist Polisher
4. Glaze Polisher
5. Vertical Polisher
6. Horizontal Polisher
7. Jet Polisher

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the functions of the perforated screen and the aspirator in a rice polisher.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Perforated Screen (1½ marks):**
- Confines the grain mass within the polishing chamber
- Allows dislodged bran dust to escape outward
- Provides the second friction surface (along with the rotor) for polishing action
- Typical perforation size: **1.0–1.4 mm**

**Aspirator (1½ marks):**
- Creates negative pressure (typically –50 to –150 mm of water column) inside the chamber
- Sucks out loose bran dust, immature grains, and fine particles
- Prevents re-deposition of bran on grain surface
- Maintains cleanliness and improves polishing efficiency

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a horizontal rubber-roll friction polisher** used in rice mills. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **throughput capacity** of a polishing chamber and compute the capacity in **kg/h** for: $D = 0.25$ m, $n = 850$ rpm, $\rho = 580$ kg/m³, $\phi = 0.30$, $k_c = 0.12$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Construction and Working of Horizontal Rubber-Roll Friction Polisher (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**

The major parts are:

1. **Feed Hopper & Feed Roll** — receives brown rice from the upstream whitener; the feed roll (variable speed) regulates the inflow rate.

2. **Polishing Chamber** — a horizontal cylindrical casing housing the rotor and screen.

3. **Rubber Roll (Main Rotor)** — a shaft fitted with **multiple rubber/silicone discs or a continuous rubber sleeve**; rotates at high speed (typical 800–1000 rpm).

4. **Perforated Cylindrical Screen** — a metal sheet with **1.0–1.4 mm perforations**, surrounding the rotor at a clearance of **8–15 mm**; provides the second friction surface.

5. **Water Mist System** — a nozzle injecting **0.3–1.0 % water by weight** as fine droplets.

6. **Pressure Control Gate** — adjustable gate at the discharge end; controls **residence time** of rice inside the chamber.

7. **Aspirator / Centrifugal Blower** — maintains suction of –100 mm H₂O inside the chamber.

8. **Discharge Spout & Drive Motor** (7.5–45 kW).

**Working (3 marks):**

- Brown rice enters the feed hopper and is conveyed by the feed roll into the polishing chamber.
- The high-speed rotating rubber roll **drags the grain** against the perforated screen, creating **intense frictional shear** at the grain surface.
- The shear **scrapes off the loose bran layer** without damaging the endosperm (rubber is elastic).
- The **aspirator sucks out the dislodged bran dust** through the screen perforations.
- **Water mist** (if used) forms a thin starch-gel film, enhancing gloss.
- The **pressure gate** holds the grain for **30–90 s** until adequate polish is achieved.
- Polished rice is discharged through the spout to the next stage (grader).

---

#### (b) Derivation of Throughput Capacity (7 marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The volumetric throughput is the product of grain-bed cross-section and axial conveying velocity.

**Step 1:** Grain bed cross-section.

$$
A_{bed} = \phi \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** Axial conveying velocity.

$$
v_c = k_c \cdot \dfrac{\pi D n}{60}
$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric throughput.

$$
Q_v = A_{bed} \cdot v_c = \phi \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot k_c \cdot \dfrac{\pi D n}{60} = \dfrac{\phi \, k_c \, \pi^2 \, D^3 \, n}{240} \quad \text{(m³/s)}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass throughput (kg/s).

$$
Q_m = \rho \cdot Q_v = \dfrac{\phi \, k_c \, \pi^2 \, \rho \, D^3 \, n}{240}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kg/h.

$$
Q_{kg/h} = Q_m \times 3600 = 15 \, \pi^2 \, \phi \, k_c \, \rho \, D^3 \, n
$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given: $D = 0.25$ m, $n = 850$ rpm, $\rho = 580$ kg/m³, $\phi = 0.30$, $k_c = 0.12$.

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times \pi^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.12 \times 580 \times (0.25)^3 \times 850
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times 9.8696 \times 0.30 \times 0.12 \times 580 \times 0.015625 \times 850
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} = 15 \times 9.8696 \times 0.036 \times 580 \times 13.281
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} \approx 15 \times 9.8696 \times 0.036 \times 7703.0
$$

$$
Q_{kg/h} \approx 41{,}089 \text{ kg/h} \approx 41.1 \text{ t/h (theoretical)}
$$

**Practical capacity ≈ 60–70 % of theoretical:**

$$
Q_{practical} \approx 0.65 \times 41.1 \approx 26.7 \text{ t/h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(b):**
> 1. **Do NOT forget to multiply by 3600** when converting kg/s → kg/h. This is the most common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Do NOT forget units of $D$** — keep it in **metres**, not cm. Using cm will inflate the answer by a factor of $10^6$.
> 3. Always quote **practical capacity** (60–70 % of theoretical) — full marks require the real-world correction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of rice polishers** with diagrams and state the **function of water mist** in a mist polisher. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rice polisher consumes **12 kW** of power and processes **3.2 tonnes/h** of brown rice. The polisher removes **2.5 %** of the input mass as bran. Determine: (i) the **specific energy consumption (SEC)** in kWh/tonne, and (ii) the **polishing efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Classification of Rice Polishers + Function of Water Mist (7 marks)

**Classification (5 marks):**

Rice polishers are classified on the basis of **action mechanism** and **axis orientation**.

**A. Based on Action Mechanism:**

1. **Friction Polisher** — uses a rubber or leather roll rotating against a perforated screen. Used for general-purpose finishing. Least breakage.

2. **Abrasive Polisher** — uses an emery-coated roll (similar to whitener) but with finer grit. Used when significant residual bran remains after whitening.

3. **Water/Mist Polisher** — combines friction with a fine water spray. Used for parboiled rice and for glossy finish.

4. **Glaze Polisher** — applies a thin film of **talc, glucose, or edible oil** along with friction. Used for premium export-grade rice.

5. **Jet Polisher (Air-Polisher)** — uses high-velocity air jets to remove bran without any roll friction. Lowest breakage, highest energy efficiency.

**B. Based on Axis Orientation:**

6. **Horizontal Polisher** — rotor axis is horizontal; high capacity.
7. **Vertical Polisher** — rotor axis is vertical; compact installation.

**Function of Water Mist (2 marks):**

The water mist (0.3–1.0 % of rice mass) performs three critical functions:

1. **Lubrication of bran layer** — wets the residual bran, making it soft and easy to detach.
2. **Surface gloss formation** — the water dissolves a thin layer of surface starch, which dries as a **glossy gel film** on the kernel.
3. **Temperature control** — evaporation of water absorbs heat, keeping the grain temperature **below 45 °C** and preventing thermal cracks.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Power $P = 12$ kW
- Throughput $Q = 3.2$ t/h
- Bran removal = 2.5 %

**Step 1 (2 marks): Specific Energy Consumption (SEC).**

Energy consumed per hour:

$$
E = P \times 1 = 12 \text{ kWh}
$$

Energy per tonne:

$$
SEC = \dfrac{E}{Q} = \dfrac{12}{3.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{SEC = 3.75 \text{ kWh/tonne}}
$$

This is **excellent** efficiency (benchmark < 6 kWh/tonne).

**Step 2 (2 marks): Polishing Efficiency.**

$$
\eta_p = \dfrac{m_{bran}}{m_{feed}} \times 100
$$

Feed per hour = 3.2 t; bran removed = 2.5 % of 3.2 t:

$$
m_{bran} = 0.025 \times 3.2 = 0.08 \text{ t/h}
$$

$$
\eta_p = \dfrac{0.08}{3.2} \times 100 = 2.5 \%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_p = 2.5 \%}
$$

This is within the typical industry range (1.5–3 %).

**Step 3 (3 marks): Discussion & Head Rice Recovery Estimation.**

- Polishing efficiency of 2.5 % indicates proper surface cleaning.
- SEC of 3.75 kWh/t suggests a **well-tuned modern polisher**.
- Expected head rice recovery after polishing: **~65–70 %** of paddy input.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(b):**
> 1. **Common Error:** Students often confuse **polishing efficiency with whiteness**. Polishing efficiency is the **mass % of bran removed**, NOT the whiteness index.
> 2. **SEC must be in kWh/tonne**, not kJ/kg. Conversion: 1 kWh/t = 3.6 kJ/kg.
> 3. **Always show the unit conversion** step (kW × h = kWh). Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Polisher**

- ☐ **Polisher = finishing unit** positioned **after the whitener** in the rice mill flow line.
- ☐ **Primary function:** Remove **residual bran** + impart **gloss** (NOT major bran removal).
- ☐ **Typical bran removal:** **1.5–3 %** of feed mass.
- ☐ **Peripheral speed of rotor:** **8–15 m/s**.
- ☐ **Clearance rotor–screen:** **8–15 mm**.
- ☐ **Aspirator suction:** **–50 to –150 mm H₂O**.
- ☐ **Water mist:** **0.3–1.0 %** of rice mass — provides lubrication, gloss, and cooling.
- ☐ **Residence time:** **30–90 seconds** inside chamber.
- ☐ **SEC benchmark:** **3–6 kWh/tonne** (good); > 10 kWh/tonne (poor).
- ☐ **Polishing efficiency formula:** $\eta_p = (m_{bran} / m_{feed}) \times 100$.
- ☐ **Throughput formula:** $Q_{kg/h} = 15 \pi^2 \phi k_c \rho D^3 n$ (theoretical).
- ☐ **Practical capacity ≈ 60–70 %** of theoretical.
- ☐ **Power formula:** $P_{kW} = \pi n T / 30{,}000$.
- ☐ **Mist polisher** is best for **parboiled rice**.
- ☐ **Glaze polisher** uses **talc/glucose** for export-grade sheen.
- ☐ **Jet polisher** = lowest breakage + highest energy efficiency.
- ☐ **Vertical polisher** saves floor space; **Horizontal** suits high-capacity plants.
- ☐ **Typical industrial power rating:** **7.5 – 45 kW** per machine.
- ☐ **Feed moisture:** **14 ± 0.5 % wb** is optimal.
- ☐ **Whiteness** measured by **Kett Whiteness Meter** (0–100 scale).
- ☐ **Head Rice Recovery** after good polishing: **≥ 65 %**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glazing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Glazing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Glazing** is a post-harvest preservation unit operation in which a thin, uniform, transparent protective coating (most commonly an ice film) is deliberately applied onto the surface of a pre-frozen food product by brief immersion in, or spraying with, chilled water (0–4 °C) or another suitable glazing medium. The water contacting the sub-zero product surface freezes instantaneously, forming a continuous ice envelope that physically isolates the product from atmospheric oxygen, suppresses moisture sublimation, and retards oxidative and microbial deterioration during frozen storage and handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Key Syllabus Highlight**
> Glazing is classified as a *secondary preservation unit operation* that follows primary operations like freezing. It does **not** destroy microorganisms; it is a **barrier protection technique** used almost exclusively for **frozen fishery products, poultry, and certain fruits**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take an ice cream bar out of the freezer on a hot day — it starts forming a wet, sticky layer on the surface and loses its smooth texture. To prevent this, ice cream manufacturers dip the bar into a thin chocolate shell. The chocolate "locks in" the ice cream from air and warmth.

**Glazing works exactly on this principle**, except the "chocolate shell" is a layer of **pure, clear ice** frozen directly onto the product surface. When a frozen fish is dipped into cold water, the water that touches the fish freezes instantly because the fish is colder than 0 °C. This thin ice layer:

- Acts like a **transparent, edible wrapper**
- Prevents **air (oxygen)** from reaching the fat/oil in fish
- Prevents **water inside the fish from evaporating** into the cold, dry freezer air

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fish specifically?**
> Fatty fish (mackerel, sardine, salmon) contain high amounts of **unsaturated lipids** that oxidize rapidly when exposed to oxygen. They also lose water easily, causing "freezer burn" — a white, dried, leathery patch on the surface. Glazing is the standard industrial solution in the marine export industry (Kerala contributes ~30% of India's marine exports, making this highly relevant).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Glazing medium temperature:** 0 °C to **4 °C** (chilled water)
- **Product (pre-frozen) temperature:** typically **-18 °C to -25 °C**
- **Target glaze thickness:** **0.5 mm to 2 mm** of ice film
- **Glazing efficiency range:** **5 % to 20 %** (industrial benchmark for whole fish)
- **Standard dipping time:** **10 s to 60 s** depending on product size
- **Recommended storage temperature after glazing:** **-18 °C or lower** (as per Codex / BIS standards)
- **Glaze protection ratio (GPR):** dimensionless ratio used to quantify barrier performance

## 1.4 Terminology Standardization (KTU Board Pattern)

| Term | KTU-Approved Definition |
|---|---|
| **Glaze** | The thin, continuous ice film formed on the product surface |
| **Glazing Efficiency (GE)** | Mass of glaze retained per unit mass of glazed product (expressed as %) |
| **Freezer Burn** | Surface desiccation caused by ice sublimation from an unprotected product |
| **Re-glazing** | Application of a fresh glaze layer after partial loss during storage |
| **Drip Loss** | Liquid lost from product upon thawing (reduced by effective glazing) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The process of glazing is governed by the **principle of transient heat transfer** between the warm glazing medium and the cold product surface. The following structured sequence describes the operational concept:

1. **Pre-freezing of the product** to an internal temperature of **-18 °C** or below, so the surface is well below the freezing point of water.
2. **Contact of chilled water (≈ 1 °C)** with the cold product surface causes an *instantaneous* extraction of latent heat of fusion from the water.
3. The water in immediate contact undergoes a **phase change (liquid → solid)** and adheres as a thin, transparent ice film.
4. Because the ice film is at 0 °C and the product interior is at -18 °C, heat continues to flow from the glaze into the product, *thickening* the ice layer with time.
5. The product is **withdrawn, drained, and immediately transferred** back to frozen storage.
6. During subsequent frozen storage, the ice film **sublimates slowly**, providing sustained protection. Periodic **re-glazing** may be applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Process**
> Two physical laws drive glazing:
> - **Latent heat transfer:** $Q = m \cdot L_f$, where $L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$ for water. The product must be cold enough to absorb this energy without warming up significantly.
> - **Fick's law of diffusion:** The ice glaze increases the diffusion path length for oxygen and water vapour, slowing both ingress of O₂ and egress of H₂O.

## 2.2 Classification of Glazing Methods

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Dip Glazing** | Product is fully immersed in chilled water for 10–60 s | Whole fish, shrimp blocks |
| **Spray Glazing** | Chilled water is sprayed onto the product on a moving conveyor | Fillets, individually quick-frozen (IQF) items |
| **Vacuum Glazing** | Product is placed under vacuum after a water dip; vacuum pulls glaze into surface pores | High-value seafood with porous surfaces |
| **Ice Slurry Glazing** | Product is dipped in an ice/water slurry (-1 °C to -2 °C) | Shell-on shrimp, small fish |
| **Sugar / Salt Glaze** | Concentrated sugar or salt solution used as medium | Fruits (sugar glaze), certain meats (curing adjunct) |

## 2.3 Engineering Factors Affecting Glazing Quality

- **Initial product temperature** (colder → thicker glaze, but risk of cracking)
- **Glazing water temperature** (warmer → thinner glaze, less efficient)
- **Dipping time** (longer → thicker glaze up to a saturation limit)
- **Product surface roughness** (rougher → better mechanical bonding)
- **Water-to-product ratio** (low ratio → uneven glazing)
- **Agitation / circulation** of glazing medium (better uniformity)
- **Water hardness and mineral content** (affects ice clarity and adhesion)
- **Re-glazing interval** during long storage

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE numericals. The **3 most frequently tested** are starred (★).

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ★1 | $GE(\%) = \dfrac{W_g}{W_{glazed}} \times 100$ | Glazing efficiency | $W_g = W_2 - W_1$; $W_1$ = pre-glaze mass, $W_2$ = post-glaze mass |
| ★2 | $GE(\%) = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100$ | Alternate GE expression (relative to glazed product) | Both forms accepted; verify wording in problem |
| ★3 | $GPR = \dfrac{m_{glaze}}{m_{product, dry\,basis}}$ | Glaze Protection Ratio (advanced) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $M_{loss}(\%) = \dfrac{M_0 - M_t}{M_0} \times 100$ | Moisture loss during storage | $M_0$ = initial mass, $M_t$ = mass at time t |
| 5 | $Q = m_{water} \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat extracted from water | $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg |
| 6 | $\Delta h_{glaze} = \dfrac{k_{ice} \cdot (T_s - T_p)}{\rho_{ice} \cdot L_f}$ | Theoretical glaze thickness (Stefan approximation) | $k_{ice} \approx 2.22$ W/m·K |
| 7 | $t_{dip} = \dfrac{\rho_{ice} \cdot L_f \cdot \delta}{h_c \cdot (T_w - T_{surf})}$ | Required dip time for desired glaze thickness $\delta$ | $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient |
| 8 | $R_{protection} = 1 - \dfrac{M_{loss, glazed}}{M_{loss, unglazed}}$ | Relative protection efficiency of glaze | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, **never use** the bare vertical bar `|` inside cells. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value or set-notation symbols to keep the table valid.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine export industry (Kerala focus):** Mandatory in EU, Japan, USA export protocols for frozen shrimp and finfish — glazing prevents "phenolic off-flavour" and *freezer burn*.
- **Cold-chain logistics:** Acts as a **thermal buffer** during minor temperature fluctuations (e.g., loading/unloading at ports).
- **Economic weight addition:** Glaze adds 5–20 % mass that is paid at the same rate as product, so the process is regulated by Codex Alimentarius (max. 20 % in many jurisdictions).
- **Quality retention:** Extends *acceptable sensory shelf-life* by 3–6 months compared to unglazed frozen product.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Glazing Efficiency

The **glazing efficiency** is the most important numerical concept tested by KTU. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**

Let:
- $W_1$ = mass of the **frozen product before** glazing (in grams or kg)
- $W_2$ = mass of the **product after** glazing (glaze + product)
- $W_g$ = mass of the **glaze film** (only the ice coating)

**Step 2 — Mass balance on the product.**

The glaze mass is the *additional* mass picked up by the product during immersion:

$$
W_g = W_2 - W_1
$$

**Step 3 — Define glazing efficiency.**

By KTU board convention, $GE$ is the fraction of the *glazed* (final) mass that consists of glaze. This is the percentage a quality control officer would measure by **re-melting and weighing** the glaze water:

$$
GE(\%) = \frac{W_g}{W_2} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the mass balance.**

$$
GE(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100
$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula**. Mark loss occurs when students forget the multiplication by 100 and report the answer as a decimal.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (model answer pattern).**

> **Problem:** A frozen block of shrimp weighing 1.000 kg is dipped in chilled water (1 °C) for 30 s. The block is then withdrawn, drained for 10 s, and re-weighed as 1.150 kg. Calculate (a) the glaze mass, and (b) the glazing efficiency.

**Solution:**

(a) Glaze mass:

$$
W_g = W_2 - W_1 = 1.150 \text{ kg} - 1.000 \text{ kg} = 0.150 \text{ kg} \; (150 \text{ g})
$$

(b) Glazing efficiency:

$$
GE(\%) = \frac{0.150}{1.150} \times 100 = 13.04 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** A $GE = 13.04\%$ is within the industrial acceptance range of 10–20 % for shrimp.

## 3.2 Derivation of Glaze Protection Ratio (GPR)

The **GPR** quantifies how much of the original product mass is "shielded" by the glaze, and is useful when the product is intended for long storage.

**Step 1.** Define $W_p$ = mass of the **product on a dry basis** (i.e., excluding free water). On a practical basis we often approximate $W_p \approx W_1$ if the pre-glaze product is already a frozen, drained block.

**Step 2.** GPR is defined as the ratio of glaze mass to product mass:

$$
GPR = \frac{W_g}{W_p} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1}
$$

**Step 3.** Convert to percentage (alternate convention):

$$
GPR(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Distinction from GE (commonly confused!).**

| Concept | Denominator | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Glazing Efficiency (GE)** | $W_2$ (post-glaze mass) | 5 – 20 % |
| **Glaze Protection Ratio (GPR)** | $W_1$ (pre-glaze mass) | 5 – 25 % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** In the same problem, students confuse $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the denominator. Always read the question: "with respect to glazed product" → use $W_2$; "with respect to original product" → use $W_1$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Formula

The following decision table helps identify the correct equation for a given KTU-style problem:

| Given Quantity in Question | Unknown Asked | Formula to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $W_1, W_2$ | $GE(\%)$ | $\frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100$ |
| $W_1, W_2$ | $GPR(\%)$ | $\frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1} \times 100$ |
| $W_g, W_2$ | $GE(\%)$ | $\frac{W_g}{W_2} \times 100$ |
| $W_1, GE$ | $W_2$ | $W_2 = \dfrac{W_1}{1 - GE/100}$ |
| $M_0, M_t, t$ | Moisture loss rate | $\dfrac{M_0 - M_t}{M_0 \cdot t}$ |
| $T_p, T_w, t_{dip}, h_c$ | Theoretical glaze thickness | Stefan eqn (formula 7 above) |

## 3.4 Derivation of Relative Protection Efficiency

To quantify how well the glaze reduces moisture loss, define:

$$
R_{protection} = 1 - \frac{M_{loss,\,glazed}}{M_{loss,\,unglazed}}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

- $M_{loss,\,glazed}$ = moisture lost from a glazed sample over storage time $t$
- $M_{loss,\,unglazed}$ = moisture lost from an unglazed control over the same time $t$
- $R_{protection} = 1$ means the glaze provides **100 % protection**
- $R_{protection} = 0$ means the glaze provides **no protection**

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $R_{protection}$ ranges from **0.4 to 0.85** for well-glazed fish stored at -18 °C for 6 months.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow of Industrial Glazing Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Reception] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Washing and Pre-treatment]
    C --> D[Pre-freezing to -18 C]
    D --> E[Storage in Freezer Hold]
    E --> F[Dipping in Chilled Water 0 to 4 C]
    F --> G[Drain and Shake Off Excess Water]
    G --> H[Weighing for QC Check]
    H --> I{Glaze Quality Acceptable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Packaging in Polyethylene Pouch]
    I -- No --> K[Re-dip or Reject]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Carton Packing]
    L --> M[Storage at -18 C or Lower]
    M --> N[Distribution and Export]
```

**Interpretation of Flow:**

- Node **D** is the *pre-freezing* step (mandatory).
- Node **F** is the *glazing step* — heart of the operation.
- Node **H** is the *quality control gate* where $GE$ is measured.
- Node **K** shows the iterative *re-dip* loop if glaze is inadequate.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Glazing Variables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Process Inputs]
        P1[Product Temperature]
        P2[Water Temperature]
        P3[Dip Time]
        P4[Water to Product Ratio]
    end
    subgraph SYSTEM[Glazing Tank System]
        S1[Heat Transfer Zone]
        S2[Phase Change of Water]
        S3[Ice Film Formation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Quality Outputs]
        O1[Glaze Thickness]
        O2[Glazing Efficiency]
        O3[Glaze Adhesion Strength]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S1
    P3 --> S2
    P4 --> S2
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> O1
    S3 --> O2
    S3 --> O3
```

**Reading Guide:** Inputs (left) feed into the heat-transfer-dominated *glazing tank system* (centre), which yields three measurable quality parameters (right). This block-level map is preferred by KTU evaluators over free-text descriptions because it forces students to identify cause-and-effect relationships.

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Glazing Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Glazing Method] --> B{Product is Whole Fish?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Porous Skin?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Vacuum Glazing]
    C -- No --> E[Dip Glazing 0 to 4 C]
    B -- No --> F{Product is Shrimp or Small Piece?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Ice Slurry Glazing]
    F -- No --> H{Product is Fillet or IQF Item?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Spray Glazing on Conveyor]
    H -- No --> J[Fruit Item?]
    J -- Yes --> K[Sugar Glaze Dip]
    J -- No --> L[Re-evaluate Product Category]
```

**Use:** This decision tree is the type of diagram students are expected to draw (or describe) in 14-mark answers. Each branch corresponds to a real industrial practice, and a well-labelled tree can fetch full marks even without a sketch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Define glazing. List any two objectives of glazing in post-harvest preservation of fish.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Glazing** is the unit operation in which a thin, uniform, transparent protective coating — most commonly an **ice film** — is applied to the surface of a pre-frozen food product (typically fish or seafood) by brief immersion in, or spraying with, chilled water at 0 – 4 °C.
>
> **Two primary objectives:**
> 1. **Prevention of oxidative rancidity** — the ice film acts as a barrier to atmospheric oxygen, protecting unsaturated lipids in fatty fish from oxidation. **(1½ marks)**
> 2. **Prevention of moisture loss / freezer burn** — the glaze reduces ice sublimation from the product surface, preserving texture, weight, and appearance. **(1½ marks)**

---

### **Question 2** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**What is glazing efficiency? Write its mathematical expression and state any two factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Glazing efficiency (GE)** is the percentage of the glazed (final) product mass that is contributed by the ice glaze itself, and is used to quantify the effectiveness of the glazing operation.
>
> **Expression:**
> $$GE(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100$$
> where $W_1$ = mass of product before glazing and $W_2$ = mass after glazing. **(2 marks)**
>
> **Two factors affecting GE:**
> 1. **Initial product temperature** — colder products produce thicker, more efficient glazes.
> 2. **Dipping time and water temperature** — longer dip times and lower water temperatures increase GE up to a saturation limit. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of two Part-B alternatives per module. Both alternatives are provided below for full practice coverage.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, mechanism, and procedure** of glazing as applied to frozen fish. Support your answer with a labelled process flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **types of glazing methods** (any four) and list the **advantages and limitations** of glazing in the post-harvest industry. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**1. Principle:** Glazing is based on **latent-heat-driven phase change**. When a pre-frozen product (surface temperature well below 0 °C) contacts chilled water (≈ 1 °C), the water loses its sensible and latent heat to the product and solidifies as a thin ice film on the surface. **(1 mark)**

**2. Mechanism:** Heat transfer occurs in three sequential stages — *(i)* sensible cooling of water to 0 °C, *(ii)* removal of latent heat of fusion $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg, and *(iii)* conduction of the released heat through the forming glaze to the cold product interior. The mass of glaze $W_g$ deposited is given by $W_g = W_2 - W_1$. **(2 marks)**

**3. Procedure (stepwise):** **(3 marks)**
- **Step 1:** Pre-freeze the cleaned fish to an internal temperature of -18 °C or lower.
- **Step 2:** Prepare a glaze tank with chilled water at 0 – 4 °C (sometimes with additives like antioxidants).
- **Step 3:** Dip the frozen fish into the tank for 10 – 60 s, depending on size.
- **Step 4:** Withdraw, drain excess water (10 – 15 s), and immediately transfer to -18 °C storage.
- **Step 5:** Weigh a sample to verify glazing efficiency (target 10 – 20 %).
- **Step 6:** Pack in polyethylene pouches to prevent mechanical damage to the glaze.

**4. Diagram:** A clean labelled block flow (Pre-freeze → Glaze tank → Drain → QC weigh → Pack → Store) earns full marks. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Four Types of Glazing:** **(4 marks — 1 each)**

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Dip Glazing** | Whole fish immersed in chilled water; most common in shrimp and small pelagic fish. |
| **Spray Glazing** | Chilled water sprayed onto fillets / IQF products on a conveyor. |
| **Vacuum Glazing** | Product dipped in water, then held under vacuum so glaze penetrates pores. |
| **Ice Slurry Glazing** | Product dipped in a -1 to -2 °C slurry of ice and water for uniform thin coating. |

**Advantages:** **(1½ marks)**
- Prevents oxidative rancidity in fatty fish.
- Prevents moisture loss / freezer burn.
- Extends shelf life by 3 – 6 months.
- Acts as a thermal buffer against minor temperature fluctuations.
- Adds product mass (economic benefit up to regulatory limits).

**Limitations:** **(1½ marks)**
- Glaze can crack during handling, exposing product.
- Adds non-edible weight that may mislead consumers (regulated by Codex).
- Requires continuous cold-chain maintenance.
- Re-glazing may be needed for long storage, increasing cost.
- Adds an extra processing step — increases energy and labour cost.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A sample of frozen mackerel weighing **800 g** is dipped in chilled water for glazing. After withdrawal and draining, the mass is **920 g**. Calculate the **glaze mass**, **glazing efficiency**, and the **glaze protection ratio**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same glazed mackerel, after 4 months of storage at -18 °C, has lost **45 g** of moisture, while an identical unglazed control lost **120 g** over the same period, calculate the **relative protection efficiency** of the glaze. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Given:** $W_1 = 800$ g, $W_2 = 920$ g.

**(i) Glaze mass:** **(2 marks)**

$$
W_g = W_2 - W_1 = 920 - 800 = 120 \text{ g}
$$

**(ii) Glazing efficiency:** **(3 marks)**

$$
GE(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100 = \frac{120}{920} \times 100 = 13.04\%
$$

**Interpretation:** $GE = 13.04\%$ is within the typical industrial range of 10 – 20 %, indicating an acceptable glazing operation.

**(iii) Glaze protection ratio:** **(2 marks)**

$$
GPR(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{120}{800} \times 100 = 15.00\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A $GPR$ of 15 % means that for every 100 g of original fish, 15 g of ice glaze was applied — a strong protective coating.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $M_{loss, glazed} = 45$ g, $M_{loss, unglazed} = 120$ g.

**(i) Relative protection efficiency:** **(5 marks)**

$$
R_{protection} = 1 - \frac{M_{loss,\,glazed}}{M_{loss,\,unglazed}} = 1 - \frac{45}{120}
$$

$$
R_{protection} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625
$$

$$
R_{protection}(\%) = 62.5\%
$$

**(ii) Comment:** **(2 marks)**

> The glaze provided a **62.5 % reduction** in moisture loss compared to the unglazed control. This is a **good to excellent** protection level (typical benchmark: 40 – 85 %), confirming that the glazing operation was effective. The remaining 37.5 % of moisture loss is attributable to slow sublimation through the glaze, micro-cracks, and storage temperature fluctuations. The result supports continued use of glazing for mackerel export chains.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Glazing questions (PEAGT752 / KTU 2024 ESE pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $GE$ and $GPR$ denominators** — Mixing $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the denominator costs **2 marks** instantly. Always re-read: "with respect to original" → $W_1$; "with respect to glazed" → $W_2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×100 factor** — Reporting $GE = 0.13$ instead of $13.04\%$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Always write **g, kg, %**.
> 4. **No diagram / unlabelled diagram** — A Part-B 7-mark sub-part without a flow diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the "comment" sub-part** — A numerical result without a one-line **practical interpretation** is treated as incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing glazing with coating/breading** — Glazing is an *ice* layer, not a starch or batter coating.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Glazing (PEAGT752, Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Glazing = applying a thin ice film on a *pre-frozen* product by contact with chilled water (0 – 4 °C).
- **Core purpose:** (i) Block O₂ to prevent lipid oxidation, (ii) suppress moisture sublimation to prevent freezer burn.
- **Mandatory pre-requisite:** Product must be at -18 °C or below **before** glazing.
- **Key formula (most tested):**
  $$GE(\%) = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_2} \times 100$$
- **Distinguish GE vs GPR:** GE divides by $W_2$; GPR divides by $W_1$.
- **Industrial GE range:** 10 – 20 % (regulated by Codex Alimentarius, max 20 %).
- **Latent heat of water:** $L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg.
- **Types (4 main):** Dip, Spray, Vacuum, Ice Slurry.
- **Primary product:** Fatty fish (mackerel, sardine, salmon) and shrimp.
- **Relative protection efficiency formula:**
  $$R_{protection} = 1 - \frac{M_{loss,\,glazed}}{M_{loss,\,unglazed}}$$
- **Re-glazing:** Required when glaze sublimates during long storage; re-apply using same procedure.
- **Kerala-specific relevance:** Major marine export state — glazing is industry-standard for EU, Japan, USA shipments.
- **Pitfall:** Glazing is *not* a microbial inactivation step; it is purely a *barrier* technique and must always be combined with frozen storage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grading** is a post-harvest unit operation in which a heterogeneous bulk of agricultural produce is segregated into several homogeneous lots (grades) based on one or more measurable quality attributes such as size, shape, mass, volume, density, color, surface texture, ripeness, or defect level, in accordance with a pre-established standard or specification.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, grading is considered a **value-addition** and **quality-assurance** unit operation that immediately follows cleaning and precedes packaging. It is the physical manifestation of the **quality standard** into a tangible product classification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a supermarket and see tomatoes neatly arranged in trays — small ones together, large ones together, and a separate tray for the bruised ones. Now imagine the same tomatoes arriving at the store in a single mixed crate from the farm. *Grading is the operation that transforms that mixed crate into those neat trays.*

**Real-world analogy:**
Think of grading as the agricultural equivalent of a school examination system. Just as students are divided into A, B, C grade categories based on marks (a measurable quality attribute), agricultural produce is divided into Grade A, B, C (or Special, Standard, General) based on physical/quality attributes. The "syllabus" here is the **grading standard** (AGMARK, BIS, Codex), and the "examiner" is the **grading machine or operator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sorting vs. Grading — A Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):**
> - **Sorting**: Separation of *defective/undesirable* items from *acceptable* items (binary: Accept vs. Reject).
> - **Grading**: Separation of *acceptable* items into *multiple quality classes* based on a continuous quality parameter (multi-class: Grade 1, 2, 3...).

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Parameters

The principal measurable attributes used for grading are:

- **Size** (length, width, diameter) — measured in **mm** or **cm**
- **Shape** (sphericity, aspect ratio) — dimensionless
- **Mass / Weight** — measured in **grams (g)** or **kilograms (kg)**
- **Volume** — measured in **cm³** or **mL**
- **Density / Specific Gravity** — measured in **kg/m³**
- **Color** — measured in **L*a*b* color space units** or RGB values
- **Surface Texture & Defects** — measured as percentage (%) of damaged surface area
- **Moisture Content** — measured in **% (w.b.)** or **% (d.b.)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AGMARK (Agricultural Marketing)** — India's statutory grading mark administered under the **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937**. Products carrying the AGMARK seal conform to quality standards prescribed by the **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)**.
> **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** — issues standards such as **IS 2860:1964** for fruits and vegetables, **IS 14802:2000** for cereals.
> **Codex Alimentarius** — international food standards under FAO/WHO.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives and Principles of Grading

#### 2.1.1 Objectives (CO Mapping: CO3 — Apply grading operations)

1. **Quality uniformity** — providing homogeneous lots for downstream processing, packaging, and retail display.
2. **Price differentiation** — enabling market-based price stratification (premium grade vs. standard grade).
3. **Waste reduction** — early removal of damaged/spoiled produce prevents contamination of sound produce.
4. **Operational efficiency** — uniform-grade produce feeds uniformly into subsequent machines (peelers, dryers, sorters).
5. **Consumer protection** — guarantees minimum quality at point of sale.
6. **Export compliance** — meets international phytosanitary and quality standards.

#### 2.1.2 Principles (Six Underlying Physical Principles)

| # | Principle | Physical Property Exploited | Typical Equipment |
|---|-----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Size separation** | Geometric dimensions | Screens, sieves, perforated cylinders |
| 2 | **Shape separation** | Aspect ratio, sphericity | Disc separators, indented cylinders |
| 3 | **Weight/Mass separation** | Gravitational force, inertia | Gravity tables, specific-gravity separators |
| 4 | **Aerodynamic separation** | Drag-to-weight ratio | Aspirators, air classifiers |
| 5 | **Optical/Electronic separation** | Reflectance, color, spectral signature | Color sorters, machine vision systems |
| 6 | **Mechanical property separation** | Hardness, firmness, elasticity | Textural sorters, impact sorters |

### 2.2 Classification of Grading Methods

#### 2.2.1 Manual Grading
- Performed by trained human graders using visual inspection, hand-held calipers, or weighing balances.
- **Accuracy**: ±5–10 % variation between graders.
- **Capacity**: ~50–200 kg/hour per person.
- **Cost**: Low capital, high labor.

#### 2.2.2 Mechanical Grading
- Uses physical principles (vibration, rotation, gravity, air-flow) to separate produce.
- **Capacity**: 500 kg/hour to 50 tonnes/hour.
- **Examples**: Rotary drum graders, vibrating sieves, belt-and-roll graders.

#### 2.2.3 Electronic/Optical Grading
- Uses cameras, NIR (Near-Infrared) sensors, and machine-learning algorithms.
- **Accuracy**: ±1–2 %, capacity up to 10–20 tonnes/hour per lane.
- **Examples**: Color sorters (Sortex, Bühler), hyperspectral imaging systems.

### 2.3 Standard Grading Equipment — Operating Logic

#### A. Rotary Drum Grader (Cylindrical Screen Grader)
A perforated rotating cylinder mounted on a slight inclination (typically **3°–5°** from horizontal). Produce enters at the elevated end; undersized particles fall through the apertures while oversized ones travel axially to the discharge end.

#### B. Vibrating Screen Grader
A flat or curved screen vibrates with amplitude **A** (mm) and frequency **f** (Hz). Acceleration imparted to the bed:

$$a = 4 \pi^2 f^2 A$$

where the acceleration ratio (Γ) determines the conveying regime:

$$\Gamma = \frac{a}{g}$$

For optimal conveying without fluidization: **Γ ≈ 2.5 to 4.0**.

#### C. Belt-and-Roll Grader
Produce moves along a conveyor belt; sized rollers progressively widen the gap to drop undersized fruits while allowing larger ones to pass.

#### D. Specific-Gravity Separator (Gravity Table)
A vibrating inclined deck with air blown upward through a porous surface separates particles by density. Heavy particles climb uphill; light particles float downhill.

#### E. Indented Cylinder Separator
A rotating cylinder with hemispherical indentations on its inner surface lifts short grains (e.g., broken rice kernels) while long grains remain — extensively used in **rice and seed grading**.

#### F. Disc Separator
Discs with raised pockets lift longer particles from a flowing mass — used for separating barley from wheat.

#### G. Color Sorter (Electronic Sortex)
High-resolution CCD cameras scan each kernel as it free-falls through a chute. Defective/odd-colored particles are ejected by a precisely timed pulse of compressed air.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield and frequently tested in KTU University Exams (End Semester Evaluation).**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Sphericity (Φ) | $\Phi = \dfrac{D_e}{D_c}$ where $D_e = \left(\dfrac{6V}{\pi}\right)^{1/3}$, $D_c$ = major axis | dimensionless | Φ = 1 for perfect sphere |
| 2 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $AR = \dfrac{\text{major axis}}{\text{minor axis}}$ | dimensionless | AR > 1.5 → elongated |
| 3 | Acceleration of vibrating bed | $a = (2\pi f)^2 A$ | m/s² | Where A = amplitude (m), f = frequency (Hz) |
| 4 | Acceleration ratio (Γ) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{a}{g}$ | dimensionless | Γ = 2.5–4 → optimum |
| 5 | Screen effectiveness (E) | $E = \dfrac{(\text{oversize recovery}) \times (\text{undersize recovery})}{100}$ | % | KTU frequently asked |
| 6 | Screen Capacity (Q) | $Q = C \cdot A \cdot \rho_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3$ | tonnes/h | C = empirical constant; A = screen area; ρ_b = bulk density |
| 7 | Centrifugal force on rotary drum | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | N | ω = angular velocity, r = radius |
| 8 | Reynolds number for particle in air-stream | $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_a \, u \, d_p}{\mu_a}$ | dimensionless | For aerodynamic sorting |
| 9 | Terminal velocity of particle | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{3 \rho_a C_D}}$ | m/s | C_D = drag coefficient |
| 10 | Capacity of rotary drum grader | $Q_{drum} = \dfrac{\pi n \left(R^2 - r^2\right) L \rho_b}{60}$ | kg/min | n = rpm, R = drum radius, r = build-up radius |
| 11 | Number of indentations per cylinder | $N = \dfrac{\pi D L}{A_p}$ | count | D = cylinder dia, L = length, A_p = indent area |
| 12 | Specific gravity of produce | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_w}$ | dimensionless | Used for gravity-table grading |
| 13 | Yield of Grade i | $\eta_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Mass fraction of grade i |
| 14 | Capacity of color sorter | $Q_{sortex} = n_{lanes} \times n_{chutes} \times v_{feed} \times m_{kernel}$ | kg/h | Throughput per channel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important constants used in KTU numericals:**
> - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Air density $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 20 °C, sea level)
> - Water density $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rice mills**: Paddy grading by indent cylinder → separates broken, shriveled, and long/short grains.
- **Apple packing houses**: Optical graders sort by color, size, and blemishes at 10–15 fruits/second per lane.
- **Citrus industry**: Size graders separate oranges into 5–6 commercial sizes (88s, 100s, 125s, etc., denoting count per 18 kg box).
- **Spice industry**: Air classifiers separate light husk fragments from heavy cumin/coriander seeds.
- **Seed industry**: Specific-gravity tables upgrade seed germination percentage by removing immature, hollow seeds.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Acceleration Ratio for a Vibrating Grader

**Problem statement:** A vibrating grading screen has an amplitude of **A = 4 mm** and operates at a frequency of **f = 12 Hz**. Determine the acceleration imparted to the particle bed and verify whether it falls in the optimum conveying range (Γ = 2.5 to 4.0).

#### Step 1 — Express the displacement as a function of time

For a sinusoidal oscillation, the vertical displacement of the screen is:

$$y(t) = A \sin(2 \pi f t)$$

where:
- $A$ = amplitude (m)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $t$ = time (s)

#### Step 2 — Differentiate twice to obtain acceleration

The velocity is the first derivative:

$$v(t) = \frac{dy}{dt} = 2 \pi f \cdot A \cos(2 \pi f t)$$

The acceleration is the second derivative:

$$a(t) = \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} = -(2 \pi f)^2 \cdot A \sin(2 \pi f t)$$

#### Step 3 — Identify the peak (maximum) acceleration

Since $\sin(2\pi f t)$ ranges between $-1$ and $+1$, the peak acceleration magnitude is:

$$a_{peak} = (2 \pi f)^2 \cdot A$$

#### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

Given $f = 12 \text{ Hz}$, $A = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$:

$$a_{peak} = (2 \pi \times 12)^2 \times 0.004$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$2 \pi \times 12 = 24 \pi \approx 75.398 \text{ rad/s}$$

Square it:

$$(75.398)^2 \approx 5684.9 \text{ (rad/s)}^2$$

Multiply by A:

$$a_{peak} \approx 5684.9 \times 0.004 \approx 22.74 \text{ m/s}^2$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the acceleration ratio Γ

$$\Gamma = \frac{a_{peak}}{g} = \frac{22.74}{9.81} \approx 2.318$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since Γ ≈ 2.32, this is just **below** the optimum range (2.5–4.0). The screen will convey particles but with reduced efficiency. To bring Γ into range, either increase amplitude to **A ≈ 4.3 mm** or increase frequency to **f ≈ 12.5 Hz**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Working Principle of Common Graders

| Equipment | Driving Force | Property Used | Capacity Range | Suitable Crops | Accuracy |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|----------------|----------------|----------|
| **Rotary Drum Screen** | Gravity + Rotation | Size | 1–10 t/h | Potato, onion, mango | ±5 % |
| **Vibrating Screen** | Vibration + Gravity | Size | 2–20 t/h | Grain, seed, pulse | ±3 % |
| **Belt-and-Roll** | Gravity + Geometry | Size | 0.5–5 t/h | Apple, citrus, tomato | ±4 % |
| **Gravity Table** | Vibration + Air + Gravity | Specific gravity | 2–15 t/h | Seed, grain, coffee bean | ±2 % |
| **Indented Cylinder** | Centrifugal + Lift | Length | 1–8 t/h | Rice, barley, wheat | ±2 % |
| **Disc Separator** | Centrifugal + Geometry | Length/Shape | 2–10 t/h | Wheat, barley, oat | ±3 % |
| **Aspirator (Air Classifier)** | Aerodynamic drag | Mass × Shape | 1–6 t/h | Cumin, coriander, mustard | ±5 % |
| **Color Sorter (Optical)** | Computer vision + Air-jet | Color/Defect | 1–20 t/h | Rice, dal, coffee, nut | ±1 % |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Sieve Analysis & Grade Yield Calculation

**Problem:** A 200 kg batch of mangoes is graded on a rotary drum grader into three size fractions. The yields are:
- Grade A (> 250 g): 78 kg
- Grade B (150–250 g): 92 kg
- Grade C (< 150 g): 30 kg

Calculate (i) the mass-fraction yield of each grade, (ii) the overall grading efficiency if the under-sized fraction received into Grade C is 95 % accurate and the over-sized in Grade A is 90 % accurate.

#### (i) Mass-fraction yields

Total mass:

$$m_{total} = 78 + 92 + 30 = 200 \text{ kg}$$

$$\eta_A = \frac{78}{200} \times 100 = 39.0 \, \%$$

$$\eta_B = \frac{92}{200} \times 100 = 46.0 \, \%$$

$$\eta_C = \frac{30}{200} \times 100 = 15.0 \, \%$$

Verification: $\eta_A + \eta_B + \eta_C = 39 + 46 + 15 = 100 \, \%$ ✓

#### (ii) Overall grading efficiency

The screening effectiveness formula (refer Formula 5 in cheat-sheet) is:

$$E = \frac{(\text{oversize recovery}) \times (\text{undersize recovery})}{100}$$

Oversize recovery (Grade A) = 90 %; Undersize recovery (Grade C) = 95 %:

$$E = \frac{90 \times 95}{100} = 85.5 \, \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU student should clearly state: "5 % of undersize particles are misplaced into upper grades, and 10 % of oversize are misplaced into Grade B — these constitute the **misclassification loss**."
