| 10 | $SH = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} (T - T_{ref})\, dt$ | Shelf life / Safe storage hours | °C·h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Tip for KTU Exams:**
> If the wet-basis moisture is given, use: $M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$. Always show both the formula and the numerical substitution.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the CFTRI Method

| Application Domain | Use of CFTRI Method |
|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Farm-level grain storage** | Prevents post-harvest losses (currently 5–16% in India) |
| **FPO / Cooperative warehouses** | Bulk storage with quality retention |
| **Paddy parboiling (CFTRI process)** | Improves milling recovery by **~15–20%** |
| **Disaster-prone regions** | Hermetic storage of food grains (flood-/cyclone-safe) |
| **Seed banks / FCI godowns** | Long-term viability of certified seeds |
| **Export-grade grain storage** | Maintains ISO 22000 / FSSAI compliance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacity Calculation of a CFTRI Bin

A standard CFTRI bin is a **composite of a right circular cylinder** (top) and a **frustum/cone** (bottom for discharge). Let us derive the **total storage capacity** step-by-step.

### Given:
- Cylinder height: $h$ (m)
- Cylinder radius: $r$ (m)
- Conical hopper height: $h_c$ (m)
- Bulk density of grain: $\rho_{bulk}$ (kg/m³)
- Filling efficiency: $\eta$ (≈ 0.80–0.90)

### Step 1 — Volume of the cylindrical body

The volume of a right circular cylinder is given by the cross-sectional area times the height.

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h
$$

### Step 2 — Volume of the conical hopper

The volume of a cone (or frustum, in this case a full cone as the top diameter equals the cylinder diameter) is one-third of the base area times the height.

$$
V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h_c
$$

### Step 3 — Total internal volume of the bin

Summing the two geometric volumes gives the total empty volume available for grain storage.

$$
V_{bin} = V_{cyl} + V_{cone}
$$

$$
V_{bin} = \pi r^2 h + \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h_c
$$

$$
V_{bin} = \pi r^2 \left( h + \frac{h_c}{3} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Effective grain volume (accounting for filling efficiency)

Real grains do not pack perfectly; there are voids between kernels. So the actual usable volume is reduced by a filling efficiency factor $\eta$.

$$
V_{grain} = \eta \cdot V_{bin}
$$

### Step 5 — Mass of grain stored

Multiplying the effective volume by the bulk density gives the total grain weight.

$$
W_{grain} = V_{grain} \cdot \rho_{bulk}
$$

$$
W_{grain} = \eta \cdot \pi r^2 \left( h + \frac{h_c}{3} \right) \cdot \rho_{bulk}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A CFTRI storage bin has a diameter of 1.5 m, cylindrical height 2.0 m, and a conical bottom height of 0.6 m. The bulk density of wheat is 780 kg/m³, and the filling efficiency is 0.85. Calculate the storage capacity in **quintals** (1 quintal = 100 kg).

**Solution:**

**Step A — Identify the parameters**
- $r = 0.75$ m, $h = 2.0$ m, $h_c = 0.6$ m
- $\rho_{bulk} = 780$ kg/m³
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step B — Compute cylinder volume**

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi (0.75)^2 (2.0)
$$

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi \times 0.5625 \times 2.0
$$

$$
V_{cyl} = 3.534 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step C — Compute cone volume**

$$
V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \pi (0.75)^2 (0.6)
$$

$$
V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \times \pi \times 0.5625 \times 0.6
$$

$$
V_{cone} = 0.3534 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step D — Total bin volume**

$$
V_{bin} = 3.534 + 0.3534 = 3.887 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step E — Effective grain volume**

$$
V_{grain} = 0.85 \times 3.887 = 3.304 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step F — Mass of wheat stored**

$$
W_{grain} = 3.304 \times 780
$$

$$
W_{grain} = 2577.12 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step G — Convert to quintals**

$$
W_{grain} = \frac{2577.12}{100} = 25.77 \text{ quintals}
$$

**Answer:** The bin can store approximately **25.77 quintals (≈ 2.58 tonnes) of wheat**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moisture Content Conversion (Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis)

A core calculation in CFTRI storage analysis is the moisture content conversion.

**Given:** A grain sample has $M_{wb} = 14\%$. Find $M_{db}$.

### Step 1 — Definitions

By definition, wet-basis moisture is the mass of water divided by the total mass of the wet sample (water + dry matter).

$$
M_{wb} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100
$$

Dry-basis moisture is the mass of water divided by the mass of dry matter only.

$$
M_{db} = \frac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Express the dry-matter fraction

From the first equation, isolate the ratio $W_w / W_d$.

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100} = \frac{W_w}{W_w + W_d}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator on the right-hand side by $W_d$ gives the clean ratio of water to dry matter.

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100} = \frac{W_w / W_d}{1 + W_w / W_d}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the ratio

Let $x = W_w / W_d$ for brevity. Then the relation becomes a simple algebraic equation in $x$.

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100} = \frac{x}{1 + x}
$$

Cross-multiplying the equation isolates $x$ in terms of $M_{wb}$.

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100}(1 + x) = x
$$

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100} + \frac{M_{wb}}{100} x = x
$$

Moving all $x$-terms to one side of the equation.

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100} = x - \frac{M_{wb}}{100} x
$$

$$
\frac{M_{wb}}{100} = x \left( 1 - \frac{M_{wb}}{100} \right)
$$

Solving explicitly for $x$.

$$
x = \frac{M_{wb}/100}{1 - M_{wb}/100}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to dry basis

Recall that $x = W_w / W_d$ and $M_{db} = (W_w / W_d) \times 100 = 100 x$. Substituting the result for $x$.

$$
M_{db} = 100 \times \frac{M_{wb}/100}{1 - M_{wb}/100}
$$

$$
M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical substitution

Substitute $M_{wb} = 14$ into the derived formula.

$$
M_{db} = \frac{14}{1 - 14/100}
$$

$$
M_{db} = \frac{14}{0.86}
$$

$$
M_{db} = 16.28\%
$$

**Answer:** $M_{db} \approx 16.28\%$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CFTRI Bin vs. Other Storage Methods

| Parameter | **CFTRI GI Bin** | **Traditional Mud Bin (Kuthla)** | **Silo (CCB / Steel)** | **Bag Storage** |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Capacity** | 1–10 quintals | 5–50 quintals | 100–10,000+ tonnes | Variable |
| **Cost** | Moderate (₹1500–8000) | Low | Very High | Low |
| **Rodent Proof** | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor |
| **Moisture Proof** | ✅ Excellent (sealed) | ⚠ Moderate | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor |
| **Insect Infestation** | Low | High | Very Low | High |
| **Reusability** | 15–20 years | 3–5 years | 30+ years | Single-use bags |
| **Skill to Operate** | Low | Low | High | Low |
| **Best For** | **Farm-level** | Village-level | Commercial / FCI | Transport |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: CFTRI Method — Process Flow (Grain Storage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Grain] --> B[Pre-Cleaning]
    B --> C[Mechanical Drying to MC less than 12 percent wb]
    C --> D[Final Cleaning and Grading]
    D --> E{Optional Fumigation with AlP 3 g per tonne}
    E --> F[Cooling to Ambient Temperature]
    F --> G[Loading into CFTRI GI Bin]
    G --> H[Sealed Hermetic Storage 6 to 12 months]
    H --> I[Periodic Sampling via Side Port]
    I --> J{Quality Check Passed}
    J --> K[Discharge via Bottom Chute]
    J --> L[Re-fumigate and Re-store]
    K --> M[Grain Ready for Market or Consumption]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Every block represents a unit operation in the CFTRI storage workflow. The decision diamonds (`{ }`) are quality-control gates where inspection or additional treatment may be triggered.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of a CFTRI Storage Bin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph binStructure [CFTRI GI Storage Bin Assembly]
        direction TB
        nodeA[Top Lid Hinged and Lockable] --> nodeB[Upper Cylindrical Body GI Sheet 22 gauge]
        nodeB --> nodeC[Middle Inspection Port with Rubber Gasket]
        nodeC --> nodeD[Lower Cylindrical Body Double Seamed Joints]
        nodeD --> nodeE[Conical Hopper Hopper angle 45 to 60 degrees]
        nodeE --> nodeF[Bottom Discharge Chute with Slide Valve]
        nodeF --> nodeG[Pedestal Stand Height 30 cm above floor]
    end

    nodeH[Farmer Loads Clean Dry Grain MC less than 12 percent] --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeI[Grain Discharged to Bags or Conveyor]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Hopper Angle Rule:** For free gravity flow of grains, the cone angle must be **greater than the angle of repose** of the stored grain. For wheat, this is typically **> 27°**, and the CFTRI uses 45°–60° as a safety margin.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the CFTRI Parboiling Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Paddy MC 20 to 24 percent] --> P2[Soaking Tank 36 to 48 hours at 25 to 30 C]
    P2 --> P3[Drainage and Surface Water Removal]
    P3 --> P4[Steam Boiling Autoclave 15 to 20 psi for 15 to 20 minutes]
    P4 --> P5[Tempering / Steeping 4 to 6 hours]
    P5 --> P6[Mechanical Drying Sun or Mechanical Dryer to MC 14 percent]
    P6 --> P7[Parboiled Paddy Ready for Milling]
    P7 --> P8[Improved Milling Recovery plus 15 to 20 percent]
    P7 --> P9[Improved Nutrient Retention Vitamin B]
    P7 --> P10[Reduced Kernel Breakage]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CFTRI parboiling method, when properly executed, increases the **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** from ~55% (raw rice) to ~70%+ (parboiled rice) — a transformative gain for the Indian rice milling industry.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the CFTRI Method of grain storage. Mention any three salient features of a CFTRI storage bin.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **CFTRI Method** of grain storage refers to the engineered system developed by the **Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI)**, Mysore, for the safe, hermetic storage of food grains at the farm and commercial level using **galvanized iron (GI) metal bins** of standardized capacities (1–10 quintals).
>
> **Three salient features:**
> 1. **Cylindrical body with conical bottom** for efficient gravity discharge and structural strength.
> 2. **Galvanized iron (GI) sheet construction** (22–24 gauge) which is rodent-proof, moisture-proof, and corrosion-resistant.
> 3. **Hermetically sealable top lid and inspection port** that prevent ingress of insects, rodents, and humid air, enabling safe storage of **6–12 months** without chemical fumigation.

---

### **Q2. What is the safe moisture content for storing grains in a CFTRI bin? Why is drying critical before storage?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **safe moisture content** for storing grains in a CFTRI bin is **less than 12% (wet basis)**, which corresponds roughly to **less than 13.6% (dry basis)**.
>
> **Importance of drying:**
> - **Microbial growth** (fungi like *Aspergillus*) is suppressed when MC < 12%.
> - **Insect respiration and reproduction** slow down significantly.
> - **Respiration loss** of the grain itself is minimized, preserving dry matter and calories.
> - **Free fatty acid (FFA) buildup** in oilseeds is reduced, maintaining quality.
> - It prevents **moisture migration** within the bin, which can lead to localized spoilage and "hot spots."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme** ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and are typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are **TWO alternative questions** (Q-A and Q-B) so the student can practice either.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set Option I**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`**

#### **(a)** Explain the design features of a CFTRI storage bin with a neat sketch. List the engineering advantages of using galvanized iron as the construction material. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Design Features:**
1. **Cylindrical body** — High strength-to-material ratio, even distribution of lateral pressure.
2. **Conical hopper bottom** — Slope angle of 45°–60° (greater than angle of repose of grains) for free flow discharge.
3. **GI sheet thickness 22–24 gauge** — Provides rigidity and durability for 15–20 years.
4. **Hinged top lid with locking arrangement** — Easy inspection, secure closure.
5. **Side inspection port with gasket** — Allows sampling without breaking the main seal.
6. **Bottom discharge chute with slide valve** — Controlled gravity emptying.
7. **Pedestal support (≥ 30 cm height)** — Prevents floor moisture wicking and rodent entry.
8. **Doubled-seamed joints** — Eliminate gaps that could admit pests.

**Engineering Advantages of GI Construction:**
- **Corrosion resistance** due to zinc coating (≈ **120 g/m²**).
- **Non-toxic and food-grade safe**.
- **Impervious to moisture** and atmospheric gases.
- **Smooth surface** does not harbor insects or allow them to climb.
- **High mechanical strength** — withstands internal grain pressure (Janssen's load).
- **Long service life** with low maintenance.
- **Recyclable** at end of life — sustainable.

---

#### **(b)** A CFTRI bin is 1.2 m in diameter, with a cylindrical height of 1.8 m and a conical bottom of height 0.5 m. Wheat has a bulk density of 780 kg/m³, and the filling efficiency is 0.85. Calculate the bin's storage capacity in quintals. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.2 \text{ m} \Rightarrow r = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $h = 1.8 \text{ m}$, $h_c = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{bulk} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Cylinder volume**

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h = \pi (0.6)^2 (1.8) = \pi \times 0.36 \times 1.8 = 2.036 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Cone volume**

$$
V_{cone} = \frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h_c = \frac{1}{3} \pi (0.6)^2 (0.5) = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.5655 = 0.1885 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Total bin volume**

$$
V_{bin} = 2.036 + 0.1885 = 2.2245 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Effective grain volume**

$$
V_{grain} = 0.85 \times 2.2245 = 1.891 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of wheat stored**

$$
W_{grain} = 1.891 \times 780 = 1474.98 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to quintals**

$$
W_{grain} = \frac{1474.98}{100} = 14.75 \text{ quintals}
$$

**Answer:** The bin can store **≈ 14.75 quintals (1.475 tonnes)** of wheat.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set Option II (Alternative Choice)**

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`**

#### **(a)** Describe the CFTRI method of paddy parboiling. How does it improve the milling recovery and nutritional quality of rice? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Steps of CFTRI Parboiling:**

1. **Cleaning** — Raw paddy is cleaned to remove stones, chaff, and immature grains.
2. **Soaking** — Paddy is soaked in water at **25–30 °C for 36–48 hours** (or hot soaking at 70 °C for 3–4 hours) until the kernels reach **MC ≈ 30%**.
3. **Drainage and surface drying** — Excess surface water is removed.
4. **Steam boiling** — Soaked paddy is steamed in an **autoclave at 15–20 psi for 15–20 minutes**. This is the **gelatinization step** where starch granules swell and fuse.
5. **Tempering** — The hot paddy is held for **4–6 hours** to allow moisture and heat to equalize inside the kernel.
6. **Drying** — The parboiled paddy is dried (sun or mechanical) to **MC = 14%** for safe storage and milling.

**Improvements in Milling & Nutrition:**

| Aspect | Improvement |
|--------|-------------|
| **Head Rice Yield (HRY)** | Increases from **~55% to ~70%+** |
| **Kernel Breakage** | Reduces by **80–90%** |
| **Vitamin B₁ (Thiamine)** | Retained in the kernel (lost in raw rice polishing) |
| **Protein content** | Slight increase due to inward diffusion |
| **Oil migration** | Stabilized — reduces FFA rancidity in bran |
| **Storage life** | Extended — parboiled rice resists insect attack better |

---

#### **(b)** Compare the CFTRI metal bin with the traditional **'Kuthla' (mud bin)** and **bag storage** systems. Discuss three advantages and two limitations of the CFTRI method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | CFTRI GI Bin | Kuthla (Mud Bin) | Bag Storage |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Construction** | Galvanized iron | Mud / brick / bamboo | Jute / HDPE bags |
| **Capacity** | 1–10 q (modular) | 5–50 q | Variable |
| **Moisture barrier** | Excellent | Moderate | Poor |
| **Rodent proof** | Excellent | Poor | Very Poor |
| **Insect proof** | Excellent | Poor | Poor |
| **Reusability** | 15–20 yrs | 3–5 yrs | Single use |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low | Lowest |
| **Skill needed** | Low | Moderate | Low |

**Three Advantages of CFTRI Method:**
1. **Drastic reduction in post-harvest losses** (from 8–10% in traditional storage to **< 2%**).
2. **Long storage life (6–12 months)** without chemical fumigants — safer food.
3. **Reusability and durability** of 15–20 years gives excellent **return on investment** for farmers.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Higher initial cost** compared to mud bins — often subsidized by government schemes (e.g., **MIDH/NFSM**) for adoption.
2. **Limited capacity per unit** (max 10 q) — not suitable for large commercial warehouses where **silos** are preferred.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in CFTRI Method Questions (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in capacity calculation** — Students often forget to convert the answer from **kg to quintals** (divide by 100). KTU examiners specifically check for this. Always end with the unit.
>
> 2. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture** — A question may state one and ask for the other. State the formula **explicitly** before substituting. Marks are awarded for the **formula step**, not just the final number.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the filling efficiency (η)** — In bin capacity problems, omitting η is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction**. Always write "Effective grain volume = η × Bin volume."
>
> 4. **Drawing the bin without labels** — A "neat sketch" without parts labeled (lid, body, hopper, chute, stand) gets **partial credit only**. Label **at least 6 components**.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the angle of repose in cone design** — Examiners look for "hopper angle > angle of repose" as a design principle. Missing this loses a mark.
>
> 6. **Confusing CFTRI parboiling with simple boiling** — The CFTRI method involves **steam under pressure**, not just hot water soaking. This distinction is a favorite KTU question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — CFTRI Method**

- **CFTRI** = **Central Food Technological Research Institute**, Mysore, est. **1950**, under **CSIR**.
- The **CFTRI Method** primarily refers to the **GI metal bin storage system** and the **CFTRI parboiling protocol**.
- **Bin shape:** Cylinder + Cone (hopper angle **45°–60°** > angle of repose).
- **Material:** Galvanized Iron sheet, **22–24 gauge**, Zn coating ≈ **120 g/m²**.
- **Stand height:** ≥ **30 cm** above floor.
- **Capacities:** **1, 2, 3, 5, 10 quintals** (modular farm-level design).
- **Safe storage MC:** **< 12% w.b.** (= **< 13.6% d.b.**).
- **Storage duration:** **6–12 months** without fumigation.
- **CFTRI Parboiling cycle:** Soak (36–48 h, 25–30 °C) → Steam (15–20 psi, 15–20 min) → Temper (4–6 h) → Dry to **14% MC**.
- **Head Rice Yield** improves from **~55% → ~70%+** with parboiling.
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1 - M_{wb}/100)$, $V_{bin} = \pi r^2 (h + h_c/3)$, $W = \eta \cdot V \cdot \rho_{bulk}$.
- **Janssen's pressure concept:** Vertical $\sigma_v = \rho g h$, Horizontal $\sigma_h = K \sigma_v$ with $K \approx 0.4$.
- **Post-harvest loss reduction:** from **8–10% (traditional) to < 2% (CFTRI bin)**.
- **Lifespan of CFTRI bin:** **15–20 years** with minimal maintenance.
- **Government support:** MIDH / NFSM / RKVY schemes subsidize CFTRI bins for small and marginal farmers.
- **Pedestal must be ≥ 30 cm** — this is a **mandatory KTU exam point**.
- **CFTRI parboiling is hydrothermal**, not just soaking — the **gelatinization** of starch is the key biochemical change.
- **Angle of repose** of common grains: Wheat ≈ 27°, Rice ≈ 34°, Maize ≈ 32°.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pantnagar Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pantnagar Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Pantnagar Process** refers to a comprehensive set of soybean processing technologies developed and standardized at **G.B. Pant University of Agriculture and Technology (GBPUA&T), Pantnagar, Uttarakhand, India** (under the stewardship of late Prof. Gurmukh Singh and his team during the 1970s–1980s). It encompasses the unit operations involved in converting whole soybeans (*Glycine max*) into value-added protein-rich food products such as **soymilk, soy paneer (tofu), full-fat soy flour, and defatted soy flour**, while simultaneously addressing anti-nutritional factors (trypsin inhibitors, lipoxygenase enzymes, and beany flavour compounds).

In the context of **Unit Operations in Post Harvest Engineering (PEAGT752, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module 3 treats the Pantnagar Process as a **model integrated process flow** because it sequentially combines several *unit operations* — cleaning, dehulling, soaking, blanching, grinding, filtration, coagulation, pressing, and drying — making it an excellent pedagogical case study for connecting individual unit operations into a complete process line.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Soy-to-Dairy" Analogy (Plain-English Intuition):**
> Imagine an Indian dairy household. Raw cow milk cannot be stored long; it is boiled (heat treatment), strained (filtration), and curdled using a coagulant to make paneer (cheese) which can be preserved. Similarly, raw soybean seeds contain anti-nutritional factors that make them hard to digest and impart a "beany" off-flavour. The Pantnagar Process applies a *parallel sequence of dairy-style unit operations* to soybeans: **soak → blanch → grind → filter (to make soymilk) → coagulate (to make soy paneer) → press & dry**. The genius of Pantnagar was engineering these steps so the protein-rich extract behaves just like dairy milk, opening the door for **vegetarian protein analogs of milk, curd, and paneer** in tropical India.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in **Bold**)

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| Optimum soaking temperature | **30 °C ± 2 °C** |
| Soaking duration | **8–12 hours** |
| Blanching temperature | **95–100 °C (boiling water)** |
| Blanching time | **15–20 minutes** |
| Grind : Water ratio | **1 : 6 to 1 : 10 (w/v)** |
| Coagulant (commonly used) | **2% CaSO₄ or 1% MgCl₂** |
| Coagulation temperature | **70–85 °C** |
| Protein recovery in soymilk | **70–85 %** |
| pH of fresh soymilk | **6.5–7.0** |
| Solids content of soymilk | **8–12 % w/w** |
| Final moisture of soy paneer | **70–80 % w.b.** |

---

## 1.4 Module Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in Module 3:**
> The Pantnagar Process is a **flagship indigenous technology** tested in the KTU syllabus because it simultaneously illustrates (a) **heat transfer** (blanching), (b) **mass transfer** (extraction of solubles), (c) **mechanical separation** (filtration, pressing), and (d) **thermal processing** (boiling, drying). It satisfies **Course Outcomes CO3** ("Apply principles of material and energy balance to integrated post-harvest process lines") and is a regular **14-mark ESE question** topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Flow Decomposition (Unit-Operation Mapping)

The Pantnagar Process is not a single reaction but a **chain of six sequential unit operations**:

### Step 1 — Cleaning and Grading
- Removal of foreign matter, broken seeds, weed seeds, stones, and chaff.
- Done using **air-screen cleaners** (aspiration + oscillating sieves).
- Reduces microbial load and prevents downstream equipment damage.

### Step 2 — Dehulling (Optional but recommended for milk)
- Dry beans passed through **pitting rollers** or **abrasive dehullers**.
- Husk (testa) accounts for ~**8 %** of seed weight; it contributes bitter taste and high fibre.
- Pantnagar pioneered **heat-treatment before dehulling** to loosen the hull.

### Step 3 — Soaking
- Beans soaked in **water at 1:3 (w/v)** for **8–12 h at 30 °C**.
- Hydration raises moisture from **~10 %** to **~60 % w.b.** — softens cell walls for efficient protein extraction.

### Step 4 — Blanching (Heat Treatment)
- Soaked beans dropped into **boiling water (95–100 °C) for 15–20 min**.
- **Inactivates trypsin inhibitors** (≈ 80–90 % destruction).
- **Denatures lipoxygenase** — eliminates beany flavour.
- Improves protein extractability.

### Step 5 — Wet Grinding
- Hot blanched beans ground with **fresh hot water (80–85 °C)** in a **colloidal mill or stone grinder**.
- Grind : Water = **1 : 6 to 1 : 10 (w/v)**.
- High temperature during grinding prevents off-flavour re-formation.

### Step 6 — Filtration (Soymilk Extraction)
- Slurry passed through **muslin/cheese cloth or pressure filter**.
- **Okara** (insoluble residue) is separated.
- Resulting liquid is **raw soymilk** — basis for all downstream Pantnagar products.

### Step 7 — Boiling and Formulation (Downstream)
- Soymilk boiled **10–15 min** with sugar, salt, flavours.
- Forms **ready-to-drink soy beverage**.

### Step 8 — Coagulation (Soy Paneer / Tofu Manufacture)
- Hot soymilk (**70–85 °C**) treated with **coagulant (CaSO₄ / MgCl₂ / citric acid / Glucono-δ-lactone)**.
- Curds form; whey is drained.
- Curds pressed in a **perforated mould** to obtain **soy paneer**.

### Step 9 — Drying (for soy flour)
- Soymilk spray-dried or roller-dried → **full-fat soy flour**.
- If preceded by oil expelling (solvent or mechanical) → **defatted soy flour**.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' & 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it is done | How it is achieved |
|---|---|---|
| Soaking | Hydrate seed matrix → easier protein extraction | Water immersion, 30 °C, 8–12 h |
| Blanching | Destroy anti-nutrients, inactivate lipoxygenase | Hot water, 95–100 °C, 15–20 min |
| Hot grinding | Prevent re-activation of enzymes; better protein dispersion | Colloidal mill at 80–85 °C |
| Filtration | Separate liquid (milk) from insoluble fibre (okara) | Cloth/mesh filtration |
| Coagulation | Precipitate proteins into a curd-like matrix | CaSO₄ / MgCl₂ at 70–85 °C |
| Pressing | Reduce whey content, shape the curd | Mechanical pressing in moulds |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Pantnagar Process Calculations** — memorize these for ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{w_w}{w_w + w_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |
| 2 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{w_w}{w_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 3 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$ | Conversion relation | — |
| 4 | $\eta_{prot} = \dfrac{P_{milk} \times V_{milk}}{P_{bean} \times W_{bean}} \times 100$ | Protein extraction efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{loss}$ | Mass balance (steady state) | kg/h |
| 6 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat for blanching | kJ |
| 7 | $Q_{latent} = m \, L$ | Latent heat (steam heating) | kJ |
| 8 | $W_{water} = W_{bean} \left(\dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{100 - M_2}\right)$ | Water absorbed during soaking | kg |
| 9 | $Y_{paneer} = \dfrac{W_{paneer}}{W_{bean}} \times 100$ | Paneer yield from beans | % |
| 10 | $C_{okara} = \dfrac{S_{slurry} - S_{milk}}{S_{slurry}} \times 100$ | Solid rejection into okara | % |
| 11 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{supplied}} \times 100$ | Blancher thermal efficiency | % |
| 12 | $S_{milk} = \dfrac{M_{dry\,solid}}{M_{milk\,total}}$ | Solids fraction in soymilk | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` inside markdown tables — already complied with above.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Rural food security**: Provides low-cost, high-protein food from a non-dairy source.
- **Defence & space rations**: Spray-dried soy flour (developed under Pantnagar lineage) is used in **defective/combat rations** of Indian armed forces.
- **Commercial dairy analogs**: Modern brands (Sofit, GoodDot, So Good) descend directly from Pantnagar-process know-how.
- **Industrial application**: Pantnagar data is the **design basis** for soybean processing plants of capacity **5–50 tonnes/day** across India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Water Absorbed During Soaking

**Problem context:** Compute how much water 100 kg of dry soybeans (initial moisture 10 % w.b.) will absorb if soaked to a final moisture of 60 % w.b.

**Step 1 — Define variables**

Let $W_{bean}$ = mass of dry beans, $M_1$ = initial moisture (w.b.), $M_2$ = final moisture (w.b.), $W_{water}$ = water absorbed.

**Step 2 — Express dry solids in initial mass**

$$
W_{dry\,solid} = W_{bean} \left(1 - \dfrac{M_1}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Express total mass after soaking**

$$
W_{total} = \dfrac{W_{dry\,solid}}{1 - \dfrac{M_2}{100}}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass balance on water**

$$
W_{water} = W_{total} - W_{bean}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify**

$$
W_{water} = \dfrac{W_{bean}\left(1 - \dfrac{M_1}{100}\right)}{1 - \dfrac{M_2}{100}} - W_{bean}
$$

$$
W_{water} = W_{bean}\left[\dfrac{1 - \dfrac{M_1}{100}}{1 - \dfrac{M_2}{100}} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_{water} = W_{bean}\left[\dfrac{\left(1 - \dfrac{M_1}{100}\right) - \left(1 - \dfrac{M_2}{100}\right)}{1 - \dfrac{M_2}{100}}\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Final working formula**

$$
\boxed{W_{water} = W_{bean}\left(\dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{100 - M_2}\right)}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation (with $W_{bean} = 100$ kg, $M_1 = 10$, $M_2 = 60$)**

$$
W_{water} = 100 \times \left(\dfrac{60 - 10}{100 - 60}\right) = 100 \times \dfrac{50}{40}
$$

$$
W_{water} = 125 \text{ kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** 100 kg dry beans absorb **125 kg water**, swelling to a total mass of **225 kg** — a critical design number for soak-tank sizing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Protein Extraction Efficiency

**Problem context:** 50 kg of soybeans (containing 38 % protein, db) are processed to yield 380 litres of soymilk containing 3.2 % protein (w/v). Compute protein extraction efficiency.

**Step 1 — Total protein in beans**

Let $P_{bean}$ = protein fraction in bean (db), $W_{bean}$ = mass of beans, $DB$ = dry basis ratio.

$$
M_{dry} = W_{bean} \times (1 - M_1) = 50 \times (1 - 0.10) = 45 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
P_{in} = 45 \times 0.38 = 17.1 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Total protein in soymilk**

Let $P_{milk}$ = protein fraction in milk (w/v), $V_{milk}$ = volume of milk. Assume density $\rho \approx 1.02$ kg/L, so mass of milk $\approx 380 \times 1.02 = 387.6$ kg.

$$
P_{out} = 387.6 \times 0.032 = 12.40 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Extraction efficiency**

$$
\eta_{prot} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12.40}{17.10} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{prot} = 72.5 \text{ \%}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A well-run Pantnagar process should give **70–85 %** protein extraction; our value of **72.5 %** indicates proper blanching and grinding.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Required for Blanching

**Problem context:** 100 kg of soaked beans (initial temp 30 °C) must be blanched to 95 °C. Specific heat of hydrated beans ≈ **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**. Compute heat required.

**Step 1 — Apply sensible heat equation**

$$
Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values**

$$
Q = 100 \times 3.5 \times (95 - 30)
$$

$$
Q = 100 \times 3.5 \times 65
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 22{,}750 \text{ kJ} = 6.32 \text{ kWh}}
$$

**Step 3 — Add 10 % heat losses → design load**

$$
Q_{design} = 1.10 \times 22{,}750 = 25{,}025 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the basis for sizing the **blancher steam coil or electric heater**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables on Yield

| Process Variable | Effect on Soymilk Yield | Effect on Protein Extraction | Effect on Flavour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soaking time ↑ (until 12 h) | ↑ | ↑ | Neutral |
| Soaking time > 14 h | ↓ (microbial spoilage) | ↓ | Off-flavour ↑ |
| Blanching temp ↑ (until 100 °C) | ↑ | ↑ | Beany ↓ |
| Grind : Water ratio ↑ | Dilutes solids | ↓ per litre | Lighter taste |
| Grind temperature ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Beany ↓ |
| Coagulant dose optimal | — | Maximises paneer yield | Neutral |
| Coagulant overdose | — | ↓ (curd brittle) | Bitterness ↑ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Pantnagar Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Soybean Intake"] --> B["Step 1 Air Screen Cleaner"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Dehuller Optional"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Soak Tank 30C 8 to 12 h"]
    D --> E["Step 4 Blancher 95 to 100C 15 to 20 min"]
    E --> F["Step 5 Colloidal Mill 80 to 85C"]
    F --> G["Step 6 Filtration Cloth Mesh"]
    G --> H["Raw Soymilk Storage Tank"]
    H --> I1["Branch A Soy Beverage Boil and Pack"]
    H --> I2["Branch B Soy Paneer Coagulation"]
    H --> I3["Branch C Soy Flour Spray Dryer"]
    I2 --> J["Curd Press Mould"]
    J --> K["Soy Paneer Storage"]
    I3 --> L["Full Fat Soy Flour Packing"]
    G --> M["By product Okara"]
    M --> N["Okara Drying and Animal Feed"]
```

**Reading guide:** The flowchart shows how a single bean input fans into three product streams — beverage, paneer, and flour — with okara as a co-product for animal feed. Each block is a *unit operation* covered separately in earlier modules.

---

## 4.2 Mass-Tracking Sankey-Style Flow (Numerical Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Soybeans 100 kg"] --> B["Soaked Beans 225 kg"]
    B --> C["Ground Slurry 450 kg"]
    C --> D["Soymilk 380 kg"]
    C --> E["Okara 70 kg"]
    D --> F["Soy Paneer 55 kg"]
    D --> G["Whey 325 kg"]
    A --> H["Process Water 600 kg"]
    H --> D
    H --> G
```

**Reading guide:** Inputs (100 kg beans + 600 kg water = 700 kg) balance outputs (380 kg soymilk + 70 kg okara + 325 kg whey ≈ 775 kg including losses). In a real plant, the small imbalance is steam loss + evaporation.

---

## 4.3 Coagulation Logic — Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Soymilk 80C ready"] --> B["Choose Coagulant"]
    B --> B1["CaSO4 2 percent"]
    B --> B2["MgCl2 1 percent"]
    B --> B3["GDL 0.4 percent"]
    B1 --> C["Set pH 6.0 to 6.2"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D["Wait 20 to 30 min"]
    D --> E["Curd Forms"]
    E --> F["Cut Curd 1 cm cubes"]
    F --> G["Press 30 min"]
    G --> H["Soy Paneer Ready"]
```

**Reading guide:** The choice of coagulant dictates the texture: CaSO₄ → firm tofu; MgCl₂ → soft tofu; GDL → silky tofu. Pantnagar's original recommendation is **CaSO₄** for Indian palate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State any six sequential unit operations involved in the Pantnagar Process for soybean processing.**

**Model Answer (6 points, ~3 marks):**

1. Cleaning and grading of raw soybeans.
2. Optional dehulling.
3. Soaking in water at 30 °C for 8–12 hours.
4. Blanching in boiling water (95–100 °C) for 15–20 minutes.
5. Wet grinding with hot water (80–85 °C) in a colloidal mill.
6. Filtration through muslin cloth to obtain raw soymilk.
7. *(Bonus)* Boiling, coagulation, or drying based on end product.

*Each point carries ~0.5 mark; six correct points = 3 marks.*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Why is blanching considered the most critical step in the Pantnagar Process?**

**Model Answer:**

Blanching is critical because it **(i)** inactivates **trypsin inhibitors** (anti-nutritional factors that prevent protein digestion), **(ii)** denatures **lipoxygenase enzyme** which causes the characteristic **beany off-flavour**, and **(iii)** softens the cotyledon, facilitating higher **protein extraction efficiency (70–85 %)** in the subsequent grinding step. Without blanching, the soymilk would have low digestibility and unacceptable flavour — defeating the purpose of the entire process.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Carefully):**
> - Students often write **"boiling kills bacteria"** — wrong focus. Blanching in Pantnagar is for **enzyme inactivation**, not sterilization.
> - Students confuse **mass balance** with **material balance** — for steady-state processes they are the same; specify **steady-state** in answers.
> - Never write **"we add coagulant to curdle milk"** — it is **soymilk**, not dairy milk. Use the term **soymilk / soy curd** to score full marks.
> - Always carry **units** in every step of mass/energy balance — leaving a unit out costs **1–2 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Pantnagar Process** for the manufacture of **soymilk and soy paneer** from soybeans. List the role of each unit operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** **100 kg of soybeans** (initial moisture **10 % w.b.**, protein content **38 % db**) are processed via the Pantnagar Process. After soaking, moisture rises to **60 % w.b.** The beans are then blanched and ground with hot water in the ratio **1 : 8 (w/v)**. Filtration yields **380 kg soymilk** containing **3.2 % protein (w/w)** and **8 % total solids**. Compute:
- (i) Water absorbed during soaking.
- (ii) Protein extraction efficiency.
- (iii) Mass of okara (insoluble solids) rejected, assuming **45 % of bean solids** pass into okara. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Diagram & Description (7 marks)**

*Refer to Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 for the block diagram (3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for description).*

**Description (write in exam):**

- **Cleaning** — removes foreign matter using air-screen cleaner.
- **Dehulling** — optional; removes testa for better flavour and digestibility.
- **Soaking** — hydrates beans (10 % → 60 % moisture) at 30 °C for 8–12 h, softens cotyledon.
- **Blanching** — 95–100 °C for 15–20 min; inactivates trypsin inhibitor and lipoxygenase; eliminates beany flavour.
- **Wet grinding** — at 80–85 °C with water (1 : 8 ratio); disperses protein into colloidal suspension.
- **Filtration** — separates soymilk from okara using cloth/mesh.
- **Boiling** — sterilizes and develops flavour in soymilk beverage.
- **Coagulation** — adds CaSO₄ at 70–85 °C; forms soy curd.
- **Pressing** — removes whey, shapes curd into soy paneer.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Water absorbed during soaking:**

$$
W_{water} = W_{bean}\left(\dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{100 - M_2}\right) = 100 \times \left(\dfrac{60 - 10}{100 - 60}\right) = 100 \times \dfrac{50}{40}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{water} = 125 \text{ kg}}
$$

**(ii) Protein extraction efficiency:**

Dry bean mass $= 100 \times (1 - 0.10) = 90$ kg

Protein in beans $= 90 \times 0.38 = 34.2$ kg

Protein in soymilk $= 380 \times 0.032 = 12.16$ kg

$$
\eta_{prot} = \dfrac{12.16}{34.2} \times 100 = \boxed{35.6 \text{ \%}}
$$

*(Note: This lower-than-typical value suggests heavy losses — useful for examiner's discussion on grinding efficiency.)*

**(iii) Mass of okara:**

Total solids in beans $= 90$ kg

Solids going to okara $= 90 \times 0.45 = 40.5$ kg

Mass of okara (assuming it contains ~25 % moisture w.b.):
$$
W_{okara} = \dfrac{40.5}{1 - 0.25} = \boxed{54.0 \text{ kg}}
$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks each for sub-parts i, ii, iii + 1 mark for correct units and significant figures.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of blanching and hot grinding** in the Pantnagar Process. Why are these two steps considered **indispensable**? Mention the **temperature, time, and biochemical changes** involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Pantnagar soy paneer plant, **500 kg/h of soaked beans** (50 % moisture w.b., 35 % protein db) are processed. Soymilk obtained has **5 % total solids** with **40 % of the solids being protein**. Paneer obtained has **75 % moisture** and **18 % protein (w.b.)**. Calculate:
- (i) Mass flow rate of soymilk if bean solids are **80 % extracted**.
- (ii) Mass of paneer produced per hour.
- (iii) % protein recovery in paneer relative to total protein input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of Blanching & Hot Grinding (7 marks)**

**Blanching (3.5 marks):**
- Heat treatment at **95–100 °C for 15–20 min** in boiling water.
- **Biochemical changes:** (i) Denaturation of **trypsin inhibitors** (Kunitz and Bowman-Birk) — improves protein digestibility from ~50 % to >90 %. (ii) Inactivation of **lipoxygenase** enzyme — eliminates **beany off-flavour** by stopping oxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids. (iii) Softening of cell wall pectin — facilitates subsequent grinding.

**Hot Grinding (3.5 marks):**
- Done at **80–85 °C** (well above lipoxygenase re-activation threshold of 60 °C).
- **Functions:** (i) Disperses protein bodies into colloidal suspension forming soymilk. (ii) Maintains high temperature to **prevent re-formation** of beany flavour. (iii) Achieves **protein extraction efficiency of 70–85 %**.

**Why indispensable:** Without these two steps, the process yields a low-protein, off-flavoured, poorly digestible product — defeating the entire purpose of the Pantnagar technology.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Mass flow rate of soymilk:**

Dry solids in beans per hour $= 500 \times (1 - 0.50) = 250$ kg/h

Solids extracted into soymilk $= 250 \times 0.80 = 200$ kg/h

Since soymilk has 5 % total solids:
$$
\dot{m}_{milk} = \dfrac{200}{0.05} = \boxed{4{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

**(ii) Mass of paneer produced per hour:**

Protein in soymilk $= 200 \times 0.40 = 80$ kg/h (since 40 % of solids is protein)

Assume **60 %** of soymilk protein is retained in paneer (typical coagulation recovery):
Protein in paneer $= 80 \times 0.60 = 48$ kg/h

Paneer has 18 % protein (w.b.), so:
$$
\dot{m}_{paneer} = \dfrac{48}{0.18} = \boxed{266.7 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

**(iii) % Protein recovery in paneer:**

Total protein input in beans $= 250 \times 0.35 = 87.5$ kg/h

$$
\% \text{ recovery} = \dfrac{48}{87.5} \times 100 = \boxed{54.9 \text{ \%}}
$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks each for sub-parts + 1 mark for proper units & logic.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pantnagar Process = G.B. Pant University technology** for soybean → soymilk / soy paneer / soy flour.
- ✅ Developed by **Prof. Gurmukh Singh et al.** at Pantnagar, Uttarakhand.
- ✅ **Six core unit operations:** Cleaning → Soaking → Blanching → Grinding → Filtration → (Coagulation / Drying).
- ✅ **Soaking:** 30 °C, 8–12 h, moisture rises **10 % → 60 % w.b.**
- ✅ **Blanching:** 95–100 °C, 15–20 min → destroys **trypsin inhibitor & lipoxygenase**.
- ✅ **Hot grinding:** 80–85 °C, **1 : 6 to 1 : 10** water ratio.
- ✅ **Coagulant:** **CaSO₄ (2 %)** preferred at Pantnagar; curd at **70–85 °C**.
- ✅ **Protein extraction efficiency** target: **70–85 %** — key benchmark for examiner.
- ✅ **Okara** is the by-product (insoluble residue) — used as animal feed.
- ✅ **Mass balance formulas** $W_{water} = W_{bean}\left(\dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{100 - M_2}\right)$ and $\eta_{prot} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ are **must-memorize**.
- ✅ **Heat for blanching** calculated by $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ (use $c_p \approx 3.5$ kJ/kg·K for hydrated beans).
- ✅ **Use units everywhere**; always state **steady-state** assumption for mass balance.
- ✅ **Examiner trap:** Blanching is for **enzyme inactivation**, NOT sterilization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for ESE:**
> "**Soak–Blanch–Grind–Filter** to get **soy milk**; **Coagulate–Press** to get **soy paneer**; **Spray-dry** to get **soy flour**." — memorize this single sentence and you can write 14 marks confidently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CIAE Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CIAE Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **CIAE Method** refers to the standardized design methodology developed by the **Central Institute of Agricultural Engineering (CIAE), Bhopal**, India, for the structural and functional design of **grain storage bins and silos**. It is a unified Indian standard procedure that adapts Janssen's classical bin pressure theory to the specific physical and frictional properties of Indian food grains (wheat, paddy, rice, maize, sorghum, pearl millet, soybean, etc.), accounting for the unique bulk density, angle of internal friction, and wall friction coefficient of each commodity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Reference**
> The CIAE Method is essentially a **modified Janssen approach** calibrated with empirical pressure coefficients derived from full-scale silo tests conducted on Indian grain varieties. It governs the design of vertical walls, hoppers, roofs, foundations, and aeration systems for both **shallow and deep bins**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine stacking sugar in a tall cylindrical container with your hands inside a thin polythene liner:
- The bottom layer carries the **full weight** of the sugar above it (this is what simple gravity pressure $P_v = \rho g h$ would predict).
- However, in reality, a portion of that weight is **transferred to the sidewalls** through friction between the sugar particles and the container wall.
- So the bottom layer does NOT carry the full hydrostatic weight — most of it is "hanging" on the walls.

This wall-friction-supported loading is exactly what the **CIAE Method (based on Janssen's equation)** calculates. The "magic" is that the grain behaves like a **self-supporting mass** that partially unloads itself onto the bin walls through shear stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** A 30 m tall wheat silo does NOT exert 30 m of water-equivalent pressure at the bottom. Only about 10–15 m of equivalent pressure is realized. The CIAE Method gives the exact numbers for Indian grains.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Wheat) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk Density | $\gamma$ | **8.0 kN/m³ (≈ 800 kg/m³)** | kN/m³ |
| Angle of Internal Friction | $\phi$ | **25°** | degrees |
| Angle of Wall Friction | $\phi'$ | **22°** | degrees |
| Lateral Pressure Ratio (Rankine) | $K$ | **0.406** | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of Wall Friction | $\mu$ | **0.404** | dimensionless |
| Hopper Slope Angle | $\theta_h$ | **45° – 60°** | degrees |
| Typical Bin Height-to-Diameter Ratio | $H/D$ | **0.5 to 4.0** | dimensionless |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — The Janssen–CIAE Framework

The classical **Janssen Equation (1895)** assumed a single uniform bulk solid with a constant lateral pressure ratio. The **CIAE Method extends this** by:

1. Categorizing Indian grains into **four friction classes** (low, medium, high, very high).
2. Providing **commodity-specific charts** for vertical pressure, lateral pressure, and hopper pressure.
3. Recommending **safety factors** based on grain type and bin geometry.
4. Including **dynamic load multipliers** for filling/emptying cycles.

### 2.1.1 Why "Why" and "How" of Each Step

- **Why lateral pressure ratio K matters:** Grain does not behave like a fluid; it transfers load partially as horizontal thrust on walls. K quantifies this ratio.
- **How friction supports the load:** Vertical friction forces $\tau = \mu \cdot \sigma_h$ on the wall surface act upward, supporting a portion of the overburden weight.
- **Why deep vs. shallow distinction:** For $H/D < 0.5$, hydrostatic (Rankine) assumptions apply. For $H/D \geq 1.5$, Janssen behavior dominates. The CIAE Method provides **transition curves** between these regimes.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}$ | Rankine active lateral pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\mu = \tan\phi'$ | Coefficient of wall friction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $q = \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K}$ | Janssen characteristic pressure | kN/m² |
| 4 | $P_v = q \left(1 - e^{-h/q}\right)$ | Vertical pressure at depth $h$ | kN/m² |
| 5 | $P_h = K \cdot P_v$ | Horizontal (lateral) pressure on wall | kN/m² |
| 6 | $P_w = \mu \cdot P_h$ | Frictional pressure transferred to wall | kN/m² |
| 7 | $P_{v,hopper} = P_{v,top} + \gamma \cdot H_h$ | Vertical pressure at hopper apex | kN/m² |
| 8 | $P_{h,hopper} = \dfrac{P_v}{\cos^2\theta_h}$ | Lateral pressure on hopper slope | kN/m² |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{D}{2\mu K}$ | Characteristic bin radius (Janssen) | m |
| 10 | $F_{roof} = \gamma \cdot D / 4$ | Distributed load on bin roof (CIAE) | kN/m² |
| 11 | $V_{hopper} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{12} \cdot H_h$ | Hopper cone volume | m³ |
| 12 | $T_{wall} = \dfrac{P_h \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | Required wall thickness (cylindrical) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In KTU exams, always substitute numerical values for $K$, $\mu$, and $\gamma$ at the very first step. Examiners award partial credit for the substituted form, not the symbolic one.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Distribution System (PDS) Silos:** The Food Corporation of India (FCI) and Central Warehousing Corporation (CWC) use CIAE-designed silos for buffer stock of wheat and rice.
- **Farm-Level Storage:** CIAE designs are used by manufacturers of small-to-medium galvanized iron (GI) bins distributed through the **Ministry of Agriculture** schemes.
- **Export Terminals:** Indian port-side grain terminals (Kandla, Mundra, Vishakhapatnam) follow CIAE parameters for silo design.
- **Aeration System Sizing:** The pressure values from CIAE charts drive the fan and duct sizing for temperature/moisture control.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Janssen's Vertical Pressure Equation (Foundation of CIAE Method)

### 3.1.1 Free Body Diagram Analysis

Consider a horizontal slice of grain at depth $h$ inside a cylindrical bin of diameter $D$.

**Forces acting on the slice of thickness $dh$:**

- Weight of the slice (downward): $W = \gamma \cdot A \cdot dh$, where $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Vertical pressure from above (downward): $P_v \cdot A$
- Vertical pressure from below (upward): $(P_v + dP_v) \cdot A$
- Frictional support from wall (upward): $\tau \cdot \pi D \cdot dh$

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium Equation

Sum of vertical forces = 0:

$$
\gamma \cdot A \cdot dh + P_v \cdot A - (P_v + dP_v) \cdot A - \tau \cdot \pi D \cdot dh = 0
$$

Simplifying (the $P_v \cdot A$ terms cancel):

$$
\gamma \cdot A \cdot dh - dP_v \cdot A - \tau \cdot \pi D \cdot dh = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
dP_v = \gamma \cdot dh - \dfrac{\tau \cdot \pi D}{A} \cdot dh
$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting Geometric and Frictional Relations

Substitute $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, $\tau = \mu \cdot P_h$, and $P_h = K \cdot P_v$:

$$
dP_v = \gamma \cdot dh - \dfrac{\mu \cdot K \cdot P_v \cdot \pi D}{\pi D^2 / 4} \cdot dh
$$

$$
dP_v = \gamma \cdot dh - \dfrac{4 \mu K P_v}{D} \cdot dh
$$

### 3.1.4 Separation of Variables

$$
\dfrac{dP_v}{dh} = \gamma - \dfrac{4 \mu K}{D} \cdot P_v
$$

Let $a = \dfrac{4\mu K}{D}$ and rearrange to standard linear form:

$$
\dfrac{dP_v}{dh} + a \cdot P_v = \gamma
$$

### 3.1.5 Integrating with Boundary Condition

Apply $P_v(0) = 0$ at the top free surface. The integrating factor is $e^{ah}$:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dh}\left(P_v \cdot e^{ah}\right) = \gamma \cdot e^{ah}
$$

$$
P_v \cdot e^{ah} = \dfrac{\gamma}{a} \cdot e^{ah} + C
$$

Using $P_v(0) = 0$:

$$
0 = \dfrac{\gamma}{a} + C \implies C = -\dfrac{\gamma}{a}
$$

### 3.1.6 Final Janssen Equation

$$
P_v = \dfrac{\gamma}{a} \left(1 - e^{-ah}\right)
$$

Substituting back $a = \dfrac{4\mu K}{D}$:

$$
\boxed{P_v = \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h / D}\right)}
$$

This is the **fundamental vertical pressure equation** of the CIAE Method. The factor $\dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K}$ is the **characteristic pressure $q$** and represents the asymptotic maximum vertical pressure the bin can sustain regardless of height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** As $h \to \infty$, $P_v \to q$. This means that beyond a certain height (≈ 2.5 to 3 times the bin diameter), adding more grain does NOT increase the bottom pressure! This is a counter-intuitive but well-verified result.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wall Friction Force and Hopper Loads

The total wall friction force on a vertical wall of height $H$ is found by integrating:

$$
F_w = \int_0^H \mu \cdot P_h(h) \cdot \pi D \cdot dh
$$

Substituting $P_h = K P_v$ and the Janssen expression:

$$
F_w = \mu K \pi D \int_0^H \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K}\left(1 - e^{-4\mu K h/D}\right) dh
$$

$$
F_w = \dfrac{\gamma \pi D^2}{4} \left[H + \dfrac{D}{4\mu K}\left(e^{-4\mu K H/D} - 1\right)\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** This expression represents the **load the wall must support**. The vertical wall is essentially a load-bearing structural element, not just an enclosure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hopper (Conical) Pressure

For a conical hopper with half-angle $\alpha$ (where $\alpha = 90° - \theta_h$), the vertical pressure at the apex is:

$$
P_{v,apex} = P_{v,top} + \gamma \cdot H_h
$$

where $H_h$ is the vertical height of the hopper cone. The lateral pressure on the inclined hopper surface is found by resolving the principal stress (assumed vertical) onto the inclined plane:

$$
P_{h,hopper} = \dfrac{P_{v,apex}}{\cos^2\theta_h}
$$

### 3.3.1 Numerical Worked Example (CIAE Design of Wheat Silo)

**Given:** Bin diameter $D = 6$ m, Wheat bulk density $\gamma = 8.0$ kN/m³, $K = 0.406$, $\mu = 0.404$, Vertical wall height $H = 18$ m, Hopper height $H_h = 3$ m, Hopper slope angle $\theta_h = 45°$.

**Step 1: Characteristic pressure $q$**

$$
q = \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 6}{4 \times 0.404 \times 0.406} = \dfrac{48.0}{0.6564} \approx 73.13 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Vertical pressure at $h = 18$ m (bottom of vertical wall)**

$$
P_v = 73.13 \left(1 - e^{-18/73.13}\right) = 73.13 \left(1 - e^{-0.2461}\right)
$$

$$
P_v = 73.13 \left(1 - 0.7819\right) = 73.13 \times 0.2181 \approx 15.95 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Lateral pressure on wall**

$$
P_h = K \cdot P_v = 0.406 \times 15.95 \approx 6.48 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Friction force per unit wall area (vertical)**

$$
P_w = \mu \cdot P_h = 0.404 \times 6.48 \approx 2.62 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Vertical pressure at hopper apex (top of hopper)**

$$
P_{v,top} = P_v(18) = 15.95 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Vertical pressure at hopper bottom (apex)**

$$
P_{v,apex} = 15.95 + 8.0 \times 3 = 15.95 + 24.0 = 39.95 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 7: Lateral pressure on hopper slope**

$$
P_{h,hopper} = \dfrac{39.95}{\cos^2 45°} = \dfrac{39.95}{0.5} = 79.90 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Note:** The hopper lateral pressure (≈80 kN/m²) is **more than 12 times the wall lateral pressure** (≈6.5 kN/m²). This is why hopper bottoms fail more frequently than cylindrical walls and require special reinforcement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Pressure Regimes

| Depth $h$ (m) | $P_v$ (kN/m²) | $P_h$ (kN/m²) | Hydrostatic Equivalent | Janssen Reduction (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0% |
| 3 | 2.93 | 1.19 | 24.00 | 88% |
| 6 | 5.62 | 2.28 | 48.00 | 88% |
| 9 | 8.10 | 3.29 | 72.00 | 89% |
| 12 | 10.39 | 4.22 | 96.00 | 89% |
| 15 | 12.50 | 5.08 | 120.00 | 90% |
| 18 | 14.46 | 5.87 | 144.00 | 90% |
| $\infty$ (asymptote) | 73.13 | 29.69 | $\infty$ | 100% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice the ~90% reduction factor throughout. This is the central engineering message of the CIAE Method — never use hydrostatic pressure for grain bin design. Always use Janssen/CIAE equations.
