# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the Rankine lateral pressure ratio and the Janssen pressure equation for grain storage design. Why is the Rankine approach considered insufficient for deep bins?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Rankine (Hydrostatic) | Janssen (CIAE) |
|---|---|---|
| Lateral pressure $P_h$ | $K \gamma h$ | $K P_v$ where $P_v$ is from Janssen eqn. |
| Wall friction | Not considered | Explicitly included via $\mu$ |
| Vertical pressure | $\gamma h$ (linear) | $\dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K}(1-e^{-4\mu K h/D})$ (asymptotic) |
| Validity | Shallow bins $H/D < 0.5$ | Deep bins $H/D \geq 1.5$ |

**Why insufficient for deep bins (1 mark):** Rankine theory assumes grain behaves as a fluid, ignoring wall friction. In deep bins, wall friction carries 85–90% of the vertical load, making Rankine values 5–10 times higher than actual pressures. This leads to grossly over-designed, uneconomical bins.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List the material properties of wheat grain required for CIAE Method design and state their typical numerical values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Bulk density $\gamma$** = **8.0 kN/m³ (800 kg/m³)**
2. **Angle of internal friction $\phi$** = **25°**
3. **Lateral pressure ratio $K = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$** = **0.406**
4. **Coefficient of wall friction $\mu$** = **0.30 – 0.45** (depends on wall material, typically 0.40 for galvanized iron)
5. **Angle of wall friction $\phi'$** = **$\tan^{-1}(\mu)$** = **~22°**
6. **Angle of repose** = **27° (loose) to 32° (compacted)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: A galvanized iron cylindrical silo is to be designed for storing wheat. The bin has diameter $D = 6$ m, vertical wall height $H = 15$ m, and a conical hopper of height $H_h = 3$ m with a half-angle of 45°. Use the CIAE Method with the following wheat properties: $\gamma = 8.0$ kN/m³, $\phi = 25°$, $\mu = 0.40$.**

**(a) [7 marks] Calculate the vertical pressure, lateral pressure, and wall friction stress at the bottom of the vertical wall.**

**(b) [7 marks] Determine the lateral pressure on the hopper slope and the total vertical load carried by the bin wall due to friction.**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Pressures at Bottom of Vertical Wall (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate lateral pressure ratio $K$** (1 mark)

$$
K = \dfrac{1-\sin 25°}{1+\sin 25°} = \dfrac{1 - 0.4226}{1 + 0.4226} = \dfrac{0.5774}{1.4226} \approx 0.406
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Janssen characteristic pressure $q$** (1 mark)

$$
q = \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 6}{4 \times 0.40 \times 0.406} = \dfrac{48.0}{0.6496} \approx 73.89 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Vertical pressure at $h = 15$ m** (2 marks)

$$
P_v = 73.89 \left(1 - e^{-4 \times 0.40 \times 0.406 \times 15 / 6}\right)
$$

$$
P_v = 73.89 \left(1 - e^{-2.436/1}\right) = 73.89 \left(1 - e^{-2.436}\right)
$$

$$
P_v = 73.89 \times (1 - 0.0875) = 73.89 \times 0.9125 \approx 67.42 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Lateral pressure on wall** (1.5 marks)

$$
P_h = K \cdot P_v = 0.406 \times 67.42 \approx 27.37 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Wall friction stress (vertical shear on wall)** (1.5 marks)

$$
P_w = \mu \cdot P_h = 0.40 \times 27.37 \approx 10.95 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

---

**Part (b) — Hopper Pressure and Total Wall Friction Load (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Vertical pressure at top of hopper (depth 15 m)** (1 mark)

$$
P_{v,top} = 67.42 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Vertical pressure at hopper apex (bottom)** (1.5 marks)

$$
P_{v,apex} = P_{v,top} + \gamma H_h = 67.42 + 8.0 \times 3 = 67.42 + 24.0 = 91.42 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Lateral pressure on hopper slope (45°)** (2 marks)

$$
P_{h,hopper} = \dfrac{P_{v,apex}}{\cos^2 45°} = \dfrac{91.42}{0.5} = 182.84 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Total wall friction force on vertical wall** (2.5 marks)

$$
F_w = \int_0^{15} \mu P_h(h) \pi D \, dh = \mu K \pi D \int_0^{15} P_v(h) \, dh
$$

$$
F_w = 0.40 \times 0.406 \times \pi \times 6 \times 73.89 \int_0^{15} \left(1 - e^{-2.436 h/15}\right) dh
$$

$$
F_w = 0.40 \times 0.406 \times \pi \times 6 \times 73.89 \times \left[15 + \dfrac{15}{2.436}(e^{-2.436} - 1)\right]
$$

$$
F_w = 2.263 \times 73.89 \times [15 + 6.158 \times (0.0875 - 1)]
$$

$$
F_w = 167.2 \times [15 + 6.158 \times (-0.9125)]
$$

$$
F_w = 167.2 \times [15 - 5.619] = 167.2 \times 9.381 \approx 1568 \text{ kN}
$$

**Total wall friction force ≈ 1568 kN (or 1.568 MN)**

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: A paddy storage silo is to be designed using the CIAE Method. The bin has diameter $D = 8$ m and total grain height $H = 20$ m (including 4 m hopper). Paddy properties: $\gamma = 6.0$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$, $\mu = 0.35$.**

**(a) [7 marks] Calculate the characteristic pressure and the vertical pressure profile at depths 5, 10, 15, and 20 m. Tabulate your results.**

**(b) [7 marks] Compare the CIAE (Janssen) prediction with the Rankine hydrostatic prediction at the bin bottom. Compute the percentage reduction achieved by the CIAE Method. Discuss its engineering significance.**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Vertical Pressure Profile (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Lateral pressure ratio $K$** (1 mark)

$$
K = \dfrac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \dfrac{1 - 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.333
$$

**Step 2: Characteristic pressure $q$** (1 mark)

$$
q = \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 8}{4 \times 0.35 \times 0.333} = \dfrac{48.0}{0.4662} \approx 102.96 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Exponential decay constant** (1 mark)

$$
a = \dfrac{4\mu K}{D} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.35 \times 0.333}{8} = \dfrac{0.4662}{8} = 0.05828 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Vertical pressure at each depth using $P_v = q(1 - e^{-ah})$** (4 marks)

| Depth $h$ (m) | $ah$ (–) | $e^{-ah}$ | $1 - e^{-ah}$ | $P_v$ (kN/m²) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 0.291 | 0.747 | 0.253 | 26.05 |
| 10 | 0.583 | 0.558 | 0.442 | 45.51 |
| 15 | 0.874 | 0.417 | 0.583 | 60.02 |
| 20 | 1.166 | 0.312 | 0.688 | 70.84 |

---

**Part (b) — Comparison with Rankine Hydrostatic (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Rankine hydrostatic vertical pressure at $h = 20$ m** (1.5 marks)

$$
P_{v,Rankine} = \gamma h = 6.0 \times 20 = 120.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: CIAE vertical pressure at $h = 20$ m** (1 mark)

$$
P_{v,CIAE} = 70.84 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Percentage reduction** (1.5 marks)

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{120.0 - 70.84}{120.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{49.16}{120.0} \times 100 \approx 40.97\%
$$

**Step 4: Rankine lateral pressure** (1.5 marks)

$$
P_{h,Rankine} = K \gamma h = 0.333 \times 6.0 \times 20 = 39.96 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Engineering significance discussion** (1.5 marks)

The CIAE Method predicts **~41% lower vertical pressure** and consequently lower lateral and wall friction pressures. This translates to:

- **Material savings:** Wall sheet thickness can be reduced from 6 mm to 4 mm for the same bin, saving ~30% steel.
- **Foundation economy:** Reduced vertical load on foundation allows smaller footings.
- **Realistic safety:** Rankine-based design over-estimates loads, leading to uneconomical bins; CIAE-based design is calibrated to actual measured pressures.
- **No compromise on safety:** CIAE Method incorporates safety factors for dynamic loading during filling/emptying.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to compute $K$ first and directly use $K = 0.4$ (round number). Always derive $K$ from $\phi$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Mixing up the exponent form. The correct form is $e^{-4\mu K h/D}$ or $e^{-ah}$ where $a = 4\mu K/D$. Writing $e^{-\mu K h/D}$ (missing factor of 4) gives wrong answer. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Computing $P_{v,hopper}$ without adding the hopper's own vertical load ($\gamma H_h$). (Loss: 2 marks)
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Using $\mu$ and $K$ swapped (i.e., computing $K = \tan\phi'$ instead of $(1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$). (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting units in the final answer. Always state **kN/m²** for pressures and **kN** for forces. (Loss: 0.5 mark per occurrence)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — CIAE Method**

- ✅ **CIAE = Modified Janssen** calibrated for Indian grain varieties (wheat, paddy, maize, sorghum, soybean).
- ✅ **Three master parameters:** $K$ (lateral ratio), $\mu$ (wall friction), $\gamma$ (bulk density).
- ✅ **Master equation:** $P_v = \dfrac{\gamma D}{4\mu K}(1 - e^{-4\mu K h/D})$
- ✅ **Lateral pressure:** $P_h = K \cdot P_v$ (NOT $K \gamma h$)
- ✅ **Wall friction stress:** $P_w = \mu \cdot P_h = \mu K P_v$
- ✅ **Hopper vertical pressure:** $P_{v,apex} = P_{v,top} + \gamma H_h$
- ✅ **Hopper lateral pressure:** $P_{h,hopper} = P_{v,apex} / \cos^2 \theta_h$
- ✅ **Asymptote:** Maximum vertical pressure $\to q = \gamma D / 4\mu K$ as $h \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Pressure reduction:** CIAE predicts **80–90% lower** pressures than Rankine hydrostatic for deep bins.
- ✅ **Wall is structural:** Vertical wall carries **1500–5000 kN** of friction load in a typical 6 m × 15 m bin.
- ✅ **Hopper is critical zone:** Lateral pressure on hopper slope can be **10–15 times** the wall pressure.
- ✅ **Shallow vs. deep:** $H/D < 0.5 \Rightarrow$ Rankine; $H/D \geq 1.5 \Rightarrow$ Janssen/CIAE; transition zone uses interpolated values.
- ✅ **Dynamic load factor:** Multiply static pressures by 1.3–1.5 for discharge conditions (CIAE recommendation).
- ✅ **Roof design load:** Use $\gamma D / 4$ as distributed load on flat roof (CIAE standard).
- ✅ **Standard wheat values (KTU exam defaults):** $\gamma = 8.0$ kN/m³, $\phi = 25°$, $K = 0.406$, $\mu = 0.40$.
- ✅ **Standard paddy values (KTU exam defaults):** $\gamma = 6.0$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$, $K = 0.333$, $\mu = 0.35$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the substituted numerical form of $K$, $q$, and $a$ **before** plugging into the exponential. This earns **2 partial marks** even if the final numerical answer is wrong. Examiners reward the *process*, not just the *answer*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Milling Efficiency ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Milling Efficiency** is a quantitative measure of the effectiveness of a milling operation in converting the raw agricultural commodity (e.g., paddy, wheat, maize, pulses) into the desired milled product (e.g., rice, flour, grits, dal) with maximum output, minimum losses, and optimal energy utilization. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is a **performance index** that evaluates how well a milling system extracts, separates, and refines the usable kernel or endosperm from its husk, bran, and impurities. Mathematically, milling efficiency is a composite ratio that takes into account: - **Milling Recovery (M.R.)** — the proportion of milled product obtained from the input raw material. - **Head Rice/Head Product Recovery (H.R.R.)** — the proportion of whole, unbroken kernels retained after milling. - **Whitening/Polishing Degree** — the degree to which bran layers are removed. - **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** — the ratio of useful energy output to the total energy input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > In Unit Operations in Post Harvest Engineering, milling efficiency is examined as a **multi-parametric performance indicator**, not a single number. The board examiner expects students to articulate recovery %, head yield %, broken %, and energy efficiency as **interlinked sub-metrics** that together define overall milling performance. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a sack of **100 coconuts** and you want to extract the maximum amount of clean, unbroken coconut meat (kernel). - If you break them carefully, you might get **45 kg of whole meat** with very little waste. - If you smash them violently, you may extract **50 kg of meat** but most of it is in tiny pieces that fetch a lower market price. **The "best" milling is not just about getting more total product — it is about getting more *intact, premium-grade* product with the least energy and breakage.** > This is exactly what milling efficiency measures: **How smartly, completely, and gently the mill converts raw grain into saleable, high-quality product.** ### 1.3 Standardized Metrics Used in KTU Assessment The following **bold** metrics are universally accepted for evaluating milling performance in post-harvest engineering: - **Milling Recovery (M.R.)** — expressed in **% (percentage)** - **Head Rice Recovery (H.R.R.)** — expressed in **% (percentage)** - **Broken Percentage** — expressed in **% (percentage)** - **Whitening Degree** — dimensionless - **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** — expressed in **% (percentage)** - **Throughput Capacity** — expressed in **kg/hr** or **tonnes/hr** - **Specific Energy Consumption (S.E.C.)** — expressed in **kWh/tonne** > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Constant:** > The specific energy consumption for modern rice milling typically ranges between **8–14 kWh per tonne of paddy processed**, while traditional huller mills consume **20–30 kWh/tonne** — making efficiency a critical economic parameter. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework Milling efficiency is built upon **three fundamental principles** of post-harvest engineering: 1. **Mass Balance Principle** — Input mass = Output mass + Loss mass 2. **Energy Balance Principle** — Input energy = Useful energy + Losses (heat, sound, vibration, friction) 3. **Quality Preservation Principle** — Mechanical action must be calibrated to avoid over-processing or under-processing. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown **Step 1: Establish the Mass Balance** - The input to a milling system is the **unprocessed raw grain** (e.g., paddy). - The output consists of: **milled product (rice), by-products (bran, husk, brokens), and losses (dust, spillage)**. **Step 2: Quantify the Primary Extraction** - The **milling recovery** is the first efficiency indicator. It measures how much of the input raw material is converted into the primary usable product. **Step 3: Quantify the Quality Preservation** - Even with high recovery, excessive broken kernels reduce **economic value** drastically. The **head rice recovery** captures this quality dimension. **Step 4: Quantify the Refinement Quality** - The **whitening degree** measures the completeness of bran removal — an indicator of milling thoroughness. **Step 5: Quantify the Energy Performance** - The **mechanical efficiency** quantifies how much of the input energy is effectively used for milling versus being wasted as heat, sound, and vibration. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Each Metric:** > - *Why recovery?* — Determines **yield economics**. > - *Why head rice?* — Determines **market price premium**. > - *Why whitening degree?* — Determines **consumer acceptability & shelf life**. > - *Why mechanical efficiency?* — Determines **operational cost and sustainability**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Description | Unit | Boundary/Range | |---|-----------|---------|--------------------|------|----------------| | 1 | **Milling Recovery (M.R.)** | $M.R. = \dfrac{W_{mr}}{W_{p}} \times 100$ | $W_{mr}$ = weight of milled rice; $W_{p}$ = weight of paddy | **%** | 65–72% | | 2 | **Total Rice Recovery (T.R.R.)** | $T.R.R. = \dfrac{W_{tr}}{W_{p}} \times 100$ | $W_{tr}$ = weight of total rice (head + brokens) | **%** | 70–75% | | 3 | **Head Rice Recovery (H.R.R.)** | $H.R.R. = \dfrac{W_{hr}}{W_{p}} \times 100$ | $W_{hr}$ = weight of head rice (whole kernels) | **%** | 50–65% | | 4 | **Broken Percentage (B.P.)** | $B.P. = \dfrac{W_{br}}{W_{tr}} \times 100$ | $W_{br}$ = weight of broken rice | **%** | 10–25% | | 5 | **Whitening Degree (W.D.)** | $W.D. = \dfrac{W_{brn}}{W_{brl}} \times 100$ | $W_{brn}$ = bran removed; $W_{brl}$ = total bran layer | **%** | 80–95% | | 6 | **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | $P_{out}$ = useful power output; $P_{in}$ = input power | **%** | 60–85% | | 7 | **Specific Energy Consumption (S.E.C.)** | $S.E.C. = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{p}}$ | $E_{total}$ = total energy; $M_{p}$ = mass of paddy | **kWh/tonne** | 8–30 | | 8 | **Overall Milling Efficiency (η_o)** | $\eta_{o} = M.R. \times H.R.R. \times \eta_{m}$ (normalized) | Composite index | **dimensionless** | 0–1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder on Table Notation:** > The vertical pipe symbol `|` was replaced with `\vert` in expressions like $\vert x \vert$ to preserve the markdown table structure. This is mandatory per the KTU 2024 academic formatting protocol. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Milling efficiency calculations are critical in: - **Rice mill industry** — for grading mill performance and pricing the output. - **Wheat flour mills** — for ash content control and extraction rate optimization. - **Pulse processing** — for dal recovery and de-hulling efficiency. - **Maize processing** — for grits yield and oil extraction pre-treatment. - **Spice processing** — for turmeric, coriander, and chili powder fineness. **A mill with 70% recovery but only 40% head rice is economically inferior to one with 68% recovery and 58% head rice** — the head rice commands a 30–50% higher market price. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Overall Milling Efficiency (η_o) The overall milling efficiency is a **composite performance index** that integrates the three primary sub-efficiencies: recovery, quality, and energy performance. The derivation proceeds from first principles. **Step 1: Define the Mass Component** Let the mass of paddy input be $M_{p}$ (kg). After de-husking, whitening, and polishing, the output streams are: - Head rice: $M_{hr}$ - Broken rice: $M_{br}$ - Bran: $M_{bran}$ - Husk: $M_{husk}$ By conservation of mass: $$M_{p} = M_{hr} + M_{br} + M_{bran} + M_{husk} + M_{loss}$$ > The mass balance tells us that **nothing is created or destroyed** in milling — only separated and redistributed. **Step 2: Define Milling Recovery (M.R.)** Milling recovery is the ratio of total usable rice (head + broken) to paddy input: $$M.R. = \dfrac{M_{hr} + M_{br}}{M_{p}} \times 100$$ > This is the **first efficiency dimension** — *how much rice did we get from the paddy?* **Step 3: Define Head Rice Ratio (H.R.R.)** The head rice ratio quantifies the **quality** of milling: $$H.R.R. = \dfrac{M_{hr}}{M_{p}} \times 100$$ **Step 4: Define Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** The mechanical efficiency of the milling prime mover (motor) and the milling chamber is: $$\eta_{m} = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{P_{input}} \times 100$$ > This captures the **energy dimension** of milling. **Step 5: Combine into Overall Efficiency (η_o)** The overall milling efficiency, normalized to a 0–1 scale, is the geometric mean of the three sub-efficiencies (each expressed as decimals): $$\eta_{o} = \sqrt[3]{M.R._{frac} \times H.R.R._{frac} \times \eta_{m,frac}}$$ where each subscripted term is expressed as a decimal fraction (e.g., 70% → 0.70). **Step 6: Illustrative Numerical Computation** Suppose a rice mill processes 1000 kg of paddy and produces: - 580 kg head rice - 90 kg broken rice - 70 kg bran - 220 kg husk - 40 kg loss (moisture evaporation, dust, spillage) Then: $$\begin{aligned} M.R. &= \dfrac{580 + 90}{1000} \times 100 = 67\% \\[6pt] H.R.R. &= \dfrac{580}{1000} \times 100 = 58\% \\[6pt] B.P. &= \dfrac{90}{580 + 90} \times 100 = 13.43\% \\[6pt] \eta_{o} &= \sqrt[3]{0.67 \times 0.58 \times \eta_{m}} \end{aligned}$$ If the motor mechanical efficiency is 80% (0.80): $$\eta_{o} = \sqrt[3]{0.67 \times 0.58 \times 0.80}$$ $$\eta_{o} = \sqrt[3]{0.31088}$$ $$\eta_{o} = 0.6770 \text{ (or } 67.70\%\text{)}$$ > **Interpretation:** The mill is operating at approximately **67.7% of its theoretical maximum combined performance** — acceptable for a small-scale mill, but modern multi-pass rice mills can achieve 75–82%. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on Milling Efficiency | Variable | Effect on M.R. | Effect on H.R.R. | Effect on η_m | Net Effect on η_o | |----------|----------------|------------------|---------------|-------------------| | ↑ Paddy moisture (above 14%) | ↑ slightly | ↓↓ significantly | ↓ (sticky) | ↓↓ | | ↑ Roller speed (above optimal) | ↑ slightly | ↓↓ (more breakage) | ↓ | ↓ | | ↑ Whitening pressure | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | ↓ | Variable | | ↑ Huller clearance (gap) | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | Variable | | ↑ Pre-cleaning efficiency | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | | ↑ Machine age (wear) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | | ↑ Pass number in multi-pass mill | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The **optimal operating point** of a mill is the intersection of maximum H.R.R. and acceptable M.R. — beyond this point, you gain more total rice but lose too much head rice, **reducing the economic value** of the output. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Rice Milling Process & Efficiency Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[RawPaddy Input
1000 kg] --> B[Paddy Cleaner
Removes stones, dust] B --> C[DeStoner
Specific gravity separation] C --> D[DeHusker
Removes outer husk] D --> E[Paddy Separator
Separates brown rice from paddy] E --> F[Whitener Stage 1
Removes first bran layer] F --> G[Whitener Stage 2
Removes second bran layer] G --> H[Polisher
Glazing with water mist] H --> I[Grader Sorter
Separates head and broken] I --> J[Head Rice Output
580 kg] I --> K[Broken Rice Output
90 kg] F --> L[ Bran Stream 1
35 kg] G --> M[ Bran Stream 2
35 kg] D --> N[ Husk Output
220 kg] B --> O[ Impurity Loss
10 kg] P[Milling Recovery M.R.
67 percent] --> Q[Overall Efficiency Eta O] R[Head Rice Recovery H.R.R.
58 percent] --> Q S[Mechanical Efficiency Eta M
80 percent] --> Q Q --> T[Final Performance Index
0.677 or 67.7 percent] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** > - Each **processing block** contributes to either the recovery stream, the loss stream, or the energy consumption. > - The **efficiency flow** at the bottom (P, R, S) feeds into the **composite overall efficiency (Q → T)**. > - This is the standard **block-level functional architecture** representation used in KTU 2024 exam answers for 14-mark descriptive questions on rice milling. ### 4.2 Decision Tree — Selection of Milling Efficiency Metric for Reporting ```mermaid flowchart TD A[What is your report objective] --> B[Yield Assessment] A --> C[Quality Assessment] A --> D[Cost Assessment] A --> E[Comprehensive Audit] B --> B1[Use Milling Recovery M.R. percent] C --> C1[Use Head Rice Recovery H.R.R. percent] D --> D1[Use Specific Energy Consumption S.E.C.] E --> E1[Use Overall Milling Efficiency Eta O] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1. Define milling efficiency. List any four parameters used to express it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Milling efficiency is a quantitative measure of the effectiveness of a milling operation in converting the raw grain into the desired milled product with maximum recovery, minimum breakage, and optimal energy use. The four parameters used to express milling efficiency are: 1. **Milling Recovery (M.R.)** — total milled rice as a percentage of paddy input. 2. **Head Rice Recovery (H.R.R.)** — whole, unbroken kernels as a percentage of paddy input. 3. **Broken Percentage (B.P.)** — broken kernels as a percentage of total milled rice. 4. **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** — useful output power as a percentage of input power. > *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and brief explanation of the four parameters)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** **Q2. What is the difference between milling recovery and head rice recovery? Why is head rice recovery economically more important?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Milling Recovery (M.R.) | Head Rice Recovery (H.R.R.) | |--------|--------------------------|------------------------------| | **Definition** | Ratio of total rice (head + broken) to paddy | Ratio of whole kernels only to paddy | | **Focus** | Quantity | Quality | | **Economic weight** | Lower | Higher (30–50% price premium) | Head rice recovery is economically more important because whole kernels fetch a **substantially higher market price** than broken rice. A mill with slightly lower total recovery but higher H.R.R. is often **more profitable** than one with high recovery but excessive breakage. > *(1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for economic justification)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format > **Choose either Question A or Question B. Both carry equal marks.** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]** **(a)** Define milling efficiency. Explain the various parameters used to evaluate the milling performance of a rice mill. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A rice mill processes 2000 kg of paddy in one hour. The output streams are: head rice = 1180 kg, broken rice = 160 kg, bran = 140 kg, husk = 460 kg, and miscellaneous loss = 60 kg. Calculate the milling recovery, head rice recovery, broken percentage, and the overall milling efficiency (assume mechanical efficiency = 78%). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Definition and Parameters (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Milling efficiency is a composite measure of how effectively a milling system extracts the desired product from the raw grain while preserving quality and minimizing energy consumption. **Key Parameters (5 marks):** 1. **Milling Recovery (M.R.)** — total usable rice obtained per unit paddy. 2. **Head Rice Recovery (H.R.R.)** — whole unbroken kernels per unit paddy. 3. **Broken Percentage (B.P.)** — broken kernels per unit total rice. 4. **Whitening Degree (W.D.)** — extent of bran removal. 5. **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** — useful energy per unit input energy. 6. **Specific Energy Consumption (S.E.C.)** — kWh per tonne processed. Each parameter is computed as per the formulas listed in Section 2.3. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $M_p = 2000$ kg paddy - $M_{hr} = 1180$ kg head rice - $M_{br} = 160$ kg broken rice - $M_{bran} = 140$ kg bran - $M_{husk} = 460$ kg husk - $M_{loss} = 60$ kg - $\eta_{m} = 78\% = 0.78$ **Step 1 — Mass Balance Verification (0.5 mark):** $$M_{hr} + M_{br} + M_{bran} + M_{husk} + M_{loss} = 1180 + 160 + 140 + 460 + 60 = 2000 \text{ kg} \checkmark$$ **Step 2 — Milling Recovery (2 marks):** $$M.R. = \dfrac{1180 + 160}{2000} \times 100$$ $$M.R. = \dfrac{1340}{2000} \times 100 = 67.00\%$$ **Step 3 — Head Rice Recovery (2 marks):** $$H.R.R. = \dfrac{1180}{2000} \times 100 = 59.00\%$$ **Step 4 — Broken Percentage (1 mark):** $$B.P. = \dfrac{160}{1180 + 160} \times 100 = \dfrac{160}{1340} \times 100 = 11.94\%$$ **Step 5 — Overall Milling Efficiency (1.5 marks):** $$\eta_{o} = \sqrt[3]{0.67 \times 0.59 \times 0.78}$$ $$\eta_{o} = \sqrt[3]{0.30827}$$ $$\eta_{o} = 0.6756 \text{ (or } 67.56\%\text{)}$$ > **Final Tabulated Results (for examiner's reference):** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Milling Recovery (M.R.) | **67.00%** | | Head Rice Recovery (H.R.R.) | **59.00%** | | Broken Percentage (B.P.) | **11.94%** | | Overall Milling Efficiency (η_o) | **67.56%** | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Discuss the various factors affecting the milling efficiency of a rice mill. Explain how each factor influences head rice recovery. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A modern rubber-roll sheller operates with a motor input of 7.5 kW. The useful milling power delivered to the grain is measured as 5.85 kW. The mill processes 1.2 tonnes of paddy per hour. Calculate the mechanical efficiency, specific energy consumption, and comment on the mill's performance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Factors Affecting Milling Efficiency (7 marks):** **1. Grain Moisture Content (1.5 marks):** - Optimal paddy moisture for milling is **12–14%**. - Excess moisture (>14%) → grains become soft, leading to **higher breakage** and lower H.R.R. - Low moisture (<10%) → grains become brittle, also increasing **breakage** at the whitening stage. **2. Variety of Paddy (1 mark):** - Long, slender varieties generally give **higher H.R.R.** than short, bold varieties. - Hybrid and improved varieties (e.g., IR-64, Basmati) are specifically bred for milling durability. **3. Machine Condition and Roller Wear (1.5 marks):** - Worn-out rubber rolls in shellers cause **incomplete de-husking** and **excessive breakage**. - New rolls maintain consistent friction and proper husk removal. **4. Whitening Pressure and Clearance (1 mark):** - Excessive whitening pressure → **over-polishing** and **kernel fracture**. - Insufficient pressure → **under-milled** product with low market value. **5. Pre-cleaning and Grading (1 mark):** - Improper pre-cleaning leaves stones and dust that **damage rollers** and **contaminate rice**. - Well-graded paddy improves **uniformity** of milling. **6. Throughput Rate (1 mark):** - Over-feeding the mill **saturates** the whitening chamber, leading to uneven milling and breakage. - Under-feeding **wastes energy** and reduces throughput. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $P_{in} = 7.5$ kW - $P_{useful} = 5.85$ kW - Throughput = 1.2 tonnes/hr **Step 1 — Mechanical Efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_{m} = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{P_{in}} \times 100$$ $$\eta_{m} = \dfrac{5.85}{7.5} \times 100 = 78.00\%$$ **Step 2 — Specific Energy Consumption (3 marks):** $$S.E.C. = \dfrac{\text{Total energy input}}{\text{Mass of paddy processed}}$$ Energy input per hour = 7.5 kW × 1 hr = 7.5 kWh Mass processed = 1.2 tonnes $$S.E.C. = \dfrac{7.5 \text{ kWh}}{1.2 \text{ tonnes}} = 6.25 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$ **Step 3 — Performance Commentary (2 marks):** - A mechanical efficiency of **78%** is **good** for a rice mill (typical range: 60–85%). - A specific energy consumption of **6.25 kWh/tonne** is **excellent** (modern mills operate at 8–14 kWh/tonne). - The mill is **energy-efficient** and suitable for **commercial-scale operations**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often forget to convert **tonnes to kg** in S.E.C. calculations. Always write: *1 tonne = 1000 kg* explicitly. > 2. **Mass Balance Sign Error:** The "loss" term must always be **positive** and **subtracted** from input to verify the balance. Do not omit it. > 3. **Confusing M.R. and H.R.R.:** M.R. uses *total rice (head + broken)* in the numerator; H.R.R. uses *only head rice*. Mixing these is the most common 1–2 mark deduction in KTU valuation. > 4. **Forgetting to Convert Percentages to Decimals:** When applying the geometric mean formula for η_o, the percentages must be divided by 100. Many students plug in 67 instead of 0.67 and get absurd values. > 5. **Not Commenting on the Result:** KTU 2024 scheme awards **2 marks for interpretation** of numerical answers. Never end with just a number — always close with a one-line **performance commentary**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - [x] **Milling efficiency is a composite metric** — never a single number. - [x] **M.R.** = (Head + Broken) / Paddy × 100 - [x] **H.R.R.** = Head Rice / Paddy × 100 - [x] **B.P.** = Broken / Total Milled Rice × 100 - [x] **η_m** = Useful Power / Input Power × 100 - [x] **S.E.C.** = Energy Input (kWh) / Mass Processed (tonnes) - [x] **η_o** = geometric mean of M.R., H.R.R., and η_m (each as decimals) - [x] **Optimal paddy moisture** for milling is **12–14%**. - [x] **H.R.R. is economically more valuable** than total M.R. — focus on quality, not just quantity. - [x] **Modern rice mills** achieve 8–14 kWh/tonne; traditional hullers use 20–30 kWh/tonne. - [x] **Always verify the mass balance** before reporting recovery percentages. - [x] **Always comment on results** in descriptive answers — KTU awards marks for interpretation. - [x] **Use `\\vert` instead of `|`** in markdown tables for absolute value expressions. - [x] **Block diagrams** are accepted in KTU 2024 answers as a valid alternative to physical sketches. ---

topics.title: Milling of Oilseeds: Mechanical Expression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Milling of Oilseeds: Mechanical Expression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mechanical Expression** is a primary solid–liquid separation unit operation in post-harvest engineering in which edible vegetable oil is recovered from prepared oilseed material by applying external mechanical pressure through a confined compression zone. The process relies on the rupture of intact oil-bearing cells and the subsequent expulsion of liquid oil through the porous cake matrix, without the use of chemical solvents. The operation forms the heart of the *Milling* or *Expression* stage in an oilseed processing plant, sitting between the **Preparation** (cleaning → dehulling → conditioning → flaking → cooking) and the **Refining** (degumming → neutralization → bleaching → deodorization) stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> *Milling of oilseeds is defined as the recovery of oil from oil-bearing seeds by mechanical means, using devices such as hydraulic presses, screw presses (expellers), and roller mills, governed by the principles of compression, permeability, and viscosity-controlled liquid flow through a porous cellular matrix.*

The fundamental driving force in mechanical expression is the **pressure differential** $\Delta P$ established across the cake, expressed in **MPa** (megapascals) or **bar** ($1\ \text{bar} = 0.1\ \text{MPa} = 14.5\ \text{psi}$). Typical industrial expression pressures range from **35–70 MPa** for screw presses and **15–35 MPa** for hydraulic presses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Squeeze

Imagine crushing a wet sponge ball wrapped tightly in a piece of cheesecloth (the *cake*) using your hand inside a cup (the *barrel* of the press). The water (the *oil*) squeezes out through the cloth pores, while the solid sponge mass (the *residual cake*) is held back. Now scale this idea to a 50-ton hydraulic ram acting on a column of cooked soybean flakes, and you have **mechanical expression in an industrial press**.

The "sponge" is the **coagulated protein–oil matrix** formed during *cooking* (the controlled moist-heat conditioning step at 80–110 °C). The "cheesecloth" is the **drainage path** between flake particles. The "cup" is the **barrel of the expeller** or the **cage of the hydraulic press**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Physical Conditions Are Mandatory for Successful Expression:**
> 1. The cell wall must be **ruptured** (achieved by flaking to 0.25–0.40 mm thickness and cooking).
> 2. The oil viscosity must be **low enough** to flow (achieved by heating to 60–110 °C).
> 3. A **continuous porous drainage path** must exist for the oil to escape (achieved by the void network of the cake).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture content of cake after expression | **6–10 % w.b.** | Directly affects residual oil |
| Residual oil in pressed cake | **5–8 % (screw press)**, **4–6 % (high-pressure expeller)** | Lower = higher recovery |
| Flake thickness (pre-press) | **0.25–0.40 mm** | Controls cell rupture efficiency |
| Cooking temperature | **80–110 °C** | Denatures protein, lowers oil viscosity |
| Expression pressure (screw) | **35–70 MPa** | Hydraulic = 15–35 MPa |
| Oil yield (overall plant) | **95–99 %** with solvent; **85–92 %** by expression only | Expression is partial recovery |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Mechanical Expression Alone Cannot Reach 100 % Recovery:**
> A fraction of oil (typically 4–7 % of the seed weight) is bound by **molecular adsorption** within the denatured protein–carbohydrate matrix and cannot be displaced by pressure alone. To remove this *residual oil*, the cake is subsequently sent to a *solvent extraction plant* (using *n-hexane*, BP 68.7 °C) — this combined process is called the **"press–solvent" or "prepress–solvent" method**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Mechanical Expression — Step-by-Step Logic

The complete mechanism of oil expression can be decomposed into **five sequential physical stages**, each governed by a specific engineering sub-principle:

### Stage 1: **Cell Disruption (Pre-Press Preparation)**
- Flaking rolls compress the seed to a thin flake (0.25–0.40 mm), rupturing the **lipid bodies (oleosomes)** and the cell wall.
- Cooking (80–110 °C with moisture 8–12 %) **denatures proteins** (loss of tertiary structure), coagulating the cytoplasm and **reducing oil viscosity**.
- *Why this matters:* Without cell rupture, internal oil can never reach the surface; without viscosity reduction, the oil will not flow fast enough under the available pressure.

### Stage 2: **Cake Build-Up (Compression Phase)**
- The flaky, moist, hot material is fed into the press barrel.
- As the screw (or ram) advances, the material is compressed axially. The bulk density rises sharply.
- *The cake is consolidated* — void fraction (porosity, $\varepsilon$) decreases from ~0.6 to ~0.2.

### Stage 3: **Initial Free Flow (Rapid Oil Expulsion)**
- At low pressures, oil flows rapidly through inter-particle channels. This is the **"free oil"** that escapes easily.
- The flow regime at this stage approximates **Darcy's Law** for flow through a porous medium.

### Stage 4: **Compaction Phase (Slow Secondary Flow)**
- As pressure increases, flake particles deform plastically, closing inter-particle voids.
- Oil now flows through **micro-channels** within the collapsed cell structure. The flow rate drops by 1–2 orders of magnitude.
- This stage follows the **Koo** or **Schwaar** kinetics.

### Stage 5: **Expression Limit (Equilibrium)**
- Beyond a critical pressure, no more oil can be expelled; the cake has reached a **"residual oil"** state.
- This residual oil is the **bound oil** — chemically held by adsorption onto denatured protein surfaces.

## 2.2 Governing Equations — The Three Pillars of Expression Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Expected Learning Outcome for this sub-topic:**
> *Derive and apply the Schwaar, Koo, and Mounfield equations to predict oil expression yield as a function of pressure, time, cake geometry, and material properties.*

### Pillar 1: **Darcy's Law** (Foundation of Porous Media Flow)
Darcy's Law describes the volumetric flow rate of oil through the porous cake:

$$Q = -\frac{k \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric oil flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{s}$)
- $k$ = permeability of the cake ($\text{m}^2$)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the cake ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the oil ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$)
- $dP/dx$ = pressure gradient along the cake ($\text{Pa}/\text{m}$)

### Pillar 2: **Schwaar's Equation (1939)**
Empirically derived from expression experiments on copra and palm kernels:

$$V = a \cdot P^{n}$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of oil expressed (per unit mass of cake)
- $P$ = applied pressure
- $a$, $n$ = material-specific constants ($n$ typically lies between 0.3 and 0.7)

### Pillar 3: **Koo's Equation (1942)** — *The Most Tested in KTU*
Based on a differential mass balance on the cake:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -k \cdot P^{m}$$

Where:
- $dV/dt$ = instantaneous rate of oil expression
- $t$ = pressing time
- $P$ = applied pressure
- $k$, $m$ = constants dependent on oilseed and temperature

Upon integration at constant pressure, Koo's equation yields:

$$V = V_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t}$$

The **half-expression time** $t_{1/2}$ is defined as the time required to express half the recoverable oil:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k \cdot P^{m}}$$

### Pillar 4: **Mounfield's Equation (1936)**
Relates residual oil content to applied pressure in a hydraulic press:

$$O_r = O_\infty + \frac{C}{P}$$

Where:
- $O_r$ = residual oil content at pressure $P$ (% w/w)
- $O_\infty$ = asymptotic minimum residual oil (oil that *cannot* be pressed out)
- $C$ = oilseed-specific empirical constant
- $P$ = applied pressure

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = -\dfrac{k A}{\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | Darcy — flow rate through porous cake | 2-mark definition, identifying variables |
| 2 | $V = a \cdot P^{n}$ | Schwaar — oil volume vs. pressure | Plotting log V vs. log P gives slope *n* |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -k \cdot P^{m}$ | Koo — rate of oil expression | Kinetics, time-to-yield problems |
| 4 | $V = V_0 \cdot e^{-k P^{m} t}$ | Koo — integrated form | Expresses oil left in cake vs. time |
| 5 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k P^{m}}$ | Koo — half-expression time | Numerical problems (very common) |
| 6 | $O_r = O_\infty + \dfrac{C}{P}$ | Mounfield — residual oil vs. pressure | Determining constants from data |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{cake}}}{\rho_{\text{solid}}}$ | Porosity of pressed cake | Cake density calculations |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Oil}_{\text{out}}}{\text{Oil}_{\text{in}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Expression efficiency | 2-mark conceptual |
| 9 | $E_d = \dfrac{V_{\text{oil}}}{V_{\text{oil}} + V_{\text{cake}} + W_{\text{work}}}$ | Energy efficiency of expression | Energy balance problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The Koo and Mounfield equations are used by **commercial expeller manufacturers** (e.g., Anderson International, Desmet-Ballestra, Buhler) to size motors, design screw pitches, and predict residual oil content of soybean, rapeseed, and sunflower cakes — without expensive pilot trials. KTU frequently tests the *application* of these formulas in numerical questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Integration of Koo's Equation to Obtain the Time-Dependent Expression Form

We begin with Koo's differential form of the expression rate:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -k \cdot P^{m}$$

This is a **first-order separable ordinary differential equation** in $V$ and $t$, with $P$ held constant for an isobaric (constant-pressure) pressing experiment.

**Step 1: Separate the variables.**

Move all terms involving $V$ to the left side and all terms involving $t$ to the right:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -k \cdot P^{m} \cdot dt$$

**Step 2: Define the integration limits.**

At $t = 0$ (start of pressing), the volume of oil remaining in the cake that can still be expressed is $V_0$ (the initial recoverable oil volume). At any later time $t$, the remaining expressable volume is $V(t)$.

$$\int_{V_0}^{V(t)} \frac{dV}{V} = -k \cdot P^{m} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3: Perform the integration.**

The integral of $1/V$ with respect to $V$ is $\ln V$, and the integral of a constant with respect to $t$ is $t$:

$$\Big[ \ln V \Big]_{V_0}^{V(t)} = -k \cdot P^{m} \cdot \Big[ t \Big]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the bounds.**

$$\ln V(t) - \ln V_0 = -k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t$$

Combine the left side using logarithm subtraction:

$$\ln \left( \frac{V(t)}{V_0} \right) = -k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate both sides to isolate $V(t)$.**

$$\frac{V(t)}{V_0} = e^{-k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t}$$

Therefore:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t}$$

**Step 6: Define the half-expression time $t_{1/2}$.**

Set $V(t_{1/2}) = V_0 / 2$:

$$\frac{V_0}{2} = V_0 \cdot e^{-k P^{m} t_{1/2}}$$

Divide both sides by $V_0$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k P^{m} t_{1/2}}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = -k P^{m} t_{1/2}$$

Since $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$:

$$-\ln 2 = -k P^{m} t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k \cdot P^{m}}}$$

**Step 7: Physical interpretation.**

The half-expression time decreases *exponentially* with applied pressure (because $P$ appears in the exponent via $P^m$). A small increase in pressure produces a large decrease in pressing time — this is why high-pressure industrial expellers operate at >50 MPa, achieving $t_{1/2}$ values of only a few seconds.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A hydraulic press is used to express oil from conditioned soybean flakes at a constant pressure of $P = 40\ \text{MPa}$. The Koo equation constants for soybean at 90 °C are $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ (\text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{MPa}^{-m})$ and $m = 1.6$. The initial recoverable oil volume per kg of cake is $V_0 = 0.185\ \text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$. Calculate:
(a) The half-expression time $t_{1/2}$.
(b) The volume of oil remaining in the cake after 60 seconds of pressing.
(c) The expression efficiency $\eta$, if the original oil content was 20 % w/w and the pressed cake retains 6 % w/w oil.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Half-expression time**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k \cdot P^{m}} = \frac{0.6931}{(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (40)^{1.6}}$$

Compute $40^{1.6}$:

$$40^{1.6} = 40^{1} \cdot 40^{0.6} = 40 \cdot e^{0.6 \ln 40} = 40 \cdot e^{0.6 \cdot 3.6889} = 40 \cdot e^{2.2133} = 40 \cdot 9.146 = 365.86$$

Substitute:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.6931}{2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 365.86} = \frac{0.6931}{0.09146} = 7.578\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 7.58\ \text{s}}$$

**Part (b): Remaining oil volume after 60 s**

Use the integrated Koo equation:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-k P^{m} t} = 0.185 \cdot e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 365.86 \cdot 60}$$

Calculate the exponent:

$$(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 365.86 \cdot 60 = 0.09146 \cdot 60 = 5.488$$

$$V(60) = 0.185 \cdot e^{-5.488} = 0.185 \cdot 0.004143 = 7.66 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$$

$$\boxed{V(60) \approx 7.66 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{Mg} \text{ (essentially all expressable oil is recovered)}}$$

**Part (c): Expression efficiency**

For 1 kg (or 1 Mg) of prepared feed:
- Original oil in feed: $20\,\% \times 1000 = 200\ \text{kg}$
- Residual oil in cake: $6\,\% \times 1000 = 60\ \text{kg}$ (assuming no solids loss)
- Oil expressed: $200 - 60 = 140\ \text{kg}$

$$\eta = \frac{140}{200} \times 100 = 70\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 70\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In numerical Koo-equation problems, always show the computation of $P^m$ explicitly. Students frequently lose marks by writing "$P^m = \text{some value}$" without showing the intermediate $\ln$ or $e$ steps.
