# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define mechanical expression. List any two types of mechanical expression equipment used in oilseed milling.**

**Model Answer:**

Mechanical expression is the process of recovering oil from oil-bearing seeds by applying external mechanical pressure through a confined compression zone, forcing the oil out of the ruptured cells while retaining the solid matrix as a pressed cake.

Two types of mechanical expression equipment:
1. **Hydraulic press** — uses a piston (ram) driven by hydraulic fluid to compress the cake in batch operation; pressures of 15–35 MPa; used for cocoa, coconut, palm kernel, and small-batch premium oils.
2. **Screw press (expeller)** — uses a rotating worm shaft of increasing pitch inside a slotted barrel; pressures of 35–70 MPa; the most common industrial expression device for soybean, rapeseed, and sunflower.

*(Acceptable third: Roller mill, used for cocoa and pre-pressing.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Koo's equation for the rate of oil expression. Define the term *half-expression time*.**

**Model Answer:**

Koo's equation (1942) gives the instantaneous rate of oil expression from a porous cake as a function of applied pressure:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -k \cdot P^{m}$$

where $V$ is the volume of expressable oil remaining, $t$ is the pressing time, $P$ is the applied pressure, and $k$ and $m$ are empirical constants that depend on the oilseed type and temperature.

**Half-expression time ($t_{1/2}$)** is defined as the time required, under a constant pressure $P$, to express half of the total recoverable oil from the cake. It is obtained by integrating Koo's equation and is given by:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k \cdot P^{m}}$$

It is a key design parameter for sizing industrial expression equipment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Module-level questions carry 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means you must attempt either **Question A** *or* **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Derive Koo's integrated equation for oil expression. Show that the half-expression time is inversely proportional to $P^m$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A screw expeller processes soybean flakes at $P = 55\ \text{MPa}$ and 95 °C. The Koo constants are $k = 3.1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}^{-1}\ \text{MPa}^{-m}$ and $m = 1.5$. The original recoverable oil volume is $V_0 = 0.20\ \text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$. Calculate:
- (i) The half-expression time.
- (ii) The volume of oil remaining after 45 s of continuous pressing.
- (iii) The expression efficiency if the residual oil in the cake is 5.5 % w/w and the original oil content was 21 % w/w. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

The derivation proceeds from Koo's differential form to the integrated form, as detailed in Section 3.1 of these notes. The key steps are:

**Step 1:** Write Koo's differential rate equation:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = -k \cdot P^{m}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables for an isobaric (constant pressure) process:

$$dV = -k \cdot P^{m} \cdot dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $V_0$ at $t = 0$ to $V(t)$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{V_0}^{V(t)} dV = -k \cdot P^{m} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$V(t) - V_0 = -k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t$$

$$V(t) = V_0 - k \cdot P^{m} \cdot t \quad \text{(linear form for small ranges)}$$

For a first-order exponential recovery model, the more rigorous form is:

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-k P^{m} t}$$

**Step 5:** Set $V(t_{1/2}) = V_0/2$:

$$\frac{V_0}{2} = V_0 \cdot e^{-k P^{m} t_{1/2}}$$

**Step 6:** Take the natural logarithm:

$$-\ln 2 = -k P^{m} t_{1/2}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $t_{1/2}$:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k P^{m}} = \frac{0.6931}{k P^{m}}$$

**Step 8:** Establish the inverse proportionality: Since $k$ is constant, $t_{1/2} \propto 1/P^m$, which means $t_{1/2} \cdot P^m = \text{constant}$. On a log–log plot of $t_{1/2}$ vs. $P$, the slope is $-m$. **Q.E.D.**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Half-expression time:**

$$P^{m} = 55^{1.5} = 55 \cdot \sqrt{55} = 55 \cdot 7.416 = 407.9\ \text{MPa}^{1.5}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.6931}{(3.1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 407.9} = \frac{0.6931}{0.1264} = 5.48\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 5.48\ \text{s}}$$

**(ii) Volume of oil remaining after 45 s:**

$$V(45) = V_0 \cdot e^{-k P^{m} t} = 0.20 \cdot e^{-(3.1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 407.9 \cdot 45}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$(3.1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 407.9 \cdot 45 = 0.1264 \cdot 45 = 5.690$$

$$V(45) = 0.20 \cdot e^{-5.690} = 0.20 \cdot 0.003409 = 6.82 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{Mg}$$

$$\boxed{V(45) \approx 6.82 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{Mg}}$$

Interpretation: At 45 s, virtually all expressable oil has been recovered; further pressing yields negligible additional oil.

**(iii) Expression efficiency:**

For 1 kg of feed:
- Original oil = 21 % × 1000 = 210 g
- Residual oil in cake = 5.5 % × 1000 = 55 g
- Oil expressed = $210 - 55 = 155$ g

$$\eta = \frac{155}{210} \times 100 = 73.8\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 73.8\,\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the steps involved in the **preparation of oilseeds** for mechanical expression, with emphasis on flaking and cooking. State the typical process parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain **Mounfield's equation** for mechanical expression. A cottonseed cake was pressed at three different pressures and the residual oil contents were recorded as follows:

| Pressure (MPa) | Residual Oil (% w/w) |
|---|---|
| 15 | 9.8 |
| 25 | 7.2 |
| 40 | 5.6 |

Determine the constants $O_\infty$ and $C$ in Mounfield's equation $O_r = O_\infty + C/P$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Oilseed Preparation (7 marks)**

The proper preparation of oilseeds is essential for maximizing oil recovery in mechanical expression. The major steps are:

1. **Cleaning** — Removal of foreign matter (stones, dust, stems) using magnetic separators, vibrating screens, and air classifiers. Typical purity target: 99 %.

2. **Dehulling (Optional)** — Removal of the outer fibrous hull (e.g., sunflower, soybean, groundnut) using impact dehullers or disk hullers. Improves protein content of the meal and reduces fibre load.

3. **Conditioning / Tempering** — Addition of moisture (or steam) to bring the seed to a uniform moisture content of **8–12 % w.b.** This plastsizes the seed for flaking and prevents shattering.

4. **Flaking** — Passage of conditioned seeds through a pair of smooth or corrugated rolls rotating at differential speeds (1:1.05 to 1:1.25). The flake thickness is reduced to **0.25–0.40 mm**. This step is **critical** because it ruptures the oil-bearing cells (oleosomes), exposing the oil for downstream expression.

5. **Cooking** — Moist-heat treatment of the flakes in a stack cooker or rotary cooker at **80–110 °C** for **15–30 minutes**. The functions of cooking are:
   - Denature proteins (irreversibly coagulate them).
   - Reduce oil viscosity (warm oil flows faster).
   - Inactivate anti-nutritional factors (e.g., trypsin inhibitor in soybean).
   - Coagulate the phospholipid–protein complex to reduce refining losses.

6. **Optional: Extrusion / Expanding** — For high-capacity plants, the cooked flakes are passed through an expander to form porous collets — this improves solvent percolation in the subsequent extractor (if prepress–solvent route is used).

**Typical process parameters summary:**
- Moisture after conditioning: 8–12 %
- Flake thickness: 0.25–0.40 mm
- Cooking temperature: 80–110 °C
- Cooking time: 15–30 min
- Moisture after cooking: 5–7 %

---

**Part (b) — Mounfield's Equation (7 marks)**

Mounfield's equation (1936) is an empirical relationship between the **residual oil content** of a pressed cake and the **applied pressure**:

$$O_r = O_\infty + \frac{C}{P}$$

where:
- $O_r$ = residual oil in the pressed cake (% w/w)
- $O_\infty$ = asymptotic minimum residual oil (% w/w) — represents the bound oil that cannot be removed by pressure
- $C$ = empirical constant related to oilseed compressibility and cake permeability
- $P$ = applied pressure (MPa)

**Physical Significance:** As $P \rightarrow \infty$, $O_r \rightarrow O_\infty$. Thus, $O_\infty$ is the *floor* value of residual oil achievable by any mechanical means.

**Determination of constants from the given data:**

We have three data points: $(P, O_r) = (15,\ 9.8),\ (25,\ 7.2),\ (40,\ 5.6)$.

Rearranging: $O_\infty = O_r - C/P$.

To find $C$ and $O_\infty$, set up two equations using any two points. Using points 1 and 3:

$$9.8 = O_\infty + \frac{C}{15} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$5.6 = O_\infty + \frac{C}{40} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Subtract (ii) from (i):

$$9.8 - 5.6 = \frac{C}{15} - \frac{C}{40}$$

$$4.2 = C \left( \frac{1}{15} - \frac{1}{40} \right) = C \left( \frac{8 - 3}{120} \right) = C \cdot \frac{5}{120} = \frac{C}{24}$$

$$C = 4.2 \times 24 = 100.8$$

Substitute back into (i):

$$9.8 = O_\infty + \frac{100.8}{15} = O_\infty + 6.72$$

$$O_\infty = 9.8 - 6.72 = 3.08\ \%\ \text{w/w}$$

**Verify using point 2:** $O_r = 3.08 + 100.8/25 = 3.08 + 4.032 = 7.11\ \%\ \text{w/w}$ (vs. observed 7.2 %, error ≈ 1.3 %, acceptable).

$$\boxed{O_\infty \approx 3.08\ \%\ \text{w/w}, \quad C \approx 100.8}$$

This means cottonseed press cake has a *floor residual oil* of about 3.08 % — beyond this, mechanical expression cannot recover more oil, and solvent extraction would be required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_0$ with total oil content.** $V_0$ in Koo's equation is the *recoverable* (expressable) oil volume, *not* the total oil in the seed. Always subtract the bound-oil fraction.
> 2. **Sign error in $dV/dt$.** Koo's equation has a *negative* sign because $V$ *decreases* with time. Forgetting this flips the exponential decay to growth — a guaranteed zero.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in Mounfield's equation.** The constant $C$ has units of (% w/w)·(MPa). Students often forget to state units in the final answer, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign when taking $\ln(1/2)$.** $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$, *not* $+\ln 2$. This single error propagates through the entire $t_{1/2}$ calculation.
> 5. **Not defining the boundary conditions** in derivations. KTU awards 2–3 marks specifically for writing "at $t = 0$, $V = V_0$".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mechanical expression** is a solid–liquid separation process that uses pressure alone (no solvent) to recover oil from oilseeds.
- **Five-stage mechanism:** cell disruption → cake build-up → free oil flow → compaction phase → expression limit.
- **Darcy's Law** is the foundational transport equation for oil flow through the porous cake.
- **Schwaar's equation** ($V = aP^n$) is purely empirical and is used when only pressure data is available.
- **Koo's equation** ($dV/dt = -kP^m$) is the *most-tested* model in KTU; integrated form is $V(t) = V_0 e^{-kP^m t}$.
- **Half-expression time** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / (kP^m)$ decreases *exponentially* with pressure.
- **Mounfield's equation** ($O_r = O_\infty + C/P$) predicts *residual oil* from applied pressure and identifies the bound-oil floor $O_\infty$.
- **Pre-treatment is critical:** flaking (0.25–0.40 mm) + cooking (80–110 °C, 15–30 min) are the two non-negotiable steps.
- **Two main equipment types:** hydraulic press (15–35 MPa, batch, premium oils) and screw expeller (35–70 MPa, continuous, industrial).
- **Expression alone cannot reach 100 % recovery** — typically 85–92 %. For >98 % recovery, the **prepress–solvent** method is used.
- **Variables that improve expression yield:** ↑ pressure, ↑ temperature, ↓ moisture (after cooking), ↓ flake thickness, ↑ pressing time.
- **Memorize the three KTU-favorite equations** and the *physical meaning of every variable* — the KTU theory paper is built around these three.
- **In numerical problems**, always show $P^m$ computation, always state units in the final answer, and always verify with one extra data point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screw Press
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Screw Press # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Screw Press (Expeller):** A screw press is a **continuous mechanical extraction device** used in post-harvest processing for the recovery of oil from oilseeds. It consists of a **helical screw (worm shaft)** rotating concentrically inside a **perforated cage or barrel**, which progressively compresses the oil-bearing material. Oil escapes through the barrel perforations under high pressure, while the residual solid **cake** is extruded through a restricted **discharge cone (choke)** at the far end. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAGT752**, the screw press is classified under **Mechanical Oil Expression Equipment** and is a fundamental continuous-press apparatus in any solvent-free or pre-press oil extraction plant. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Garlic & Ginger Juicer:** > Think of the kitchen juicer where you push ginger into a chamber and rotate a screw. The screw drags the material forward into an ever-narrowing space. As the space shrinks, the material is **squeezed harder and harder**, and the liquid (juice) oozes out through small holes in the chamber wall, while the dry pulp is pushed out as a thin "worm" of waste from the tip. > > **A screw press does exactly this — only it is built for tonnes per hour and hundreds of atmospheres of pressure.** ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Worm / Screw Shaft** | The rotating helical screw that conveys and compresses the material | | **Cage / Barrel** | Perforated cylindrical housing that retains solids but allows oil to drain | | **Choke / Cone** | Adjustable restriction at the discharge end controlling back-pressure | | **Cake** | Solid residue expelled after oil expression | | **Crud / Foots** | Solid impurities present in the expelled crude oil | | **Compression Ratio (CR)** | Ratio of feed cross-section area to discharge cross-section area | ## 1.4 Typical Operating Range (Industry Standard) | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | **Throughput Capacity** | **1 – 50 tonnes/day** | | **Residual Oil in Cake** | **4 – 8 % (single press)**, 2 – 3 % (with second press) | | **Screw Rotational Speed** | **5 – 40 rpm** (slow speed = higher pressure) | | **Operating Pressure** | **1400 – 7000 bar (≈ 2000 – 10000 psi)** in the high-pressure zone | | **Barrel Length-to-Diameter Ratio** | **L/D ≈ 4 : 1 to 10 : 1** | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic The operation of a screw press follows a strict sequence of mass and energy transformations: 1. **Feeding Zone** — Cleaned, conditioned (moisture- and temperature-adjusted) oilseed enters through the hopper and fills the flights of the screw. 2. **Conveying & Pre-Compression Zone** — Rotation of the screw drags the material forward; the **root diameter** of the screw increases progressively, reducing the free volume. 3. **Main Compression Zone** — Material is subjected to high **mechanical pressure** (and partially, shear). Liquefied oil is expelled through the cage perforations and collected in a trough. 4. **Metering / Discharge Zone** — The cake reaches the choke. Adjusting the choke diameter alters the **back-pressure**, hence the residual oil in the cake. 5. **Cake Discharge** — The compressed cake emerges as a continuous ribbon or pellets from the cone. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Choke Adjustment:** Closing the choke → smaller discharge area → higher back-pressure → more oil extraction BUT lower throughput and higher wear. The operator balances this trade-off. ## 2.2 Main Components in Detail - **Hopper with Feeder:** Gravity-fed or screw-fed, sometimes steam-jacketed for seed preheating. - **Horizontal Screw Shaft:** Made of high-grade alloy steel. The shaft's **root diameter** increases toward the discharge end to build up pressure (this is the single most important design feature). - **Barrel / Cage:** Composed of **vertical or horizontal bars** (clamped together) with narrow gaps (typically **0.25 – 0.8 mm**) that act as drainage slits for oil but retain seed particles. - **Choke / Cone Assembly:** A truncated cone at the end, axially movable to vary the annular opening. - **Drive System:** Gear-reducer + electric motor (usually **3 – 25 kW** depending on capacity). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All symbols below are KTU-conventional. Use `\vert` instead of $\vert$ in tables to avoid breaking markdown. | # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p\cdot N$ | Volumetric capacity per minute | m³/min | | 2 | $W = Q\cdot \rho_{b}\cdot 60$ | Mass throughput per hour (mass capacity) | kg/h | | 3 | $CR = \dfrac{D^{2}-d^{2}}{D_{1}^{2}-d_{1}^{2}}$ | Compression Ratio (feed / discharge area) | dimensionless | | 4 | $\eta_{oil} = \dfrac{O_{in}-O_{cake}}{O_{in}}\times 100$ | Oil extraction efficiency | % | | 5 | $O_{res}\,\% = \dfrac{O_{cake}}{M_{cake}}\times 100$ | Residual oil in cake | % | | 6 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power required at the screw shaft | W (or kW) | | 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{F}{A_{eff}}$ | Pressure rise across compression zone | Pa | | 8 | $M_{feed} = M_{cake} + M_{oil} + M_{loss}$ | Overall mass balance | kg/h | Where: - $D$ = outer diameter of screw (m) - $d$ = root / shaft diameter at feed (m) - $p$ = pitch of the flight (m) - $N$ = rotational speed (rpm) - $\rho_{b}$ = bulk density of seed ($\text{kg/m}^{3}$) - $D_{1}, d_{1}$ = corresponding diameters at discharge - $O_{in}, O_{cake}$ = oil content in feed / cake - $T$ = torque on the screw (N·m) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Screw presses dominate **small- and medium-scale rural oil mills** in India (groundnut, coconut, sesame, mustard, niger seed) because they: - Require **no solvent** (process is purely mechanical — safer & cleaner). - Operate **continuously** (24 × 7 possible) — suitable for commercial enterprises. - Are **simple to maintain** and can be operated by semi-skilled labour. - Couple well with downstream **filtration and refining (neutralization, bleaching, deodorization)** units. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Volumetric Throughput of a Screw Press ### Statement Derive an expression for the **mass throughput per hour** $W$ of a screw press in terms of screw geometry, speed and bulk density of the seed. ### Step 1: Geometry of One Screw Flight Consider one complete flight (one pitch length $p$) of the screw. The annular cross-section available for material flow is: $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2}) $$ where $D$ = outer flight diameter and $d$ = root (shaft) diameter. ### Step 2: Volume Per Revolution For one revolution, the screw advances one pitch length $p$, so the theoretical volume displaced per revolution is: $$ V_{rev} = A \cdot p = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p $$ ### Step 3: Volumetric Throughput Per Minute If the screw rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm): $$ Q = V_{rev}\cdot N = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p\cdot N \quad (\text{m}^{3}\text{/min}) $$ ### Step 4: Convert to Mass Throughput Multiplying by the bulk density $\rho_{b}$ of the seed and converting minutes to hours (factor of 60): $$ W = Q \cdot \rho_{b} \cdot 60 $$ $$ \boxed{\;W = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p\cdot N\cdot \rho_{b}\cdot 60\;\; \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}}\;} $$ ### Step 5: Discussion of Slip Factor In reality, not all the material is "pushed" forward — some slips back between the flight and barrel wall. Hence we introduce a **volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v}$** (typically $0.6$ to $0.85$): $$ W_{actual} = \eta_{v}\cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p\cdot N\cdot \rho_{b}\cdot 60 $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Compression Ratio ### Statement Show that the compression ratio of a screw press is the ratio of the feed annulus area to the discharge annulus area. ### Step 1: Definition Compression Ratio (CR) is defined as the **ratio of free cross-sectional area at the feed end to that at the discharge end** (this is how "compression" is geometrically generated by the screw). $$ CR = \frac{A_{feed}}{A_{discharge}} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute Annular Areas $$ A_{feed} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2}-d^{2}), \qquad A_{discharge} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_{1}^{2}-d_{1}^{2}) $$ ### Step 3: Simplify $$ \boxed{\;CR = \dfrac{D^{2}-d^{2}}{D_{1}^{2}-d_{1}^{2}}\;} $$ ### Step 4: Typical Numerical Check Suppose $D = 150\,\text{mm}$, $d = 75\,\text{mm}$ (feed) and $D_{1}=150\,\text{mm}$, $d_{1}=130\,\text{mm}$ (discharge): $$ CR = \frac{150^{2}-75^{2}}{150^{2}-130^{2}} = \frac{22500-5625}{22500-16900} = \frac{16875}{5600} \approx 3.01 $$ So a CR of about **3 : 1** — typical for a single-stage press. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Oil Extraction Efficiency ### Statement From the mass balance, derive the expression for the **fractional recovery** of oil. ### Step 1: Mass Balance on Oil $$ M_{feed}\cdot X_{o,f} = M_{oil} + M_{cake}\cdot X_{o,c} $$ where $X_{o,f}$ and $X_{o,c}$ are mass fractions of oil in feed and cake respectively. ### Step 2: Rearrange for Oil Output $$ M_{oil} = M_{feed}\cdot X_{o,f} - M_{cake}\cdot X_{o,c} $$ ### Step 3: Define Efficiency as a Ratio $$ \eta_{oil} = \frac{M_{oil}}{M_{feed}\cdot X_{o,f}} = 1 - \frac{M_{cake}\cdot X_{o,c}}{M_{feed}\cdot X_{o,f}} $$ ### Step 4: Substitute Typical Numbers If $X_{o,f} = 0.40$ (40 % oil in copra), $X_{o,c} = 0.06$ (6 % residual in cake), and assume $M_{cake}/M_{feed}\approx 0.62$ (mass loss to oil + some moisture loss): $$ \eta_{oil} = 1 - \frac{0.62\times 0.06}{0.40} = 1 - \frac{0.0372}{0.40} = 1 - 0.093 = 0.907 $$ i.e. **≈ 90.7 % oil recovery** in a single pressing — typical for copra/mechanical expeller. --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Power Requirement at the Shaft ### Step 1: Mechanical Power from Rotational Motion $$ P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} $$ ### Step 2: Express Torque in Terms of Pressure and Geometry Torque on the screw is dominated by the frictional/shearing resistance of the material against the barrel. Approximating by an effective mean pressure $\bar{P}$ acting on the screw's projected wetted surface: $$ T = \bar{P}\cdot f\cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{2}\cdot L $$ where $f$ = friction coefficient (typically $0.05$ – $0.20$ for oilseed pastes) and $L$ = effective length of compression zone. ### Step 3: Combine $$ P = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\cdot \bar{P}\cdot f\cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{2}\cdot L $$ $$ \boxed{\;P \approx \dfrac{\pi^{2} N \bar{P} f D^{2} L}{60}\quad \text{(W)}\;} $$ This expression is used by KTU examiners to estimate **motor sizing** for the screw press. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Quantity | Formula | Designer Controls It By | |---|---|---| | Throughput $W$ | $W = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2}-d^{2})pN\rho_{b}\cdot 60$ | Pitch $p$, speed $N$ | | Compression Ratio $CR$ | $CR = (D^{2}-d^{2})/(D_{1}^{2}-d_{1}^{2})$ | Geometry of root profile | | Oil Recovery $\eta_{oil}$ | $1 - \frac{M_{cake}X_{o,c}}{M_{feed}X_{o,f}}$ | Choke setting, conditioning | | Power $P$ | $\frac{\pi^{2}N\bar{P}fD^{2}L}{60}$ | Speed, friction, barrel length | | Cake Residual Oil | $X_{o,c}\,(M_{cake})$ | Back-pressure via choke | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional / Architectural Flow of a Screw Press > [!NOTE] > A physical cross-section is impossible to render in Mermaid (no curved solids). Below is a **block-level functional architecture** showing how feed, energy, mass and information flow through the screw press. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Oilseed Hopper] --> B[Feed Screw and Steam Jacket] B --> C[Conveying Zone of Worm Shaft] C --> D[Pre Compression Zone] D --> E[Main Compression Zone inside Perforated Cage] E -- Oil escapes through cage slits --> F[Crude Oil Collection Trough] E -- Compressed cake moves forward --> G[Choke and Cone Assembly] G --> H[Cake Discharge Outlet] I[Electric Motor] -- via Gear Reducer --> C J[Choke Adjustment Lever] --> G K[Temperature and Pressure Sensors] --> L[Control Panel] L --> I L --> J ``` ## 4.2 Zone-Wise Operating Regime Map ```mermaid flowchart LR Z1[ZONE 1 FEED HOPPER
LOW PRESSURE ATMOSPHERIC] --> Z2[ZONE 2 PRE COMPRESSION
PRESSURE 50 TO 200 BAR] Z2 --> Z3[ZONE 3 MAIN COMPRESSION
PRESSURE 1500 TO 7000 BAR] Z3 --> Z4[ZONE 4 METERING DISCHARGE
CHOKE CONTROLLED] Z4 --> Z5[CAKE EXITS AT 5 TO 8 PERCENT RESIDUAL OIL] ``` ## 4.3 Cause–Effect Diagram of Factors Affecting Oil Yield ```mermaid flowchart TB FACTORS[INPUT FACTORS] --> CP[CAKE RESIDUAL OIL PERCENT] FACTORS --> YLD[OIL YIELD PERCENT] FACTORS --> F1[Seed Moisture Content] FACTORS --> F2[Seed Temperature] FACTORS --> F3[Screw Rotational Speed] FACTORS --> F4[Choke Opening Area] FACTORS --> F5[Particle Size of Crushed Seed] FACTORS --> F6[Cake Restriction Level] F1 -- optimum 3 to 6 percent moisture --> CP F2 -- optimum 100 to 110 degree celsius --> CP F3 -- lower speed higher pressure lower residual --> CP F4 -- smaller opening higher pressure --> CP F5 -- smaller particle more surface area --> YLD F6 -- higher restriction more oil expelled --> CP ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Screw Geometry (Increasing Root Diameter) ```mermaid flowchart LR SEG1[SEGMENT 1
ROOT DIAMETER SMALL d1
FEED END] --> SEG2[SEGMENT 2
ROOT DIAMETER d2 MEDIUM] SEG2 --> SEG3[SEGMENT 3
ROOT DIAMETER d3 LARGE
DISCHARGE END] SEG1 --- NOTE1[Free Volume Maximum
Low Pressure] SEG3 --- NOTE3[Free Volume Minimum
Maximum Pressure] ``` > The visual idea: the **root diameter increases progressively** from feed to discharge, geometrically generating the rising pressure that drives oil out through the cage. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAGT752]` **Define a screw press. List its main components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A screw press is a continuous mechanical device used for extracting oil from oilseeds by progressively compressing the seed against a perforated cage using a rotating helical screw (worm shaft). **Main Components (any three, 1 mark each):** 1. **Hopper with feeder** — admits the conditioned seed. 2. **Screw (worm) shaft** — with progressively increasing root diameter; conveys and compresses. 3. **Perforated cage / barrel** — drains oil but retains solids. 4. **Choke / cone mechanism** — adjustable back-pressure control. 5. **Drive unit** — motor + gearbox. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAGT752]` **What is compression ratio in a screw press? Why is it important?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **compression ratio (CR)** is the ratio of the free annular cross-section at the feed end to that at the discharge end of the screw: $$ CR = \frac{D^{2}-d^{2}}{D_{1}^{2}-d_{1}^{2}} $$ **Importance (2 marks):** - It directly governs the **maximum pressure** developed in the barrel. - A **higher CR** → higher pressure → **lower residual oil in cake** BUT lower throughput and higher wear. - Designers select CR (typically 3 : 1 to 8 : 1) based on the oilseed type and desired extraction efficiency. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer any ONE full question (a + b) for 14 marks. --- ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PEAGT752]` (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a screw press** used for oil extraction. Describe the function of each zone. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** A screw press consists of a **horizontal worm shaft** rotating inside a **perforated cage** driven by a motor through a **reduction gearbox**. **Working — Zone-wise (5 marks):** 1. **Feeding Zone:** Conditioned oilseed enters via the hopper. Steam jackets may preheat the seed to **80 – 110 °C** to denature proteins and improve oil fluidity. 2. **Conveying Zone:** The rotating screw pushes the material forward. Pitch of the screw determines residence time. 3. **Pre-Compression Zone:** Root diameter of the screw begins to increase. The free volume between flights decreases, gently compacting the seed. 4. **Main Compression Zone:** Maximum pressure (up to ~**7000 bar**) is achieved. **Liquid oil is squeezed out through the cage perforations** (typical slit width 0.25 – 0.8 mm) and is collected in a trough beneath the cage. 5. **Metering / Discharge Zone:** The partially de-oiled cake reaches the **choke cone**. The choke restricts the exit area, generating back-pressure. The cake exits as a continuous ribbon containing **4 – 8 % residual oil**. **Sketch (2 marks):** Functional flow diagram accepted (see Section 4 of these notes). --- **(b)** A screw press has the following specifications: - Outer screw diameter $D = 150\,\text{mm}$ - Root diameter at feed $d = 75\,\text{mm}$ - Pitch $p = 75\,\text{mm}$ - Rotational speed $N = 30\,\text{rpm}$ - Bulk density of seed $\rho_{b} = 600\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ - Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v} = 0.75$ **Calculate:** (i) the volumetric throughput $Q$, and (ii) the mass throughput $W$ in kg/h. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Volumetric Throughput (3 marks)** $$ Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p\cdot N $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.150^{2}-0.075^{2})\cdot 0.075\cdot 30 $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.0225 - 0.005625)\cdot 0.075\cdot 30 $$ $$ Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.016875)\cdot 2.25 $$ $$ Q = 0.7854 \times 0.016875 \times 2.25 \approx 0.0298\;\text{m}^{3}\text{/min} $$ **(ii) Mass Throughput (4 marks)** Theoretical (without $\eta_{v}$): $$ W_{th} = Q \cdot \rho_{b} \cdot 60 = 0.0298 \times 600 \times 60 \approx 1072.8\;\text{kg/h} $$ Applying volumetric efficiency: $$ W_{actual} = \eta_{v}\cdot W_{th} = 0.75 \times 1072.8 \approx \boxed{804.6\;\text{kg/h}} $$ Hence the press can handle roughly **800 kg of seed per hour** in real conditions. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PEAGT752]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting oil yield** in a screw press. How is the **choke mechanism** used to control extraction efficiency? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Factors (5 marks):** 1. **Seed Moisture Content** — Optimal moisture **3 – 6 %** for most oilseeds. Too dry → brittle, low yield. Too wet → slippage, low pressure. 2. **Seed Temperature** — Pre-heating to **100 – 110 °C** lowers oil viscosity and ruptures cell walls, improving yield. 3. **Particle Size** — Crushed/flake must be uniform (typically **0.25 – 0.5 mm** thickness). 4. **Screw Speed** — Lower speed = longer residence time = higher pressure = more oil expressed. 5. **Compression Ratio** — Higher CR (up to ~10 : 1) increases pressure. 6. **Choke Opening** — Smaller opening = greater back-pressure. 7. **Cage Condition** — Clean, sharp-edged slits give higher drainage. **Choke Mechanism (2 marks):** The choke cone at the discharge end can be moved axially to **reduce or enlarge the annular exit area**. A **smaller exit** builds higher back-pressure inside the barrel, squeezing out more oil but reducing throughput. Operators adjust it to balance yield vs. capacity for each seed type. --- **(b)** A screw press receives **1000 kg/h** of groundnut with **45 % oil content**. The expelled cake has **7 % residual oil**, and the cake flow rate is **520 kg/h**. Determine: (i) the actual oil output per hour, and (ii) the **oil extraction efficiency**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - Feed rate $M_{feed} = 1000\,\text{kg/h}$ - Oil in feed = 45 % → $M_{oil,\,in} = 0.45 \times 1000 = 450\,\text{kg/h}$ - Cake rate $M_{cake} = 520\,\text{kg/h}$ - Oil in cake = 7 % → $M_{oil,\,cake} = 0.07 \times 520 = 36.4\,\text{kg/h}$ **(i) Oil Output (3 marks)** $$ M_{oil,\,out} = M_{oil,\,in} - M_{oil,\,cake} = 450 - 36.4 = \boxed{413.6\;\text{kg/h}} $$ **(ii) Extraction Efficiency (4 marks)** $$ \eta_{oil} = \frac{M_{oil,\,out}}{M_{oil,\,in}} \times 100 = \frac{413.6}{450} \times 100 \approx \boxed{91.91\,\%} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes to hours** when applying the throughput formula — loses 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing cake oil content** as a fraction of *feed* instead of *cake mass* — a classic trap; re-read the question carefully. > 3. **Ignoring the volumetric efficiency $\eta_{v}$** in design problems — most KTU papers now include it. > 4. **Not stating units** in final answer — deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. > 5. Skipping the **labelled sketch/diagram** in working-principle questions — lose 1–2 marks. --- ## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Screw Press (PEAGT752 · Module 3)** - ✅ Screw press = **continuous, mechanical, solvent-free oil extractor**. - ✅ Key components: **hopper, worm shaft, cage, choke cone, drive**. - ✅ Root diameter **increases** from feed → discharge → generates pressure. - ✅ Cage slit width: **0.25 – 0.8 mm**. - ✅ Throughput formula: $W = \eta_{v}\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2}-d^{2})\cdot p\cdot N\cdot \rho_{b}\cdot 60$. - ✅ Compression ratio: $CR = (D^{2}-d^{2})/(D_{1}^{2}-d_{1}^{2})$; typical **3 : 1 – 10 : 1**. - ✅ Oil extraction efficiency: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{M_{cake}X_{o,c}}{M_{feed}X_{o,f}}$. - ✅ Residual cake oil (single press): **4 – 8 %**; (with re-press): **2 – 3 %**. - ✅ Optimal seed moisture: **3 – 6 %**; pre-heat temperature: **100 – 110 °C**. - ✅ Lower screw speed → higher pressure → higher oil yield, lower capacity. - ✅ **Choke** = primary field-adjustable parameter for back-pressure control. - ✅ Power equation (memory): $P \approx \dfrac{\pi^{2}N\bar{P}fD^{2}L}{60}$. - ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers and **draw a labelled diagram** for descriptive questions.

topics.title: Hydraulic Press
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Press # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HYDRAULIC PRESS (KTU 2024 Definition)** > A **hydraulic press** is a machine that uses a hydraulic cylinder to generate a large compressive force, governed by **Pascal's Law of Fluid Transmission of Pressure**, in which a small force applied on a smaller piston (plunger) is transmitted through an incompressible fluid to produce a magnified force on a larger piston (ram). In the context of **Post-Harvest Engineering**, it is the primary unit operation used for **liquid-solid separation**, particularly for the extraction of oils from oilseeds (e.g., coconut, groundnut, sesame, mustard), juices from sugarcane and fruit pulps, and the dewatering/compaction of agricultural produce. In the KTU 2024 scheme, the hydraulic press is classified as a **Pressure-Driven Mechanical Separation Equipment** under the broader domain of *Unit Operations in Post-Harvest Engineering*. The course code **PEAGT752** places it under the module dealing with mechanical expression and extraction processes, where the prime mover is **static fluid pressure** rather than dynamic mechanical shearing. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lemon and the Pinch" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Squeeze Principle** > Imagine a water-filled balloon connected to two tubes of different diameters — one narrow (like a syringe needle) and one wide (like a syringe barrel). If you push the narrow tube with a small force, the water (incompressible) shifts and pushes out the wide tube with a much larger force. The hydraulic press works on the *exact same principle*: a small effort on a small plunger is "amplified" into a huge pressing force on a large ram. The total volume of water is conserved — what you give up in **distance**, you gain in **force** (this is the classic trade-off explained by *Conservation of Energy*). The mathematical consequence is profound: a force of just **10 N** on a **1 cm²** piston can theoretically generate a force of **10,000 N** on a **1000 cm²** ram, provided friction and fluid leakages are neglected. The fluid acts as a "force transmitter" — it does not multiply energy, but it **redistributes it across a different area**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Density of hydraulic oil (mineral-based)**: $\rho \approx 870 \text{ to } 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil**: $\beta \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \text{ GPa}$ (this makes oil nearly incompressible — the very property that makes hydraulic presses work) - **Standard working pressure** in post-harvest presses: $P = 5 \text{ to } 25 \text{ MPa}$ (50–250 bar) - **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** is a dimensionless ratio - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** of a well-designed press: typically $\eta = 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95$ (i.e., 85% to 95%) > [!TIP] > **Why "Presses" Matter in Post-Harvest Engineering** > India is one of the world's largest producers of oilseeds, and the **expeller press** and **hydraulic press** are the backbone of small and medium-scale oil extraction industries. The hydraulic press is preferred over screw expellers when the oil-bearing material is delicate (e.g., coconut, sesame) or when high-quality, cold-pressed oil is desired. ## 1.4 Role in the Post-Harvest Value Chain | Stage of Value Chain | Role of Hydraulic Press | |---|---| | Oil extraction | Expelling oil from copra, groundnut, sesame, mustard, sunflower | | Juice extraction | Sugarcane crushers (hydraulic variants), pomegranate, citrus | | Dewatering | Pressing of fermented cocoa, dewatered pulp, forage | | Briquetting | Compaction of biomass (sawdust, rice husk) into fuel briquettes | | Curing/forming | Pressing of tea, tobacco, and rubber sheets | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Governing Principle — Pascal's Law (Re-stated for Hydraulic Press) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PASCAL'S LAW (1623–1662)** > *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel."* Mathematically, for a hydraulic press with two pistons (areas $A_1$ and $A_2$) connected by an incompressible fluid: $$\boxed{P_1 = P_2}$$ $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$ where: - $F_1$ = Effort / Input force applied on the small plunger (N) - $F_2$ = Load / Output force exerted by the large ram (N) - $A_1$ = Cross-sectional area of the small plunger (m²) - $A_2$ = Cross-sectional area of the large ram (m²) ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working A **hydraulic press** operates through the following sequence: 1. **Effort Application** — A small force $F_1$ is applied on the **plunger (small piston)** of cross-section $A_1$, usually via a hand lever, pedal, or motor-driven pump. 2. **Pressure Generation in the Fluid** — This force creates pressure $P = F_1/A_1$ inside the hydraulic fluid (oil or water-glycol mixture). Because the fluid is essentially incompressible, the pressure is transmitted instantly. 3. **Pressure Transmission** — The pressurized fluid travels through a connecting passage (with non-return valves for safety) to the larger cylinder housing the **ram (large piston)**. 4. **Force Multiplication on the Ram** — The same pressure $P$ acts on the larger area $A_2$, producing a magnified force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2$. 5. **Load Compression** — The ram descends, pressing the **cake** (the material being processed, e.g., copra or sugarcane bagasse) against a fixed platen. The oil/juice flows out through drainage channels. 6. **Ram Retraction** — Once the pressing cycle is complete, a release valve opens, and the ram is retracted (either by gravity, spring, or a secondary low-pressure pump circuit). ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Force Multiplication The fluid does **not** create energy out of nothing — this would violate the **First Law of Thermodynamics**. What it does is **redistribute energy** by trading *distance* for *force*: - The small plunger travels a **long distance** $x_1$. - The large ram travels a **short distance** $x_2$. - By volume conservation: $A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$ This is why the **Mechanical Advantage** (force ratio) and the **Velocity Ratio** (distance ratio) are reciprocals of each other. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table is a **must-memorize** reference for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). All marks for derivations and numerical problems will be drawn from these equations. | # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pressure on plunger | $P$ | $F_1 / A_1$ | Pa (N/m²) | Equals pressure on ram | | 2 | Pascal's equation | — | $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ | — | Core principle | | 3 | Theoretical Mechanical Advantage | $MA_{th}$ | $A_2 / A_1$ | dimensionless | Ideal (frictionless) case | | 4 | Actual Mechanical Advantage | $MA_{act}$ | $F_2 / F_1$ | dimensionless | With friction | | 5 | Velocity Ratio | $VR$ | $A_1 / A_2$ or $x_2 / x_1$ | dimensionless | Always $\leq 1$ | | 6 | Volume conservation | — | $A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ | m³ | Incompressibility condition | | 7 | Efficiency | $\eta$ | $MA_{act}/VR$ or $(W_{out}/W_{in})\times 100\%$ | % or fraction | $\eta \leq 1$ | | 8 | Effort at handle (lever) | $F_1$ | $W \cdot L_2 / L_1$ | N | If lever of lengths $L_1, L_2$ is used | | 9 | Hydraulic power | $P_h$ | $P \cdot Q$ | W (J/s) | $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s) | | 10 | Ram velocity | $v_2$ | $Q / A_2$ | m/s | Where $Q$ is pump discharge | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM PITFALL** > A common mistake students make is to confuse **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** with **Velocity Ratio (VR)**. Remember: > - **MA = Load / Effort** (force ratio, > 1 for a press) > - **VR = Effort displacement / Load displacement** (distance ratio, < 1 for a press) > - **Efficiency $\eta$ = MA / VR** (always $\leq 1$, since friction exists) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In **post-harvest engineering**, hydraulic presses serve a multi-fold purpose: 1. **Quality preservation** — Cold pressing (no heat) preserves volatile aromatic compounds, vitamins, and antioxidants in oils. 2. **Energy efficiency** — Hydraulic systems can sustain high pressures with relatively low energy input, since fluid transmission is nearly lossless. 3. **Versatility** — The same press can be adapted for different commodities by changing the cage/filter size. 4. **Safety** — Modern presses incorporate **pressure relief valves**, **burst discs**, and **load limiters** — making them inherently safer than high-RPM mechanical alternatives. ## 2.6 Classification of Hydraulic Presses (KTU 2024) > [!NOTE] > **Types of Hydraulic Presses relevant to PEAGT752** **(a) Based on action:** - **Single-Acting Press** — Pressure applied in one direction only; ram returns by gravity or spring. - **Double-Acting Press** — Pressure applied in both directions; ram extends and retracts actively. **(b) Based on construction:** - **Open-Type (Cage Press)** — Material is wrapped in cloth and pressed between plates. - **Closed-Type (Box / Pot Press)** — Material is enclosed in a perforated chamber. - **Continuous Press** — Used in industrial oil mills (e.g., *Bollman's basket extractor*). **(c) Based on commodity:** - **Oil Press** (copra, groundnut) — high pressure (10–25 MPa). - **Cane Press** (sugarcane) — moderate pressure (5–10 MPa), high throughput. - **Briquetting Press** (biomass) — very high pressure (20–50 MPa). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Force Amplification from Pascal's Law and Volume Conservation ### **Statement:** Prove that the load $F_2$ exerted by a hydraulic press is related to the effort $F_1$ by: $$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ and that the corresponding **displacement ratio** is $x_2 = x_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$. ### **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Define the system.** Consider two pistons in a closed hydraulic circuit: - Plunger (small piston) of cross-sectional area $A_1$ - Ram (large piston) of cross-sectional area $A_2$ - An incompressible hydraulic fluid (e.g., oil) fills the space between them. **Step 2 — Apply an effort.** Let an effort force $F_1$ be applied to the plunger, pushing it inward by a small displacement $x_1$. **Step 3 — Compute the plunger pressure.** By definition of pressure: $$P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Pascal's Law.** Since the fluid is incompressible and static (neglecting velocity head): $$P_1 = P_2 = P$$ **Step 5 — Compute the ram force.** The same pressure $P$ acts on the larger ram area $A_2$: $$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_1} \cdot A_2$$ $$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$ **Step 6 — Apply volume conservation.** The volume of fluid displaced by the plunger equals the volume gained on the ram side: $$V_{displaced} = A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$ **Step 7 — Solve for the ram displacement:** $$\boxed{x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}}$$ **Step 8 — Mechanical Advantage (theoretical):** $$MA_{th} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 9 — Velocity Ratio:** $$VR = \frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 10 — Efficiency (ideal vs real):** $$\eta = \frac{MA_{act}}{VR} = \frac{F_2/F_1}{x_1/x_2}$$ For an ideal press, $\eta = 1$. In reality, $\eta < 1$ due to friction in seals, fluid leakage, and pressure drops in valves. ### **Numerical Verification (Worked Example for KTU ESE):** > **Problem (3-mark type):** A hydraulic press has a plunger of diameter $d_1 = 25 \text{ mm}$ and a ram of diameter $d_2 = 200 \text{ mm}$. An effort of $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$ is applied on the plunger. Find the load that can be lifted and the pressure developed. **Solution:** Areas: $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.200)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 \approx 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$ Pressure (Pascal's Law): $$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4.074 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.407 \text{ MPa}$$ Load on ram: $$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 4.074 \times 10^{5} \times 0.03142 \approx 1.280 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = 12.8 \text{ kN}$$ Alternatively, using the area ratio directly: $$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 200 \times \frac{0.03142}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 200 \times 64 = 12{,}800 \text{ N}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The area ratio is $(200/25)^2 = 64$. This is the **theoretical MA**. The press can therefore lift a load 64 times heavier than the effort. ## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Lever on the Plunger Side In many practical hydraulic presses (e.g., manual coconut oil presses), the effort is applied via a **lever arm** rather than directly. Let the lever have arms of length $L_1$ (effort side) and $L_2$ (plunger side). By the principle of moments (assuming massless lever and pivot at fulcrum): $$F_1 \cdot L_1 = F_{plunger} \cdot L_2$$ $$\boxed{F_{plunger} = F_1 \cdot \frac{L_1}{L_2}}$$ Combined with hydraulic force amplification, the **overall MA** becomes: $$MA_{overall} = \frac{L_1}{L_2} \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Realistic Example (KTU 2019–2023 trend):** - $L_1 = 600 \text{ mm}, L_2 = 100 \text{ mm}, A_2/A_1 = 64$ - Overall MA = $(600/100) \times 64 = 6 \times 64 = 384$ - Effort $F_1 = 50 \text{ N}$ → Load = $50 \times 384 = 19{,}200 \text{ N} = 19.2 \text{ kN}$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Indices of a Hydraulic Press | Index | Formula | Typical Value (Post-Harvest) | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Theoretical MA | $A_2 / A_1$ | 20–200 | Pure geometric amplification | | Actual MA | $F_2 / F_1$ | 15–180 | Includes friction losses | | Velocity Ratio | $A_1 / A_2$ | 0.005–0.05 | Speed of ram (slow for high MA) | | Mechanical Efficiency | $MA_{act}/MA_{th}$ or $W_{out}/W_{in}$ | 0.85–0.95 | Quality of design | | Volumetric Efficiency | $V_{out}/V_{in}$ | 0.95–0.99 | Quality of seals | | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | 0.80–0.92 | Combined indicator | | Pressing Pressure | $F_2/A_2$ | 5–25 MPa | Determines oil yield | | Cake Moisture (output) | measured | 6–10% (oil) | Quality of extraction | | Oil Recovery (yield) | $M_{oil}/M_{seed}$ | 30–45% (single press) | Cold press efficiency | ## 3.4 Energy Analysis (Brief) For a complete stroke, the work done by the effort equals the work done on the load (energy conservation in the ideal case): $$W_{in} = F_1 \cdot x_1$$ $$W_{out} = F_2 \cdot x_2$$ In the ideal (frictionless, leak-free) press: $$F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$$ Substituting $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$ and $x_2 = x_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$: $$F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = F_1 \cdot x_1 \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms **energy is conserved** — it is only *redistributed* between force and distance. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram: Hydraulic Press System > [!IMPORTANT] > The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a hydraulic press used in post-harvest oil extraction, showing the major subsystems, fluid pathways, and control elements. The diagram avoids physical drawing (which Mermaid cannot render faithfully) and instead represents the **information and force flow** between components. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[EFFORT SOURCE
Hand Lever or Motor Pump] --> B[PLUNGER ASSEMBLY
Small Piston Area A1] B --> C[CHECK VALVE 1
Non-Return to Reservoir] B --> D[HIGH PRESSURE FLUID
Hydraulic Oil P] D --> E[RELIEF VALVE
Safety Burst Protection] D --> F[RAM CYLINDER
Large Piston Area A2] F --> G[RAM
Pressing Member] G --> H[CAKE / MATERIAL
Copra Groundnut Sesame] H --> I[PERFORATED CAGE

Storage and Cooling] L --> A classDef power fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef fluid fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef press fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef product fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 class A power class B,C,D,E,K,L fluid class F,G,H,I press class J product ``` ### **Diagram Interpretation (For KTU Board Exam Answer Scripts):** 1. **Effort Source (A)** — The operator's hand or an electric motor provides mechanical energy. 2. **Plunger (B)** — Converts the linear/rotary input into hydraulic pressure. 3. **Non-Return Valve (C)** — Prevents backflow of high-pressure oil to the reservoir. 4. **High-Pressure Fluid (D)** — The "working medium" that transmits pressure per Pascal's Law. 5. **Relief Valve (E)** — A critical safety element; opens at a preset pressure (e.g., 25 MPa) to protect against overpressure. 6. **Ram Cylinder (F)** — Houses the large piston; the force amplification occurs here. 7. **Ram (G)** — The descending member that physically compresses the cake. 8. **Cake (H)** — The oil-bearing material being processed. 9. **Perforated Cage (I)** — Allows oil/juice to escape while retaining the solid cake. 10. **Oil Collection Tray (J)** — Gathers the expressed oil for filtration and storage. ## 4.2 Force and Pressure Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT SIDE"] P1[Effort F1] --> PA1[Pressure F1 over A1] end subgraph FLUID["HYDRAULIC FLUID"] PA1 --> TRANS[Pascal Transmission
P1 equals P2] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SIDE"] TRANS --> PA2[Pressure F2 over A2] PA2 --> P2[Load F2 equals F1 times A2 over A1] end classDef io fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41 class P1,PA1,TRANS,PA2,P2 io ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block: Single-Acting vs Double-Acting Press ```mermaid flowchart LR SA[Single Acting Press] --> SA1[Pressure in ONE direction only] SA1 --> SA2[Ram returns by Gravity or Spring] SA2 --> SA3[Used for: Copra, Mustard] DA[Double Acting Press] --> DA1[Pressure in BOTH directions] DA1 --> DA2[Active extension and retraction] DA2 --> DA3[Used for: Sugarcane, Cocoa] classDef s fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 classDef d fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 class SA,SA1,SA2,SA3 s class DA,DA1,DA2,DA3 d ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern (Quick Reference) | Component | Marks | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | KTU Tag | |---|---|---|---| | Part A — Short Answer (2 Qs × 3 marks) | 6 | Remember / Understand | CO-3, CO-4 | | Part B — Long Answer (1 of 2, 14 marks) | 14 | Apply / Analyze / Evaluate | CO-3, CO-4 | | **Total** | **20** | — | Module 3 | --- ## 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State Pascal's Law. Explain how it is applied in a hydraulic press.** **Model Answer:** Pascal's Law states that *pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid*. In a hydraulic press, a small effort $F_1$ is applied on a plunger of small area $A_1$, generating a pressure $P = F_1/A_1$. This pressure is transmitted undiminished through the hydraulic fluid to the ram of larger area $A_2$. The resulting force on the ram is: $$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ Since $A_2 \gg A_1$, a small input force produces a magnified output force — this is the **working principle of the hydraulic press**, used widely in post-harvest oil and juice extraction. --- ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Mechanical Advantage, Velocity Ratio, and Efficiency of a hydraulic press. How are they related?** **Model Answer:** - **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** is the ratio of load lifted to the effort applied: $MA = F_2/F_1$. In a hydraulic press, theoretically $MA = A_2/A_1$. - **Velocity Ratio (VR)** is the ratio of effort displacement to load displacement: $VR = x_1/x_2 = A_2/A_1$. - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** is the ratio of useful work output to work input, expressed as a percentage. It is also the ratio of actual MA to VR: $$\eta = \frac{MA_{act}}{VR}$$ In an ideal frictionless press, $\eta = 1$. In real presses, $\eta$ is typically 0.85 to 0.95 due to seal friction, fluid leakage, and valve pressure drops. --- ## 5.3 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a hydraulic press** used in the extraction of coconut oil. Mention the role of the relief valve and perforated cage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic press has a plunger of diameter **30 mm** and a ram of diameter **300 mm**. An effort of **350 N** is applied to the plunger. Calculate: - (i) The pressure developed in the fluid. - (ii) The load lifted by the ram. - (iii) The displacement of the ram if the plunger moves down by **40 mm**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Construction and Working of a Hydraulic Press (Coconut Oil Extraction):** A typical **coconut oil hydraulic press** consists of the following components: 1. **Reservoir** — Stores hydraulic oil and provides a return path. 2. **Hand Pump / Motor Pump** — Generates fluid flow and pressure. 3. **Plunger (Small Piston)** — Of small diameter (typically 20–50 mm); the effort $F_1$ is applied here. 4. **High-Pressure Cylinder** — Connects the plunger side to the ram side via a non-return valve. 5. **Ram (Large Piston)** — Of large diameter (200–400 mm); exerts the amplified force. 6. **Perforated Cage / Filter Box** — Holds the crushed copra (dried coconut kernel) and allows oil to escape while retaining the solid cake. 7. **Collection Tray** — Collects the expressed coconut oil. 8. **Relief Valve** — A safety device set to open at a pre-set maximum pressure (e.g., 20 MPa), preventing damage to the system. 9. **Pressure Gauge** — Indicates the operating pressure. **Working:** - Crushed copra is placed inside the perforated cage wrapped in filter cloth. - The pump is activated; pressure builds up in the fluid. - The plunger is pushed down, raising the pressure $P = F_1/A_1$. - This pressure, transmitted via Pascal's Law, acts on the ram of area $A_2$. - The ram descends, compressing the copra cake. - Oil flows out through the perforations and is collected. - Once pressure reaches a set value, the relief valve opens and returns excess fluid to the reservoir. > The **relief valve** protects the system from overpressure damage. The **perforated cage** allows oil to drain while retaining the solid cake — both are indispensable in post-harvest extraction. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $d_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}, \; d_2 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}, \; F_1 = 350 \text{ N}, \; x_1 = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.040 \text{ m}$ **Step 1 — Calculate areas:** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.300)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.09 = 0.07069 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Pressure developed (Pascal's Law):** $$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{350}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4.951 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.495 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Load lifted by the ram:** $$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 4.951 \times 10^{5} \times 0.07069 \approx 3.50 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = 35.0 \text{ kN}$$ (Alternative check using area ratio: $A_2/A_1 = 0.07069 / 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} = 100$; $F_2 = 350 \times 100 = 35{,}000 \text{ N}$ ✓) **Step 4 — Displacement of the ram:** By volume conservation, $A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$: $$x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 40 \times \frac{1}{100} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) Pressure $\approx 0.495 \text{ MPa}$ - (ii) Load $\approx 35 \text{ kN}$ - (iii) Ram displacement $= 0.4 \text{ mm}$ --- ### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`** **(a)** Define **Mechanical Advantage**, **Velocity Ratio**, and **Efficiency** of a hydraulic press. Derive the relationship between them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic press is used in a small-scale oil mill. The plunger diameter is **25 mm**, the ram diameter is **250 mm**, and the effort applied is **250 N** via a hand lever of mechanical advantage **5** (i.e., the lever multiplies the applied effort by 5). If the press operates at an **overall efficiency of 90%**, determine: - (i) The actual load that can be lifted. - (ii) The pressure in the hydraulic fluid. - (iii) The volume of oil delivered per stroke if the plunger travels **50 mm** in one stroke. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Derivation of MA, VR, and Efficiency Relationship:** Let the effort on the plunger be $F_1$ and the load lifted be $F_2$. Let the plunger move down by $x_1$ and the ram move up by $x_2$. **Mechanical Advantage (MA):** $$MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1}$$ For the ideal case (Pascal's Law): $$MA_{th} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Velocity Ratio (VR):** $$VR = \frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \quad \text{(by volume conservation: } A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2\text{)}$$ **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Work input $= F_1 \cdot x_1$ Work output $= F_2 \cdot x_2$ $$\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \frac{F_2 \cdot x_2}{F_1 \cdot x_1} = \frac{F_2/F_1}{x_1/x_2} = \frac{MA}{VR}$$ $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{MA_{act}}{VR}}$$ Since friction always exists, $MA_{act} < VR$ and hence $\eta < 1$. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $d_1 = 25 \text{ mm}, \; d_2 = 250 \text{ mm}, \; F_{applied} = 250 \text{ N}, \; MA_{lever} = 5, \; \eta = 0.90, \; x_1 = 50 \text{ mm}$ **Step 1 — Effective plunger effort (with lever):** $$F_1 = F_{applied} \times MA_{lever} = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate areas:** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.250)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical load (Pascal's Law):** $$F_{2,th} = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 1250 \times \frac{0.04909}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1250 \times 100 = 125{,}000 \text{ N} = 125 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Velocity Ratio:** $$VR = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 100$$ **Step 5 — Actual load (using efficiency):** $$\eta = \frac{MA_{act}}{VR} = \frac{F_2/F_1}{VR}$$ $$F_2 = F_1 \cdot VR \cdot \eta = 1250 \times 100 \times 0.90 = 112{,}500 \text{ N} = 112.5 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 6 — Pressure in the fluid:** $$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{1250}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2.546 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 2.55 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 7 — Volume of fluid displaced per stroke:** $$V = A_1 \cdot x_1 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.050 = 2.455 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 24.55 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) Actual load $= 112.5 \text{ kN}$ - (ii) Pressure $\approx 2.55 \text{ MPa}$ - (iii) Volume per stroke $\approx 24.55 \text{ cm}^3$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS** > > 1. **Unit confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently write area in cm² but plug force in N, leading to dimensionally inconsistent answers. Always convert diameters to **metres** before computing $A = \pi d^2/4$. > > 2. **Forgetting the lever MA (–2 marks):** If the problem mentions a hand lever, the effective effort $F_1$ on the plunger is $F_{applied} \times MA_{lever}$, not just $F_{applied}$. > > 3. **Mixing up $MA$ and $VR$ (–2 marks):** Remember, $MA = A_2/A_1$ but $VR = A_2/A_1$ as well — they are equal in the *ideal* case. The actual MA *is lower* than VR because of friction, and that's why $\eta < 1$. > > 4. **Skipping unit conversions in the final answer (–1 mark):** Always state answers with proper units: N, kN, MPa, m³, cm³, mm. > > 5. **Not drawing a labeled diagram (–2 to –3 marks in part (a)):** A hydraulic press question in KTU almost always requires a labeled schematic. Even a simple block diagram earns partial credit. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **EXAM-DAY RAPID REVISION — HYDRAULIC PRESS** - ✅ **Pascal's Law** is the foundation: $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$. - ✅ **Force amplification** = area ratio $= (d_2/d_1)^2$. - ✅ **Displacement ratio** is the *inverse* of force ratio: $x_2 = x_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$. - ✅ **MA = Load / Effort** ; **VR = Effort distance / Load distance**. - ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = MA_{act} / VR$ — always $\leq 1$. - ✅ **Energy is conserved**, not created — force is gained at the cost of distance. - ✅ **Hydraulic fluid** must be **incompressible** (oil, not gas). - ✅ **Single-acting** press: pressure in one direction only. **Double-acting**: pressure in both directions. - ✅ **Key safety elements**: relief valve, non-return valve, pressure gauge, burst disc. - ✅ **Post-harvest applications**: oil extraction (copra, groundnut, sesame), juice pressing, biomass briquetting. - ✅ **Working pressure range**: 5–25 MPa for agricultural presses. - ✅ **Typical efficiency**: 85–95% for a well-designed unit. - ✅ **Lever on the input side** multiplies the effective effort: $F_{plunger} = F_{hand} \times (L_1/L_2)$. - ✅ **Volume conservation**: $A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ — remember this for displacement problems. - ✅ **Master the three worked examples** above — variations of these appear almost every KTU exam cycle. --- **END OF NOTES — HYDRAULIC PRESS (Module 3, PEAGT752)**

topics.title: Solvent Extraction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Solvent Extraction Methods # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solvent Extraction** is a mass-transfer based unit operation in which a soluble constituent (the *solute*, typically vegetable oil) is separated from an insoluble solid matrix (the *raffinate* or *extracted meal*) by intimate contact with a liquid solvent. The solvent preferentially dissolves the oil, forming a solution termed the **miscella**. The process relies on the principle of **selective diffusion** of the solute through the porous solid structure into the bulk solvent, driven by a concentration gradient. In the post-harvest engineering context, solvent extraction is universally employed to recover residual oil (typically **0.5% – 1.5%** residual fat) from oilseed cakes/flakes that have already been mechanically pre-pressed, achieving overall oil recovery efficiencies of **>98%**. ### Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Answers) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Solute** | The component to be extracted (e.g., triglyceride oil) | | **Solvent** | The selective extracting liquid (e.g., n-hexane, b.p. 65–70 °C) | | **Miscella** | The solution of oil + solvent leaving the extractor | | **Raffinate / Spent Meal** | The oil-depleted solid residue discharged from the extractor | | **Marc** | The solid matrix after extraction (cellular structure) | | **Desolventizing** | Removal of residual solvent from meal and miscella | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Tea Bag in Hot Water** 🍵 > Imagine placing a tea bag (the solid matrix containing the soluble flavor compounds) into a cup of hot water (the solvent). After a few minutes, the water turns brown because the tea compounds have diffused out of the tea leaves into the water. If you replace the water with fresh hot water, more tea compounds come out — this is the principle of **multi-stage counter-current extraction** used industrially. The remaining tea leaves in the bag represent the "spent meal," and the colored water represents the "miscella." **Another Intuition — The Sponge and Ink:** Think of a sponge (the porous oilseed flake) soaked in oil. If you dip it into a clean bath of solvent, the solvent "rinses" the oil out of the sponge's pores. The oil concentration gradient (high inside sponge → low in bulk solvent) is the **driving force** of extraction. ## 1.3 Why Solvent Extraction is Critical in Post-Harvest Engineering - **Residual Oil Recovery**: Mechanical expression alone leaves 6–18% oil in the cake; solvent extraction reduces this to **<1%**. - **Economy**: Solvent (hexane) is recovered (>99.5%) and recycled, making it economically viable. - **Quality of Meal**: Produces high-protein defatted meal for animal feed. - **Versatility**: Applicable to soybean, sunflower, canola, rice bran, groundnut, cottonseed, and copra. ## 1.4 Ideal Solvent Properties (KTU Board Favorite) > [!IMPORTANT] > A perfect solvent for oilseed extraction must satisfy the following criteria: > 1. **High solvent power** for the oil (high solubility) > 2. **Selectivity** — must not extract non-oil components (gums, pigments, phospholipids) > 3. **Low boiling point** (easy recovery) — n-hexane at **69 °C** is the industry standard > 4. **Chemical inertness** — must not react with oil or meal > 5. **Low latent heat of vaporization** — for energy-efficient recovery > 6. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, inexpensive, and readily available** > 7. **Low viscosity** — for better penetration into cellular matrix **Industrial Reality**: **n-Hexane** (a petroleum fraction, primarily C₆H₁₄ isomers) is the most widely used solvent globally, despite its flammability, because of its outstanding balance of all the above properties. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Sequential Mechanisms of Solvent Extraction The extraction of oil from a solid matrix occurs in **three distinct stages** that follow one another during the residence time of solids in the extractor. Understanding these is *critical* for KTU Module-3 questions. ### Stage 1: Free Oil Washing (Unrestricted Drainage) — t = 0 to ~5 min - **What happens**: Solvent contacts the flake surface. The solvent simply **washes off** the ruptured cells' oil film and capillary oil on the exterior. - **Rate of extraction**: **Very fast** — limited only by mixing and surface contact. - **Mechanism**: Bulk convection of the miscella away from the solid surface. - **Fraction of oil removed**: ~**70–80%** of total extractable oil. > [!NOTE] > This stage is so fast that it is often considered a *physical* washing step rather than a true diffusion process. ### Stage 2: Diffusion-Controlled Extraction (Restricted Drainage) — t = 5–30 min - **What happens**: Oil trapped inside intact/unruptured cells must diffuse through the cell wall and porous structure to reach the surface. - **Rate of extraction**: **Slow**, governed by Fick's Law of Diffusion. - **Mechanism**: Molecular diffusion of triglycerides through the cell wall membrane (lipid-protein matrix). - **Fraction of oil removed**: ~**15–25%** additional. ### Stage 3: Slow Final Diffusion (Bound Oil Removal) — t > 30 min - **What happens**: Oil molecules chemically/physically associated with proteins (lipoprotein complexes) are slowly released. - **Rate of extraction**: **Extremely slow**; not economical to pursue in industry beyond a certain point. - **Mechanism**: Disruption of lipoprotein bonds, possibly aided by temperature. ## 2.2 Factors Affecting Solvent Extraction Efficiency (KTU 14-Mark Favorite) > [!IMPORTANT] > A 14-mark question on "factors affecting solvent extraction" appears almost every KTU cycle. Memorize the following: 1. **Particle Size of Flake** - Smaller size → greater surface area → faster extraction - Too small → fines clog percolation, channel solvent, and cause drainage problems - **Optimum flake thickness**: **0.20–0.35 mm** for soybeans 2. **Moisture Content of Flake** - High moisture (>12%) → solvent cannot penetrate cells; water blocks pores - Low moisture (<5%) → flakes become brittle, generate fines - **Optimum**: **8–10%** for soybeans 3. **Temperature** - Higher temperature → lower oil viscosity → higher diffusion coefficient - **Industrial range**: **50–60 °C** (limited by hexane boiling point at atmospheric pressure = 69 °C) 4. **Solvent-to-Solid Ratio** - Higher ratio → greater concentration gradient → faster extraction - **Industrial range**: **0.8:1 to 1.5:1** (solvent:meal, w/w) 5. **Contact / Residence Time** - Standard extractor residence: **30–90 minutes** (counter-current continuous extractors) 6. **Agitation / Mixing Intensity** - Enhances mass transfer coefficient in the miscella film around particles ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $J = -D \, \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** (mass flux $J$) | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | | 2 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_t - C_\infty}{C_0 - C_\infty}\right) = -k \, t$ | **First-order extraction kinetics** (concentration decay of oil in solid) | Dimensionless | | 3 | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot \Delta C_{lm}$ | **Overall mass transfer rate** (miscella draining) | $\text{kg/s}$ | | 4 | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = k \cdot A \cdot (C_s - C_b)$ | **Convective mass transfer** at particle surface | $\text{kg/s}$ | | 5 | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu r}$ | **Stokes–Einstein Diffusion Coefficient** | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | | 6 | $N = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_A - x_R}{1 - x_R}\right]}{\ln R}$ | **Number of ideal stages** (Kremser equation for counter-current leaching) | Dimensionless | | 7 | $R = \dfrac{L}{V} = \dfrac{\text{Solvent flow rate}}{\text{Solid flow rate}}$ | **Solvent-to-solid ratio** | $\text{kg/kg}$ | | 8 | $\eta_{extraction} = \dfrac{m_{oil,extracted}}{m_{oil,total}} \times 100\%$ | **Extraction efficiency** | % | | 9 | $\Delta C_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta C_1 - \Delta C_2}{\ln(\Delta C_1 / \Delta C_2)}$ | **Log-mean concentration difference** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | 10 | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{m_{miscella}}{m_{meal}}$ | **Miscella-to-Retained-Ratio** (extractor operating parameter) | $\text{kg/kg}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Symbols** (Kremser equation): > $x_A$ = mass fraction of solute in feed (solid) | $x_R$ = mass fraction in raffinate | $R = L/V$ solvent-to-solid ratio | $L$ = solvent flow | $V$ = solids flow. ## 2.4 The Kremser Equation — The Mathematical Heart of Multi-Stage Extraction For a **counter-current** extractor with $N$ ideal stages: $$N = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_A - x_R / (1 + R)}{x_N - x_R / (1 + R)}\right]}{\ln(1 + R)}$$ Where: - $x_A$ = solute concentration in incoming solid (feed) - $x_N$ = solute concentration in exiting solid (underflow) - $R = L/V$ (solvent-to-solid mass ratio) > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility**: The Kremser equation lets a process engineer determine the **number of equilibrium stages** required to achieve a target residual oil content in the meal, given an operating solvent ratio. This is the design equation for sizing extractors in KTU numerical problems. ## 2.5 Industrial Solvent Recovery — Material Balance Framework The miscella leaving the extractor contains typically **20–30% oil** and **70–80% solvent**. This must be separated by: 1. **Evaporation** (long-tube vaporizer) 2. **Stripping** (steam-stripping column) 3. **Condensation and recovery** of solvent vapors Mass balance on the miscella system: $$F_{miscella} \cdot x_{oil} = F_{vapor} \cdot y_{solvent} + F_{crude} \cdot z_{oil}$$ Where $F$ = mass flow rates and $x$, $y$, $z$ are mass fractions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Extraction Kinetics Model We derive the governing differential equation for **Stage 2 (Diffusion-Controlled)** extraction. This is a standard KTU numerical problem. ### Setup Let: - $C(t)$ = oil concentration in the solid at time $t$ (kg oil / kg dry solid) - $C_\infty$ = oil concentration in the solid at infinite time (essentially the equilibrium residual) - $k$ = first-order extraction rate constant (min⁻¹) - $C_0$ = initial oil concentration in the solid ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** The rate of oil removal from the solid is assumed to follow first-order kinetics, i.e., the rate is proportional to the deviation from equilibrium: $$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k\,(C - C_\infty)$$ **Step 2:** Separate the variables: $$\frac{dC}{C - C_\infty} = -k\,dt$$ **Step 3:** Integrate both sides from $t = 0$ (where $C = C_0$) to an arbitrary time $t$ (where $C = C_t$): $$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C - C_\infty} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 4:** The left-hand side integrates to a logarithm: $$\Big[\ln(C - C_\infty)\Big]_{C_0}^{C_t} = -k\,t$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate the definite integral: $$\ln(C_t - C_\infty) - \ln(C_0 - C_\infty) = -k\,t$$ **Step 6:** Combine the logarithms: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_t - C_\infty}{C_0 - C_\infty}\right) = -k\,t$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the canonical KTU extraction kinetics equation.** It is linear in $t$ and forms the basis for all extraction efficiency calculations. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem [14-Mark Pattern]**: In a pilot solvent extraction of soybean flakes, the initial oil concentration in the solid is $C_0 = 0.20$ kg oil/kg dry solid. At infinite extraction time, the residual oil concentration reaches $C_\infty = 0.005$ kg/kg. The first-order rate constant at 55 °C is $k = 0.08$ min⁻¹. Calculate: > **(a)** the oil concentration after 30 minutes, and > **(b)** the time required to reach 99% of the equilibrium extraction. ### Part (a) — Concentration at t = 30 min **Step 1:** Substitute into the first-order kinetics equation: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{30} - 0.005}{0.20 - 0.005}\right) = -0.08 \times 30$$ **Step 2:** Compute the right-hand side: $$-0.08 \times 30 = -2.40$$ **Step 3:** Exponentiate both sides: $$\frac{C_{30} - 0.005}{0.195} = e^{-2.40}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the exponential: $$e^{-2.40} = 0.0907$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $C_{30}$: $$C_{30} - 0.005 = 0.195 \times 0.0907 = 0.01769$$ $$C_{30} = 0.01769 + 0.005 = 0.02269 \text{ kg oil/kg solid}$$ **Step 6:** Compute extraction efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{0.20 - 0.02269}{0.20 - 0.005} \times 100\% = \frac{0.17731}{0.195} \times 100\% \approx 90.93\%$$ > **Result**: After 30 min, the oil concentration drops to **0.0227 kg/kg** (~**90.9%** of equilibrium extraction achieved). --- ### Part (b) — Time to Reach 99% of Equilibrium Extraction **Step 1:** "99% of equilibrium extraction" means the oil remaining in the solid is 1% of the deviation $(C_0 - C_\infty)$: $$C_t - C_\infty = 0.01 \times (C_0 - C_\infty)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the kinetics equation: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.01(C_0 - C_\infty)}{C_0 - C_\infty}\right) = -k\,t$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the logarithm: $$\ln(0.01) = -k\,t$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $t$: $$t = \frac{-\ln(0.01)}{k} = \frac{\ln(100)}{0.08}$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate: $$t = \frac{4.6052}{0.08} = 57.57 \text{ min}$$ > **Result**: Approximately **57.6 minutes** are required to reach 99% of the equilibrium extraction. This justifies the industrial residence time of ~60 min in continuous extractors. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Log-Mean Concentration Difference (LMCD) This is a critical mass-transfer quantity for calculating the **overall driving force** in continuous extractors. ### Setup In a counter-current extractor, the concentration difference between the solid phase and the miscella phase changes along the length. If the inlet driving force is $\Delta C_1$ and the outlet driving force is $\Delta C_2$, the **effective average** is the log-mean: $$\Delta C_{lm} = \frac{\Delta C_1 - \Delta C_2}{\ln(\Delta C_1 / \Delta C_2)}$$ ### Derivation (Sketch) For a differential slice $dA$ of contact area, the differential mass transferred is: $$dN = K \cdot (C_s - C_b) \cdot dA$$ For linear concentration profiles (constant $K$), integrating between the two ends yields the LMCD form, provided the rate is governed by: $$\frac{dN}{dA} = K \cdot (C_s - C_b)$$ and the mass balance $N = L \cdot (y_1 - y_2) = V \cdot (x_1 - x_2)$ is linear. The standard integral: $$\int_{\Delta C_1}^{\Delta C_2} \frac{d(\Delta C)}{\Delta C} = -\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta C_1}{\Delta C_2}\right)$$ combined with the linear relationship $N = K \cdot \Delta C_{lm} \cdot A_{total}$ gives the LMCD result. > [!NOTE] > **Special case**: If $\Delta C_1 \approx \Delta C_2$, use the arithmetic mean — this occurs in extractors with very high solvent flow (constant driving force). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Solvent Extraction Plant (Mermaid) > [!IMPORTANT] > The diagram below represents the **end-to-end flow** of an industrial continuous solvent extraction plant. This block diagram is a **Board-Examiner favorite** for 7-mark sub-questions in KTU ESE. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Oilseed Flakes Feed
Pre-pressed cake] --> A1[Pre-treatment
Drying and Flaking] A1 --> A2[Extractor Unit
Counter-current contact] S0[Fresh n-Hexane
Solvent Storage] --> A2 A2 --> B1[Full Miscella
Oil plus Hexane 20 to 30% oil] A2 --> C1[Spent Meal
Oil less than 1%] B1 --> B2[Long-Tube Evaporator
First stage concentration] B2 --> B3[Steam Stripping Column
Final solvent removal] B3 --> B4[Crude Oil
Residual solvent less than 50 ppm] B3 --> B5[Solvent Vapor plus Steam] B5 --> B6[Condenser
Heat exchanger] B6 --> B7[Solvent Water Separator
Decanter] B7 --> S0 C1 --> C2[Desolventizer Toaster
DTDC Steam jacket] C2 --> C3[Defatted Meal
