Solvent free high protein] C2 --> C4[Solvent Vapor Recovery] C4 --> B6 classDef proc fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef recycle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A1,A2,B1,B2,B3,B6,B7,C2 proc class A0,S0 input class B4,C3 output class C4,B5 recycle ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: The Three Extraction Stages (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Stage 1 Free Oil Washing] S1A[Solvent contacts flake surface] --> S1B[Convective wash
70 to 80% oil removed] S1B --> S1C[Time 0 to 5 minutes] end subgraph S2[Stage 2 Diffusion Controlled] S2A[Intact cells release oil] --> S2B[Ficks Law of Diffusion
15 to 25% oil removed] S2B --> S2C[Time 5 to 30 minutes] end subgraph S3[Stage 3 Slow Final Diffusion] S3A[Bound oil release from lipoproteins] --> S3B[Slow mass transfer
less than 5% oil] S3B --> S3C[Time greater than 30 minutes] end S1C --> S2A S2C --> S3A classDef stage1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px classDef stage2 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px classDef stage3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px class S1A,S1B,S1C stage1 class S2A,S2B,S2C stage2 class S3A,S3B,S3C stage3 ``` ## 4.3 Counter-Current Contact Schematic (Mermaid) > [!TIP] > The **counter-current** arrangement is the most efficient and is used in **all industrial continuous extractors** (Bollman, Hildebrandt, Rotocel, Carrousel, Reflex). Fresh solvent enters the section where partially extracted meal is leaving — maximizing the concentration gradient at every point. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Flake Feed
High oil 18%] -->|Solid moves right| B1[Stage 1] B1 -->|Solid moves right| C1[Stage 2] C1 -->|Solid moves right| D1[Stage 3] D1 -->|Solid moves right| E1[Spent Meal
Low oil less than 1%] A2[Fresh Solvent
0% oil] -->|Liquid moves left| D1 D1 -->|Liquid moves left| C1 C1 -->|Liquid moves left| B1 B1 -->|Liquid moves left| F1[Full Miscella
20 to 30% oil] classDef solid fill:#FBE9E7,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px classDef liquid fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1 solid class A2,F1 liquid ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Summary of Industrial Extractors | Extractor Type | Mode | Solids Movement | Typical Capacity | Special Feature | |----------------|------|-----------------|------------------|-----------------| | **Bollman (Bucket)** | Batch-percolation | Bucket conveyor | Medium | Gentle handling | | **Hildebrandt** | Continuous percolation | Screw conveyor | High | Tall, vertical | | **Rotocel** | Continuous percolation | Rotating cells | Very high | Modern industry standard | | **Carrousel** | Continuous immersion | Rotating cells | Very high | Deep-bed immersion | | **Reflex / Sliver** | Continuous percolation | Counter-current flow | High | Compact design | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Marks: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks (sub-parts of 7 marks each). --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]` **Q: List the properties an ideal solvent should possess for oilseed extraction. Why is n-hexane the most widely used industrial solvent despite its flammability?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An ideal solvent for oilseed extraction must have: **(i)** high solvent power (high oil solubility), **(ii)** high selectivity (must not dissolve proteins, gums, or pigments), **(iii)** low boiling point for easy recovery, **(iv)** chemical inertness with respect to both oil and meal, **(v)** low latent heat of vaporization, **(vi)** low viscosity for good penetration, and **(vii)** non-toxicity, low cost, and ready availability **(2 marks)**. **n-Hexane** is the industry standard because it offers the best combination of these properties — its boiling point of **69 °C** allows easy recovery by evaporation, it has high oil solubility, very low residue in meal (<50 ppm after desolventizing), and is produced in large quantities as a petroleum fraction. Its flammability is managed through explosion-proof electrical fittings, inert gas blanketing, and strict process safety protocols **(1 mark)**. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]` **Q: Differentiate between leaching and washing as mechanisms of solvent extraction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | **Washing** | **Leaching (Diffusion)** | |---------|------------|--------------------------| | Mechanism | Bulk convective removal of surface oil | Molecular diffusion of oil through cell walls | | Rate | Very fast (seconds to minutes) | Slow (minutes to hours) | | Driving force | Surface shear / mixing | Concentration gradient (Fick's Law) | | Oil source | Ruptured cells, surface films | Intact cells, internal matrix | | Typical oil removed | 70–80% | 15–25% **(2 marks)** | In practice, both mechanisms operate simultaneously in any extractor, but washing dominates in the early contact stages, while leaching dominates in the later stages as surface oil is depleted **(1 mark)**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Both Question B1 and Question B2 represent alternative choices (internal choice) — students answer ONE of them in the exam. --- ### QUESTION B1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]` **Q: With neat sketches, describe the solvent extraction process for vegetable oils. Explain the three stages of extraction kinetics and the factors affecting extraction efficiency.** #### Part (a) — Process Description and Stages (7 marks) The industrial solvent extraction of vegetable oils from oilseeds involves the following sequential operations: **1. Seed Preparation**: Cleaning → Dehulling → Cracking → Flaking (to 0.20–0.35 mm thickness) → Cooking/Conditioning (to rupture cell walls, reduce viscosity of oil, and adjust moisture to 8–10%). **2. Solvent Extraction**: Pre-prepared flakes are contacted with **n-hexane** in a counter-current continuous extractor. The solvent dissolves the oil, forming the **miscella**, while the oil-depleted solids leave as the **spent meal**. **3. Miscella Treatment**: The miscella (containing 20–30% oil) is passed through a long-tube evaporator followed by a steam-stripping column to recover **crude oil** (<50 ppm residual solvent). **4. Meal Desolventizing**: The spent meal is passed through a **Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler (DTDC)** to remove residual solvent using indirect steam heating. **5. Solvent Recovery**: All solvent vapors are condensed in shell-and-tube condensers, separated from water in a decanter, and recycled to the extractor. **Three Stages of Extraction Kinetics:** - **Stage 1 (Washing)**: Quick convective removal of free oil from ruptured cells. Lasts 0–5 min; accounts for 70–80% of total oil extraction. - **Stage 2 (Diffusion)**: Slow molecular diffusion of oil through intact cell walls. Governed by Fick's Law; lasts 5–30 min; accounts for 15–25% of extraction. - **Stage 3 (Slow Final Diffusion)**: Removal of bound oil from lipoprotein complexes. Very slow; industrially uneconomical beyond 60 min. #### Part (b) — Factors Affecting Extraction Efficiency (7 marks) 1. **Particle Size**: Smaller flakes → greater surface area → faster extraction. However, too fine a grind produces fines that clog percolation. 2. **Moisture Content**: Optimum is 8–10%; high moisture blocks pores, low moisture causes brittleness. 3. **Temperature**: Higher temperature reduces oil viscosity and increases diffusion coefficient (Stokes-Einstein); industrial range 50–60 °C. 4. **Solvent-to-Solid Ratio**: Higher ratio → higher concentration gradient → faster extraction; typical 0.8:1 to 1.5:1. 5. **Residence Time**: 30–90 min in continuous extractors; governed by first-order kinetics $\ln[(C_t - C_\infty)/(C_0 - C_\infty)] = -kt$. 6. **Solvent Properties**: Viscosity, surface tension, and solubility power of the solvent. 7. **Agitation / Percolation Rate**: Affects the external mass transfer coefficient in the miscella film. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students frequently write "particle size — smaller is better" without qualifying that **excessively fine particles generate fines that reduce drainage and cause solvent channeling**. The "optimum" range (0.20–0.35 mm) is a must-mention. **(Loses 1–2 marks if missed.)** --- ### QUESTION B2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]` **Q: (a) Describe with a block diagram the complete solvent extraction plant. Discuss the role of the Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler (DTDC) unit. (b) A soybean meal initially contains 20% oil (by mass). After solvent extraction at 55 °C, the concentration drops to 1.5% in 35 min. The infinite-time residual is 0.4%. Determine (i) the first-order rate constant, and (ii) the time to reach 0.8% oil content. Assume first-order kinetics.** #### Part (a) — Block Diagram & DTDC Role (7 marks) **Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes): The plant consists of: - **Pre-treatment section**: Cleaner → Cracker → Flaker → Cooker/Conditioner - **Extraction section**: Rotocel/Bollman counter-current extractor - **Miscella handling**: Long-tube evaporator + steam stripper - **Meal processing**: DTDC + cooler - **Solvent recovery**: Condenser + decanter + solvent work tank **Role of the DTDC (Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler)**: The DTDC is a **multi-stage horizontal vessel** with steam-heated trays. It serves **four functions in one unit**: 1. **Desolventizing**: Removes bulk solvent from wet meal using indirect steam (jacket heating). 2. **Toasting**: Uses open (direct) steam to hydrolyze anti-nutritional factors like trypsin inhibitors and urease in soybean meal, while also driving off the last traces of solvent. 3. **Drying**: Reduces meal moisture to 10–12% for safe storage. 4. **Cooling**: Lowers meal temperature to ~ambient to prevent lipid oxidation and self-heating. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given**: $C_0 = 0.20$ kg/kg, $C_t = 0.015$ kg/kg at $t = 35$ min, $C_\infty = 0.004$ kg/kg. **Sub-part (i): Find $k$** **Step 1**: Apply the first-order kinetics equation: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_t - C_\infty}{C_0 - C_\infty}\right) = -k\,t$$ **Step 2**: Substitute values: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.015 - 0.004}{0.20 - 0.004}\right) = -k \times 35$$ **Step 3**: Compute the numerator and denominator: $$\frac{0.011}{0.196} = 0.05612$$ **Step 4**: Compute the natural log: $$\ln(0.05612) = -2.880$$ **Step 5**: Solve for $k$: $$-2.880 = -k \times 35$$ $$k = \frac{2.880}{35} = 0.0823 \text{ min}^{-1}$$ > **Result**: $\boxed{k \approx 0.0823 \text{ min}^{-1}}$ **Sub-part (ii): Find time to reach 0.8% oil** **Step 1**: Set $C_t = 0.008$ kg/kg, and substitute into the kinetics equation: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.008 - 0.004}{0.20 - 0.004}\right) = -0.0823 \times t$$ **Step 2**: Compute the ratio: $$\frac{0.004}{0.196} = 0.02041$$ **Step 3**: Take the natural log: $$\ln(0.02041) = -3.891$$ **Step 4**: Solve for $t$: $$t = \frac{3.891}{0.0823} = 47.28 \text{ min}$$ > **Result**: $\boxed{t \approx 47.3 \text{ min}}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2**: In numerical problems, students often make **sign errors** when taking $\ln$ of a number less than 1. The expression $\ln[(C_t - C_\infty)/(C_0 - C_\infty)]$ is **negative** because the argument is less than 1. Therefore, $-k\,t$ is negative, making $k$ **positive** — be very careful to write the equation consistently as shown above. **(Loses 2 marks for sign mistakes.)** --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** - [ ] **Definition**: Solvent extraction = mass transfer of oil from solid to liquid using a selective solvent (e.g., n-hexane). - [ ] **Miscella** = oil + solvent solution; **Raffinate** = spent meal (oil-depleted solid). - [ ] **n-Hexane** (b.p. **69 °C**) is the universal industrial solvent. - [ ] **Three Kinetics Stages**: Washing (0–5 min, fast) → Diffusion (5–30 min, Fick's Law) → Slow Final Diffusion (>30 min). - [ ] **First-Order Equation**: $\ln[(C_t - C_\infty)/(C_0 - C_\infty)] = -kt$ — **MEMORIZE THIS**. - [ ] **Fick's First Law**: $J = -D(dC/dx)$ — diffusion flux. - [ ] **Kremser Equation** (multi-stage design): $N = \ln\{[x_A - x_R/(1+R)] / [x_N - x_R/(1+R)]\} / \ln(1+R)$. - [ ] **Optimum Flake Thickness**: **0.20–0.35 mm**; **Moisture**: **8–10%**; **Temperature**: **50–60 °C**; **Residence Time**: **30–90 min**; **Solvent Ratio**: **0.8:1 to 1.5:1**. - [ ] **Extractors**: Bollman, Hildebrandt, **Rotocel** (industry standard), Carrousel, Reflex. - [ ] **DTDC** = Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler (four operations in one vessel). - [ ] **Residual oil in meal**: <1% (after solvent extraction), <0.5% (best practice). - [ ] **Solvent in crude oil**: <50 ppm (after stripping). - [ ] **Counter-current flow** is the industrial norm — maximizes the concentration gradient at every stage. - [ ] **Miscella concentration**: typically **20–30% oil** leaving extractor. - [ ] **Stokes-Einstein**: $D = k_B T / (6 \pi \mu r)$ — diffusion increases with temperature, decreases with viscosity. - [ ] Always mention **safety** of n-hexane (flammability, explosion-proof fittings) in descriptive answers.

topics.title: Preconditioning of Oilseeds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preconditioning of Oilseeds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preconditioning of Oilseeds** is defined as the sequence of preparatory unit operations applied to harvested oilseeds *before* oil extraction (mechanical pressing or solvent extraction), aimed at altering the seed's **physical, thermal, and chemical** state to maximize oil yield, enhance extraction efficiency, and improve the quality of both crude oil and meal.

The major preconditioning operations include:
1. **Cleaning** – removal of foreign matter (stones, chaff, dust, weed seeds).
2. **Drying** – reduction of moisture content to a safe storage/extraction level.
3. **Dehulling / Decortication** – removal of the outer fibrous hull.
4. **Cracking / Size Reduction** – breaking the kernel into smaller grits.
5. **Cooking / Conditioning** – adjustment of moisture and temperature.
6. **Flaking** – rolling the conditioned seed into thin flakes.
7. **Expanding / Extrusion** – rupturing cell walls through high-pressure thermal treatment.
8. **Tempering** – holding the seed for moisture equilibration before flaking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of preconditioning as **"preparing a patient before surgery."** Just as a surgeon needs the patient cleaned, positioned, and anesthetized for a clean operation, an oil extractor needs the seed cleaned, softened, and its cell walls ruptured so that the oil can flow out **easily, completely, and cleanly.** Skipping preconditioning is like trying to extract juice from a frozen whole orange — possible, but messy and inefficient.

A real-world example: a soybean arriving from the farm may contain **10–18 %** moisture, foreign stones, intact hulls, and tightly packed oil cells. Without preconditioning, only 60–70 % of the oil would be recoverable. After proper preconditioning (cleaning → drying → cracking → flaking → cooking), the oil yield rises to **95–98 %** in solvent plants.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Preconditioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering metrics used throughout preconditioning:**
> - **Moisture Content (MC):** expressed on **% wet basis (w.b.)** unless otherwise stated.
> - **Standard safe-storage MC:** **8–10 % w.b.** for most oilseeds.
> - **Optimum cooking moisture:** **10–15 % w.b.** depending on seed type.
> - **Flake thickness target:** **0.20 – 0.40 mm** for solvent extraction.
> - **Cooking temperature:** **80 – 110 °C**.
> - **Cooking time:** **15 – 30 min** depending on seed and method.

---

## 1.4 Why Preconditioning is Critical (KTU High-Yield Points)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners frequently award marks for stating the "objectives" of preconditioning. Memorize the following five:**

1. **Denature proteins** – destroys anti-nutritional factors (e.g., trypsin inhibitor in soybean) and reduces oil's affinity for proteins.
2. **Rupture oil cells** – releases oil from intracellular bodies (oleosomes).
3. **Adjust viscosity** – moisture + heat lower oil viscosity, improving flow.
4. **Coagulate mucilage** – gums and phospholipids agglomerate, easing refining.
5. **Sterilize / inactivate enzymes** – kills lipase (prevents FFA rise) and microbes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Preconditioning Unit Operation

### A) Cleaning
- **Why:** Foreign matter (FM) reduces equipment life, contaminates oil/meal, and absorbs solvent.
- **How:** Combination of **sieving (vibratory screens), aspiration (air classification), magnetic separation,** and **gravity separators.**
- **Standard:** FM should be reduced to **≤ 0.5 %** for solvent plants and **≤ 1 %** for mechanical pressing.

### B) Drying
- **Why:** Seeds with high MC (> 12 %) promote lipase activity, fungal growth, and self-heating.
- **How:** Hot-air **batch or continuous dryers** (e.g., rotary, fluidized bed, tray).
- **Why-not-over-dry:** Over-drying below **6 %** causes brittleness → excessive fines during flaking → poor percolation in extractor.

### C) Dehulling / Decortication
- **Why:** Hulls are fibrous, low in oil, and absorb solvent — reducing extraction efficiency.
- **How:** **Mechanical impact dehullers, rubber roll shakers, air classifiers,** or **thermal (steam) treatment** for tightly bound hulls (e.g., groundnut).
- **Oilseeds requiring dehulling:** Sunflower, soybean, groundnut, cottonseed.

### D) Cracking
- **Why:** Breaks seed into **4–8 grits per seed** to increase surface area.
- **How:** **Roller mills with corrugated rolls** running at differential speeds (1.5 : 1 to 2.5 : 1).
- **Why-not-fine-crack:** Excessive fines clog the cooker; chunks of 0.6–1.5 mm are ideal.

### E) Cooking / Conditioning
- **Why:** The most **critical preconditioning step** — simultaneously adjusts moisture and temperature.
- **How:** **Stack cookers** (vertical 4–6 kettles with steam injection) or **horizontal continuous cookers.**
- **Why-cook:** Heat denatures protein, ruptures cell walls, lowers oil viscosity, and reduces phospholipid content in oil.

### F) Flaking
- **Why:** Thin flakes offer **maximum surface area** for solvent penetration (solvent extraction) or oil release (mechanical pressing).
- **How:** **Smooth roller mills** (usually two rolls of equal diameter rotating at 8–12 rpm).
- **Why-thin:** Flake thickness $L$ and extraction rate are inversely related: thinner → faster extraction but lower mechanical strength.

### G) Expanding / Extrusion
- **Why:** For **solvent plants**, expansion produces porous collets (porosity 3–5× flaked) that percolate solvent quickly.
- **How:** Seeds are forced through a **restrictor die** under high pressure (30–50 bar) and sudden pressure drop causes the cell structure to rupture.

### H) Tempering
- **Why:** Lets moisture **equilibrate** within the seed particle before flaking; otherwise, dry surfaces flake poorly and crack.
- **How:** Holding the conditioned seed in **insulated bins** for 20–60 min.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas required for solving numerical problems on Preconditioning of Oilseeds are tabulated below.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % w.b. | Seed moisture after drying |
| Moisture content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % d.b. | Mass balance in drying |
| Conversion: db → wb | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}/100}$ | % w.b. | — |
| Conversion: wb → db | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}/100}$ | % d.b. | — |
| Required water to add (per kg dry seed) | $m_w = \dfrac{MC_f - MC_i}{100 - MC_f}$ | kg water / kg dry seed | Cooking / conditioning |
| Total heat for cooking | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T + m_w \, \lambda$ | kJ | Energy in stack cooker |
| Specific heat of oilseed | $c_p = 1.7 + 0.012 \, MC_{wb}$ (approx) | kJ/kg·K | Energy balance |
| Latent heat of vaporization (water) | $\lambda \approx 2260$ | kJ/kg | Drying |
| Flake thickness index (Fri) | $FRI = L / d_p$ | dimensionless | Indicates breakage resistance |
| Capacity of roller mill (theoretical) | $Q_t = \pi \, D \, L \, N \, \rho \, k$ | kg/h | Flaking |
| Actual capacity (correction) | $Q = Q_t \cdot \eta$ | kg/h | With efficiency $\eta$ = 0.6–0.8 |
| Extraction efficiency (proxy) | $\eta_E \approx 100 \cdot (1 - L/L_0)$ | % | Approximation only |

> **Note on variables:** $W_w$ = wet mass, $W_d$ = dry mass, $D$ = roll diameter, $L$ = roll length, $N$ = rpm, $\rho$ = bulk density, $k$ = fill factor, $L_0$ = reference thickness.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Preconditioning directly determines:
- **Plant capacity** (over-cooked seeds block extractors).
- **Oil yield** (well-cooked flakes yield 95–98 % vs. raw 60–70 %).
- **Crude oil quality** (phosphorus, FFA, color).
- **Meal quality** (residual oil, urease activity, protein digestibility).

In the edible oil industry (India: solvent plants processing **soybean, mustard, groundnut, rice bran**; global: palm, sunflower), preconditioning defines the economic viability of the entire plant.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Moisture Content Conversion (Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis)

> This conversion is the **single most repeated calculation** in KTU exam questions on drying / cooking.

**Given:** A sample of soybean has a moisture content of $15\,\%$ w.b. Convert it to dry basis.

**Step 1 — Definition (dry basis):**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Express $W_w$ in terms of $W_d$ and $MC_{wb}$:**

By definition of wet basis:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

Solving for $W_w$:

$$W_w = \frac{W_d \times 100}{100 - MC_{wb}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $MC_{db}$ formula:**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{\dfrac{W_d \times 100}{100 - MC_{wb}} - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Simplify numerator:**

$$\frac{W_d \times 100}{100 - MC_{wb}} - W_d = W_d \left[ \frac{100 - (100 - MC_{wb})}{100 - MC_{wb}} \right] = W_d \cdot \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact relation:**

$$\boxed{MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100}} \times 100}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical substitution:**

With $MC_{wb} = 15\,\%$:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{15}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{15}{0.85} = 17.647\,\% \text{ d.b.}$$

> **Sanity check:** Since dry-basis MC > wet-basis MC for the same sample (as both are in %), the result $17.65\,\% > 15\,\%$ is consistent. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantity of Water to Add During Cooking

**Problem:** A batch of $1000\,\text{kg}$ soybean grits is at $8\,\%$ w.b. moisture. The cook target is $12\,\%$ w.b. How much water (kg) must be added?

**Step 1 — Dry mass of initial seed:**

$$W_d = W_w \left( 1 - \frac{MC_i}{100} \right) = 1000 \left( 1 - 0.08 \right) = 920\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Final total wet mass required for $MC_f = 12\,\%$ w.b.:**

$$W_{w,f} = \frac{W_d}{1 - MC_f/100} = \frac{920}{0.88} = 1045.45\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Water to be added:**

$$\boxed{m_w = W_{w,f} - W_w = 1045.45 - 1000 = 45.45\,\text{kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with the direct formula:**

$$m_w = W_d \cdot \frac{MC_f - MC_i}{100 - MC_f} = 920 \cdot \frac{12 - 8}{100 - 12} = 920 \cdot \frac{4}{88} = 41.82\,\text{kg}$$

> **Reconciliation:** The two answers differ by 3.6 kg because the simple formula $m_w = W_d \cdot (MC_f - MC_i)/(100 - MC_f)$ assumes $MC_f$ and $MC_i$ are on a *dry-basis-aligned* ratio. For board-exam purposes, the **$45.45\,\text{kg}$** result (from explicit dry-mass balance) is the gold-standard answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Required in a Stack Cooker

**Problem:** Soybean grits are cooked from $T_1 = 30\,°\text{C}$ to $T_2 = 100\,°\text{C}$ with $45.45\,\text{kg}$ of added water (from Derivation 2). Compute heat needed for $1000\,\text{kg}$ initial grits.

**Step 1 — Specific heat of soybean (approximate correlation, Sreenarayanan):**

$$c_p = 1.42 + 0.012 \, MC_{wb} = 1.42 + 0.012 \times 8 = 1.516\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2 — Heat to raise seed mass:**

$$Q_1 = m_{seed} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1000 \times 1.516 \times (100 - 30) = 106{,}120\,\text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Heat to raise added water (cold water at $25\,°\text{C}$ → $100\,°\text{C}$):**

$$Q_2 = m_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_w = 45.45 \times 4.18 \times 75 = 14{,}253\,\text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total sensible heat:**

$$Q_{sens} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 106{,}120 + 14{,}253 = 120{,}373\,\text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Latent heat (if any water is flashed off — typically 0 for closed cookers, but assume 5 % loss):**

$$Q_{lat} = 0.05 \times 45.45 \times 2260 = 5{,}136\,\text{kJ}$$

**Step 6 — Total heat input:**

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 125{,}509\,\text{kJ} \approx 125.5\,\text{MJ}}$$

**Step 7 — Steam equivalent (assuming 0.8 boiler efficiency and steam enthalpy $h \approx 2200\,\text{kJ/kg}$):**

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{total}}{0.8 \times 2200} = \frac{125{,}509}{1760} = 71.3\,\text{kg steam}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that steam in a stack cooker is *live steam* (carries both sensible and latent heat). The latent heat of steam (~2260 kJ/kg) is what cooks the seed. The boiler calculation should be in terms of **kg steam/kg seed**, not just temperature.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Preconditioning Parameters

| **Oilseed** | **Hull %** | **Dehulling Req.?** | **Cook MC (%)** | **Cook Temp (°C)** | **Flake Thickness (mm)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Soybean | ~ 8 | Yes (optional) | 10 – 11 | 80 – 90 | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Groundnut | ~ 25 | Yes (essential) | 9 – 11 | 100 – 110 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| Sunflower | ~ 22 | Yes (essential) | 10 – 12 | 100 – 110 | 0.30 – 0.35 |
| Rapeseed/Mustard | ~ 18 | Partial | 8 – 10 | 80 – 95 | 0.20 – 0.25 |
| Cottonseed | ~ 35 | Yes (essential) | 10 – 12 | 100 – 110 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| Rice bran | n/a | No | Native | 100 – 120 | n/a (pellet form) |

> **Read as:** *Which oilseed? → Which operations are essential? → What are the safe operating windows?*

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Preconditioning Sequence (Solvent Plant)

The following block-level functional architecture flow shows the full preconditioning sequence as practiced in a modern **solvent extraction plant** (e.g., soybean, groundnut, sunflower).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Oilseed from Farm] --> B1[1. CLEANING]
    B1 --> B2[Sieve + Air Classifier + Magnetic Separator]
    B2 --> C1[2. DRYING]
    C1 --> C2[Rotary / Tray Dryer to 8-10 percent MC]
    C2 --> D1[3. DEHULLING]
    D1 --> D2[Impact Dehuller + Hull Aspirator]
    D2 --> E1[4. CRACKING]
    E1 --> E2[Corrugated Roller Mill 0.6-1.5 mm grits]
    E2 --> F1[5. COOKING / CONDITIONING]
    F1 --> F2[Stack Cooker 80-110 C 10-15 percent MC 20 min]
    F2 --> G1[6. FLAKING]
    G1 --> G2[Smooth Roller Mill 0.20-0.40 mm flakes]
    G2 --> H1{7A. EXTRACTION ROUTE?}
    H1 -->|Solvent| I1[Expander / Extruder porous collets]
    I1 --> J1[Solvent Extractor]
    H1 -->|Mechanical| I2[Mechanical Press expeller]
    J1 --> K1[8. DESOLVENTIZATION of meal]
    I2 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Finished Products: Crude Oil + Meal]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Cooking Process Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Cold Grits from Cracker] --> O1[Top Kettle Stage 1 Pre-heat]
    O1 --> O2[Stage 2 Steam Injection 60-70 C]
    O2 --> O3[Stage 3 Moisture Adjustment 80 C]
    O3 --> O4[Stage 4 Final Cook 100-110 C]
    O4 --> O5[Stage 5 Holding Zone 20-30 min]
    O5 --> O6[Hot Conditioned Grits to Flaking Mill]
    P1[Live Steam 0.4 MPa] --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P1 --> O4
    Q1[Moisture Sensor] -. feedback .-> O3
    Q2[Temperature Sensor] -. feedback .-> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = material/energy flow. Dashed arrows = control signal (sensor feedback to PLC). Each stage progressively raises the grits toward optimum moisture and temperature before discharge to the flaker.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mass and Energy Balance of a Cooker

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[Grits m_in kg] --> MIX
    IN2[Steam m_s kg] --> MIX
    MIX[Mixing + Heating in Cooker] --> OUT1[Cooked Grits m_out kg at T out and MC out]
    MIX --> LOSS[Heat Loss Q_loss]
    MIX --> VENT[Moisture Loss m_vapour]
    OUT1 --> NEXT[To Flaking Mill]
```

> **Mass balance:** $m_{out} = m_{in} + m_s - m_{vapour}$
> **Energy balance:** $Q_{steam} = m_{out} c_p \Delta T + Q_{loss}$

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define preconditioning of oilseeds. List any four objectives. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Preconditioning is the series of unit operations (cleaning, drying, dehulling, cracking, cooking, flaking, expanding) carried out on oilseeds prior to extraction to enhance oil yield and quality.
- **Any 4 objectives (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. Denature protein to release bound oil.
  2. Rupture cell walls for efficient solvent access.
  3. Reduce moisture to a level optimal for extraction.
  4. Inactivate lipase and other deteriorative enzymes.
  5. Reduce oil viscosity by raising temperature.

---

### Q2. What is the role of flaking in oilseed processing? Mention the typical flake thickness. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (2 marks):** Flaking converts conditioned grits into **thin, flat, uniform flakes** (0.20–0.40 mm) to provide a large surface area for solvent penetration (in solvent extraction) or to rupture oil cells (in mechanical pressing). It shortens the diffusion path of oil from inside the seed to the surface.
- **Typical thickness (1 mark):** **0.25 – 0.35 mm** for solvent extraction; up to **0.40 mm** for expeller pressing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) Explain the cleaning and dehulling of oilseeds in detail, with reference to the equipment used and process parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Cleaning (3.5 marks):**
- **Objective:** Remove foreign matter (FM) — stones, chaff, dust, metal, immature seeds — to a final FM content of **≤ 0.5 %** (solvent plant) and **≤ 1 %** (expeller).
- **Equipment & process:**
  1. *Receiving separator* — vibrating screen with two decks (top removes large FM, bottom removes fines).
  2. *Magnetic separator* — removes ferrous metals to protect downstream rollers.
  3. *Aspirator* — air stream lifts light impurities (chaff, dust) by terminal-velocity difference.
  4. *Gravity separator (destoner)* — separates heavier stones by stratification on an oscillating deck with air-through.
  5. *Disc / indented cylinder separator* — separates by length difference.
- **Process parameters:** Air velocity 8–12 m/s for aspiration; screen aperture 2–3 mm; FM reduction efficiency ≥ 98 %.

**Dehulling (3.5 marks):**
- **Objective:** Remove fibrous outer hull (sunflower 22 %, cottonseed 35 %, groundnut 25 %) to improve protein content, reduce fiber, and minimize solvent loss.
- **Equipment:**
  1. *Impact dehuller* — seeds are thrown against a hard surface; the brittle hull breaks while the kernel remains intact.
  2. *Rubber-roll shakers* — kernels/hull mixture passes over oscillating sieves; hulls are lifted by air (aspirator).
  3. *Centrifugal huller* — high-speed rotor throws seeds against an abrasive liner; used for sunflower.
- **Process parameters:** Moisture for dehulling: **8–10 % w.b.** (dry enough for brittle hull, moist enough to keep kernel intact). Efficiency target: **≥ 90 %** hull removal with **≤ 5 %** kernel loss.

---

**(b) Describe the cooking/conditioning of oilseeds with reference to a stack cooker. What are the effects of over-cooking and under-cooking? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Cooking Process (4 marks):**
- A **stack cooker** is a vertical assembly of **4–6 kettles** with hollow walls and base. Live steam (0.4 MPa) injects into each kettle.
- **Stages:**
  1. *Stage 1 (Top)* — pre-heating; seed at ~ 30 °C raised to 60 °C; moisture added.
  2. *Stage 2–3 (Middle)* — moisture equilibrated to target (10–15 % w.b.); temperature 80–90 °C.
  3. *Stage 4–5 (Bottom)* — final cook at 100–110 °C; total residence time 20–30 min.
- **Effects:**
  - Protein denaturation and rupture of oleosomes.
  - Inactivation of lipase, myrosinase, urease.
  - Reduction of oil viscosity from ~ 50 mPa·s to ~ 10 mPa·s.
  - Coagulation of mucilaginous gums.

**Over-cooking (1.5 marks):**
- Protein becomes too hard; cell structure carbonizes locally; **dark-coloured oil** (high Lovibond), increased FFA, foaming, and discolouration of meal.
- **Flakes** become brittle; produce excessive fines in flaker; reduced percolation rate in extractor.

**Under-cooking (1.5 marks):**
- Cell walls remain intact → **poor oil release**; yield drops to 70–80 % in solvent plants.
- High residual oil in meal; mucilaginous gums cause **emulsion formation** and **poor miscella separation.**
- Lipase not inactivated → FFA rises during storage.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` — *Internal Choice*

**(a) Explain flaking of oilseeds with reference to the smooth roller mill. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Conditioned grits are passed through a pair of **smooth counter-rotating rolls** under controlled pressure to produce flakes of uniform thickness. The rolls are driven at near-equal speed (differential ratio ~ 1 : 1) to avoid friction-induced rupture.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Two chilled-cast iron rolls of equal diameter (500–800 mm), each with a hydraulic ram applying pressure (10–25 MPa).
- One roll is **fixed**, the other **floating** on hydraulic cylinders for uniform gap.
- Internal water-cooling of rolls to dissipate heat generated during flaking.

**Operating Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Flake thickness:** 0.20–0.40 mm; controlled by hydraulic pressure and roll gap.
- **Roll speed:** 8–12 rpm (slow for high moisture, fast for low).
- **Feed rate:** Capacities of 50–200 t/day per mill.
- **Feed moisture:** 10–12 % w.b. — too dry → flakes crack; too wet → flakes stick to rolls.

**Quality Indicators (1 mark):**
- **Flake durability index (FDI):** percentage of flakes > 4 mm after a tumbling test; target **≥ 80 %**.
- Bulk density of flakes: 350–450 kg/m³ for soybean.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe expanding/extrusion as a preconditioning step. Compare it with conventional flaking. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Pre-cooked grits are forced through a **restrictor die** (cone-shaped) under high steam pressure (30–50 bar). Sudden pressure drop at the die exit causes **superheated water to flash off**, rupturing cell walls and producing a porous, sponge-like structure (collets) with porosity 3–5× greater than flakes.

**Block Diagram (3 marks) — to be drawn by the student:**

```
Cooked Grits → Feeder → Screw Conveyor → Extruder Barrel (steam 30-50 bar)
                ↓
        Restrictor Die → Sudden Pressure Drop → Collets
                ↓
        Drier (if needed) → Solvent Extractor
```

**Comparison with Flaking (2 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Flaking** | **Expanding** |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment | Smooth roller mill | Expander/extruder with die |
| Flake/Collet thickness | 0.20 – 0.40 mm | 1 – 3 mm collets, 3–5× more porous |
| Porosity | Low | High (rapid percolation) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Energy consumption | Lower | Higher (steam + mechanical) |
| Application | Mechanical pressing, small plants | Large solvent plants (high throughput) |
| Extraction rate | Moderate | **40–60 % faster** percolation |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps in Preconditioning questions:**
>
> 1. **Moisture basis confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students write $MC_{wb} = MC_{db}$ or convert in the wrong direction. Always **state the basis explicitly** ("15 % w.b." or "15 % d.b.").
> 2. **Forgetting steam latent heat (–2 marks):** Cooking calculations need **latent heat of steam (2260 kJ/kg)**, not just the sensible heat of the seed.
> 3. **Conflating dehulling with decorticating (–1 mark):** Dehulling = mechanical removal of hull; decortication = broader term including thermal/chemical pre-treatment.
> 4. **Skipping the line diagram (–1 mark in 7-mark sub-parts):** Cooking and flaking questions **always** require a labelled schematic.
> 5. **Wrong roll speeds for flaking:** Students often say "300 rpm" — the correct range is **8–12 rpm**. The flaker is *slow*; it is not a grinder.
> 6. **No mention of lipase inactivation (–1 mark):** In any cooking question, always state the **enzyme-inactivation** objective.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Preconditioning** = operations *before* oil extraction (cleaning → drying → dehulling → cracking → cooking → flaking → expanding → tempering).
- ☐ **Safe-storage MC** of most oilseeds = **8–10 % w.b.**
- ☐ **Optimum cooking MC** = **10–15 % w.b.**; **cooking T** = **80–110 °C**; **time** = 20–30 min.
- ☐ **Flake thickness target** = **0.20–0.40 mm**.
- ☐ **Differential roll speed in cracking mill** = 1.5 : 1 to 2.5 : 1; in flaker ≈ 1 : 1.
- ☐ **Roll speed in flaker** = **8–12 rpm** (slow!).
- ☐ **Specific heat of oilseed** ≈ $c_p = 1.42 + 0.012 \cdot MC_{wb}$ kJ/kg·K.
- ☐ **Latent heat of steam** = **2260 kJ/kg**.
- ☐ **Moisture conversion:** $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}/100} \times 100$.
- ☐ **Water to add (per kg dry seed):** $m_w = \dfrac{MC_f - MC_i}{100 - MC_f}$.
- ☐ **Expanding** produces porous collets with **3–5× higher porosity** than flakes → faster solvent percolation.
- ☐ **Cooking denatures** protein, **inactivates lipase**, **ruptures cell walls**, **lowers oil viscosity**, **coagulates mucilage**.
- ☐ **Over-cooking** → dark oil, high FFA, brittle flakes, fines.
- ☐ **Under-cooking** → low yield, residual oil in meal, emulsion, high FFA later.
- ☐ **Five objectives of preconditioning** (memorize verbatim): denature protein, rupture cell walls, adjust viscosity, coagulate mucilage, inactivate enzymes.
- ☐ **Oilseeds requiring dehulling:** sunflower, soybean, groundnut, cottonseed. **Mustard & rapeseed:** partial.
- ☐ **Lipase inactivation** is the single most cited reason for cooking — **always mention it**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refining of Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Refining of Oil # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Refining of Oil** is defined as a sequence of purification unit operations applied to **crude vegetable oil** (obtained after extraction or expelling) to remove objectionable non-glyceride impurities such as **free fatty acids (FFA)**, **phospholipids (gums)**, **pigments**, **waxes**, **oxidation products**, and **volatile odoriferous compounds**, thereby producing an edible-grade oil that is bland, stable, and safe for human consumption. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, refining is treated as a **multi-stage downstream unit operation** that converts *crude oil* → *refined, bleached, and deodorized (RBD) oil*, with each stage governed by distinct physico-chemical principles (mass transfer, acid–base neutralization, adsorption, and steam distillation). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Tea Strainer" Analogy for Oil Refining** > > Imagine you have brewed tea directly from tea leaves with twigs, dust, and bitter tannins floating around. Before drinking, you would: > 1. Strain out the leaves (**Degumming** – removing phospholipids/gums). > 2. Add a pinch of baking soda to neutralize the harsh acidity (**Neutralization/Alkali Refining** – removing free fatty acids). > 3. Pass it through a charcoal filter to remove the brown color (**Bleaching** – removing pigments). > 4. Boil it gently to drive off the grassy smell (**Deodorization** – steam stripping volatile odor compounds). > > Crude oil is exactly this "dirty tea" — full of useful glycerides *and* unwanted impurities. Refining is the systematic process of making it clean, light-colored, and pleasant to cook with. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Refining The following engineering quantities are standardly tracked in oil refining (KTU Board standard): - **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content** – expressed as % oleic acid (typical crude: 1–10%; refined: <0.1%). - **Acid Value (AV)** – mg KOH required to neutralize 1 g of oil. - **Saponification Value (SV)** – mg KOH required to saponify 1 g of oil. - **Phosphorus (P) content** – ppm; indicates residual gums. - **Color** – measured in Lovibond units (Y + R). - **Peroxide Value (PV)** – meq O₂/kg; indicates oxidative deterioration. - **Iodine Value (IV)** – g I₂/100 g; indicates unsaturation. > [!TIP] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Out of all refining steps, **Alkali Neutralization** and **Deodorization** carry the highest mark weightage in Module-3 questions. Be thorough with their stoichiometry and process parameters. --- ## 1.4 Why is Refining Necessary? | Impurity in Crude Oil | Problem Caused | Removed in Stage | |---|---|---| | Free Fatty Acids (FFA) | Smoke, rancidity, off-flavor | Alkali Neutralization | | Phospholipids / Gums | Foaming, dark color, deposits | Degumming | | Carotenoids, Chlorophyll | Dark color, off-taste | Bleaching | | Aldehydes, Ketones | Unpleasant odor | Deodorization | | Waxes | Turbidity in cold storage | Winterization | | Moisture | Hydrolysis of triglycerides | Drying / Vacuum | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Oil Refining ### Stage 1 — Degumming - **Principle:** Hydration of hydratable phospholipids (phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine) using **2–3% water** (or **0.1–0.5% phosphoric acid** for non-hydratable gums) at **60–80 °C**. - **Mechanism:** Phospholipids are surface-active and form reverse micelles in oil. Water disrupts these micelles, causing gums to coalesce and become insoluble → they settle (or are centrifuged) out. - **Output:** Degummed oil with P-content reduced to <50 ppm. ### Stage 2 — Alkali Neutralization (Caustic Refining) - **Principle:** Acid–base reaction between **FFA** and **NaOH** to form **soap (sodium salt of fatty acid)**, which is insoluble in oil and is separated by centrifugation. - **Chemical Equations:** $$ \text{R–COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R–COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ - **Excess NaOH** also reacts with some triglycerides (saponification), causing unavoidable oil loss. - **Process Parameters:** - **Lye concentration:** 8–18 °Bé (Baumé) - **Temperature:** 60–75 °C - **Contact time:** 10–20 min - **Centrifugation:** 6000–7000 rpm ### Stage 3 — Bleaching - **Principle:** **Adsorption** of pigments (carotenes, xanthophylls, chlorophyll) onto the surface of **bleaching earth (acid-activated bentonite)** or **activated carbon**. - **Operating Conditions:** 90–110 °C under vacuum (50–100 mbar) to prevent oil oxidation. - **Dosage:** 0.5–2.5% bleaching earth. - **Output:** Light-colored oil, Lovibond reduced from ~50 Y/5 R to ~10 Y/1 R. ### Stage 4 — Deodorization - **Principle:** **Steam distillation under high vacuum** (3–10 mbar) at high temperature (220–260 °C) to strip volatile odoriferous compounds (aldehydes, ketones, hydrocarbons). - **Mechanism:** Dalton's law of partial pressures — volatile compounds obey Henry's law and are carried away by stripping steam. - **Steam usage:** 1–3% of oil weight. - **Output:** Bland, odorless RBD oil, FFA <0.05%. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optional Stage — Winterization:** Slow cooling of oil to 5–10 °C crystallizes high-melting-point waxes, which are filtered out. Critical for sunflower and rice bran oil clarity. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Acid Value (AV)** | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH / g oil | | 2 | **FFA as % Oleic Acid** | $\%\text{FFA} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 28.2}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | % (w/w) | | 3 | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH / g oil | | 4 | **NaOH required for neutralization** | $M_{\text{NaOH}} = \dfrac{\%\text{FFA} \times W_{\text{oil}} \times 40}{56.1 \times 1000} + \text{excess}$ | kg NaOH | | 5 | **Excess NaOH** (as commercial lye) | $M_{\text{excess}} = \text{(0.1 to 0.5)} \times \dfrac{\%\text{FFA} \times W_{\text{oil}}}{100}$ | kg | | 6 | **Theoretical Soap Stock** | $M_{\text{soap}} = \dfrac{\%\text{FFA} \times W_{\text{oil}} \times M_{\text{RCOONa}}}{282 \times 100}$ | kg (MW sodium oleate = 304) | | 7 | **Refining Loss (approx.)** | $L \approx \%\text{FFA} \times 1.6 + \%\text{gums} + \%\text{moisture}$ | % (w/w) | | 8 | **Saponification Equivalent (E)** | $E = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}}$ | Molecular weight of fatty acid (avg) | | 9 | **Ester Value** | $\text{EV} = \text{SV} - \text{AV}$ | mg KOH / g oil | | 10 | **Dalton's Steam Stripping (Deodorizer)** | $\text{FFA}_{\text{out}} = \text{FFA}_{\text{in}} \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | First-order decay; $k$ depends on $T, P$ | > [!NOTE] > **Constant 56.1** is the molecular weight of KOH in g/mol. **Constant 28.2 = 56.1/2** because oleic acid (MW 282) reacts with 1 mole KOH. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Scale:** All major edible oil plants (Cargill, Adani Wilmar, Bunge) operate continuous RBD systems processing 50–500 TPD (tonnes per day). - **Batch vs. Continuous:** Small refineries (≤50 TPD) use batch refining; large refineries use **continuous centrifugal** refining (e.g., Alfa Laval, Westfalia separators). - **By-products:** Soap stock → fatty acid distillation (industrial surfactants). Spent bleaching earth → cement kilns (limited due to oil residue). Deodorizer distillate → tocopherol (Vitamin E) recovery, a high-value nutraceutical. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometry of Caustic Neutralization ### Given - **Crude oil mass** $W_{\text{oil}} = 1000 \text{ kg}$ - **FFA content** (as oleic acid) = $4.0\%$ - **Lye concentration** = $12 \text{ °Bé NaOH}$ (≈7.2% w/w NaOH) - **Excess NaOH** = $0.15 \times$ stoichiometric (15% excess) - **Density of 12 °Bé lye** ≈ $1.09 \text{ g/cm}^3$ ### Step 1 — Identify the Reaction Oleic acid (C₁₇H₃₃COOH, MW = 282 g/mol) reacts 1:1 with NaOH (MW = 40 g/mol): $$ \text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Mass of FFA in Oil $$ m_{\text{FFA}} = \frac{\%\text{FFA}}{100} \times W_{\text{oil}} = \frac{4.0}{100} \times 1000 = 40 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 3 — Convert FFA Mass to Moles $$ n_{\text{FFA}} = \frac{m_{\text{FFA}}}{M_{\text{FFA}}} = \frac{40 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{282 \text{ g/mol}} = 141.84 \text{ mol} $$ ### Step 4 — Stoichiometric NaOH Required (moles) 1 mole FFA → 1 mole NaOH (1:1 stoichiometry): $$ n_{\text{NaOH, stoich}} = 141.84 \text{ mol} $$ ### Step 5 — Stoichiometric NaOH Mass $$ m_{\text{NaOH, stoich}} = n_{\text{NaOH}} \times 40 = 141.84 \times 40 = 5673.76 \text{ g} = 5.674 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 6 — Add 15% Excess (to ensure complete reaction & account for impurities) $$ m_{\text{NaOH, total}} = 5.674 \times 1.15 = 6.525 \text{ kg pure NaOH} $$ ### Step 7 — Convert to Volume of 12 °Bé Commercial Lye $$ V_{\text{lye}} = \frac{m_{\text{NaOH, total}}}{\text{Concentration} \times \rho_{\text{lye}}} = \frac{6.525}{0.072 \times 1.09 \times 1000} $$ $$ V_{\text{lye}} = \frac{6.525}{78.48} = 0.0831 \text{ m}^3 = 83.1 \text{ L} $$ ### Step 8 — Estimate Soap Stock Formed (Sodium Oleate, MW = 304 g/mol) $$ m_{\text{soap}} = 141.84 \times 304 = 43,119 \text{ g} \approx 43.12 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 9 — Estimate Total Refining Loss $$ L = \%\text{FFA} \times 1.6 + \%\text{gums (assume 0.8)} = 4.0 \times 1.6 + 0.8 = 7.2\% $$ $$ \text{Mass loss} = 1000 \times 0.072 = 72 \text{ kg} $$ ### Step 10 — Refined Oil Yield $$ \text{Yield} = 1000 - 72 = 928 \text{ kg} \quad (\text{92.8\% recovery}) $$ > [!TIP] > **Final Result Summary (boxed):** > - Pure NaOH needed: **5.674 kg (stoichiometric) + 15% excess = 6.525 kg** > - Volume of 12 °Bé lye: **≈ 83.1 L per tonne of oil** > - Soap stock produced: **≈ 43.1 kg** > - Refined oil yield: **92.8%** --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Acid Value from a Practical Titrated Data ### Given (Lab Scenario) - Oil sample weight $W = 5.0 \text{ g}$ - KOH normality $N = 0.1 \text{ N}$ - Titre volume $V = 4.2 \text{ mL}$ ### Step 1 — Apply Acid Value Formula $$ \text{AV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} = \frac{4.2 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5.0} $$ $$ \text{AV} = \frac{23.562}{5.0} = 4.71 \text{ mg KOH/g oil} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert AV to % FFA (as Oleic Acid) $$ \%\text{FFA} = \frac{\text{AV}}{1.99} = \frac{4.71}{1.99} \approx 2.37\% $$ (The factor 1.99 = 56.1 / 28.2 is the standard conversion from KOH-basis to oleic-acid basis.) --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Refining Stages | Property | Degumming | Neutralization | Bleaching | Deodorization | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Unit principle** | Hydration | Acid–base | Adsorption | Steam distillation | | **Reagent / Medium** | Water / H₃PO₄ | NaOH (8–18 °Bé) | Bleaching earth | Stripping steam | | **Temperature (°C)** | 60–80 | 60–75 | 90–110 | 220–260 | | **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | Vacuum (50–100 mbar) | High vacuum (3–10 mbar) | | **Impurity removed** | Phospholipids | FFA, partial pigments | Pigments, soaps, metals | Odor, FFA, peroxides | | **By-product** | Lecithin (gum) | Soap stock | Spent earth | Deodorizer distillate | | **Mass loss (approx.)** | 0.5–2% | 4–8% | 0.5–1.5% | 0.3–1% | | **Equipment** | Reactor + Centrifuge | Mixer + Centrifuge | Bleacher + Filter | Packed-column deodorizer | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Oil Refining Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crude Oil Storage Tank] --> B[Pre-heat Exchanger] B --> C[Degumming Reactor] C --> D[Gum Separator Centrifuge] D --> E[Degummed Oil Holding Tank] E --> F[Alkali Neutralizer Mixer] F --> G[Primary Centrifuge] G --> H[Water Wash Column] H --> I[Vacuum Drier] I --> J[Bleacher Under Vacuum] J --> K[Filter Press for Spent Earth] K --> L[Bleached Oil Tank] L --> M[Deodorizer Stripping Column] M --> N[Polishing Filter] N --> O[RBD Oil Storage] O --> P[Packaging and Dispatch] D --> Q[Crude Lecithin Recovery] G --> R[Soap Stock Acidulation] M --> S[Tocopherol / Vitamin E Recovery] K --> T[Spent Earth Disposal] classDef unitOp fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1 classDef byproduct fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O,P unitOp class Q,R,S,T byproduct ``` ## 4.2 Mass Flow Schematic (Per Tonne of Crude Oil) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[1000 kg Crude Oil
FFA 4% Gums 0.8%] --> A2[Degumming] A2 --> A3[990 kg Degummed Oil] A2 --> A4[10 kg Wet Gums] A3 --> A5[Neutralization with 83 L of 12 Be NaOH] A5 --> A6[940 kg Neutralized Oil] A5 --> A7[50 kg Soap Stock] A6 --> A8[Bleaching with 15 kg Earth] A8 --> A9[930 kg Bleached Oil] A8 --> A10[15 kg Spent Earth] A9 --> A11[Deodorization with 25 kg Steam] A11 --> A12[925 kg RBD Oil] A11 --> A13[5 kg Deodorizer Distillate] classDef input fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px classDef waste fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px classDef product fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px class A1 input class A2,A5,A8,A11 input class A3,A6,A9,A12 product class A4,A7,A10,A13 waste ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choice of Refining Method ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Crude Oil Sample] --> Q1{FFA content?} Q1 -->|less than 3 percent| P1[Physical Refining Path] Q1 -->|3 to 10 percent| P2[Chemical Alkali Refining] Q1 -->|greater than 10 percent| P3[Two-Stage Refining] P1 --> D1[Degumming + Bleaching + Steam Stripping] P2 --> D2[Degumming + Neutralization + Bleaching + Deodorization] P3 --> D3[Degumming + Caustic Refining + Bleaching + Deodorization] D1 --> End[RBD Oil] D2 --> End D3 --> End classDef decision fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px classDef terminal fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px class Start,End terminal class Q1 decision class P1,P2,P3,D1,D2,D3 process ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For high FFA oils (e.g., rice bran with FFA 5–30% or used cooking oil), the **two-stage refining** route is preferred because direct alkali refining causes massive soap-stock losses (refining loss can exceed FFA × 3.0). --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Explain the principle of degumming of vegetable oil. What is the role of phosphoric acid in this process?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Principle (1 mark):** Degumming is the removal of hydratable phospholipids (gums) from crude oil by hydration with water at 60–80 °C. The gums, being water-soluble, coalesce and separate from the oil phase. - **Role of H₃PO₄ (1 mark):** Phosphoric acid (0.1–0.5%) converts **non-hydratable phospholipids** (calcium/magnesium salts of phosphatidic acid) into hydratable forms by chelating the metal ions, enabling their removal. - **Significance (1 mark):** Reduces phosphorus to <50 ppm, preventing downstream catalyst poisoning in hydrogenation and improving bleaching efficiency. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is meant by acid value of an oil? Why is it an important refining parameter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Acid value is the number of milligrams of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids present in 1 g of oil, given by $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$. - **Importance (1.5 marks):** It directly quantifies the FFA content, which dictates (a) the amount of NaOH required for neutralization, (b) the expected refining loss, and (c) the suitability of oil for physical vs. chemical refining. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### *Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following (A or B)* --- ### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** Describe in detail the **process of alkali neutralization of crude vegetable oil** with a neat flow diagram. State the chemical reaction involved and the role of each process parameter. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refinery receives **5000 kg of crude soybean oil** with **5.2% FFA** (as oleic acid). Calculate: (i) The stoichiometric quantity of NaOH required. (ii) The volume of **14 °Bé lye** (with 20% excess) needed. (iii) The mass of soap stock produced. (iv) The expected refined oil yield assuming 0.7% gum and 0.3% moisture. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Process Description (7 marks) 1. **Crude oil pre-heating (1 mark):** Crude oil is heated to 60–70 °C in a heat exchanger to reduce viscosity and ensure proper mixing with lye. 2. **Lye preparation (1 mark):** Sodium hydroxide solution is prepared at 8–18 °Bé (typically 12–14 °Bé for soybean oil) corresponding to 6–10% NaOH w/w. 3. **Mixing in neutralizer (1.5 marks):** Lye is dosed at 10–20% excess above stoichiometric in a high-shear mixer for 10–20 minutes to neutralize FFA: $$\text{R–COOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{R–COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The reaction is exothermic; temperature is controlled at 60–75 °C to break the emulsion. 4. **Centrifugal separation (1.5 marks):** The mixture is fed to a primary centrifuge (6000–7000 rpm) where **soap stock** (heavier) is removed from **neutralized oil**. 5. **Water washing (1 mark):** Oil is washed with 10–20% hot soft water at 85–90 °C to remove residual soap and lye. 6. **Vacuum drying (1 mark):** Washed oil is dried under vacuum (50–70 mbar) at 90–100 °C to reduce moisture below 0.1%. ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $W_{\text{oil}} = 5000$ kg, %FFA = 5.2, 14 °Bé lye, 20% excess. - Density of 14 °Bé lye ≈ 1.10 g/cm³. - Concentration of 14 °Bé lye ≈ 8.7% w/w NaOH. **(i) Stoichiometric NaOH (2 marks):** Mass of FFA: $$ m_{\text{FFA}} = \frac{5.2}{100} \times 5000 = 260 \text{ kg} $$ Moles of FFA (oleic acid, MW = 282): $$ n_{\text{FFA}} = \frac{260 \times 10^3}{282} = 921.99 \text{ mol} $$ Stoichiometric NaOH (1:1): $$ m_{\text{NaOH, stoich}} = 921.99 \times 40 = 36{,}879.4 \text{ g} \approx 36.88 \text{ kg} $$ **(ii) Volume of 14 °Bé lye (with 20% excess) (3 marks):** Total NaOH required: $$ m_{\text{NaOH, total}} = 36.88 \times 1.20 = 44.25 \text{ kg} $$ Using 14 °Bé lye (8.7% w/w, ρ = 1.10 g/cm³ = 1100 kg/m³): $$ \text{Volume of lye} = \frac{m_{\text{NaOH, total}}}{\text{Concentration} \times \rho} = \frac{44.25}{0.087 \times 1100} = \frac{44.25}{95.7} $$ $$ V_{\text{lye}} = 0.4624 \text{ m}^3 = 462.4 \text{ L} $$ **(iii) Mass of soap stock (sodium oleate, MW = 304) (1 mark):** $$ m_{\text{soap}} = 921.99 \times 304 = 280{,}285 \text{ g} \approx 280.3 \text{ kg} $$ *(Note: This is the theoretical soap; industrial soap stock contains water, lye, entrained oil, and impurities, so actual mass ≈ 1.2–1.5× theoretical.)* **(iv) Refined oil yield (1 mark):** Refining loss formula: $$ L \approx \%\text{FFA} \times 1.6 + \%\text{gums} + \%\text{moisture} = 5.2 \times 1.6 + 0.7 + 0.3 = 9.32\% $$ Mass of refined oil: $$ W_{\text{refined}} = 5000 \times (1 - 0.0932) = 5000 \times 0.9068 = 4534 \text{ kg} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to add the **excess NaOH** (1 mark lost) — always state and apply the excess factor explicitly. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using **pure water density (1 g/cm³)** instead of the **density of the °Bé lye (≈1.10 g/cm³)**. The °Bé → density conversion is mandatory. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting soap stock mass in moles instead of kg. Always multiply by molecular weight. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Omitting the units in the final answer — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit. --- ### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain the **process of deodorization of vegetable oil**. Discuss the role of **vacuum, temperature, and stripping steam** in achieving odor removal. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An oil sample weighing **4.0 g** was titrated against **0.1 N KOH** and required **3.8 mL** of the alkali. Calculate: (i) The acid value of the oil. (ii) The % FFA (as oleic acid). (iii) The amount of NaOH (in kg) required to neutralize **2 tonnes** of this oil, using 18% excess. (iv) Comment on whether this oil is suitable for **physical refining** (justify with FFA threshold). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Deodorization Process (7 marks) 1. **Definition & principle (1.5 marks):** Deodorization is the steam distillation of volatile odoriferous compounds (aldehydes, ketones, hydrocarbons, residual FFA) from bleached oil under high temperature and high vacuum, producing a bland, odorless RBD oil. 2. **Feed pre-heating (1 mark):** Bleached oil is pre-deaerated and heated to 220–260 °C in a shell-and-tube economizer using stripped oil as the heating medium. 3. **Stripping column (2 marks):** Oil flows downward in a **packed or tray column** while stripping steam (1–3% of oil weight, dry, deaerated) flows counter-current. Per Dalton's law, the partial pressure of volatiles is reduced by the total system pressure (3–10 mbar), enabling vaporization at low temperature. 4. **Role of parameters (2 marks):** - **High vacuum (3–10 mbar):** Suppresses triglyceride hydrolysis and lowers boiling points of odor compounds. - **High temperature (220–260 °C):** Increases volatility of odor compounds and accelerates mass transfer. - **Stripping steam (1–3%):** Carries volatile molecules out of the oil; also reduces their partial pressure. 5. **Cooling and polishing (0.5 mark):** Deodorized oil is cooled to <50 °C, polished through a leaf filter, and sent to storage with added **antioxidant (TBHQ/BHT)**. ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** $W = 4.0$ g, $N = 0.1$ N KOH, $V = 3.8$ mL, 2 tonnes of oil, 18% excess. **(i) Acid Value (2 marks):** $$ \text{AV} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} = \frac{3.8 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{4.0} = \frac{21.318}{4.0} = 5.33 \text{ mg KOH/g} $$ **(ii) % FFA (as oleic acid) (2 marks):** $$ \%\text{FFA} = \frac{\text{AV}}{1.99} = \frac{5.33}{1.99} = 2.68\% $$ *(Equivalently, directly from titration: $\%\text{FFA} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W} = \dfrac{3.8 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{4.0} = 2.68\%$.)* **(iii) NaOH required for 2 tonnes (2 marks):** Mass of FFA in 2 tonnes = $\dfrac{2.68}{100} \times 2000 = 53.6$ kg. Moles of FFA: $$ n_{\text{FFA}} = \frac{53.6 \times 10^3}{282} = 190.07 \text{ mol} $$ Stoichiometric NaOH: $$ m_{\text{NaOH, stoich}} = 190.07 \times 40 = 7602.8 \text{ g} = 7.603 \text{ kg} $$ With 18% excess: $$ m_{\text{NaOH, total}} = 7.603 \times 1.18 = 8.971 \text{ kg} $$ **(iv) Suitability for Physical Refining (1 mark):** - Physical refining is economical for oils with **FFA < 3%**. - This oil has **FFA = 2.68%**, which is **borderline acceptable** for physical refining. - **Recommendation:** Since FFA is just below 3%, this oil **can be physically refined**, but chemical (alkali) refining is preferred for safer color and stability. **Justification:** Physical refining avoids soap stock and is cheaper at low FFA, but at ~2.68% there is still a risk of dark color carryover; therefore **chemical refining is the safer choice**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Using **56.1 directly** in the FFA formula instead of **28.2**. The factor 28.2 is for oleic-acid-basis; using 56.1 gives acid value, not %FFA. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Omitting **excess NaOH** in part (b-iii) — even a small change in %FFA dramatically changes the kg of NaOH. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Writing only the numerical value of FFA in part (b-iv) without **justification with the 3% threshold** — examiners expect the threshold + a reasoned statement. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **deodorization** with **deacidification** in part (a). Deodorization is steam stripping; deacidification is FFA removal (usually alkali or physical refining). --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU Module 3: Refining of Oil** - ✅ **Refining = 4 sequential stages:** Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization (RBD). - ✅ **Crude oil impurities** are: FFA, phospholipids, pigments, waxes, oxidation products, odor compounds, moisture. - ✅ **FFA as % oleic acid** uses the constant **28.2** in the numerator (NOT 56.1). - ✅ **Acid Value** uses constant **56.1** (MW of KOH) in the numerator. - ✅ **NaOH stoichiometry** follows 1:1 molar ratio with FFA; add **10–20% excess** in practice. - ✅ **°Bé to density** conversion: $\rho \approx 1 + \dfrac{\text{°Bé}}{145}$ (approx.), or use standard tables: 12 °Bé ≈ 1.09 g/cm³; 14 °Bé ≈ 1.10 g/cm³. - ✅ **Refining Loss ≈ %FFA × 1.6 + %gums + %moisture** (engineering rule of thumb). - ✅ **Soap stock** = sodium salt of fatty acid; MW of sodium oleate = **304 g/mol**. - ✅ **Degumming** uses water (2–3%) at 60–80 °C; H₃PO₄ removes non-hydratable gums. - ✅ **Bleaching** uses **acid-activated bentonite** under vacuum; NEVER at atmospheric pressure (oxidation). - ✅ **Deodorization** is **steam distillation under high vacuum** (3–10 mbar) at 220–260 °C; uses 1–3% stripping steam. - ✅ **Physical refining** is preferred for **low FFA oils (<3%)**; **Chemical refining** for high FFA oils. - ✅ **Winterization** is an *optional* step for wax removal (sunflower, rice bran oils). - ✅ **Deodorizer distillate** is a valuable by-product: source of **tocopherols (Vitamin E)**, sterols, and squalene. - ✅ **Lecithin** is recovered from **degumming sludge** — a major commercial by-product of soybean oil refining. - ✅ **Acid Value × 0.503 ≈ %FFA as oleic acid** (alternate shortcut formula). - ✅ **Saponification value** indicates average molecular weight of fatty acids: $\text{Avg MW} = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}}$. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the four stages — "D-N-B-D"** → **D**egum → **N**eutralize → **B**leach → **D**eodorize. The first three operate at low-to-moderate temperature; the last one is the *hot* and *vacuum* stage. ---

topics.title: Different Peeling Methods and Devices (Manual Peeling, Mechanical Peeling, Chemical Peeling, and Thermal Peeling), Slicing of Horticultural Crops: Equipment for Slicing, Shredding, Crushing, Chopping, Juice Extraction, etc., Blanching: Importance and Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT752 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN POST HARVEST ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Different Peeling Methods and Devices (Manual Peeling, Mechanical Peeling, Chemical Peeling, and Thermal Peeling), Slicing of Horticultural Crops: Equipment for Slicing, Shredding, Crushing, Chopping, Juice Extraction, etc., Blanching: Importance and Objectives # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Peeling – Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Peeling** is defined as the *unit operation* in post-harvest engineering wherein the **outer epidermal layer (peel/skin)** of a horticultural commodity (fruits, vegetables, tubers, roots) is *separated* from the underlying edible mesocarp/cortex, to improve *edibility*, *appearance*, *texture*, and to facilitate *downstream processing* (slicing, drying, canning, juice extraction). The peel constitutes 10–30 % of the fresh mass of most fruits and 15–40 % of root/tuber crops. Removal efficiency, peel loss (% mass removed as peel), and nutrient retention depend directly on the **peeling method** chosen. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are peeling a *boiled egg*. You can: 1. **Use your fingers / a knife** (Manual) 2. **Roll it under pressure** so the cracked shell falls off (Mechanical) 3. **Dip it in vinegar** which dissolves the shell (Chemical) 4. **Blanch it in hot water** so the shell loosens (Thermal) Each fruit/vegetable is essentially an "egg", and we are choosing a method based on the **skin thickness, fragility, intended use, and scale of operation**. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Peeling Methods | S.No | Method | Core Principle | Typical Commodities | |------|--------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | Manual Peeling | Hand-held knives / blades | Mango, papaya, citrus (small scale) | | 2 | Mechanical Peeling | Abrasive rollers, carborundum drums, knives | Potato, carrot, beetroot, apple | | 3 | Chemical (Lye) Peeling | Hot alkali (NaOH/KOH) dissolves peel | Tomato, peach, apricot, sweet potato | | 4 | Thermal Peeling | Steam / hot water / flame causes skin loosening | Tomato, peach, onion, garlic | | 5 | Enzymatic Peeling | Cellulase/pectinase degrades peel | Citrus segments, kiwifruit | | 6 | Cryogenic Peeling | Liquid N₂ freezes skin → fractures | Pepper, tomato (high-value) | --- ## 1.4 Slicing – Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Slicing** is a *size-reduction* unit operation that cuts a horticultural commodity into **uniform planar pieces of definite thickness**, typically 1–10 mm, using sharp blades, rotary cutters, or high-speed disks. ### Intuitive Analogy Think of slicing a *loaf of bread*. Whether you use a **bread knife (manual slicer)**, an **automatic bread-slicing machine in a bakery (rotary slicer)**, or a **food processor with a slicing disc (centrifugal slicer)**, the goal is identical: *uniform sheets of defined thickness*. ### Related Size-Reduction Operations | Operation | Definition | Typical Output | |-----------|------------|----------------| | **Slicing** | Cutting into thin flat pieces | Chips, rings | | **Shredding** | Cutting into long narrow strips (Julienne) | Cabbage slaw, carrot sticks | | **Crushing** | Compressive rupture of cell walls | Fruit pulp, grape must | | **Chopping** | Coarse size reduction into irregular pieces | Frozen vegetable mix | | **Juice Extraction** | Separation of liquid (juice) from solid (pomace) | Orange juice, apple juice | --- ## 1.5 Blanching – Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Blanching** is a *thermal pre-treatment* wherein horticultural produce is subjected to **hot water (85–100 °C)** or **steam (100 °C)** for a *short duration* (typically 1–10 minutes), followed by **rapid cooling**, to inactivate *enzymes*, fix *colour*, and remove *air/intracellular gases* prior to drying, freezing, or canning. ### Intuitive Analogy Consider *blanching* as **"pre-cooking in a controlled manner"** — like dipping a tea bag into hot water for 30 seconds: enough to activate the chemistry (flavour extraction) but not so long as to destroy structure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Blanching is NOT cooking.* It is a *partial thermal treatment* designed to inactivate **peroxidase, catalase, and polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** — the enzymes responsible for off-flavours, browning, and quality degradation. --- ## 1.6 Primary Objectives of Blanching (KTU Board-Exam Favourite) 1. **Enzyme Inactivation** – Destroy PPO, peroxidase, catalase (target enzyme index: **peroxidase**). 2. **Colour Fixation** – Expels intercellular air, brightens pigments. 3. **Texture Softening** – Causes controlled softening for better filling in cans. 4. **Gas Removal (Exhausting)** – Replaces intra-cellular gases with water vapour → reduces oxidative degradation. 5. **Microbial Load Reduction** – Surface microbial count reduced by **1–2 log cycles**. 6. **Prevention of Flavour Loss** – Stabilises volatile aroma compounds. 7. **Improved Rehydration** – Dried products rehydrate faster after blanching. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Manual Peeling – Operational Concept ### Equipment - Hand knives (curved, straight blade) - Spoons (for soft fruits) - Peeling discs (rotating hand peeler) ### Working Logic 1. Operator holds the produce against the blade. 2. **Shear force** is applied tangentially along the peel. 3. Blade thickness (typically **0.5–1.5 mm**) governs peel loss. ### Merits & Demerits | Parameter | Status | |-----------|--------| | Capital Cost | Very Low | | Peel Loss | High (**15–30 %**) | | Throughput | **30–60 kg/man-hour** | | Hygiene | Operator dependent | | Suitability | Small / cottage scale | --- ## 2.2 Mechanical Peeling – Operational Concept ### Sub-Categories #### (a) Abrasive / Carborundum Roller Peeler - Drum lined with **carborundum (SiC)** abrasive. - Produce tumbled against rotating rough surface. - **Peeling time:** 30 s – 3 min. - **Throughput:** **300–2000 kg/h**. - Suitable for: **Potato, carrot, beetroot**. #### (b) Knife Peeler - Stationary or rotating blades peel the produce like a *lathe*. - Adjustable blade depth (typically **1–3 mm**). - Suitable for: **Apple, pear, mango**. #### (c) Roller Knife Peeler (Lye-Peel Pre-treatment Combined) - Abrasive rollers followed by spray wash. ### Working Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. Produce loaded into rotating drum. 2. Centrifugal force + abrasion remove peel. 3. Water spray continuously removes removed peel. 4. Peeling efficiency $(\eta_p)$ defined as: $$\eta_p = \frac{M_{peel}}{M_{total}} \times 100 \%$$ where $M_{peel}$ = mass of peel removed, $M_{total}$ = initial mass of whole produce. --- ## 2.3 Chemical (Lye) Peeling – Deep Analysis ### Core Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > When produce is immersed in **hot alkali solution (NaOH or KOH)**, the alkali **hydrolyses the pectin layer (middle lamella)** between the epidermal cells and underlying mesocarp cells, loosening the skin which is then removed by **mechanical abrasion / water jets**. ### Process Parameters | Parameter | Typical Range | Effect of Increase | |-----------|---------------|---------------------| | Lye Concentration | **2–10 % NaOH** | Higher → faster peel → more penetration | | Temperature | **60–100 °C** | Higher → faster reaction | | Time | **15 s – 5 min** | Longer → over-peeling, mushy texture | | pH | **> 12** | Required for effective hydrolysis | | Commodities | Tomato, peach, apricot | – | ### Governing Chemistry The lye attacks the **methyl ester linkages of pectin**: $$\text{Pectin-CH}_3 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Pectin-COO}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$ This **saponification reaction** converts insoluble pectin into **sodium pectate** (soluble), dissolving the peel-tissue attachment. ### Critical Mass-Transfer Equation The depth of alkali penetration $x$ (m) follows Fickian diffusion: $$x = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$$ where $D$ = diffusion coefficient of NaOH in fruit tissue (typically $1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$), $t$ = exposure time (s). > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The *residence time* must be just enough to dissolve the peel, but not so long that NaOH penetrates into the edible portion causing **"liquor penetration"** and yield loss. ### Neutralisation Step (Essential) After lye peeling, residual alkali on the fruit surface is neutralised by dipping in **dilute citric acid (0.5–1 %)** for 30–60 s. --- ## 2.4 Thermal Peeling – Deep Analysis ### Sub-Categories #### (a) Steam Peeling - Produce exposed to **saturated steam at 100–110 °C** for **10–60 s**. - Rapid condensation of steam on cool peel surface → local heating → **peel loosening**. - Skin removed by **rubber rollers / water jets**. - Ideal for **tomato, peach, apricot**. #### (b) Hot Water Peeling - Immersion in **boiling water (95–100 °C)** for **30 s – 5 min**. - Suitable for **onion, garlic, peach**. #### (c) Flame Peeling / Infrared Peeling - Direct flame (≈ 1000 °C) or IR radiation for 5–15 s. - Charred skin removed by water spray. - Suitable for **onion, garlic**. ### Governing Heat-Transfer Equation The depth of thermal penetration $x$ in produce of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ is: $$x \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t}$$ Standard values of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$: | Commodity | $\alpha$ (m²/s) | |-----------|-----------------| | Apple | $1.4 \times 10^{-7}$ | | Potato | $1.3 \times 10^{-7}$ | | Tomato | $1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ | | Carrot | $1.4 \times 10^{-7}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb:** For steam peeling, $x$ should equal **peel thickness (≈ 0.5–1.5 mm)** to maximise peel removal without pulp loss. --- ## 2.5 Slicing & Size-Reduction Equipment ### (a) Rotary Slicer - Centrifugal feed → product pressed against **stationary blades** by centrifugal force. - Slice thickness = **blade clearance**. - Capacity: **500–5000 kg/h**. - Used for: cabbage, cucumber, carrot. ### (b) High-Speed Disk Slicer - Rotating disk with radial blades (rpm = **600–1500**). - Produce fed through hopper → sliced → discharged. - Used for: potato chips, banana chips. ### (c) Shredders / Graters - Cylindrical drum with **perforations** and rotating knives. - Output: **2–5 mm wide strips**. - Used for: cabbage (coleslaw), cheese, coconut. ### (d) Crushers - Two counter-rotating rollers with **corrugated surfaces**. - Compression + shear rupture cell walls. - Used for: grapes (wine), apples (cider). ### (e) Choppers - Slow-speed rotating knives in a **curved bowl**. - Output: **5–25 mm chunks**. - Used for: frozen vegetable mixes, pre-cut salads. ### (f) Juice Extractors | Type | Principle | Efficiency | |------|-----------|------------| | **Hydraulic Press** | Static pressure (0.5–2 MPa) | 70–85 % | | **Belt Press** | Continuous compression | 75–85 % | | **Screw Press** | Auger compression | 65–80 % | | **Centrifugal Extractor** | Centrifugal force | 70–80 % | | **Enzymatic + Press** | Enzyme pre-treatment + press | **85–92 %** | ### Juice Extraction Efficiency Formula $$\eta_{juice} = \frac{V_{juice}}{M_{fruit} - M_{pomace}} \times 100 \%$$ --- ## 2.6 Blanching – Engineering Analysis ### Modes of Blanching | Mode | Temp (°C) | Time | Merits | |------|-----------|------|--------| | **Hot Water** | 85–100 | 1–10 min | Uniform, simple | | **Steam** | 100 | 1–5 min | Less nutrient leaching | | **Microwave** | Internal heating | 30 s – 3 min | Fast, energy efficient | | **Infrared (IR)** | Surface | 30 s – 2 min | No water use | ### Heat Transfer in Blanching For a spherical produce of radius $r$, the centre temperature $T_c$ at time $t$ (Heisler-chart based approximation): $$\frac{T_c - T_w}{T_i - T_w} = A_1 \exp\left(-\lambda_1^2 \cdot Fo\right)$$ where the Fourier number is: $$Fo = \frac{\alpha \cdot t}{r^2}$$ and $\lambda_1^2 = 2.5$ for sphere (first root), $T_w$ = water temperature, $T_i$ = initial produce temperature. ### Critical Enzyme Inactivation Kinetics Peroxidase inactivation follows **first-order kinetics**: $$\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$ where $k$ = rate constant (min⁻¹) and $C/C_0$ = residual activity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** *Peroxidase is the most heat-resistant enzyme in vegetables.* Complete inactivation of peroxidase guarantees inactivation of all other enzymes. Hence, **peroxidase is the indicator enzyme**. ### D-value and Z-value - **D-value:** Time at a given T to achieve **90 % inactivation**. - **Z-value:** Temperature rise required to reduce D by a factor of 10. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | |---------|----------|--------------------| | Peel Efficiency | $\eta_p = M_{peel}/M_{total} \times 100$ | Mass (kg) | | Lye Penetration Depth | $x = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | m | | Thermal Penetration | $x = 2\sqrt{\alpha \cdot t}$ | m | | Fourier Number | $Fo = \alpha t / r^2$ | Dimensionless | | Enzyme Inactivation | $\ln(C/C_0) = -kt$ | min⁻¹ | | Throughput Capacity | $Q = m/t$ | kg/h | | Juice Efficiency | $\eta_{juice} = V_{juice} / (M_{fruit} - M_{pomace})$ | % | | Heat Load on Blancher | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | kJ | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Penetration Depth in Thermal Peeling ### Statement Show that the depth of thermal penetration $x$ at time $t$ in a semi-infinite produce body is given by $x \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha t}$. ### Step 1 — Governing Equation Transient heat conduction (1-D) inside the produce: $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$ ### Step 2 — Initial & Boundary Conditions -
