At $t = 0$: $T(x, 0) = T_i$ (initial produce temperature). - At $x = 0$: $T(0, t) = T_s$ (surface temperature = steam temperature). - At $x \to \infty$: $T(\infty, t) = T_i$. ### Step 3 — Introduce Dimensionless Temperature $$\theta = \frac{T - T_i}{T_s - T_i}$$ So the equation becomes: $$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}$$ with conditions: $\theta(x,0) = 0$, $\theta(0,t) = 1$, $\theta(\infty, t) = 0$. ### Step 4 — Error-Function Solution (Standard Method) Using the method of similarity variable $\eta = x / (2\sqrt{\alpha t})$, the solution is: $$\theta(x, t) = \text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$ where $\text{erfc}$ is the complementary error function. ### Step 5 — Penetration Depth Definition Conventionally, penetration depth $x$ is defined where the surface temperature has effectively penetrated such that the **isotherm at $T_i$ has moved to depth $x$**. For engineering approximation, take the depth where the local temperature reaches $\theta = 0.1$ (i.e., 10 % of full thermal change): $$\text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right) = 0.1$$ Looking up error-function tables: $$\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \approx 1.16$$ ### Step 6 — Final Expression $$\boxed{x \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha t}}$$ ### Step 7 — Real-World Substitution For a tomato with $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and $t = 30$ s: $$x = 2\sqrt{(1.5 \times 10^{-7})(30)}$$ $$x = 2\sqrt{4.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$x = 2 \times 2.121 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{x \approx 4.24 \text{ mm}}$$ This is the depth of thermal damage → the skin down to **≈ 4 mm depth** is loosened and easily removed by water jets. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Peroxidase Inactivation Time ### Statement Determine the time required for 90 % inactivation of peroxidase at $T = 90 °\text{C}$ for carrots, given $k = 0.42 \text{ min}^{-1}$. ### Step 1 — Kinetic Equation $$\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k t$$ For 90 % inactivation: $$\frac{C}{C_0} = 0.1$$ ### Step 2 — Solve for t $$\ln(0.1) = -k t$$ $$t = \frac{-\ln(0.1)}{k}$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation $$t = \frac{-(-2.303)}{0.42}$$ $$t = \frac{2.303}{0.42}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 5.48 \text{ min}}$$ ### Interpretation At 90 °C, carrot peroxidase requires **≈ 5.5 min** for 90 % inactivation. Industrial blanchers typically over-design for 99.9 % inactivation (D-value safety margin). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Peeling Methods | Criterion | Manual | Mechanical | Chemical (Lye) | Thermal | |-----------|--------|------------|----------------|---------| | Capital Cost | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | | Operating Cost | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | | Throughput | ★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | Peel Loss (%) | 20–30 | 15–25 | 8–15 | 5–12 | | Quality Retention | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | | Effluent Load | None | Water | High (Alkaline) | Low (Steam condensate) | | Operator Skill | High | Medium | Low | Low | | Energy | Nil | Electric | Heat + Chemical | Heat | | Scale | Cottage | Small-Medium | Medium-Large | Large | --- ## 3.4 Slicing/Crushing/Extraction – Engineering Derivation ### Power Required for Rotary Slicing Power $P$ required to slice a continuous product stream at throughput $Q$ kg/h against shear stress $\tau$ Pa with cut length $L_c$ m per kg: $$P = \frac{Q \cdot \tau \cdot L_c}{3.6 \times 10^6} \quad (\text{kW})$$ ### Juice Yield Expression (Holmes Continuity) For a hydraulic press: $$Y_{juice} = \frac{V_{juice}}{M_{fruit}} \times \rho_{juice}$$ $$\eta_{press} = \frac{M_{juice}}{M_{fruit} - M_{pomace}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Comparative Advantage — Peeling Methods > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Pattern Tip:** When asked *"Which is the best peeling method for tomato on industrial scale?"*, the standard KTU answer is **Lye peeling**, followed by **steam peeling** as the next preferred method. Always justify with the following key points: > 1. Lowest peel loss (5–10 %). > 2. Continuous operation possible. > 3. Minimal damage to inner pulp. > 4. Suitable for irregular shapes (unlike mechanical). --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Flow — Peeling Method Selection Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Horticultural Produce] --> B{Scale of Operation?} B -- Cottage / Small --> C[Manual Peeling] B -- Medium --> D{Peel Type?} B -- Large Industrial --> E{Heat-Resistant Commodity?} D -- Thin Skin --> F[Abrasive Roller Mechanical] D -- Thick Skin --> G[Knife Mechanical] E -- Yes --> H[Steam / Hot Water Thermal Peeling] E -- No --> I[Lye Chemical Peeling] C --> J[Output: Peeled Produce] F --> J G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[Wash + Neutralize if needed] K --> L[Next Unit Operation: Slicing / Dicing / Pulping] ``` ## 4.2 Lye Peeling Process Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Produce] --> B[Washing Tank] B --> C[Lye Tank
NaOH 2 to 10 percent
60 to 100 degree C] C --> D[Residence Time 15s to 5min] D --> E[Mechanical Abrasion Roller] E --> F[Water Spray Wash] F --> G[Citric Acid Neutralizer
0.5 to 1 percent] G --> H[Final Water Rinse] H --> I[Peeled Produce Output] C -.-> J[Lye Recirculation and Reuse] J -.-> C ``` ## 4.3 Blanching Process Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Produce] --> B[Sorting and Washing] B --> C[Blanching Unit
Hot Water 90 to 100 degree C
or Steam] C --> D[Time 1 to 10 min] D --> E[Quench Tank
Cold Water 4 to 10 degree C] E --> F[Dewatering Shaker] F --> G[Blanched Produce] C -.-> H[Peroxidase Test Check] H -.-> C ``` ## 4.4 Slicing / Shredding / Crushing / Juice Extraction Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Blanched / Peeled Produce] --> B{Operation Required?} B -- Slicing --> C[Rotary Slicer or Disk Slicer] B -- Shredding --> D[Shredder Drum with Knives] B -- Chopping --> E[Curved Bowl Chopper] B -- Crushing --> F[Twin Roller Crusher] B -- Juice --> G{Juice Method?} G -- Hydraulic --> H[Hydraulic Press] G -- Screw --> I[Screw Press] G -- Centrifugal --> J[Centrifugal Extractor] G -- Enzymatic --> K[Pectinase Cellulase Pretreat then Press] C --> L[Downstream Processing] D --> L E --> L F --> L H --> L I --> L J --> L K --> L ``` ## 4.5 Juice Extraction Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fruit Reception] --> B[Washing] B --> C[Sorting] C --> D[Size Reduction
Crushing or Slicing] D --> E[Optional Enzyme Pretreatment] E --> F[Pressing or Centrifugation] F --> G[Crude Juice] G --> H[Filtration] H --> I[Clarified Juice] F --> J[Pomace Byproduct] J --> K[Byproduct Use
Animal Feed or Pectin] ``` ## 4.6 Enzyme Inactivation Kinetics in Blanching ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initial Enzyme Activity C0] --> B[Heat Applied 85 to 100 degree C] B --> C[Time t min] C --> D[Residual Activity C] D --> E{C less than threshold?} E -- No --> C E -- Yes --> F[Blanching Complete] F --> G[Cold Water Quench] G --> H[Stabilised Produce] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between hot water blanching and steam blanching. State two advantages of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Hot Water Blanching | Steam Blanching | |-----------|---------------------|-----------------| | Medium | Boiling water (85–100 °C) | Saturated steam (100 °C) | | Heat transfer | Convection (water → produce) | Condensation (steam → produce) | | Nutrient leaching | **High** (water-soluble vitamins leached out) | **Low** (minimal leaching) | | Energy requirement | High (water heating + latent losses) | Lower (latent heat only) | | Uniformity | High | Moderate (surface heating) | | Product suitability | Most vegetables | Leafy vegetables, large fruits | **Advantages of Hot Water Blanching:** (1) Uniform heating, (2) Simple equipment, (3) Suitable for bulk produce. **Advantages of Steam Blanching:** (1) Better nutrient retention, (2) No leaching of vitamins, (3) Lower effluent volume. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Define the term "lye peeling". Explain the role of NaOH concentration and temperature.** **Model Answer:** *Lye peeling* is a chemical peeling method in which the outer skin of fruits/vegetables is removed by immersing the produce in a **hot alkali solution (typically NaOH 2–10 %)** at elevated temperature (60–100 °C). The alkali **hydrolyses the pectin in the middle lamella** of the epidermal cells, loosening the peel which is then removed by mechanical abrasion. - **NaOH concentration** ↑ → faster peel removal BUT increased risk of NaOH penetration into pulp. - **Temperature** ↑ → faster reaction rate, shorter residence time, but softens texture. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write *"peeling removes dirt"* and *"slicing reduces size"*. These are *not* engineering answers. Always mention **% peel loss, throughput (kg/h), principle (shear/abrasion/chemical/thermal), equipment name, and quality parameter affected**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Peeling Methods Focus** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** #### (a) Classify the different methods of peeling horticultural produce. Describe the working principle, construction, and operational parameters of a **mechanical abrasive roller peeler** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)** #### (b) Explain the **principle, working, and engineering parameters** of **chemical (lye) peeling**. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages over thermal peeling. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Classification of Peeling Methods:** 1. Manual peeling 2. Mechanical peeling (abrasive, knife, roller-knife) 3. Chemical (lye) peeling 4. Thermal peeling (steam, hot water, flame, IR) 5. Enzymatic peeling 6. Cryogenic peeling **Construction of Abrasive Roller Peeler:** - A **horizontal cylindrical drum** lined with **carborundum (SiC) abrasive** of grit **20–60 mesh**. - The drum rotates at **200–600 rpm**. - A **water spray pipe** runs along the length of the drum. - A **discharge chute** at one end. **Working Principle:** 1. Produce fed into drum via hopper. 2. Centrifugal force + gravity tosses produce against abrasive lining. 3. **Shear + abrasion** removes peel uniformly. 4. Water spray continuously washes off removed peel. 5. Peeled produce discharged at outlet end. **Operational Parameters:** - Throughput: **300–2000 kg/h**. - Drum rpm: **200–600**. - Residence time: **30 s – 3 min**. - Peel loss: **15–25 %**. --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Principle of Lye Peeling:** When produce is dipped in **hot NaOH (2–10 %, 60–100 °C, 15 s–5 min)**, the alkali **hydrolyses the pectin** of the middle lamella between peel and pulp cells, dissolving the adhesive pectin layer and releasing the skin. **Process Steps:** 1. Washing and sorting of produce. 2. Immersion in **hot NaOH** for controlled time. 3. **Mechanical scrubbing** with rubber rollers or high-pressure water jets. 4. **Neutralisation** in **0.5–1 % citric acid** for 30–60 s. 5. Final water rinse. **Key Engineering Parameters:** | Parameter | Range | |-----------|-------| | NaOH concentration | 2–10 % | | Temperature | 60–100 °C | | Time | 15 s – 5 min | | Acid neutraliser | 0.5–1 % citric acid | | Residence scrubber | 5–20 s | **Advantages of Lye Peeling over Thermal:** 1. **Lower peel loss** (5–10 % vs 8–15 %). 2. **Uniform peeling** even for irregular shapes (e.g., tomato). 3. **High throughput** (continuous). 4. **Less heat damage** to inner pulp. **Disadvantages:** 1. Generates **alkaline effluent** → requires wastewater treatment. 2. Risk of **residual NaOH** if neutralisation is improper. 3. Higher capital cost than thermal peeling. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Slicing / Blanching Focus** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** #### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **rotary slicer**. Discuss the factors affecting slice thickness and throughput. **(7 marks)** #### (b) Define blanching. Explain its **importance and objectives** in post-harvest processing. Differentiate between **hot water and steam blanching** with reference to nutrient retention. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Rotary Slicer — Construction & Working:** A rotary slicer consists of: 1. **Cylindrical drum** rotating at **600–1500 rpm**. 2. **Stationary curved blades** mounted inside the drum casing. 3. **Feed hopper** at one end. 4. **Discharge chute** at the other end. **Working:** - Produce fed into the rotating drum. - **Centrifugal force** throws produce against stationary blades. - Blades slice produce to a thickness equal to **blade clearance**. - Sliced pieces discharged through chute. **Factors Affecting Slice Thickness:** 1. Blade clearance (set between **1–10 mm**). 2. Blade sharpness. 3. Drum rpm (higher rpm → thinner slice). **Factors Affecting Throughput:** 1. Drum rpm. 2. Blade length. 3. Feed rate. 4. Produce moisture content. --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Definition:** Blanching is a *short-duration thermal pre-treatment* (hot water or steam at 85–100 °C for 1–10 min) applied to horticultural produce before drying, freezing, or canning, primarily to inactivate spoilage enzymes. **Importance and Objectives:** | Objective | Mechanism | |-----------|-----------| | **Enzyme inactivation** | Denatures peroxidase, catalase, PPO | | **Colour fixation** | Expels intra-cellular gases | | **Texture softening** | Controlled gelatinisation of pectin | | **Microbial reduction** | 1–2 log cycle kill | | **Improves drying rate** | Reduces case-hardening | | **Removes off-flavours** | Stops enzymatic browning | **Comparison — Hot Water vs Steam Blanching on Nutrient Retention:** | Nutrient / Property | Hot Water | Steam | |---------------------|-----------|-------| | Vitamin C retention | 60–75 % | **80–90 %** | | Vitamin B retention | 70–80 % | **85–95 %** | | Sugar leaching | **High** | Low | | Salt loss | High | Low | | Texture softening | Greater | Moderate | **Conclusion:** *Steam blanching is preferred for nutrient-sensitive produce* like leafy greens and vitamin-rich fruits, while *hot water blanching* is preferred for *uniform bulk processing* of vegetables like carrots and peas. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Writing "peeling removes dirt"** — WRONG. Peeling removes *epidermal tissue*, not dirt. Always state the engineering reason. > 2. **Confusing *blanching* with *cooking*** — Blanching is partial enzyme inactivation, NOT cooking. Cooking is full gelatinisation. > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always write throughput in **kg/h**, concentration in **% (w/v)**, time in **seconds or minutes**. > 4. **Skipping flow diagrams in 14-mark questions** — KTU valuation key explicitly awards **2 marks** for a *labelled diagram*. > 5. **Writing "lye is acid"** — Lye is **alkali (NaOH/KOH)**, pH > 12. Marks deducted for this confusion. > 6. **Stating "peroxidase inactivation is for colour"** — Peroxidase is the **indicator enzyme**; the real quality objective is to stop **PPO (polyphenol oxidase)** which causes browning. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Peeling** is a primary size-reduction operation preparing produce for downstream processing. - **Manual peeling** has 20–30 % peel loss; suited only for cottage scale. - **Mechanical abrasive roller peeler** uses **carborundum** lining at 200–600 rpm; peel loss 15–25 %. - **Knife mechanical peeler** works on the **lathe principle**; suited for apples/mangoes. - **Lye peeling** uses **hot NaOH (2–10 %, 60–100 °C)**; principle is **pectin hydrolysis** of the middle lamella. - **Chemical peeling must be followed by acid neutralisation** (0.5–1 % citric acid). - **Thermal peeling** uses **steam or hot water**; governed by $x \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha t}$. - **Steam peeling** causes less nutrient loss than hot water peeling. - **Rotary slicer** produces slices by **centrifugal force throwing produce onto stationary blades**. - **Shredders** produce julienne strips; **choppers** produce irregular chunks. - **Crushers** rupture cell walls for pulp; **juice extractors** separate liquid from pomace. - **Hydraulic press** gives highest juice yield (70–85 %); **enzymatic pretreatment + press** gives 85–92 %. - **Blanching** is a **thermal pre-treatment**, NOT cooking. - **Indicator enzyme for blanching** = **peroxidase** (most heat-resistant). - **Blanching objectives**: enzyme inactivation, colour fixation, gas expulsion, microbial reduction, texture softening. - **Hot water blanching** has more nutrient leaching; **steam blanching** has better retention. - **Enzyme inactivation kinetics** follow **first-order**: $\ln(C/C_0) = -kt$. - **Peroxidase D-value** at 90 °C for carrots ≈ **5–6 min** for 90 % inactivation. ---

topics.title: Blanching Methods, Effects on Food (Nutrition, Colour, Pigment, Texture), Physiological Changes During Ripening, Maturity Index, Firmness, Sugar Acid Ratio, Respiration, Pre-Cooling
